2 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Foreword
As we share the 2025 edition of the IFRC International First Aid, Resuscitation and Education Guidelines, I
am struck by both the enduring relevance of first aid and the remarkable progress made across our Red
Cross and Red Crescent network to ensure that life-saving knowledge is placed in the hands of millions.
Over the past five years, the world has continued to face profound challenges: public health emergencies,
natural disasters driven by climate change, conflict and displacement, and rising mental health needs. In
every crisis, one constant remains - people helping people by placing humanity at the centre of our work.
But how? First aid remains one of the most empowering tools we have to preserve life, reduce suffering,
and foster resilience in communities.
First aid is not only part of our legacy - it is central to our future. It stands at the heart of the IFRC Strategy
2030, anchoring our ambition to support strong local action and global solidarity. As we build a future
rooted in community health, equity, and preparedness, first aid is an essential pathway to achieving our
collective vision.
This 2025 edition marks a significant milestone in the global leadership of the International Federation of
Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) in first aid education. This comes on the heels of other
important milestones. In 2022, the General Assembly of the IFRC unanimously adopted its new First Aid
Policy and its First Aid Vision 2030 with the objective of repositioning first aid at the core of our action. In
2023, the IFRC became a collaborating member of the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation;
an extraordinary achievement that affirms the credibility, scientific rigour and global impact of our work.
What distinguishes these Guidelines is not only their foundation in up-to-date research and best
practices, but their grounding in the diverse realities of our Red Cross and Red Crescent network. With 191
National Societies operating in vastly different social, economic, and cultural contexts, our approach to
first aid is uniquely shaped by local wisdom and needs.
The contributions from every region of the world have made this edition truly global and deeply
committed to the operational reality of everyday life in our National Societies. Through our
contextualization and engagement, more than 60 National Societies and IFRC offices contributed their
knowledge and experience directly to the work. The Guidelines serve as both a resource and a reflection of
the Red Cross Red Crescent’s unparalleled reach and commitment to first aid for all. They are built not
only on scientific evidence, but on the lived practice and insight of thousands of first aid trainers,
education programme designers, volunteers, and learners around the world.
I commend the IFRC Global First Aid Reference Centre and IFRC Health and Care Department for their
stewardship of this process. I also thank the many National Societies, researchers, and practitioners who
have contributed their voices, knowledge, and passion. These Guidelines are a testament to our shared
belief that everyone, everywhere, should have the confidence and ability to save a life.
Sincerely,
Jagan Chapagain
Secretary General
International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Acknowledgements
The International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines (referred to as the Guidelines)
is an international publication made in collaboration with Red Cross Red Crescent Societies, first
aid specialists and task-forces. This collaboration of work by volunteers and staff is coordinated
by the Global First Aid Reference Centre and a dedicated steering committee. The Global First
Aid Reference Centre warmly thanks and values all these important contributions.

10 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

INTRODUCTION

12 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid is a core area of work of the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent
Societies (IFRC). All 191 National Red Cross Red Crescent Societies around the globe deliver first
aid services, or implement first aid education or awareness programmes.
We believe that first aid is a vital initial step in providing effective and rapid intervention, which
can help reduce injury and suffering and improve the chances of survival. Taking immediate
action and applying appropriate first aid measures makes a difference. Having high-quality,
evidence-based first aid education available to people worldwide contributes to building safer
and healthier communities by preventing and reducing risks in daily emergency, disaster and
crisis situations.
First aid is part of a common vision: to inspire, encourage, facilitate and promote humanitarian
activities by alleviating human suffering and respecting human dignity. We believe everyone has
the potential to save lives. First aid education and services are essential tools for achieving these
goals.
We advocate for first aid to be accessible to all and that at least one person in each household
has access to learning first aid, regardless of their socio-economic status or other potentially
discriminatory factors.
IFRC first aid vision 2030
The IFRC First Aid Policy and the IFRC First Aid Vision 2030 were adopted by the General
Assembly of Red Cross and Red Crescent National Societies in June 2022.
The First Aid Policy states that first aid is an act of humanity, showing a willingness to help
others and save lives without discrimination through impartiality, neutrality and independence.
First aid, as we know it today, is built through the unity of individuals who contribute through
voluntary service, reflecting the universality of need throughout the world.
In 2030, first aid is:
● Universal in that it is available to all who are motivated to help themselves, their families
and communities in an emergency, with one person trained in first aid by National Red
Cross Red Crescent Societies in every home, workplace and school.
● Recognized as a force of humanity that is integral to the foundations of the IFRC and
National Societies, as reflected in the IFRC Strategy 2030, and recognized as the partner
of choice by public authorities, other stakeholders and the public.
● Mainstreamed in the activities of the IFRC to build resilience, accountability and trust
within communities globally.
The IFRC First Aid Vision 2030 and its operational framework are part of IFRC First Aid Policy
2030.
This First Aid Vision 2030 covers six priority action areas. These priority action areas serve to focus
the efforts of the IFRC and National Societies regarding first aid. The areas are:
1. First aid education
2. Standards and qualifications
3. Research, evidence and evaluation
INTRODUCTION | 13

4. First aid in schools
5. Commercial first aid
6. Pre-hospital emergency care and operations
Based on its deep commitment to science-based first aid and resuscitation, and considering the
longstanding collaboration with the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR),
the IFRC became a collaborating member of ILCOR in June 2023.
About the IFRC Global First Aid Reference Centre
The IFRC Global First Aid Reference Centre (GFARC) was established in 2012. The Reference
Centre is hosted and supported by the French Red Cross and collaborates with 191 National Red
Cross and Red Crescent Societies, as well as scientific bodies, academic institutions and private
sectors.
The Reference Centre aims to reduce the number of deaths and the severity of injuries by
supporting National Societies in scaling up and improving the quality of first aid education and
services worldwide. It works closely with National Societies to enhance first aid programmes and
services based on science, facilitate knowledge sharing, promote good practice, distribute skills
and advocate for first aid for all.
To ensure the IFRC first aid is evidence-based and remains relevant to national and local
contexts, the Reference Centre works with National Societies’ first aid, medical and education
experts, and the ICRC, and participates in numerous studies and research. The IFRC
International first aid and resuscitation guidelines 2025 is a key outcome of such an effort.
Number of people reached
In 2023, more than 22 million people were trained in first aid by NationalRed Cross Red Crescent
Societies, according to the global survey carried out in 2025 by the Reference Centre. 126 out of
191 National Societies responded to the survey. Around the globe, the National Red Cross Red
Crescent Societies have almost 150,000 active first aid trainers.
Every year more than 100 National Red Cross Red Crescent Societies participate in the World
First Aid Day on the second Saturday of September with the support of the Reference Centre.
During this event, almost one million volunteers and staff are mobilized and reach out to
millions of people through social media and public events with the aim of raising awareness
among the public and partners about the importance of first aid, particularly first aid education.
About the 2025 IFRC Guidelines
The International first aid resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 evaluate and report on
the science and best practices behind first aid, resuscitation and education. The 2025 Guidelines
have been produced with the main goal of supporting National Societies in harmonizing first
aid practices across the Red Cross Red Crescent Movement by providing a strong evidence base.
This includes working in collaboration with many partners, including those that are part of the
Movement, such as National Societies, IFRC Region and field offices, the International
Committee of the Red Cross Red Crescent, reference centres and other international
organizations, such as ILCOR. This forms a key part of the quality assurance that people receive
14 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

first aid education in accordance with the most up-to-date science and best practices from
experts and practitioners.
The International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines form the basis of various
programmes developed by the IFRC to improve and promote high-quality first aid programmes
across the National Societies. The International First Aid Attestation delivered by GFARC is a
continuous quality improvement process and recognition, awarded when a first aid training
course provided by a National Red Cross Red Crescent Society, is consistent with the latest IFRC
Guidelines. GFARC’s international first aid education programme aims to develop trainers of
trainers within National Societies based on the latest pedagogical science, in order to distribute
high-quality education skills throughout the training systems of the NationalRed Cross Red
Crescent Societies.
The Guidelines do not replace first aid manuals and associated educational materials. Instead,
they serve as the foundational resources for first aid programme designers to develop their first
aid materials such as manuals, programmes, courses, learning apps, digital products, public
information and associated educational materials. National Societies should use and adapt the
Guidelines according to their local cultural, linguistic, technological, environmental and legal
contexts, including the local prevalence of injuries and illnesses. The adaptation must also
consider the populations’ capacities and available resources. In addition, the Guidelines provide
scientific evidence for first aid programme managers and designers to make strategic decisions.
First aid education
Through engaging with first aid education and the fundamental principles of the IFRC, we
reinforce the values of humanity by giving people the skills, confidence and willingness to help.
It is the role of the Red Cross Red Crescent Movement to champion first aid education as a
universal means to help people engage in more helping behaviours and to live more safely, be
more resilient and provide care when needed.
The IFRC views first aid as one of the greatest humanitarian acts, for which first aid education
has an integral part in building personal and communal resilience. As such, it is the duty of the
IFRC to advocate for and provide effective, high-quality, affordable and accessible first aid
education to everyone.
The IFRC’s effectiveness will be magnified if first aid actions are recognized and encouraged by
healthcare professionals. Embedding first aid education into national health policies and culture,
both as a life skill that is learned by all, and a resilience and emergency response tool that is
recognized as a vital part of health systems, is an important role for the NationalRed Cross Red
Crescent Societies. The 2025 Guidelines promote evidence-based practice as the foundation for
first aid education by ensuring that the best available scientific evidence is combined with the
preferences and resources of the target group and the practical experience and expertise of
experts in the field.
A fundamental distinction is made between harmonization and standardization. The intention is
not to have a separate technique for each situation or to produce recommendations to be
implemented in the same way everywhere regardless of context, but rather to establish a
consensus on minimum agreed-upon principles. This consensus is based on a critical review of
INTRODUCTION | 15

the available evidence and information learnt from the experiences of the Red Cross Red
Crescent Movement.
Link to IFRC’s Strategy 2030
First aid is central to the five global challenges identified in the IFRC’s Strategy 2030:
1. Climate and environmental crises: first aid as a resilience enabler allows communities to
prepare for and respond to local climate-related emergencies.
2. Evolving crises and disasters: first aid education develops communities’ capacity to
respond immediately, particularly in cases where emergency medical services are
delayed or hindered.
3. Growing gaps in health and well-being: first aid empowers communities in prevention,
health promotion and psychological first aid according to common injuries and health
priorities, reducing the burden on health systems.
4. Migration and identity: first aid facilitates the inclusion of vulnerable people and migrants
to learn first aid and save lives, regardless of their situation.
5. Values, power and inclusion: first aid is an act of humanity, showing a willingness to save
lives without discrimination.
The Guidelines provide evidence and good practice in first aid knowledge, treatment and
education to contribute to the IFRC 2030 Strategy. By promoting and using proven first aid
techniques to address key emergency-related injuries, we can build local communities’
preparedness and response capacity.
Definitions
First aid is the immediate assistance provided to an ill or injured person until professional help
arrives. It is concerned not only with physical illness or injury but also with psychosocial support
for people who are emotionally distressed due to experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event.
First aid interventions seek to alleviate suffering, prevent further illness or injury and promote
recovery.
First aid provider is defined as someone trained in first aid who should recognize, assess and
prioritize the need for first aid. The first aid provider provides care using appropriate
competencies, recognizes limitations and seeks additional care when needed.
This document refers to the ill or injured person requiring first aid as the person. First aid
educators should use the appropriate term according to their local contexts. This person may be
described as the person in need of care, patient, victim, casualty or other terms.
Emergency refers to an incident where first aid is required. An emergency can be small or large
and can happen in a private or public space.
Emergency medical services (EMS) signify that the first aid provider should quickly access the
next available higher level of care. In some places, EMS may mean an ambulance service and
hospital, while in others it may mean contacting a local healthcare worker or travelling to a field
hospital. Programme designers will need to adapt the language according to the local context
in which it is used.
16 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid education is a programme that develops behaviours, knowledge or skills in first aid
procedures and techniques. This programme has defined outcomes and is provided by qualified
facilitators or published physically or virtually.
First aid services may provide first aid at public events or respond to potential emergencies,
among others. These services are based on agreements established between local authorities,
event organizers or local communities and the organization providing the service.
Levels of first aid provider
The levels of providers available around the world vary considerably. The continuum below
describes the increasing level of training and responsibility of providers. The Guidelines support
first aid, resuscitation and education for the following levels of first aid provider.

Other roles in providing medical care
There are other roles that provide medical care. These are often referred to collectively as
advanced care. They include:
First responder
● A first responder has a professional responsibility to respond as a primary role of their
position.
● First responders are often dispatched by a central organization and are directed to the
incident; they are typically the first step in a formal emergency medical service.
Paramedic
● A paramedic provides advanced care according to their profession in varied
environments.
● A paramedic may be the first responder to arrive in an emergency.
● A paramedic can often provide sufficient care for the person to return to their activities;
however, a paramedic also provides transport services to other health services as required
(e.g. hospital).
In-hospital care
● In-hospital care refers to the services provided by doctors, nurses and other healthcare
professionals who deliver emergency care within the hospital setting. Those providing
in-hospital care work in a controlled environment with access to all required tools and
professionals available.
Other terms used frequently in these Guidelines
CPR Cardiopulmonary resuscitation
CEBaP Centre for Evidence-based Practice (Belgian Red Cross–Flanders)
ICRC International Committee of the Red Cross
IFRC International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

ILCOR International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation
The Movement The Red Cross Red Crescent Movement
Using the Guidelines 2025
Because this document is extensive, it is encouraged to use its built-in navigation features to
move efficiently between sections.
● Key topics are linked throughout the guidelines to help readers make connections across
content; for example, CPR is generally linked to the topic on unresponsiveness and
abnormal breathing for adults and adolescents, with a clear cross-link to the child and
baby topic.
● An easy-to-access table of contents on the inside cover allows quick navigation to
guideline chapters.
● The topic index offers a more comprehensive way to locate specific subjects across
contexts and populations.
● The gear icon ( ⚙) at the bottom of even-numbered pages provides direct access to the
topic index from anywhere in the document.
● Referencing is integrated through in-text citations in parentheses throughout, with a
consolidated reference list at the end of the document, organized into four sections for
ease of use: Contextualizing first aid and first aid education, First aid education, First aid
and Resuscitation.
Future development
These Guidelines are not exhaustive. For this edition, topics requested by National Societies and
others considered current and relevant have been included. First aid topics that were not
reviewed have been excluded from this list. National Societies are invited to reach out to the
Global First Aid Reference Centre if there are topics for which they need an evidence base to be
considered for future analysis.
INTRODUCTION | 19

Process to develop the 2025 Guidelines
Red Cross and Red Crescent National Societies independently developed their national
guidelines for many years. Since 2011, the IFRC, through the Global First Aid Reference Centre,
has published evidence-based international guidelines drawing on the best available science
and practices from the field.
In 2022, following the successful adoption of First Aid Vision 2030 at the General Assembly, the
Global First Aid Reference Centre began the process of updating and developing the
International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025. This process included
recruiting subject matter experts and conducting a needs analysis with first aid education
programme developers to determine priority topics. The Steering Committee established a
structured process for conducting evidence reviews and cataloguing evidence-based practices.
Evidence-based guidelines
Approach to the development of evidence-based guidelines
The Guidelines are developed in accordance with the principles of evidence-based practice.
Evidence-based guidelines integrate three key sources of information:
1. The best available scientific evidence, identified and collected through systematic
database searches.
2. Practical experience and expertise contributed by experts in the field.
3. Preferences, values and available resources of target groups, such as first aid providers
and people receiving first aid, to ensure recommendations are relevant and feasible.
The Steering Committee began by selecting the topics to be included in the Guidelines. In
addition to the existing topics, one new topic, anxiety and panic, was identified for inclusion. For
each topic, the Committee systematically collected up-to-date evidence.
As a leader in first aid, resuscitation and education science, the IFRC network has traditionally
worked in partnership with local resuscitation councils and their parent organizations. In 2024,
the IFRC became an official Collaborating Member with the International Liaison Committee on
Resuscitation (ILCOR) and formally contributed to the Consensus on Science and Treatment
Recommendations. For the 2025 Guidelines, ILCOR publications from 2020 to 2025 were
consulted for topics relevant to the IFRC. In addition, evidence summaries developed by the
Centre for Evidence-Based Practice of the Belgian Red Cross–Flanders were updated between
2023 and 2025.
Where an evidence summary was considered stable, meaning the evidence was of high
certainty or the intervention was widely accepted as common sense and supported by existing
evidence, no further updates were undertaken.
Education topics followed a similar development process. With input from first aid educators
and the Steering Committee, the list of education topics was revised by consolidating previous
topics and developing new ones to respond to the needs of first aid educators across the
International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. Evidence summaries were updated
through a systematic review process. A total of 5,295 manuscripts identified through systematic
20 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

searches related to first aid and first aid education were sorted by topic. Two to three first aid
education experts from diverse global regions synthesized the relevant studies to inform the
updated Guidelines.
Based on the updated evidence, draft recommendations were revised as needed. Monthly
conference calls were held to monitor progress. Each topic was developed collaboratively by
members of the clinical and education working groups.
Clinical topics were reviewed by the clinical working group, after which educational
considerations were added through a review of education-focused literature and expert input.
Topics were framed using the chain of survival behaviours. Reviewers with expertise from both
high- and low-resource settings were consulted. For topics particularly relevant to conflict
settings, reviewers from the ICRC provided expert input and additional literature insights. ILCOR
members also reviewed topics aligned with ILCOR publications to ensure consistency.
Engagement and contextualization
In spring 2025, an Engagement Committee was established to support the contextualization of
the Guidelines. This committee included 66 participants from all five IFRC Regions–Africa,
Americas, Asia-Pacific, Europe and Middle East-North Africa–and met eight times over three
months to review 24 topics. More than 500 recommendations were considered for inclusion.
Recommendations were incorporated into education considerations and chain of survival
behaviours to support local implementation.
Use of generative artificial intelligence
ChatGPT (GPT-5) was used as a language-support tool to promote consistency and clarity
throughout the document. It supported synthesis of contributions from multiple volunteer
authors and organizations and alignment of terminology. The use of generative AI reflects
emerging, accepted practices in the humanitarian sector to responsibly apply digital tools that
enhance collaboration, inclusivity and efficiency, while maintaining human oversight,
transparency and ethical standards. All content refined using generative AI was reviewed, edited
and verified by human authors to ensure accuracy, clarity and fidelity to the intended meaning.
Generative AI was used specifically to support the development of the 2025 Guidelines front
matter, Evidence to action, Contexts and Education sections.
Structure of the Guidelines
Significant effort was made to clearly link recommendations to first aid education. Each topic
now includes expanded content to support programme designers in developing confident and
capable first aid providers. Education is emphasized as the primary mechanism for building
ability and confidence, reflected in the inclusion of education in the Guidelines title.
Each topic includes:
● Key action
● Introduction
● Guidelines and good practice points (GPPs)
● Chain of survival behaviours
● Education considerations
INTRODUCTION | 21

● Scientific foundation
These sections build sequentially from the scientific foundation. While organized for practical
use by programme designers, they function as interconnected building blocks:
● Scientific foundation establishes the evidence base.
● Guidelines are derived from the scientific foundation. Good practice points are derived
from non-systematic reviews and expert experience to supplement where evidence is not
available.
● Key actions and chain of survival behaviours are derived from the Guidelines and good
practice points.
● Education considerations support programme development based on key actions and
chain of survival behaviours.
Scientific foundation and education evidence
Recommendations are informed by the ILCOR scientific reviews up to 2025, CEBaP evidence
summaries, independent scientific reviews and expert opinion.
Education evidence was guided by two research questions:
1. In laypeople, how do different first aid learning modalities compare in terms of patient,
learner, and societal outcomes?
2. What factors influence implementation of first aid education interventions across
different target groups?
Inclusion and exclusion criteria
Included studies focused on first aid education, chain of survival behaviours, lay responders (e.g.
Red Cross Red Crescent staff or volunteers), bystanders, and contextual inputs (e.g. disaster,
conflict, health crisis). Excluded studies focused on healthcare professionals or advanced
instructor training. Where evidence was limited or absent, expert consensus informed good
practice points.
First aid topics
In each topic, a summary of the scientific foundation is provided. The following evidence sources
were included:
● Up-to-date evidence summaries by CEBaP (updated between 2023 and 2025); all
summaries are available via registration to the CEBaP First Aid Evidence Summary
Database.
● Systematic reviews conducted by ILCOR; we refer to the ILCOR Consensus on Science
with Treatment Recommendations publication or to separately published ILCOR
systematic reviews in the reference list of the topics. Other relevant systematic reviews are
identified by the topic contributor.
The findings of these existing evidence sources are summarized under the sub-heading
Systematic reviews. The overall quality of the evidence (“certainty of evidence”) was taken over
from the original evidence sources. In addition, a sub-heading Non-systematic reviews is
provided for many topics. The paragraph contains additional expert opinion or findings based on
22 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

individual studies or other sources of information. These sources of information were gathered
in a non-systematic way, and were used to formulate the good practice points (i.e.
recommendations where no systematically identified evidence is available, but which are
“common sense” and there is consensus among the involved reviewers).
In addition to rigorous review by experts in the field, all topics drawing on evidence from
ILCOR have been reviewed to ensure alignment with the ILCOR Consensus on Science with
Treatment Recommendations. While the Guidelines also incorporate expert input and
context-specific considerations, this review process ensures that no content contradicts
ILCOR publications.
There are differences between the recommendations of the guidelines for cardiopulmonary
resuscitation (CPR ) in adults and children and for airway clearance.
These differences are mainly based on:
● different methodological choices
● distinct cultural and organizational contexts
● varying interpretations of the same scientific data (ILCOR).
In addition, most of these differences depend on access to emergency services, which varies
considerably, requiring for some simple and immediate actions with rapid transfer to hospital,
and for others immediate actions while waiting for advanced paramedical or medical teams to
arrive, allowing for more precise and prioritized interventions.
INTRODUCTION | 23

Guidelines and good practice points
Guidelines were developed by weighing evidence quality, benefits, harms, risks, preferences and
contextual factors. Recommendations are classified as strong (**), weak (*), or good practice
points , with implications for practice clearly defined.

Where no clear evidence was available or missing, but clinical practice or expert opinion is
available, good practice points were formulated based on the experience of National Red Cross
Red Crescent Societies or based on the non-systematic review sources provided in the scientific
foundation.
Key action
The key action describes the most important action related to the topic. For education topics, it
highlights the key takeaway for programme designers in relation to the education modality. For
first aid topics, it identifies the key action that programme designers should emphasize to
learners. Inclusion of the key action supports the development of training and educational
materials.
24 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Chain of survival behaviours
Each topic is considered through the domains of the chain of survival behaviours. These
domains represent the most effective actions to prevent, recognize or treat an illness or injury,
based on the scientific foundation and the guidelines. They are presented as instructional steps;
however, adaptation may be required to reflect different contexts.
For example, where first aid providers are unlikely to have access to specific first aid equipment
(such as clean water), appropriate alternatives should be used. Educators should emphasize the
importance of applying all feasible first aid actions to reduce pain, suffering and further harm,
even when it is not possible to apply every step in a given context or situation.
INTRODUCTION | 25

Education considerations
Education considerations describe how location, environment, access to resources and other
contextual factors may influence how a topic is taught. These considerations are informed by a
combination of evidence (where a systematic review has been conducted and referenced) and
expert opinion. Elements may include:
● Describe how location, environment, access to resources and
Context considerations
other local factors may influence how a topic is taught.
● Describe factors that programme designers should consider in
relation to learners.
● Identify and recommend strategies to reduce barriers to

providing first aid.
● Identify relevant social norms associated with the topic and
strategies to respect those norms while providing first aid.
● Identify teaching approaches, adaptations and key points to
Facilitation tips and emphasize in order to strengthen learning.
tools ● Highlight common training gaps or missteps.
● Suggest tools to support effective training.
Learning connections ● Identify links to other first aid topics or broader concepts of care.
ICRC involvement
The International Committee of the Red Cross contributed significantly to the Guidelines,
providing expertise in first aid delivery and education in conflict and low-resource settings. The
Guidelines reflect the ICRC’s emphasis on safety, contextual adaptation, trauma-informed care
and learner-centred education. They aim to harmonize education approaches across National
Societies and conflict and non-conflict settings while allowing for contextual adaptation.
26 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

CONTEXTUALIZING FIRST AID
AND FIRST AID EDUCATION

28 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Evidence to action
In line with the First Aid Vision 2030 (IFRC, 2022), this framework helps National Societies turn
evidence into action using methods that are universal, inclusive and rooted in humanity. It
recognizes that first aid is both scientific and compassionate—driven by evidence, carried out by
people and shaped by diverse contexts. Through adaptation, contextualization and
implementation, we ensure first aid education remains relevant to ever-changing needs while
helping communities strengthen their resilience.
When using these Guidelines, National Societies should consider local risk factors, healthcare
systems and capabilities, public health culture and legislation related to first aid. Common
health concerns and injuries identified by specific communities or target groups must be
addressed with attention to cultural and religious beliefs, available resources and patterns of
vulnerability.
Where possible, this process should be undertaken with the National Society’s scientific advisory
group (or equivalent). Ideally, this group includes education experts, medical experts,
researchers, first aid educators and local community representatives. Partnerships with public
authorities, academic institutions and other National
Societies can further enhance the process and promote
shared learning.
From evidence to action
Shifting the Guidelines from evidence to action consists of
three elements:
1. Adaptation: Recognizing the different personal,
community, regional and national factors that
influence both an educator’s ability to provide first aid
education and an individual’s ability to deliver care.
2. Contextualization: Matching recommendations to the
unique environments in which National Societies
operate, including the exposure to various types of risks
and settings with differing access to resources or
medical support.
See the Contexts section of the Guidelines for
more information on this element.
3. Implementation: Transforming evidence into
sustainable educational programmes that equip
learners to act confidently and effectively.
These three elements impact each other. Adaptation informs contextualization, which in turn
supports effective implementation.
CONTEXTS | 29

This framework provides National Societies with a structured approach to examine and adapt
their first aid education programmes. Its application at both national and local levels should be
reviewed periodically to ensure ongoing relevance and effectiveness, as evidence, resources and
community needs evolve.
Adaptation
Over the past three decades, the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement has worked to anchor
its Guidelines in the best available scientific evidence. Drawing on contributions from specialized
experts across the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement and the wider emergency medicine
community—such as the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice of the Belgian Red Cross and the
International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation—this approach ensures that first aid
recommendations are grounded in robust evidence and sound educational principles.
At the same time, first aid does not exist in a legal, cultural or economic vacuum. Actions
considered permissible or appropriate in one context may be restricted or interpreted differently
in another. For example, national regulations governing the administration of medications,
oxygen or the use of automated external defibrillators (AEDs) by lay responders vary widely, as do
the human, systemic and financial resources available to support first aid delivery.
Accordingly, National Societies are encouraged to interpret and adapt these Guidelines in line
with national legislation, cultural practices and local health system structures, while remaining
faithful to the underlying evidence and the Fundamental Principles of the Red Cross and Red
Crescent Movement. These International Guidelines are intended to serve as both a common
framework and a living resource, supporting National Societies in the design and continuous
improvement of first aid education, training and outreach. Guideline authors are also
encouraged to use the evidence base to inform dialogue and advocacy for policy or legislative
changes where this may help expand access to safe and effective first aid.
In a diverse world, uniformity is neither possible nor desirable. The shared aim is therefore
greater harmonization through evidence-based, context-sensitive guidance that strengthens
the global impact of first aid while respecting local realities.
Adaptation begins with understanding local factors—individual, community, regional or
national—that shape first aid delivery. These factors are interconnected and influence one
another. Identifying them allows programme developers to consider how best to approach
complex realities using the Guidelines.

A PESTEL analysis (examining political, economic, socio-cultural, technical, environmental
and legal factors) can help structure this process.
Implementation
Implementation uses the Guidelines to go from planning to actioning a first aid education
programme. It involves designing or updating first aid education so that it is effective, inclusive
and sustainable. Successful implementation depends on a strong understanding of the
Guidelines, thoughtful adaptation to local contexts, and continuous reflection on outcomes.
Implementation is not a one-time event—it is a cycle of development, delivery and
improvement that strengthens the quality and reach of first aid education over time.
Implementation typically includes several key steps:
1. Learning needs analysis
Identify and describe the learner groups your programmes aim to serve, such as school
students, drivers, community volunteers or workplace first aid providers. Assess:
● Contexts of care: Where are learners likely to provide first aid (e.g. at home, on the road, in
remote areas, in disaster or conflict settings, along a migration route)?
● Risks and priorities: What are the most common injuries or illnesses they might
encounter?
● Resources available: What tools, equipment, or medical support are realistically
accessible?
● Learner profile: What skills, knowledge and attitudes are needed to build confidence and
competence? What might be the background of the learner to be considered for an
effective first aid programme (exposure to traumatic situations, migrants, etc.)? What
specific education needs could be related to specific disabilities, for instance?
● Learner preferences: How do they like to learn? What time do they have available?
CONTEXTS | 31

Gather this information through surveys, focus groups, interviews or community consultations,
ensuring that gender, age and cultural considerations are represented. The results inform the
design of training that is both relevant and equitable.
2. Programme analysis
Evaluate your existing programmes and materials to identify strengths, weaknesses,
opportunities and threats (SWOT analysis). Consider:
● Alignment with the 2025 Guidelines and current evidence.
● Effectiveness of teaching methods (in-person, blended or digital).
● Accessibility for different learner groups, including those with disabilities or limited
literacy.
● Trainer readiness and support systems.
Use this analysis to inform your prioritization of updates or redesigns. Where possible, engage
learners and instructors in reviewing what works well and what needs improvement.
3. Programme development
Use what you uncovered from the analysis to create effective products and activities:
Revise curricula and lesson plans to reflect the most relevant topics, scenarios and examples.
● Select delivery methods that meet learners’ preferences, such as interactive workshops,
community-based demonstrations, simulations or online modules.
● Use inclusive education practices, create materials that are culturally sensitive and
accessible in multiple formats and languages.
● Plan for sustainability, including cost-recovery models, partnerships and mechanisms for
regularly updating materials and trainer skills.
GFARC is a resource available to support National Societies in developing and contextualizing
first aid programmes. Visit the GFARC website for more information.
4. Communication
Clear and honest communication supports trust, alignment and consistency across the
Movement.
● Inform trainers, volunteers and partners of programme updates and new evidence.
● Develop a communication plan to share key changes, emphasizing the rationale behind
revisions and how they improve outcomes.
● Access internal networks (newsletters, learning platforms or ToT forums) to share success
stories and lessons learned.
Regular communication reinforces the unity and credibility of first aid education within and
across National Societies.
5. Evaluation and continuous improvement
Evaluation ensures that first aid education remains responsive and effective.
32 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Monitor learner outcomes: Collect feedback through post-training surveys, assessments
or community follow-ups.
● Measure programme impact: Track indicators such as learner confidence, knowledge
retention and community reach.
● Review and revise: Use evaluation data to update content, teaching methods and trainer
development.
Consider using an outcomes evaluation toolkit or a similar framework to structure data
collection and analysis. Schedule regular reviews every two to three years or after major
contextual shifts or international Guidelines publication to keep the programme relevant.
Practical tools such as SWOT, PESTEL and outcomes evaluation frameworks can support
National Societies throughout these steps. Guidance, templates and mentoring are available
through the IFRC Global First Aid Reference Centre at first.aid@ifrc.org.
National Societies are encouraged to engage with the International First Aid Attestation (IFAA)
programme (Global First Aid Reference Centre [GFARC], n.d.-a) to develop effective
evidence-based first aid education programmes that are inclusive, contextually appropriate and
consistent with the Guidelines. The IFAA provides tools to assess quality and compare training
standards to help National Societies align their first aid courses with the Guidelines and
demonstrate accountability, consistency and excellence in first aid education. The IFAA
programme continually assesses and works to improve the quality of the National Societies’ first
aid training courses, as well as recognize the quality in the local first aid training market.
The international Training of Trainers (ToT) programme (Global First Aid Reference Centre [GF
ARC], n.d.-b) offers structured pathways for developing skilled, reflective and adaptable
educators (trainers of trainers and trainers). Trainers who complete ToT programmes are
equipped to apply within their National Society learner-centred methods, promote inclusion
and ensure alignment to the Guidelines. These educators are essential in translating
evidence-based recommendations into effective, compassionate learning experiences that
build confidence and competence in every learner.
Complementing this, the Universal First Aid App (Global First Aid Reference Centre [GFARC],
2023) is a pioneering mobile application designed to spread worldwide essential knowledge on
first aid, safety and preparedness, Epidemics and Psychological First Aid. This multifaceted app
is an appropriate tool to help learners maintain their knowledge and skills after first aid training.
The App can be adopted and contextualized by all National Societies around the world.
A living framework
The process of adaptation, contextualization and implementation is cyclical and reflective.
National Societies are encouraged to revisit these stages regularly as part of their ongoing
commitment to excellence, inclusivity and humanitarian service.
By continuously adapting, contextualizing and implementing the Guidelines—and through
engagement with global initiatives such as the International First Aid Attestation and the
Training of Trainers programme—National Societies help ensure that first aid remains a
universal act of humanity—driven by evidence, practical and accessible to all.
CONTEXTS | 33

Understanding contexts for first aid care and first aid education
First aid is shaped by the environments, risks and realities of the people who give and receive it.
Recognizing this, the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement defines a range of
contexts for providing both first aid care and first aid education. Context determines how people
learn, act and adapt when providing life-saving support. For programme designers,
understanding context is essential for tailoring education, ensuring relevance and upholding
the Movement’s commitment to making first aid accessible to everyone, everywhere.
The concept of context can be understood along two intersecting dimensions:
1. Locational contexts, which describe where first aid takes place, ranging from remote to
urban environments.
2. Situational contexts, which describe what is happening, the specific circumstances or
events that need first aid, such as disasters, armed conflict, workplace incidents,
migration or public health emergencies.
These dimensions are interrelated. A situational context—such as a natural disaster or health
epidemic—can occur anywhere along the locational continuum. For instance, an urban disaster
like Hurricane Katrina in the southern United States and a remote earthquake in Nepal both
pose unique challenges for first aiders, yet both demand the same core humanitarian
commitment: to save lives, reduce suffering and promote recovery through resilience.
Locational contexts: the remote–urban continuum
Locational context refers to geographical and infrastructural conditions, including the distance
from advanced medical care, population, available resources and communication or transport
systems. It affects both the likelihood of certain emergencies and the strategies for responding
to them.
Remote or isolated settings are characterized by long distances to hospitals, limited
communication and minimal medical infrastructure. In these environments, first aiders often
provide extended care, stabilizing patients for hours or even days. Remote contexts also include
isolated islands, deserts, mountain regions and polar environments. National Societies operating
in these areas often combine first aid training with broader survival skills, community
preparedness, and telemedicine partnerships.
Urban environments feature higher population densities, faster access to hospitals and more
formal emergency services—but also present new challenges, including crowd dynamics, traffic
congestion and complex infrastructure. The COVID-19 pandemic showed that even in
high-resource urban settings, access can collapse rapidly.
Urban programmes prioritize coordination with emergency medical systems (EMS),
mass-casualty management and psychosocial support. They must also prepare learners for
infrastructure breakdowns that temporarily blur the line between urban and remote.
Situational contexts: circumstances that shape first aid delivery
Situational contexts describe the specific conditions or events in which first aid is required. These
can occur anywhere along the locational continuum. For example, a health epidemic may affect
both remote villages and urban neighbourhoods differently at the same time.
