Recognizing the evidence gap: a high-resource bias
Much of the published research informing global first aid guidelines originates from
high-resource, urban, peacetime settings with quick access to advanced care. This creates a
knowledge gap when applying evidence to low-resource or unstable environments. Programme
designers must therefore interpret and adapt evidence, combining scientific findings with local
experience and practitioner knowledge.
For instance, CPR outcomes measured in North American hospitals cannot be directly applied
to rural areas without ambulance services. Similarly, wound care guidance based on sterile
environments must be reconsidered for settings with limited clean water. Understanding
contexts helps recognize these differences and encourages educators to contextualize, not copy,
evidence-based practices.
Models for implementing context-adapted programmes
Developing contextual understanding in programme design follows a series of steps:
1. Identify primary contexts
Map the main locational and situational contexts where your National Society provides
education. Use community risk assessments and volunteer feedback.
2. Consider access to advanced care and access to resources
This helps visualize variations across regions or target groups.
3. Adapt curriculum and methods
Adjust content, duration and teaching strategies to match the identified context.
4. Develop context-specific scenarios
Include simulations, role plays and case studies that mirror learners’ realities. For example,
train community first aid providers to respond to a flood in their village or healthcare staff
to handle power outages in hospitals.
5. Evaluate and update
Collect data and feedback to see how training performs in each context. Revise the matrix
periodically as environments and risks evolve.
36 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Common contexts across the Movement
While every National Society faces unique conditions, certain contexts are shared across the
Movement:
● Urban settings with strong EMS but diverse populations.
● Rural and remote areas with limited transport and communication.
● Disaster-prone regions, especially those affected by climate change.
● Areas of instability or migration, where neutrality and protection are key.
● Workplace and school environments, where organized preparedness saves lives.
By comparing these shared experiences through the colour matrix, National Societies can
exchange learning, strengthen global coherence and adapt innovations for their own contexts.
Conclusion
Context shapes every aspect of first aid and first aid education. The twin dimensions of locational
and situational contexts provide a structured way to analyse and respond to the diversity of
environments in which people act to save lives.
● No single “typical” first aid context exists. Every environment combines locational and
situational elements
● Contextualization is dynamic. An area can shift colours, for example, an urban area (green)
may temporarily become orange or red after a major disaster.
● Equity in education. The matrix helps identify where standard evidence-based content
(often taken from high-resource settings) needs to be adapted for lower-resource realities.
● Global coherence, local relevance. Using a common matrix across the Movement
supports mutual learning while respecting local diversity.
Whether in a remote mining camp, a city struck by disaster or a community on the move,
understanding context transforms first aid from a set of techniques into a living, adaptive act of
humanity.
CONTEXTS | 37

First aid across the urban–remote continuum
Locational contexts
The ability to provide and teach first aid depends not only on skill and knowledge but also on
context—specifically, how time, distance and resources affect access to advanced care. At one
end of the spectrum are settings where advanced care can arrive within minutes–referred to in
the Guidelines as urban; at the other are remote or isolated environments where professional
support may be days away. Most communities exist somewhere on this continuum between
these two extremes.
However, this continuum can change. A person living in an urban centre might experience
“remote conditions” during disasters that disable infrastructure, while those in isolated regions
may sometimes have improved access due to mobile clinics, industry infrastructure or
telemedicine. Understanding this variability is essential to designing both first aid systems and
first aid education programmes that prepare people to act effectively, compassionately and
safely in their specific contexts (Born et al., 2012; Jayaraman et al., 2009).
The goal of this section is to define what it means to provide and teach first aid across this
continuum and offer guidance on how to assess local conditions, tailor responses and manage
the complex realities that can occur when advanced care is delayed.
Understanding the continuum: from urban to remote
Urban areas have lots of infrastructure, resources and quick access to medical care. Examples
include cities such as Nairobi, São Paulo, Montreal and Bangkok, where hospitals, emergency
transport systems and trained health professionals are readily available. There may also be
circumstances that might seem remote, but have access to these resources and advanced care
which indicates that they could be considered urban from a first aid perspective. The average
response time for an emergency call in many urban areas is under ten minutes.
In these environments, the first aid provider’s mission is to stabilize the person—providing
sudden rescue and care until advanced responders arrive. Common scenarios include road
traffic collisions, cardiac arrests in public spaces, or injuries in industrial settings, schools and
offices. However, urban first aid can also be complicated by crowd behaviour, traffic conditions or
scene hazards such as electricity and structural instability.
Urban first aid education emphasizes recognition, rapid action and coordinated response.
Learners are taught how to contact appropriate services, manage safety, provide life-saving care
and communicate effectively with responders.
Remote contexts are characterized by delayed access to advanced care, ranging from several
hours to several days. Examples include forestry operations in northern Canada, trekking routes
in Nepal, mining sites in Western Australia or humanitarian settings in sub-Saharan Africa.
Here, first aid providers often have to keep the person alive and prevent their condition from
worsening for extended periods without external support. They may also need to improvise with
38 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

limited equipment, protect themselves from environmental hazards and coordinate transport
over challenging terrain (Born et al., 2012; Jayaraman et al., 2009)
Within this broad category, distinctions can be made:
Time to
Context  Typical resources available  Primary focus of first aid
advanced care
Urban  Minutes  Full EMS, hospitals, clinics,  Immediate stabilization
pharmacies
Rural  Hours  Delayed EMS, hospitals, clinics,  Immediate stabilization
|     |     |     | pharmacies  |     |     | and transport  |     |
| --- | --- | --- | ----------- | --- | --- | -------------- | --- |
Remote  < 24 hours  Basic clinics, nursing stations,  Life-sustaining care
limited supplies
Deep remote  24 hours–7  Field kits, radios, trained first aid  Extended care,
|     | days  |     | providers  |     |     | prevention  |     |
| --- | ----- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | ----------- | --- |
Isolated  > 7 days  Minimal supplies, self-sufficiency  Survival-level principles
required

This  continuum  highlights  that  as  one  moves  further  from  urban  to  isolated  areas,  the
responsibility shifts from external responders to local people, and preparedness, improvisation
and psychological endurance become even more important (Orkin et al., 2012)
Assessing context: the risk matrix approach
First aid providers and educators can determine where they are on the continuum by assessing
two primary factors:
1.  Time to advanced care
2.  Access to resources and training
Plotting these on a risk matrix helps determine the likely complexity of first aid needed.
|            |           |     |          | Time to advanced care  |           |     |          |
| ---------- | --------- | --- | -------- | ---------------------- | --------- | --- | -------- |
|            |           |     | <1 hour  | 1-24 hours             | 1-7 days  |     | >7 days  |
|            | High      |     |          |                        |           |     |          |
| Access to  | Moderate  |     |          |                        |           |     |          |
resources and
| training  | Limited  |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --------- | -------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|           | Minimal  |     |     |     |     |     |     |

|     |     |     |     |     |     | CONTEXTS  | |  39  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --------- | ------ |

● Low: Standard urban first aid; life-saving care opportunity, emphasize communication
and safety.
● Medium: Intermediate training; includes transport, environmental hazards.
● High: Advanced training; focus on prolonged care, resource management.
● Very high: Survival-based approach; integrate shelter, food, sanitation and self-care.
Educators and policy planners should use this framework to determine the depth of first aid
training and the level of independence required of providers. For instance, a coastal fishing
village 12 hours from the nearest hospital might prioritize wound care, infection prevention and
boat-based evacuation, while an urban emergency response team in a densely populated city
may focus on mass casualty triage, cardiac arrest management and rapid coordination with
multiple healthcare facilities.
Providing first aid across the continuum
Preparation and prevention
Preparation is the foundation of effective first aid, particularly as distance from advanced care
increases. In urban areas, preparation means knowing emergency numbers, maintaining first
aid kits and understanding local health systems. In remote areas, preparation includes pre-trip
medical screening, route planning, redundancy in supplies and knowledge of how to signal for
help.
Environmental preparation—such as carrying water filters, weather protection and spare
medication—is crucial. In a prolonged scenario, “the little things become big things”: minor
wounds can become infected, dehydration can lead to confusion and cold exposure can trigger
hypothermia (Orkin et al., 2012).
Decision-making and problem-solving
As distance from advanced care increases, decision-making becomes more independent. Urban
first aid providers are often guided remotely by dispatchers or can expect paramedics to take
over quickly. Remote first aiders, by contrast, must make ethical and logistical decisions about
priorities, evacuation routes, and when to continue or cease care.
Education for remote contexts should include scenario-based decision training, encouraging
learners to think critically and manage uncertainty. Debriefing these scenarios helps normalize
emotional responses such as fear or hesitation and builds resilience (Ratner and Katona, 2016).
Focus on care for self and others
The first aid provider’s own safety and well-being are essential. Urban providers may face
hazards such as traffic or crowds; remote providers may deal with exposure, exhaustion or
isolation. Training should include self-care techniques, rest management and recognizing
psychological strain.
Care for others must evolve with context. In urban areas, focus is on stabilizing life-threatening
conditions. In remote areas, comfort, hygiene and psychological support become equally vital.
Preventing infection, maintaining thermal needs (e.g. warming or cooling) and monitoring
changes in condition are central tasks during prolonged care. Documentation of care—whether
on paper, by radio or digitally—is important for continuity once advanced care is accessed.
40 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid education across the continuum
Differentiating educational programmes
Education must reflect the learners’ realities. Those living in remote communities—such as
Arctic settlements, pastoral regions of East Africa or mountainous areas of Nepal—require
community-based training that builds local capacity. Visitors to remote locations, such as hikers
or researchers, need shorter, targeted courses that emphasize self-reliance and communication
(Born et al., 2012).
Good practice includes:
● Getting local support from government or community organizations before launching
programmes.
● Involving learners in curriculum design to ensure cultural and linguistic relevance.
● Selecting community-based first responders through peer nomination (shown to
improve motivation and performance) (Kay, 1984; Kay & Myrick, 1982).
Educational focus by context
Context Educational emphasis
Urban Scene safety, CPR, AED use, emergency contact, psychological first aid.
Environmental awareness, transport of casualties, coordination with
Rural
clinics.
Remote/ deep Prolonged care, infection control, navigation, communication, resource
remote management.
Survival principles, sanitation, mental health, managing death,
Isolated
community resilience.
Educational strategies
Remote learners benefit from peer-to-peer and scenario-based learning. Born et al. (2012) and
Orkin et al. (2012) demonstrated that role-play, reflection and practical improvisation build
confidence more effectively than lecture formats.
Technology offers new opportunities: blended and e-learning models can reach urban learners
efficiently (Jahani et al., 2023), while printed or audio-visual materials serve isolated contexts.
Flexibility in format ensures inclusivity, especially for communities with low literacy or limited
internet access.
Education should also highlight the psychological and moral aspects of remote first
aid—preparing learners for the stress of prolonged emergencies and possible death. Integrating
mental health first aid, stress management and post-event debriefing supports resilience and
reduces burnout (Reavley et al., 2021).
Managing death in remote or isolated environments
Death is among the most challenging realities of remote first aid. When advanced care is
inaccessible, first aiders may witness death or need to confirm the end of life. Managing this
CONTEXTS | 41

moment requires not only technical awareness but also cultural, emotional and ethical
sensitivity.
Physical and logistical considerations
Physically, the first aider’s responsibilities include ensuring the scene is safe, confirming the
absence of signs of life and documenting time and circumstances. In prolonged-care
contexts—such as expeditions or remote communities—bodies may need to be preserved until
retrieval, requiring protection from environmental factors (heat, animals, insects, water).
According to World Health Organization guidelines (World Health Organization, International
Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies & International Committee of the Red Cross,
2016) respectful handling and covering of the deceased should take priority over transport when
conditions are unsafe or when decomposition risk is low.
Logistical planning is essential. Remote teams should be aware of legal protocols for contacting
authorities, community leaders or spiritual representatives. Communication devices (e.g. satellite
phones, radios) should be part of every remote expedition’s preparedness plan.
Emotional and psychological considerations
Witnessing death can trigger acute stress, guilt or trauma. Psychological first aid for responders
(peer debriefing, rest and access to counselling) reduces long-term distress. Educators should
include emotional self-care and coping strategies in all remote first aid courses.
Even in urban settings, bystanders may experience distress after resuscitation attempts.
Normalizing emotional reactions and teaching grounding techniques (controlled breathing,
focusing exercises) can prevent cumulative stress.
Spiritual and cultural sensitivity considerations
Death care is deeply influenced by cultural and spiritual traditions. In indigenous, nomadic or
faith-based communities, rituals may guide handling, burial or communication with families.
The first aider must act with respect and humility, following local customs wherever possible
and avoiding actions that conflict with spiritual beliefs (Ratner & Katona, 2016).
In multicultural expeditions or workplaces, pre-planning discussions should clarify how death
will be handled in accordance with the wishes of participants. Sensitivity to gender, privacy and
symbolism (e.g. covering the body, avoiding photography) promotes dignity and reduces
community tension.
Ethical and legal considerations
When advanced care is unavailable, ethical challenges arise: whether to continue or cease
resuscitation, how to verify death and how to communicate with families. The guiding principles
are: do no harm; act within training; and document decisions clearly. Many countries lack
specific legal frameworks for remote deaths; however, international humanitarian guidance
emphasizes the duty to protect dignity and ensure accurate reporting (World Health
Organization, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies & International
Committee of the Red Cross, 2016).
42 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Ultimately, training for remote contexts must include a discussion of death—not to instil fear,
but to prepare individuals emotionally and practically for a situation that, although rare,
profoundly shapes the human aspect of first aid.
Conclusion
Providing and teaching first aid across the urban–remote continuum means recognizing that
context determines capacity. In an urban context, systems and speed define effectiveness; in
remote regions, planning, endurance and compassion are essential. The continuum is a
dynamic spectrum where each context informs the other: urban systems can learn adaptability
from remote communities, while remote educators can adopt the structured rigour of urban
models.
Assessing one’s place on this continuum through factors such as time to advanced care,
available resources and workforce capacity enables tailored preparedness. Integrating
psychological, cultural and ethical dimensions, especially regarding death, ensures that first aid
remains not only technically sound but profoundly humane.
For the IFRC network, embracing this continuum approach affirms that first aid is universal;
whether in a crowded city street or a distant mountain pass, it is the same compassionate
impulse—to act, to care, and to preserve dignity—that defines our shared humanity.
CONTEXTS | 43

Workplace
People spend much of their daily lives in the workplace—often more than 35 hours per
week—which increases exposure to risk during this time. In many countries, first aid training and
preparedness are mandatory components of occupational safety frameworks.
Workplaces exist globally and are highly diverse, ranging from office environments and service
industries to construction sites and offshore installations. In all of these, first aid is an essential
element of health and safety management. Training and protection are often legal
requirements, supported by national or sectoral regulation.
All working people are affected by workplace safety, regardless of occupation or setting. Some
sectors—such as construction, manufacturing, agriculture and transport—pose higher risks due
to physical demands or exposure to hazardous materials. However, even in low-risk settings,
such as offices, medical emergencies such as cardiac arrest, stroke, choking or falls can occur.
Workplaces occur in every setting across the world, including formal and informal sectors, small
and large enterprises, and both private and public institutions.
In remote workplaces, such as mining, forestry or oil extraction sites, the organization remains
responsible for worker safety and health. Employers must adequately prepare workers with
training, supplies and response plans that account for delayed access to advanced care.
Individuals should have sufficient materials and knowledge to provide first aid and a clear plan
for emergency action.
In urban workplaces, access to emergency medical care is typically faster. Organizations must
have a structured emergency response plan that includes trained personnel, designated first aid
providers and clear procedures for summoning advanced care.
First aid in the workplace requires particular attention because of its universal relevance, its
frequent inclusion in legislation and the diversity of workplace conditions. Accidents and
workplace incidents represent a major global health issue. The International Labour
Organization (ILO) estimates that more than 2.78 million people die each year from
occupational accidents or work-related diseases, and approximately 374 million sustain non-fatal
injuries (International Labour Organization, 2019).
Such incidents have far-reaching consequences:
● Human and social: injury, disability or death affecting workers and families.
● Economic: loss of productivity, with an estimated four per cent annual decline in global
GDP.
● Legal and reputational: obligations and liabilities for employers.
Recognizing these concerns, at its 110th Session in 2022, the International Labour Conference
included “a safe and healthy working environment” in the ILO's framework of fundamental
principles and rights at work and designated the Occupational Safety and Health Convention,
1981 (No. 155) and the Promotional Framework for Occupational Safety and Health Convention,
2006 (No. 187) as fundamental conventions. This underscores the importance of embedding first
aid within occupational safety and health systems.
44 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

At the policy level, initiatives such as the Alliance for Workplace Awareness and Response to
Emergencies (AWARE), established in 2024 by the European Resuscitation Council, European
Society of Cardiology, European Society for Emergency Medicine (EUSEM), IFRC and others, seek
to strengthen legislation and political advocacy for workplace first aid and cardiac emergency
preparedness at the European level.
Workplace first aid should be integrated into organizational health safety management policies
and informed by the specific hazards of each setting. Competent, accredited training
organizations such as National Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies play a vital role in ensuring
high-quality delivery and sustainability.
Risks in the workplace
Risks vary according to the workplace but commonly include:
● illness or medical emergencies occurring during work hours
● physical injuries such as falls, cuts, or burns
● exposure to hazardous substances
● stress-related conditions or psychological strain.
What are the highest impact risks?
Major industrial or workplace accidents, particularly in manufacturing, mining or energy sectors,
can result in multiple casualties and significant operational disruption. Most workplaces
experience more frequent minor injuries, such as strains, cuts, or fainting, yet these can escalate
without timely care. These risks highlight the importance of ensuring that training, planning
and resourcing are proportionate to each workplace’s specific hazards.
Access to first aid resources
A workplace should provide:
● An adequate number of trained first aid providers.
● Suitable, well-maintained equipment for immediate care, such as automated external
defibrillators (AEDs). Equipment should undergo routine technical checks and
maintenance to ensure readiness.
● Easily accessible first aid kits labelled with the international white cross on a green
background.
● At least one dedicated first aid room appropriate for the size of the premises, type of
activity being carried out, and frequency of accidents.
The extent and quality of these resources are often reinforced by national legislation, industry
standards and partnerships with accredited training organizations, including NationalRed Cross
and Red Crescent Societies.
Workplaces should ensure that all staff know where to find first aid supplies and who to contact
in an emergency. Larger sites should post signage and maintain centralized records of
equipment locations and trained personnel.
CONTEXTS | 45

Access to advanced care
Access depends on the local health system and workplace location. Organizations must ensure
clear procedures for contacting emergency services and transferring casualties to medical
facilities. In urban workplaces, advanced care is typically available within minutes. In remote or
industrial settings, delays may extend to several hours or days. Employers are responsible for
planning around these timeframes and ensuring that interim care can be safely provided.
Considerations for first aid care
To strengthen the impact of first aid in workplaces, stakeholders must link it directly to
occupational safety and health (OSH) culture. Legislation varies widely, but common challenges
include:
● Limited understanding that first aid can be performed by lay persons.
● Overreliance on nearby emergency medical services in urban settings.
● Inadequate policy inclusion of first aid within OSH frameworks (where applicable).
● Limited enforcement of training requirements or refresher cycles.
● False belief that first aid knowledge is only necessary in high risk environments.
For workers to receive suitable protection:
● First aid must be clearly included within national OSH legislation.
● Training requirements should be appropriate for workplace risk levels and healthcare
capacity.
● First aid must be integrated into organizational health and safety management systems.
● Monitoring and enforcement should make sure organizations comply.
● Accredited training organizations must provide evidence-based, best-practice instruction.
● In the absence of national guidance, workplaces should aim for five to ten per cent of staff
trained and certified in first aid, with regular refreshers. This should also take into account
working shift patterns, site layout, and other considerations that affect how workers are
spread across a workplace.
● National Societies and workplace safety stakeholders should engage in advocacy and
awareness campaigns to influence legislation and promote a culture of prevention.
First aid as part of emergency response plans
First aid must be included within organizational emergency preparedness. A Medical
Emergency Response Plan should define roles, communication channels and resources. It
should link to external responders such as fire and ambulance services and align with national
legal requirements.
A comprehensive health risk assessment should include:
● chemical and physical hazards (temperature, radiation, vibration, noise)
● risks from handling equipment, machinery, or loads (including slips, trips and falls)
● psychological and ergonomic factors
● geographical and climatic conditions, such as working in tropical or cold areas, humidity)
● food and kitchen hygiene standards
46 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● existing emergency medical services and healthcare infrastructure.
Using this assessment, organizations can identify the number of trained first aid providers
required, specify equipment (kits, defibrillators, stretchers), and define clear emergency
procedures for communication and transfer.
First aid kits
Workplaces should maintain clearly labelled first aid kits, including defibrillators, in easily
accessible locations, with sufficient contents to serve the number of employees and risk profile.
Kits must be routinely inspected, sourced and restocked from quality-assured providers.
Considerations for first aid education
Educators should position workplace first aid as an essential component of occupational safety.
Training must emphasize both risk identification and appropriate emergency response, linked
to the local healthcare and emergency system.
Good practice points
● Engage national OSH authorities and align programmes with local and sectoral
regulations.
● Adapt training to specific workplace hazards and contexts (e.g. chemical, mechanical,
ergonomic).
● Incorporate online and blended learning where possible to reach more workers.
● Use workplace risk assessments to guide course content, duration and refresher
frequency.
● Define clear roles and responsibilities for designated first aid providers.
● Ensure training covers adult first aid and CPR, including defibrillator use; add child and
infant modules where relevant (e.g. schools or public-facing workplaces).
● Include legislative awareness and organization-specific procedures.
● Employers should target at least 50% of workers for basic first aid training, ensuring
coverage across all shifts.
● Establish a refresher cycle with tools such as posters, newsletters or digital reminders.
● In contexts with limited emergency healthcare, add modules on safe transport and
casualty handling.
● Align course scope with evolving best practices and local legal requirements.
● Incorporate mental health first aid for long-term well-being and workplace resilience
(Reavley et al., 2021).
● Celebrate the World Day for Safety and Health at Work (28 April) through open days,
demonstrations and awareness campaigns.
Learner considerations
● Informal and mobile workers may need flexible, community-led engagement or short,
modular learning (de Aquino et al., 2016).
● High-visibility workers such as drivers, security personnel or transport staff should be
prioritized for training as part of community resilience programmes (Jayaraman et al.,
2009; Tiska et al., 2004).
CONTEXTS | 47

● Motivation varies; incentives such as recognition or certification can improve participation
(Pellegrino & Asselin, 2020).
● Blended and e-learning models can accommodate scheduling and learning preferences
(Mancini et al., 2009; Oliver et al., 2020).
Training providers should ensure inclusive access, using visual materials, scenario-based
exercises and peer learning to maintain engagement and confidence.
Agencies for additional information
Key international agencies for workplace first aid policy and implementation include:
● Ministries of Labour or Occupational Safety and Health authorities.
● International Labour Organization (ILO).
● International Commission on Occupational Health (ICOH).
● European Resuscitation Council and Alliance for Workplace Awareness and Response to
Emergencies (AWARE).
● National Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies.
48 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Disaster
Situational context
Disasters—whether caused by nature, technology or people—are some of the hardest and most
unpredictable contexts for first aid. They can damage or destroy the systems that keep people
safe and healthy, such as hospitals and roads. This can overwhelm emergency services, cut off
communities and cause serious problems for many people. Unlike workplace or remote settings,
disasters are systemic crises that affect everything at once—the place, the responders and the
victims.
The World Health Organization (2019) defines a disaster as a serious disruption of the
functioning of a community or society that exceeds its ability to cope using available resources.
This definition highlights the central challenge for first aid: the growing gap between immediate
need and access to advanced care.
First aid in disaster contexts is not only a matter of providing medical assistance—it is also about
empowering communities with knowledge, confidence and resilience before disaster strikes.
This section outlines how to provide and teach first aid in disasters, including contextual
understanding, preparedness, psychosocial care and coordination across systems.
Understanding the disaster context
Nature and scope of disasters
Disasters can be brought on by a variety of hazards that can be categorized as natural, such as
earthquakes, floods, wildfires, hurricanes or droughts; technological, such as chemical spills or
structural failures; or human-induced, including conflict or terrorism. Increasingly, they are
cascading events—a single hazard triggering multiple crises. For example, an earthquake may
rupture gas lines, causing fires, or floods may spread waterborne disease outbreaks (Johnston et
al., 2014).
Globally, the frequency and intensity of disasters are increasing due to climate change,
urbanization and environmental degradation (World Health Organization, 2019). This means
more communities—both urban and remote—are being affected in diverse ways.
Urban disaster contexts
In urban environments, disasters can be more severe due to higher populations, complex
infrastructure and social inequality. High-rise buildings, busy roads and industrial areas can
become dangerous when damaged. In cities like Port-au-Prince (2010 earthquake) and Manila
(2013 Typhoon Haiyan), first aid providers faced challenges such as:
● collapsed structures creating crush injuries and trapping people (Bazeli et al., 2017)
● blocked roads preventing access to advanced care
● electric and gas hazards, particularly after earthquakes or explosions
● large-scale displacement and crowd management in shelters or temporary camps
● limited water and sanitation, increasing infection risk (Wilson et al., 2005).
CONTEXTS | 49

Urban disasters are especially hazardous for people living in informal settlements or without
access to transport, as evacuation becomes more challenging. This may result in additional
injuries and losses.
First aid in urban disasters focuses on triage and stabilization, where responders must manage
high casualty numbers with limited space, light and safety.
Remote disaster contexts
In contrast, remote environments deal with the challenges of distance and delay. When
disasters strike isolated regions, such as earthquakes in Nepal’s mountain districts (2015),
droughts in the Horn of Africa or cyclones in the Pacific, help may take days to arrive.
Key considerations include:
● Prolonged care: first aiders must provide extended first aid for injuries, burns or fractures
without advanced equipment.
● Environmental exposure: heat, cold, altitude or flooding can worsen injuries.
● Limited communication: power loss or lack of cell towers impedes coordination.
● Community reliance: local people become first responders, often improvising care with
household materials (Orkin et al., 2012).
Remote disasters highlight the need for local resilience—training lay people, strengthening
community leadership and pre-positioning supplies.
Comparing urban and remote considerations
Aspect Urban disaster Remote disaster

density challenges

injuries lacerations infection, exposure

control, coordination deterioration, psychological support
Understanding these distinctions helps educators and responders to adapt training, resources
and response plans appropriately.
50 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Assessing disaster risk and preparedness
Preparedness begins with contextual risk assessment, balancing hazard likelihood with local
capacity.  The  IFRC  and  the  Global  Disaster  Preparedness  Center  (GDPC)  advocate  a
hazard-impact-capacity approach: identifying what can happen, who is most at risk and how
the community can respond.
A hazard-impact matrix helps visualize likely events and responses:

The  matrix  supports  community  preparedness  planning,  making  sure  that  education and
resources align with local hazards. Preparedness must include vulnerable populations—children,
older adults, and people with disabilities—and prioritize equitable access to information and
supplies.
Providing first aid in disasters
Safety and scene management

CONTEXTS  |  51

Safety remains the first principle. Disasters often create unstable environments, including
aftershocks, debris, downed power lines and toxic materials. Providers should ensure their own
safety before providing care and continuously reassess risks (Jacobs et al., 2016).
In urban collapses, rescuers must avoid entering unsafe structures without proper assessment.
In remote areas affected by landslides or floods, environmental awareness and personal
protective equipment (e.g. gloves, boots) are crucial for preventing secondary injuries.
Common injuries and illnesses
The pattern of injuries in disasters depends on the hazard:
● Crush injuries and fractures are common after earthquakes and building collapses
(Johnston et al., 2014). These may lead to compartment syndrome, which requires
pressure relief and close limb monitoring.
● Cuts, grazes and puncture wounds from debris are widespread and can become infected,
especially when cleaning resources are limited.
● Burns and poisoning result from fires and explosions, requiring immediate cooling and
airway monitoring (Loftus et al., 2018).
● Drowning and hypothermia follow floods and tsunamis (Wilson et al., 2005).
● Heat exhaustion and dehydration occur during droughts or prolonged exposure,
particularly in humanitarian camps.
● Breathing difficulties and diarrhoeal diseases often occur during the recovery phase due
(Wilson et al., 2005) to overcrowding and contaminated water (World Health Organization,
2019).
Understanding these patterns allows responders to prioritize care between controlling bleeding,
managing shock, maintaining body temperature and preventing infection.
Triage and resource allocation
In multiple-casualty situations, triage helps direct limited resources where they are most
effective. Methods such as START (Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment) are adaptable for
low-resource environments (Cicero et al., 2018). Responders classify victims based on breathing,
circulation and consciousness, focusing their efforts on those most likely to survive.
Ethically, triage decisions can be distressing. Training must include psychological preparedness
for making and accepting such decisions (Bazeli et al., 2017).
Improvisation and adaptation
When supplies are destroyed or inaccessible, improvisation saves lives. Clothing can become
bandages, boards can be splints and clean water from bottles can irrigate wounds. Loftus et al.
(2018) and van Romburgh et al. (2019) emphasize that improvisation training builds creativity
and confidence.
However, improvisation must not compromise safety. For example, defibrillators should not be
used in standing water. Education must reinforce an understanding of the underlying first aid
principles, so that providers can adapt methods safely.
52 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Coordination and communication
Disasters blur organizational boundaries. First aid providers should coordinate with emergency
agencies, local authorities and community networks. Governments may deploy mobile hospitals,
while humanitarian agencies like the IFRC operate field clinics and reunification services.
When communication systems fail, local radio, satellite phones and messenger networks
become vital. Including these backup tools into preparedness plans ensures continued
communication when conventional systems collapse.
First aid education in disaster contexts
Preparedness through education
Education is the foundation of disaster readiness. Communities that understand local hazards,
practise drills and maintain preparedness kits respond faster and recover more effectively (Salita
et al., 2019).
Disaster first aid education should aim to:
● Develop self-efficacy and confidence to act (Ejeta et al., 2015).
● Emphasize local relevance, referencing the hazards learners are most likely to face.
● Promote prevention, including hygiene, shelter safety and mental health.
● Encourage household and community preparedness, such as emergency plans and
communication strategies.
Effective training modalities
Evidence supports blended, community-centred learning. Combining demonstrations, drills and
peer discussions helps learners retain skills under stress (Mohamed‐Ahmed et al., 2015).
Simulation exercises, such as “Disaster days,” create realistic challenges, encouraging teamwork
and improvisation (Wilkerson et al., 2008).
Where access to in-person training is limited, mobile platforms, radio broadcasts and printed
guides can provide life-saving knowledge. The IFRC’s PrepareCenter.org offers open-access
resources for community-level education and resilience building.
Community-based and inclusive learning
Training should target pre-existing community groups, such as workplaces, schools and
neighbourhood associations, since they naturally operate as response networks. Inclusive design
ensures that women, older adults, people with disabilities and marginalized groups are
represented.
Periodic refresher training (at least annually) reinforces memory and motivation. Digital tools
such as serious games have been shown to improve triage accuracy and confidence (Cicero et
al., 2018).
Psychosocial and ethical dimensions
Disasters affect both responders and survivors on a deep emotional and moral level. They may
experience acute stress, grief and moral injury. Psychological First Aid (PFA) frameworks, such as
CONTEXTS | 53

Look, Listen, Link, help first aid providers give compassionate, non-intrusive support and
promote stabilization in chaotic environments (World Health Organization, 2011). Training should
prepare providers to recognize distress, normalize emotional reactions, and connect individuals
with appropriate support systems. Following a traumatic event, peer support between
colleagues is important. In cultures where discussing emotion is stigmatized, education must
include culturally appropriate coping strategies—such as community gatherings, rituals or
spiritual practices—to sustain resilience and collective healing. Ethical dilemmas are inevitable in
disaster response; providers may have to make choices about whom to treat first, when to stop
resuscitation efforts, or how to balance a duty of care with personal safety. To help navigate these
situations, first aid education should include values-based reflection and dialogue on ethical
decision-making. Equally important are human factors such as communication, leadership and
trust, which are as vital as technical skills. Simulated team exercises and scenario-based learning
strengthen coordination, reduce errors and foster psychological readiness under pressure
(Hunziker et al., 2010).
Building resilient communities
Disaster resilience depends on preparedness, adaptability and collaboration. When
communities integrate first aid education into disaster risk reduction plans, they become
stronger and more self-reliant.
Resilience is collective: governments establish emergency systems, humanitarian organizations
coordinate response and individuals maintain preparedness plans. Each component
strengthens the whole.
The IFRC’s Global Disaster Preparedness Center remains a global reference centre for innovation
and research, supporting localized strategies for citizen preparedness and emphasizing the vital
role of first aid in saving lives when infrastructure collapses.
Conclusion
Disasters expose both vulnerability and courage. When advanced care is unreachable, the
capacity of ordinary people to provide first aid determines survival.
In both urban and remote settings, disaster first aid demands adaptability, improvisation and
humanity. Integrating context-based education, psychosocial care and ethical reflection
prepares communities to act effectively when systems fail.
For the IFRC and its partners, strengthening disaster first aid is not only a technical goal—it is a
humanitarian mandate: to empower people everywhere to protect life, dignity and hope, even in
the darkest moments.
54 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Conflict
Situational context
Armed conflict creates some of the most complex and dangerous conditions in which first aid is
delivered. Today, conflicts increasingly take place in urban and peri-urban environments, where
civilians, responders and infrastructure are exposed to violence. At the same time, many conflicts
extend into remote, frontier and isolated areas, where access to services is limited and
evacuation to advanced care may take hours or days. Unlike sudden-onset disasters or
time-limited epidemics, armed conflict is characterized by intentional violence and sustained
insecurity, requiring first aid providers to operate under continuous and unpredictable threat.
In conflict-affected settings, first aid is not only a health intervention but a life-preserving
humanitarian act, often delivered under direct threat. Responders must balance the urgency of
life-saving care with continuous assessment of security risks. The ability to adapt care, education,
and decision-making to rapidly changing conditions is essential.
For the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, first aid in conflict is grounded in
humanitarian principles and closely linked to broader efforts to protect civilians, ensure access to
care and support community resilience.
Characteristics of conflict-affected settings
Conflict settings are defined by persistent insecurity, damaged infrastructure and disrupted
health systems. Weapons, tactics and environment shape injury patterns, while access to
advanced care is often limited by insecurity, checkpoints or destroyed facilities.
Unlike disaster or epidemic contexts, the threat is often intentional and ongoing. Responders
may face direct targeting, secondary attacks or restrictions on movement. This reality profoundly
shapes how first aid is provided and taught. Understanding how conflict manifests across the
remote–urban continuum is essential for adapting first aid systems, training approaches and
expectations of care.
Conflict affects settings differently along the remote-urban continuum:
● Urban conflict often involves blast injuries, wound trauma, structural collapse and mass
casualty incidents in densely populated areas.
● Rural and remote areas may see fewer casualties overall, but longer evacuation times
and limited stabilization capacity.
● Deep remote and isolated areas frequently rely on community members as the sole
providers of initial care, with longer delays to advanced care.
Across all settings, civilians—including children, older adults and persons with disabilities—bear
a growing share of the burden of injury and death (ICRC, 2016).
CONTEXTS | 55

Health risks and injury patterns
The dominant health risks in conflict settings are traumatic injuries, particularly those associated
with weapons and explosive devices. Uncontrolled bleeding is widely recognized as the leading
preventable cause of death in conflict-related injury, making early haemorrhage control a
central first aid priority (International Committee of the Red Cross, 2024).
Common injury patterns include:
● severe external bleeding
● penetrating injuries from gunshots or shrapnel
● blast-related polytrauma
● crush injuries from collapsed structures
● burns and inhalation injuries
● fractures and soft-tissue injuries
● minor injuries and illnesses sustained during displacement or evacuation.
Psychological distress is also present. Exposure to violence, loss and displacement contributes to
acute stress reactions, grief and longer-term mental health challenges for both civilians and
responders (World Health Organization, 2013).
Providing first aid in conflict-affected settings
Access to first aid resources
Access to first aid resources in conflict settings is highly unreliable and unstable. Some contexts
may have access to trauma-focused equipment, such as tourniquets and haemostatic dressings,
while lacking basic supplies like clean water, gloves or analgesia (pain relief).
Supply chains are frequently disrupted by insecurity, blockades or population displacement.
Resources may be unevenly distributed due to security priorities or power dynamics, with
civilians often receiving delayed or inadequate access compared to armed combatants.
Community members often rely on pre-positioned kits, improvised materials or shared
resources, particularly in remote or besieged areas.
In some conflict-affected settings, access to first aid and medical resources may be deliberately
restricted or rationed based on perceived affiliation, identity or association with an opposing
side. Civilians may be denied timely care because they are viewed as belonging to an “enemy”
population, and individuals associated with opposition forces may face even greater barriers,
including the intentional withholding of assistance by authorities or weapon bearers. Such
practices are inconsistent with international humanitarian law and international human rights
law, which require the wounded and sick to be cared for without adverse distinction. While the
ICRC works actively to prevent and challenge these violations through dialogue, advocacy and
presence, access to impartial care cannot always be secured in practice.
Human resources are equally constrained. Trained healthcare workers may flee conflict zones,
leaving volunteers and community members to provide care under extreme pressure. National
Society volunteers are often positioned in affected communities, sharing the same risks and
losses as those they assist.
56 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Access to advanced care
Access to advanced care in conflict settings is unpredictable and often delayed. Health facilities
may be damaged, overwhelmed or deliberately targeted, despite international humanitarian
law protections (ICRC, 2016).
Evacuation times vary widely:
● In some urban contexts, stabilization facilities may be nearby but unsafe to reach.
● In rural or remote areas, evacuation may take many hours or days.
● In isolated or besieged settings, evacuation may be impossible for extended periods.
These realities require first aid systems to prepare for prolonged care, including monitoring,
prevention of deterioration and ethical decision-making when resources are limited. As a result,
first aid education and planning in conflict settings must assume prolonged care as a baseline
scenario rather than an exception.
Security and risk management
Security considerations fundamentally shape the delivery of first aid in conflict. Responders
must continuously assess whether it is safe to approach, treat or evacuate a casualty. Time on
scene is often deliberately minimized to reduce exposure to danger.
Local responders may demonstrate high risk tolerance, motivated by community ties and moral
obligation. Over time, this can lead to the normalization of danger and cumulative psychological
strain. International responders may operate under more restrictive security policies, which can
limit the delivery of care. Effective systems acknowledge and manage this tension rather than
ignore it.
Clinical priorities
Given injury patterns and evacuation delays, clinical priorities in conflict settings emphasize:
● rapid control of life-threatening bleeding, including use of pressure dressings, wound
packing and tourniquets
● pelvic stabilization, often using improvised methods
● basic airway and breathing support consistent with trauma priorities
● prevention of hypothermia and shock.
Care is often delivered in harsh environments, requiring adaptability and sound clinical
judgment rather than rigid adherence to protocols designed for stable settings.
First aid education in conflict-affected settings
Educational priorities
First aid education in conflict must prepare learners to make decisions under threat, not just to
execute technical skills. Training should emphasize:
● dynamic risk assessment
● when to provide care and when to withdraw
● adaptation of techniques to security constraints (e.g. the misuse of tourniquets
(International Committee of the Red Cross, 2024)
CONTEXTS | 57

● ethical considerations, including impartiality and self-protection.
Improper or unsafe application of interventions, particularly tourniquets, has been documented
in conflict settings and must be addressed through repeated, corrective practice (ICRC, 2018).
Training modalities
Experiential learning and simulation are particularly effective in this context. High-quality
training can be delivered using low-cost materials if scenarios reflect real conditions. Key
elements include:
● deliberate practice with feedback
● scenario-based training with increasing complexity
● structured debriefing to reinforce learning and address emotional impact.
Facilitators should consider participants' fatigue, stress and nutritional needs and adjust the
training pace appropriately. In many contexts, training periods also serve as rare opportunities
for rest and psychological recovery.
Learning materials and continuity
Access to digital learning is often limited by connectivity and security. Offline materials, printed
handouts and portable reference tools remain essential. The ICRC and National Societies have
developed multilingual, context-adapted materials to support continuous learning when formal
training is disrupted.
Psychosocial and ethical considerations
First aid providers in conflict settings are frequently members of affected communities. They
may experience cumulative exposure to death, injury and moral dilemmas, including situations
where care cannot be provided safely or equitably.
Psychological first aid principles, such as ensuring safety, listening without pressure and linking
to available support, are relevant not only for civilians but also for responders themselves
(International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, 2015; International Federation
of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, 2019). Peer support, informal debriefing and
recognition of emotional burden are critical to sustaining responder capacity.
Ethical challenges are also inherent to conflict response. Decisions about prioritization,
withdrawal, and self-protection must be acknowledged explicitly in training rather than left
unspoken.
Coordination and humanitarian actors
National Societies typically operate in close coordination with the ICRC in conflict settings,
benefiting from shared guidance, technical support and protection mandates. Other
humanitarian actors, local authorities and community leaders also play critical roles.
Military medical services may be present, but coordination with civilian humanitarian actors
requires careful navigation to preserve neutrality and trust.
58 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Conclusion
First aid in conflict-affected settings is shaped by violence, uncertainty and moral complexity.
The frequency of severe trauma, prolonged delays to advanced care and risks to responders
demand a model of first aid that is adaptable, security-aware and ethically grounded.
Education and preparedness must reflect these realities, equipping responders with not only
technical skills but also judgment, restraint and resilience. When appropriately supported, local
responders and communities demonstrate remarkable capacity to save lives under extreme
conditions.
For the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, strengthening first aid in conflict settings is
inseparable from the broader humanitarian commitment to protect life, dignity and access to
care.
CONTEXTS | 59

Migration
Situational context
Migration is an increasingly defining feature of the modern world. Every year, millions of people
leave their homes in search of safety, reuniting with family, livelihood or better opportunities. For
many, this journey involves navigating dangerous routes, insecure living conditions and barriers
to healthcare and basic services. These realities make access to first aid—and the ability to
deliver or receive it—an essential humanitarian need.
Within the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, first aid in migration contexts
serves not only as an emergency response but also as a means to promote dignity, trust and
inclusion. This approach aligns with the Migration Strategy for the International Red Cross and
Red Crescent Movement, which positions migration as a long-term humanitarian priority and
emphasizes dignity, access to services and community-based responses across all stages of the
migration journey (IFRC, 2025).Whether at borders, in transit zones, detention centres or urban
neighbourhoods, the capacity to deliver timely, culturally appropriate care saves lives and
strengthens community togetherness.
Migrants include asylum seekers, refugees, people displaced by conflict or disasters,
undocumented workers and individuals moving voluntarily for economic reasons. This diversity
shapes the health challenges they face and the ways first aid must be adapted. Across the
urban–remote continuum, first aid providers experience different logistical, cultural and ethical
complexities, but the humanitarian principles of neutrality, impartiality and humanity remain
constant (IFRC, 2025).
Understanding the migration context
While moving can create opportunity and safety, it also exposes people to injury, violence and
environmental hazards. During transit, migrants may experience dehydration, hypothermia,
malnutrition and exhaustion. At borders or in detention, they may face overcrowding and
limited sanitation; in destination countries, they may endure unsafe working conditions and
psychological stress linked to exclusion or uncertainty.
The relationship between migration and health is complicated (IFRC, 2025). Some migrants
experience improved health through safety and access to care, while others suffer more due to
exploitation, stigma or interrupted treatment. Structural barriers, such as legal status, poverty,
language and discrimination often increase these risks (IFRC & Red Cross Red Crescent Global
Migration Lab, 2021).
Urban settings
Urban areas attract large numbers of migrants seeking work or refuge, but may also reinforce
inequality. Migrants often live in overcrowded housing or informal settlements, where fire, injury
and communicable disease risks are high. For example, during the COVID-19 pandemic, many
migrants in cities like Kuala Lumpur and Berlin were excluded from national vaccination and
health information systems, leading to preventable illness (IFRC & Global Migration Lab, 2021).
60 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

People may technically have access to emergency care, but fear of detection or high costs may
prevent them from using it. Language barriers and unfamiliar bureaucratic systems often
discourage many from seeking help. For undocumented workers in construction or domestic
labour, workplace injuries can go unreported or untreated due to fear of deportation (IFRC,
2017).
Urban first aid programmes must include advocacy and trust building, in addition to technical
care, and offer safe, confidential spaces such as Humanitarian Service Points (HSPs) where
migrants can receive assistance without discrimination.
Remote and transit settings
Along migration routes through deserts, mountains or seas, first aid can mean the difference
between life and death. Remote settings are marked by limited infrastructure and supplies, and
delayed access to advanced care. Migrants crossing the Sahara, the Mediterranean or the Darién
Gap often face dehydration, exposure or trauma injuries without nearby health services.
In these areas, peer-to-peer first aid frequently becomes the only available response. National
Societies operating along major migration corridors, such as the Nigerian Red Cross Society,
Mauritanian Red Crescent and Mexican Red Cross, train community volunteers, including
migrants themselves, to manage common conditions including heat exhaustion, wounds and
dehydration. These initiatives demonstrate that empowering migrants as first aid providers
enhances both survival and solidarity (IFRC, 2025).
Remote and mobile outreach teams must also plan for communication and referral chains
when advanced care is hours or days away. The absence of ambulances or legal crossing
permissions may require coordination with local authorities or humanitarian corridors to
transport severely ill or injured individuals.
Access to first aid and advanced care
Migrants’ access to first aid is often inconsistent and informal. In camps or settlements, there
might not be enough trained responders, and supplies such as bandages, gloves and
disinfectants may be limited. Migrant associations or informal networks often serve as the initial
point of contact, sharing information by word of mouth about where to find assistance.
Access to advanced care, including hospitals and physicians, is even more challenging. Legal
status, documentation and cost remain primary barriers. Asylum seekers awaiting status
recognition may not be allowed access to public healthcare; undocumented migrants may
avoid hospitals altogether for fear of detention or deportation (IFRC, 2016). Even when services
are available, language and lack of digital access can make it hard to navigate complex health
systems.
These barriers lead to delayed or missed treatment, turning minor injuries into chronic or
life-threatening conditions. For example, in the German Red Cross study Unseen?! Refugees
with Disabilities in Germany, 2023, many respondents reported deterioration of existing
conditions because they could not access rehabilitation services (German Red Cross, 2023).
Addressing these gaps requires not only direct service delivery but trust-building and
confidentiality guarantees so migrants know that seeking first aid will not expose them to risk.
CONTEXTS | 61

Providing first aid in migration contexts
Key considerations
Providing first aid to migrants in vulnerable situations requires sensitivity to cultural, linguistic
and legal realities. Providers must act impartially and maintain confidentiality, making sure
there will be no legal consequences for the people to whom they provide care. Interpreters or
cultural mediators are critical for communication, though family members should not be used
to translate sensitive information.
First aid providers should also be aware of trauma exposure; many migrants have experienced
violence, loss or perilous journeys. Psychological First Aid (PFA) techniques—active listening,
empathy and reassurance—should be used with physical care.
Where trust is low, visibility and neutrality matter. Distinct Red Cross and Red Crescent emblems
help migrants identify safe assistance points. Field teams must coordinate with protection and
legal services when they suspect abuse or exploitation.
Common health and injury patterns
Migrants face injuries and illnesses linked to their environment and livelihoods:
● During transit – dehydration, hypothermia, heatstroke, exhaustion, cuts and grazes
wounds, and fractures, sprains, and strains from walking long distances.
● At sea – hypothermia, saltwater sores, drowning risk and trauma from overcrowded
vessels.
● In camps or shelters – respiratory infections, gastrointestinal illness, scabies and minor
burns from unsafe cooking areas.
● At work – falls, crush injuries, chemical exposure and repetitive strain injuries.
● Psychological distress – anxiety, insomnia, post-traumatic symptoms or grief.
First aid programmes should therefore combine basic physical interventions with psychosocial
care and referral pathways to specialized services when possible.
Urban versus remote care considerations
In urban areas, first aid provision focuses on outreach and inclusion. Mobile units or fixed HSPs in
transport hubs and city outskirts provide immediate aid, distribute hygiene kits and connect
migrants to clinics. Coordination with municipal authorities helps provide continued care while
maintaining neutrality.
In remote areas, survival is the priority. Volunteers may operate with minimal supplies,
improvising splints, pressure dressings and shelter. Communication equipment and referral
partnerships are vital. When advanced care is delayed, monitoring, documentation and
prevention of infection or dehydration become central responsibilities.
Both contexts highlight the need for context-specific training and adaptable equipment. Light,
portable first aid kits and multilingual guidance cards enhance efficiency and accessibility.
62 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid education for and with migrants
Training migrants as first aid providers
Empowering migrants through first aid education supports independence, safety and
community resilience. Training should be accessible, relevant and trauma-informed. Courses
must respect language diversity and literacy levels using visual materials, videos or
demonstrations instead of text-heavy manuals.
For migrants on the move, training sessions should be short, flexible and location-responsive,
delivered in transit centres or temporary shelters. Portable materials, such as laminated cards or
mobile apps, allow learners to refresh their skills after relocation. In urban or stable contexts,
multi-day courses can provide comprehensive instruction on both physical and psychological
first aid.
Training should focus on practical scenarios that migrants are most likely to face: dehydration,
burns, musculoskeletal injuries, fainting and stress reactions. Incorporating peer-to-peer
learning reinforces trust and cultural relevance.
Training staff and volunteers supporting migrants
For Red Cross and Red Crescent staff and volunteers, cultural competence and trauma
awareness are essential. Trainers should use participatory methods and facilitate discussion on
ethical dilemmas, confidentiality and safe referral. Sessions must emphasize that first aid
providers are humanitarian actors, not extensions of state authorities, to create a sense of safety
and trust with migrants.
Interpreters and cultural mediators should be integrated into the planning and delivery process.
Scenario-based exercises can model sensitive communication, helping participants avoid
unintentional triggers. Continuous supervision, feedback and refresher training maintain quality
and confidence (IFRC, 2017).
Psychological first aid and empowerment
Psychological First Aid should be a standard module in all migration-related programmes. It
equips responders to manage distress compassionately and empowers migrants to support one
another. Learning environments must be psychologically safe (avoiding role plays that replicate
traumatic events) and should encourage reflection on hope, coping and community strengths.
Education modalities and accessibility
First aid education in migration contexts must reflect mobility and diversity. Effective modalities
include:
● Modular learning: brief, stand-alone sessions adaptable to transit or camp settings.
● Visual instruction: diagrams, animations and video translation for multilingual audiences.
● Digital tools: where internet access is available, mobile platforms enable self-paced
learning.
● Outdoor or small-group teaching: ensures inclusivity when indoor spaces are unavailable.
● Peer facilitation: migrants co-train peers, reinforcing ownership and sustainability.
CONTEXTS | 63

Accessibility also means psychological comfort. Facilitators must acknowledge past trauma and
provide breaks, safe spaces and supportive feedback. Building confidence is as critical as
learning knowledge and skills; every participant should leave feeling capable of protecting
themselves and others.
Legal, ethical and protection considerations
Migration raises unique ethical and legal concerns. Fear of authorities may cause migrants to
hide injuries or illnesses. First aid providers must uphold confidentiality, neutrality and voluntary
consent. Personal data should be collected only when necessary and stored securely.
Providers should also be able to identify and report protection risks, such as trafficking or
gender-based violence, through established humanitarian channels. Training should clarify how
to balance immediate life-saving duties with longer-term protection obligations.
Red Cross and Red Crescent visibility—through recognizable emblems, signage and consistent
messaging—helps reassure migrants that assistance is humanitarian and independent.
Partnerships with local NGOs, legal aid groups and community leaders strengthen referral
systems and trust.
Conclusion
Migration is not a single journey but a continuum of experiences that shape health, vulnerability
and resilience. Across this continuum, whether at a remote border post or within an urban
neighbourhood, first aid remains a vital humanitarian act that restores dignity and saves lives.
Delivering and teaching first aid in migration contexts requires contextual understanding,
cultural sensitivity, and partnership with migrants themselves. By integrating first aid education
into humanitarian service delivery, the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement empowers
migrants as active agents of care rather than passive recipients.
In both urban and remote settings, inclusive and accessible first aid bridges protection, health
and hope. It ensures that no one, regardless of origin or status, is left behind..
64 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Epidemic and health crises
Situational context
Epidemics and pandemics are some of the most complex contexts for providing and teaching
first aid. They overwhelm health systems, place stress on communities and cause fear—yet
injuries, cardiac arrests and medical emergencies continue. Even when there are infection risks,
first aid must continue. Protecting both the responder and the person receiving care becomes
central to the humanitarian principle of “do no harm”.
Outbreaks such as Ebola in West Africa (2014–2016), COVID-19 (2020–2023) and regional
epidemics of cholera, measles and influenza have shown that when communities are trained
and prepared to act safely, they can save lives and build trust in public health systems (Honein
et al., 2020). First aid in epidemic settings requires not only technical skills but also risk
awareness, infection prevention and psychosocial resilience.
Protecting the first aider, the person receiving aid and bystanders remains at the heart of first
aid efforts. Continuing first aid training and services during health crises is also a strategic
priority: it strengthens community resilience and trust in healthcare providers.
The International Federation of Red Cross (IFRC) and the World Health Organization (WHO)
emphasize that first aid services and education must continue, even under movement
restrictions, using safe practices, protective equipment and creative delivery methods. These
actions support community resilience, save lives and help counter fear and misinformation.
In epidemic and pandemic contexts, first aid education guidelines and good practice
emphasize that first aid providers must always begin by assessing scene safety and the risks to
both themselves and the injured or ill person. Providers should use appropriate medical-grade
personal protective equipment (PPE), such as masks, gloves, eye protection and gowns, selected
according to the suspected mode of disease transmission. Hand hygiene is essential and should
be performed before and after every interaction, even when gloves are worn. When respiratory
transmission is suspected, guidelines recommend performing compression-only
cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and applying an automated external defibrillator (AEDs) as
soon as possible, with the person’s mouth and nose loosely covered using a surgical mask, cloth
or face covering to reduce the spread of respiratory droplets. Across all first aid
situations—including bleeding, burns and wounds—good practice encourages supporting and
promoting self-management whenever possible, thereby reducing close contact while still
providing effective care.
Understanding epidemic and health crisis contexts
Scope and characteristics
According to the WHO (2025), an outbreak occurs when disease cases suddenly increase in a
specific area. An epidemic occurs when the number of cases is higher than expected across a
region or country. A pandemic involves the disease spreading globally and continuing to spread
in communities. Regardless of the size of the outbreak, these events disrupt normal healthcare
and increase risks for responders.
CONTEXTS | 65

The One Health framework links human, animal and environmental health, reminding us that
pandemics are often cross-sectoral—coming from diseases that spread from animals and
environmental disruption. First aid systems must therefore follow national public health and
emergency frameworks on aspects such as disease monitoring, hygiene and vaccination
guidance.
These definitions, established by the WHO, should be interpreted according to the national
context and frameworks for preparedness and response to health emergencies.
Urban contexts
In cities, diseases spread faster because of large populations and crowded spaces. Shared
housing, public transportation and busy workplaces make it easier for germs to spread, and false
information can move quickly online. During COVID-19, cities like Milan, New York and São Paulo
saw more people having cardiac arrests at home or in public, forcing bystanders to balance
helping with the risk of infection (Ferrari et al., 2022).
Urban first aid providers often have access to hospitals and advanced care, but these systems
can become overloaded during a major outbreak. Hospitals might run out of space, and
providers may need to improvise, sort patients by urgency (triage) and sometimes care for
people longer in homes or community shelters. The emotional toll—fear of infection, loneliness
and stigma—can also make people less willing to help.
To prepare for outbreaks, cities should build strong infection-control systems, like handwashing
stations, good ventilation and proper waste management. They should also train first aiders,
volunteers and bystanders to safely use personal protective equipment (PPE).
Remote contexts
In remote and rural settings, outbreaks present different challenges, including delayed access to
medical care, limited diagnostic tools and minimal protective supplies. During the Ebola crisis,
rural first aid providers in Sierra Leone and Guinea often improvised isolation and hygiene
measures using local materials while waiting for medical teams to arrive. Similarly, remote
communities during COVID-19 relied on community health volunteers trained in basic infection
prevention, triage and transport coordination.
Living in isolated areas may limit infection spread initially, but it also makes it harder to get
supplies such as PPE and disinfectants. Limited internet connectivity complicates training
continuity as well. Remote first aid providers must therefore have simple, context-appropriate
infection-control tools, such as handwashing with locally produced soap, physical distancing
and outdoor training environments.
66 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Assessing risk and preparedness
Preparedness in a health crisis requires determining the type of risk to all involved. The table
below summarizes three transmission risk levels, with examples, protective priorities and
implications for first aid education.
First aid education
Transmission risk Examples Protective priorities
implications
High (respiratory) COVID-19, influenza, Masks, eye protection, Train on
tuberculosis ventilation, distancing donning/doffing PPE,
compression-only CPR
Moderate Cholera, Ebola, Gloves, gowns, hand Safe waste disposal,
(contact or meningitis hygiene, disinfection pair-checking during
droplet) PPE removal
Low Malaria, dengue Environmental control, Incorporate One Health
(vector-borne) repellents awareness
Risk assessment should be continuous. Where infection rates are high, first aid providers may
need to modify standard procedures, striking a balance between life-saving action and
self-protection. The guiding principle remains: intervene safely, not recklessly (Donovan et al.,
2024).
Typically, national and international health agencies will provide guidance for people facing an
endemic health crisis.
First aid during health crises
Protection and assessment
First aiders must always begin with scene safety and risk assessment. Observation from a
distance can help determine consciousness and breathing before contact. When possible, the
person should be encouraged to perform self-care actions, such as applying pressure to a
wound or using an inhaler, while guidance is given verbally.
Essential precautions include:
● Using medical-grade personal protective equipment (PPE, such as mask, gloves, eye
protection, gown) suited to the mode of transmission (Lindberg et al., 2020).
● Performing hand hygiene before and after every interaction, even when gloves are worn.
● Maintaining physical distance and ensuring adequate ventilation (Ferrari et al., 2022).
● Proper donning and doffing of PPE—ideally monitored by a second trained
responder—to reduce contamination risk.
In resource-limited settings, responders can use local alternatives (e.g. washable cloth masks,
improvised eye shields) but must still follow safe hygiene practices.
CONTEXTS | 67

Core first aid adaptations
● Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR): When respiratory transmission is suspected,
perform compression-only CPR and apply an automated external defibrillator (AED) as
soon as possible. Provide ventilations only when full PPE is available, and cover the
person’s mouth and nose with a light cloth or mask to reduce droplets.
● Bleeding and wounds: Use gloves, control bleeding with pressure and encourage
self-application if possible. Dispose of dressings safely.
● Burns and eye injuries: Flush with clean water or saline; avoid direct contact.
● Fainting or collapse: Position the person safely without close face-to-face contact; monitor
breathing visually.
If advanced care is delayed, first aid providers should maintain isolation and monitor the
person’s condition while preventing exposure to others.
Coordination and communication
Working together with public health authorities and humanitarian agencies keeps first aid
protocols and infection-control measures consistent. Local coordination may include referral to
isolation facilities, communication through radio or mobile networks, and documentation for
contact tracing. Honest communication builds community trust and reduces panic.
First aid education considerations in epidemic contexts
Adapting education safely
During epidemics, continuing first aid education helps communities stay prepared and resilient.
Training should follow national health directives and include infection-prevention measures
such as small class sizes, distancing, mask use and ventilation. When physical gatherings are
unsafe, distance or blended learning ensures access to essential knowledge (Ferrari et al., 2022).
Practical courses should emphasize:
● donning, doffing, and disposing of PPE under supervision
● disinfection of surfaces and training equipment (Sopwith et al., 2002)
● individual rather than shared use of mannequins, bandages and materials
● scenario-based learning that integrates infection-control decision-making.
Hybrid models that combined self-paced e-learning with short, well-ventilated in-person
sessions proved successful during COVID-19 across National Societies.
Supporting learners and trainers
