Health crises generate anxiety and fatigue. Programmes should include psychosocial support
and regular breaks for both learners and facilitators. Clear pre-course communication about
protective rules, symptoms and exclusion criteria builds confidence. Participants with symptoms
or recent exposure should be temporarily excluded and provided remote learning alternatives.
Visible information at entry points (posters, QR-codes, symptom lists) reinforces vigilance.
Learners benefit from being treated as partners in safety, actively engaged in risk reduction and
mutual protection.
68 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Hygiene and facility management
Learning spaces should ensure handwashing facilities or alcohol-based solutions (60–80%
concentration) and good ventilation. Cleaning logs should document disinfection before and
after sessions. Equipment and manuals should be assigned individually, or digital copies
provided on tablets to avoid sharing (Honein et al., 2020).
Where resources are scarce, education can take place outdoors or under open shelters to
maximize airflow while maintaining cultural sensitivity and inclusion.
Psychosocial, ethical and cultural dimensions
Health crises increase fear, stigma and ethical dilemmas. First aid providers may hesitate to help
for fear of infection or discrimination. Psychological First Aid principles—Look, Listen,
Link—provide a compassionate framework for addressing distress while maintaining boundaries
(World Health Organization & War Trauma Foundation and World Vision International, 2011).
Providers should be taught to manage fear and fatigue, use grounding techniques and access
peer support. In many cultures, collective or spiritual coping strategies (communal prayer,
storytelling or rituals) strengthen resilience and reduce stigma.
Ethically, balancing duty of care with personal safety is central. Programmes should include
guided discussions on when to act, how to use PPE responsibly and how to decline unsafe
requests without guilt. Emphasis should be placed on honest communication, empathy and
respect for local customs to build trust between providers and communities.
Conclusion
Epidemic and pandemic contexts demand adaptive, compassionate and safety-focused first aid
systems. Protecting both providers and recipients is essential not only for infection control but
for maintaining community trust and continuity of care.
Whether in a densely populated city or a remote rural settlement, first aid providers are a crucial
link in public-health resilience. By integrating infection-prevention practices,
context-appropriate education and psychosocial care, National Societies can ensure that help
continues safely even in the event of an outbreak.
Ultimately, epidemic preparedness in first aid is more than a technical measure; it is a
reaffirmation of humanitarian solidarity: that no one, regardless of circumstance, should face an
emergency alone.
CONTEXTS | 69

70 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

FIRST AID EDUCATION

72 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Central to the resilience of individuals and communities is the ability to respond effectively in a
crisis. The Movement views first aid as one of the greatest humanitarian acts and first aid
education as an integral part of building resilience. A 2025 Cochrane systematic review showed
that we can be moderately confident that first aid training of lay people (those with no formal
healthcare training) can increase their knowledge, skills and belief in their own capacity to act in
an emergency (Kendall et al., 2025). As the largest provider of first aid education in the world, the
Movement must advocate for and provide high-quality, accessible education so everyone can
give help safely and effectively in a first aid emergency. Here, we provide the essentials to aid
programme designers in doing this. The Education chapter includes a selection of contexts for
first aid education and an overview of modalities to implement high-quality first aid education.
Uniform tools to measure the quality of education interventions and their impact in terms of
behaviour change remain as gaps in practice and research (Kendall et al., 2025).
Chain of survival behaviours
The chain of survival behaviours (CoSB) defines the broad domains of first aid education.
Considering each first aid topic alongside the CoSB domains provides the scope in which to
emphasize and develop helping behaviours throughout the lifecycle of first aid education. For
example, some topics, such as stroke, usually focus on recognition, while others, such as
bleeding, usually emphasize first aid skills.
The CoSB also provides a structure for communicating educational messages. For example, we
can use media to prepare people for heat emergencies, inform them of early response or help
them recover from a heat emergency. We can use visual aids to help people recognize different
conditions, as well as scenarios and role-play to practise first aid. We can use technology to alert
people to imminent danger or to help them access support.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 73

The five domains of the CoSB
Visualized as a chain, each domain recognizes a unique yet
connected set of knowledge, skills and attitudes that help an
individual or organization limit suffering and promote healing.
1. The first domain emphasizes the role of prevention and
preparedness in reducing the impact of emergencies by
having people prepared to respond quickly and with the
necessary resources (human and tools). The act of
preparing also raises awareness of hazards and reduces
risky behaviours.
2. The second domain emphasizes early recognition of
environmental dangers and assessing the ill or injured to
make care decisions.
3. The third and fourth domains of response have two
actions that can occur at the same time if needed:
providing first aid and accessing help, if appropriate,
depending on the severity, number of first aid providers
and resources.
4. Last in the sequence is the domain of recovery, which can
occur with or without medical care. Notably, the recovery
process should include care and support for the
responder.
5. A holistic first aid education programme must help the learner move through each
domain to have the best outcome in an actual emergency. The approach taken to help
learners through each domain may be unique and learned separately or as part of an
integrated programme. Developers need to help learners consider how their knowledge
and skill acquisition can help support their behaviour change for each part of the chain.
Assessment of first aid curricula should ensure that each domain is accounted for in the
overall educational experience of learning first aid.
74 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Implementation of first aid education
National Societies and teams responsible for delivering first aid education should consider how
their programmes can support broader organizational goals.
At the most basic (elemental) level, first aid education covers physiological, psychological and
social content that promotes the health and resilience of individuals, communities and
populations. These topics, detailed in the Clinical chapters of the Guidelines, can be grouped
into courses.
When designing campaigns to implement these courses, it is important to consider the needs
of the target learners, available resources (such as time and budget) and desired outcomes.
Courses may stand alone or be part of larger public campaigns aimed at changing behaviours
on a broader, population level in response to identified issues or opportunities.
At the corporate level, each National Society is responsible for overseeing the implementation
and evaluation of these programmes. This includes aligning them with the Fundamental
Principles, using evidence-based practices and ensuring high-quality instruction.
Principles of first aid education
Principles of first aid education exist to support designers in developing first-aid programmes
that match the needs of their learners.
These principles should be applied to any first aid education intervention:
● Link to learners: Consider all aspects of the learner group (age, gender, responsibilities,
needs, etc). Adapt your approach so it is relevant and based on contexts they recognize.
● Provide variety: Use a variety of activities to engage the learner, develop their skills and
construct their knowledge.
● Keep it simple: Restrict content to what is necessary and keep learning messages simple:
avoid overloading learners with content, topics or techniques that they are unlikely to
come across or would not be able to use.
● Encourage discovery: Allow time for learners to explore and reflect on what they have
learned to develop their attitude and confidence to help.
● Be clear: Use language that the learners understand and that builds confidence. Avoid
scientific language or overly complex theories.
● Be outcome-driven: Identify learner outcomes (such as knowledge and confidence) and
measure how these change between the start and end of your education intervention.
Approaches to building first aid education
Understanding emergency response behaviour
Teaching first aid requires a well-structured approach, tailored to different individuals and
groups. Below are three theories that can help guide this process. People's responses to
emergencies can vary based on their demographics, culture, past experiences and other factors,
such as media exposure. First aid education needs to consider these aspects to promote both
short-term and long-term behaviour changes.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 75

Integrative Model of Behaviour Prediction
The Integrative Model of Behaviour Prediction, developed by Fishbein and Yzer in 2003,
combines the Theory of Reasoned Action and the Theory of Planned Behaviour. This model
emphasizes that attitude, subjective norms and perceived control influence behaviour. For first
aid education to be effective, it is crucial to understand learners’ motivations. By focusing on why
learners want to help and showing them the impact they can make, we can boost their
confidence and increase their likelihood of assisting in emergencies.
Transtheoretical Model
The Transtheoretical Model, proposed by Prochaska and DiClemente in 1983, breaks down
behaviour change into stages: from not being aware of a behaviour to knowing about it,
planning to adopt it, implementing it and maintaining it. This model may help organize a
first-aid education element, course or campaign into stages to support behaviour change. For
first aid education, this means identifying where learners are in this process and designing
targeted approaches. For instance, raising awareness about evolving climate-related hazards is
different from updating workplace first aid courses with the latest medical practices.
Experiential Learning Theory
Kolb's Experiential Learning Theory from 1984 suggests that people learn best through
experience and reflection. In first aid training, this means creating scenarios that allow learners
to practise and reflect on their actions when practising scenarios at the elemental and course
levels. This hands-on approach helps them test and refine their responses in a safe environment,
enhancing their readiness for real emergencies.
76 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Continuum of educational activities
A continuum of group activities offers a structured approach to training exercises, making them
highly applicable to first aid education strategies. By progressing through discussion-based
exercises (e.g. seminars, workshops, tabletop scenarios) and operations-based exercises (e.g.
drills, functional and full-scale exercises), organizations can scaffold first aid training from basic
knowledge acquisition to complex, real-world application. This allows National Red Cross Red
Crescent Societies and branches to work beyond individual training and look at readiness levels
of families, communities, businesses and populations to manage health and safety risks.
For first aid education, this continuum enables educators to introduce foundational concepts,
such as CoSB, in traditional courses, but also through seminars and tabletop exercises, allowing
learners to explore prevention, recognition and response concepts in a low-pressure
environment and relevant contexts before practising hands-on skills in drills and scenarios that
simulate emergencies.
Integrating this framework with first aid campaigns and corporate-level work helps ensure
learners achieve competence across all CoSB domains by matching exercise complexity and
modality to learning objectives. For instance, early exercises can focus on knowledge retention
and recognition (CoSB domains 1 and 2). More advanced, scenario-based drills allow learners to
practise providing aid and accessing help (CoSB domains 3 and 4). Post-event debriefs can
facilitate reflection on recovery and responder well-being (CoSB domain 5). This methodology
supports continuous evaluation and improvement, making first aid education responsive to
learner needs and better aligned with the behavioural outcomes essential for community
resilience.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 77

Motivation to learn first aid
Key action
Consider the individual’s and/or population’s specific motivation to learn and use this to inform
the educational approach, resources and content included in first aid education.
Introduction
People have different motivations to learn first aid, the strongest being a requirement to attend
a course (e.g. for work or school). Other motivations include, for example, if learners have a friend
or family member at risk of illness or injury, if they live far away from healthcare services, or if
there is limited access to EMS. When learners are motivated, they are more engaged and
invested. This enhances their retention and application of skills. Personal relevance, practical
applications and positive learning environments can increase motivation. Ultimately,
motivations must outweigh barriers to attending.
Guidelines
● First aid programme designers should advocate for first aid education and recertification
as a requirement for specific groups, such as schoolchildren, new drivers and employees,
to organizations and legislatures.**
● First aid education should be made accessible (available, affordable and focused) to those
who care for at-risk family members, vulnerable populations and those in remote
locations.**
● Self-directed learning completed in a familiar context (e.g. at home) may improve
individuals’ motivation to achieve the learning outcomes successfully.*
Good practice points
● Use community communications, contexts and networks to increase motivation for
learning.
Chain of survival behaviours
Motivation is incorporated throughout the CoSB. Different motivations exist for attending
training (prevention and preparedness).
Education considerations
Learner considerations
● By aligning first aid training with the specific motivations to attend, programme
designers can create more efficient and effective campaigns.
● Understanding the motivations and needs of the audience will then support first aid
education strategies used within programmes.
● A positive attitude towards first aid is helpful as it can lead to an increased willingness to
engage in first aid training and application in emergencies. Promoting the relevance and
78 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

importance of first aid across various age groups and educational backgrounds could
further enhance these positive attitudes.
Facilitation tips
● Encourage learners to share their first aid experiences, as this will help to understand their
motivation for attending and resolve any concerns they have about providing care in an
emergency.
Scientific foundation
Workplace and educational requirements
● Job and role-specific necessity: Cariou and Pelaccia (2017) and Platz et al. (2000) found
that legal or regulatory mandates, such as workplace requirements, significantly
influence individuals' decisions to seek out first aid education, often more so than
personal reasons, like having an at-risk family member. Weaker evidence was found
regarding the duty to care in sports, awareness of opportunities (Fortington et al., 2019);
recertifying after certification had expired, and “to be prepared/ just in case” (Bouland et
al., 2017).
● Educational and training settings: The importance of training in schools is highlighted by
Supraja et al. (2021) who found that teachers who learned of their ignorance of dental
trauma were motivated to learn more. Similarly, Jaskiewicz and Timler (2023) found that
raising awareness of training opportunities and the need for training increased people's
motivation to attend future courses.
Family and personal circumstances
● Parental and caregiver responsibility: Many studies suggest that personal experiences,
particularly related to caring for children or elderly family members, are significant
motivators for learning first aid. Huang et al. (2016) identified the characteristics of
Chinese students who were willing to learn CPR. They considered the development of the
local emergency system to be excellent, cardiovascular diseases were present within their
family, and they had previously heard about CPR. Kliegel et al. (2000) noted that
individuals with cardiovascular at-risk family members who had taken a one-day CPR
class were more likely to motivate their family members to attend at a higher rate than a
control group.
Cost and accessibility
● Affordability and accessibility: Fortington et al. (2017) and Pearn et al. (1980) also
highlighted that reducing the cost of first aid training would likely increase participation
rates, especially among at-risk populations.
● Self-led and home-based learning: Studies by Greenberg et al. (2012) and Kliegel et al.
(2000) revealed that individuals with an at-risk family member or those who have been
advised by a doctor to learn CPR often preferred self-led learning or home-based courses,
as these formats are more flexible and convenient. This suggests that alternative learning
methods, such as online or at-home training, could increase engagement, especially
among individuals who find traditional course formats challenging.
● At-risk populations: Huang et al. (2016) found that having an at-risk family member can
positively influence one's motivation to learn first aid. However, this motivation is not as
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 79

strong as other factors, such as legal requirements or workplace mandates. This suggests
that while personal circumstances play a role, structured, compulsory training may be a
more powerful motivator for many individuals.
80 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid education for children
Key action
Encourage children to develop essential first aid knowledge and skills and foster a lifelong
learning mindset.
Introduction
First aid education involves teaching children the knowledge and skills necessary to respond to
emergencies. This topic explores how to effectively develop first aid abilities in children of various
ages, focusing on methods to help them retain these skills and attitudes across different
settings and contexts.
Guidelines
● Contextual relevance: First aid programmes should be designed based on the
educational pathway outlined by the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP),
ensuring that the content is appropriate for children’s intellectual, social and behavioural
development.**
● Engagement through active learning: Combining hands-on training with secondary
methods (e.g. educational songs) can enhance children’s ability to retain knowledge and
increase their confidence in using these skills during emergencies.**
● Schoolteacher training: Training teachers to deliver first aid education is often more
effective, time- and cost-efficient, and contextually relevant than relying on external
medical facilitators.**
Good practice points
● Create a positive learning environment: Create a space where learning is recognized and
rewarded, and where children are comfortable asking questions and participating.
● Use relevant scenarios: Design learning experiences around real-life situations that
encourage children to draw on their own life experiences. This helps them understand
the importance and practical application of first aid.
● Repetition: Repeated exposure to first aid concepts and skills helps children reinforce and
retain their knowledge. While the ideal frequency of repetition is not definitively
established, periodic refreshers are beneficial.
● Age-appropriate learning:
○ In settings where access to medical care is complex, it may be more appropriate to
teach children how to seek medical help at age 9 or 10. This knowledge should be
revisited until they are 18 years old.
○ Teach children the importance of handwashing before and after administering first
aid, especially in environments where infectious diseases are prevalent. This
practice should also be revisited throughout childhood and adolescence.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 81

○ Teach younger children how to assess emergency scenes and call for help through
activities appropriate for their age. This helps children understand their role in
emergencies and the importance of scene management.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Always ensure that first aid programmes for children align with your organization's child
protection policies, as well as those of any partner organizations (e.g. schools).
● Choose the timing of first aid programmes carefully (e.g. avoid school exam periods).
Facilitation tips
● Keep messages simple: Ensure that first aid messages are short, clear and focused on
practical outcomes. Use rhymes or songs to help retain information or desired behaviours.
● Appropriate language: Tailor language to the age and experience of the children. Avoid
medical terms and use words that are easy for children to understand.
● Start with what they know: Begin by asking children what they already know about the
body and why first aid is important. This allows you to build on their existing knowledge.
● Acknowledge experiences: Facilitate discussions where children can share their
experiences with emergencies or illnesses. This helps to create a supportive learning
environment, particularly for children who may have experienced trauma (e.g. loss of a
family member or a serious accident). Make connections between first aid and everyday
life so that children can see the usefulness of first aid in their daily lives.
● Recognize progress: Praise children’s progress and highlight their efforts and
achievements.
● Peer learning: Support the development of first aid clubs or groups where children and
young people can teach and learn from each other. Peer influence, especially from older
children, can significantly enhance learning.
● Family engagement: Encourage children to share their first aid knowledge with their
families, fostering a wider community understanding of first aid.
● Integrate first aid into other subjects: Enhance children’s interest in first aid by integrating
it into various subjects, such as biology or physical education classes.
● Simulation-based learning: Encourage teachers to incorporate first aid learning through
simulations, where children practise real-world scenarios in a safe and controlled
environment.
Facilitation tools
● Scenario-based learning: Create engaging, age-appropriate scenarios that allow children
to apply their life experiences. Some examples include:
○ role-playing with scripted scenarios
○ simulating various injuries or medical conditions
○ encouraging children to develop their own emergency scenarios
○ practising first aid in less conventional environments (e.g. in a vehicle or outdoors)
rather than just in a classroom setting. Always ensure that these activities follow
the latest child protection policies.
82 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Interactive learning: Use games, quizzes, videos and online apps to make learning fun and
interactive.
● Teacher support: Share short webinars or instructional videos with useful tips and
guidance to help teachers who may lack confidence in delivering first aid education.
● Blended learning: Use a variety of learning approaches, such as flipped learning or
blended learning, which combine online content with in-person instruction. These
methods can yield results similar to those of traditional classroom-based teaching.
Scientific foundation
The educational pathway developed by CEBaP focuses on first aid education for children,
incorporating insights from systematic reviews (2015, 2019) and emphasizing the impact on
knowledge, skills and attitudes across different age groups, especially in low and middle-income
countries. The pathway uses an "encourage, know, repeat" framework, where the "encourage"
and "repeat" stages are designed for facilitators and the "know" stage is for learners. This
pathway, plus findings from additional sources, are summarized here:
1. Basic first aid skills: Evidence suggests children as young as six can learn basic first aid.
Teaching strategies, such as problem-solving and cooperative learning, can enhance
outcomes, although the evidence is of low certainty. Contextual adaptations should be
considered in all cases. For example, in contexts where access to medical care is more
complex (such as low-resource settings), teaching this topic might be best delayed until
children are 9 or 10 years old. In contexts where disease prevalence is higher, an important
focus of first aid education should be handwashing (De Buck et al., 2015; Suwanpairoj et
al., 2020).
2. Skill development timeline: Children should learn basic first aid for burns, bleeding and
skin wounds by the ages of 7–8, with more advanced topics such as CPR introduced by
the ages of 11–14 (De Buck et al., 2015; Dumcke et al., 2021).
3. Learning CPR in school: Many authors recommend CPR training in schools (Bohn et al.,
2012; Böttiger, 2015; Böttiger et al., 2016; Ellis et al., 2020; Kehlert et al., 2024; Lanzas et al.,
2022; Lukas et al., 2016; Pivač et al., 2021; Reveruzzi et al., 2016; Semeraro et al., 2021, 2023),
indicating that early and compulsory CPR education can lead to lasting behavioural
changes and improved outcomes in cardiac arrest situations. In the Kids Save Lives study,
Lukas et al. (2016) concluded that repeated annual CPR training for at least three years
can result in long-lasting learning. Furthermore, Kehlert et al. (2024) highlighted that
trained schoolteachers can effectively teach CPR without medical staff, and Lanzas et al.
(2022) found that teacher-led training is equally effective and more cost-efficient than
training conducted by health professionals.
4. Learning approaches: Suwanpairoj et al. (2020) showed that simulation-based training
improves students' confidence and skills in basic life support (BLS). Cons-Ferreiro et al.
(2023) studied the “flipped classroom” approach and found no significant differences
between flipped learning and conventional methods in teaching BLS, indicating both are
effective. Semeraro et al. (2024) developed a blended learning programme that integrates
lectures, hands-on training and virtual reality, improving students' readiness to respond in
emergencies. Jaskiewicz & Timler (2023) noted a preference among Polish youth for
hybrid training methods that incorporate online resources alongside traditional
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 83

instruction. Eley et al. (2019) advocated for web-based games like Body Busters to
improve knowledge among junior students (ages 7–11), suggesting that further levels
could enhance learning for older students. Allan et al. (2023) found that peer- and
self-directed training methods are as effective as instructor-led approaches, emphasizing
the need for practical training opportunities.
5. Mental health first aid: Studies by Rossetto et al. (2020) and Yousefian and Mohamadirizi
(2023) highlighted the effectiveness of mental health first aid training in improving
self-efficacy and literacy among adolescents. Research by Martínez-Isasi et al. (2022)
suggests that culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) youth benefit from tailored
mental health first aid training, which enhances their skills and increases their intention
to seek help.
84 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Online and blended learning
Key actions
● Use online and blended learning to develop first aid competencies for both adults and
children to improve flexibility and effectiveness in first aid education.
Introduction
Online learning refers to self-directed or facilitator-led interactive tools that learners access
through digital devices. Blended learning combines online self-study with supervised and
coordinated, facilitator-led sessions. Both approaches offer flexibility and accessibility for diverse
audiences.
In recent years, digital content for online first aid training has experienced significant growth. It
is essential to be vigilant about the reliability of this content.
The benefits include:
● Flexibility: Learners can access materials and complete coursework at their own pace and
convenience.
● Cost-effective: Reduces the need for travel and physical resources, lowering overall
training costs.
● Interactive learning: Combines online modules with hands-on practice for a
comprehensive learning experience.
● Accessibility: Provides access to expert instruction and resources regardless of geographic
location.
● Consistency: The use of video provides consistent modelling to support consistent
delivery.
● Engagement: Multimedia and interactive elements help to maintain learner interest and
improve retention.
Guidelines
● Online learning can be an effective method for teaching first aid knowledge, particularly
for topics like heart attack, asthma treatment, burns treatment and defibrillator use.**
● Online learning can be a cost-effective method for public information campaigns.*
● Consider using a blended learning approach for cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and
Basic Life Support (BLS) training, as it can be effective for knowledge transfer and
increasing confidence.*
● Blended learning approaches can offer additional benefits such as flexibility and reduced
training costs.*
Good practice points
● Ensure that proper safeguarding measures are in place, especially for children.
● Combine self-directed learning with facilitated sessions that focus on practical skills.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 85

● Check the material sourced from the internet to ensure its reliability.
● Encourage peer interactions to influence learners' attitudes and confidence.
● Promote repetition and practice outside official learning time.
● Use interactive videos that incorporate exercises, quizzes or games to reinforce learning
and encourage engagement.
● Include links to additional resources, such as websites or reference materials, to
encourage learners to further explore their knowledge.
Chain of survival behaviours
There is evidence that online learning can increase knowledge. Combining online learning with
face-to-face sessions can be an effective way to develop practical skills. Both knowledge and
practical skills are critical for building confidence and a willingness to act. Therefore, programme
developers can use blended and online learning to support learners across all five domains of
the CoSB: prevent and prepare, early recognition, accessing help, first aid and self-recovery.
Education considerations
Context considerations
A variety of adult and child learner audiences may use online learning. Learners’ cultural and
socio-economic backgrounds may influence their confidence and ability to complete online
learning. Online learning can be highly effective but relies heavily on technology. Consider the
learners’ context, including their access to technology, internet connectivity and technical
literacy.
Consider taking the following actions to best support the learning experience:
● Provide offline access options and printable resources.
● Consider data privacy and security, especially for minors.
● Offer technical support and encourage peer support networks to enhance engagement.
Learner considerations
Online learning can have social benefits if learners can interact and collaborate; however,
learners’ age, as well as cultural and socio.economic backgrounds, may influence their
confidence and ability to use online apps or tools. Confirm how familiar and comfortable your
learners are with online learning.
Facilitation tips
● Use online learning to complement face-to-face facilitation, improving learners’
knowledge and skills. Note that while there is evidence that online learning can improve
learners’ knowledge, there is not enough evidence to suggest it can be used as a
standalone tool for developing skills.
● Encourage learners to revisit the online content for reference and retention. This may help
them to retain knowledge for longer periods of time.
● Implement adequate safeguarding measures, especially for more vulnerable learners.
● Explore different delivery methods, including mobile apps, using games (gamification)
and multimedia (e.g. 3D videos, augmented or virtual reality).
86 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Scientific foundation
Recent research has extensively examined the efficacy of technology-enhanced learning
methods in first aid education. This review synthesizes key findings to inform the guidelines for
first aid instruction.
Online learning
Bylow et al. (2019, 2020) advocated for web-based education formats that integrate various
learning activities to improve BLS outcomes. While Reder et al. (2006) found independent
computer learning to be effective for acquiring CPR knowledge, they noted challenges in
teaching physical skills through this medium. Yeung et al. (2017) demonstrated that interactive
games like "Lifesaver" were most effective when combined with face-to-face facilitation.
Doucet et al. (2018) found no significant difference in knowledge or skills between app-based
learning and facilitator-led courses, though the latter excelled in specific tasks. Mancini et al.
(2009) demonstrated that online learning is non-inferior to facilitator-led courses, despite
self-directed learners scoring lower on practical skills. Brannon et al. (2009) observed that
pre-class videos improved CPR skill performance among parents when viewed before hands-on
training.
Blended learning approaches
A systematic review by Abuejheisheh et al. (2023) revealed that blended learning significantly
enhances knowledge and skills retention in BLS compared to traditional methods, while
reducing costs. Elgohary et al. (2022) corroborated these findings, indicating that blended
learning is as effective as traditional methods for participant satisfaction and skill development.
Oliver et al. (2020) found that blended learning improves learners' willingness and confidence
more than facilitator-led sessions alone. Ko et al. (2024) reported that increased practice
opportunities during in-person sessions contribute to better CPR compression speed
performance.
A randomized controlled trial on first aid blended learning in adult lay people in Rwanda showed
a significant improvement in knowledge, skills, self-efficacy and willingness to help, compared to
no training, and showed that outcomes were non-inferior to facilitator-led training (Kendall et al.
2026).
Innovative approaches
Semeraro et al. (2024) reported high engagement levels with virtual reality, particularly among
younger students. Cons-Ferriero et al. (2023) found flipped classroom approaches as effective as
traditional methods for teaching CPR to primary students. Kim and Cho (2023) demonstrated
that medi-VR simulation resulted in higher CPR performance compared to flipped learning. In
mental health first aid, Reavley et al. (2021) found that both blended and eLearning formats led
to long-term improvements in knowledge and intentions to help, with blended courses showing
superior outcomes.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 87

Digital technologies in health management
Saeidnia et al. (2022) found that mobile apps could improve self-care skills in health
management, particularly in underdeveloped countries. Myers et al. (2021) emphasized the
benefits of online CPR training to increase accessibility. Yousefian and Mohamadirizi (2023)
recommended the Information-Motivation-behavioural Skills (IMB) model to enhance CPR
self-efficacy among high school students.
Basic life support
The Education, Implementation and Teams Taskforce of ILCOR performed a systematic review in
2024 on the topic of self-directed digital-based training versus facilitator-led CPR training in
adults and children (Eastwood et al., 2024) . They suggested that either is appropriate for training
CPR or AED skills in people aged 10 and above. They suggested using self-directed digital
training when facilitator-led training is not accessible, or when quantity over quality is required. A
2022 scientific review on the topic of blended learning revealed mixed findings regarding the
effectiveness of facilitator-led and blended approaches in terms of skills and knowledge. This led
to their recommendation of a blended learning approach where resources and accessibility
permit (Lockey et al., 2022).
Conclusion
While digital learning tools show promise in improving health-related knowledge and skills
across diverse populations, results for skill development are mixed. Blended learning
approaches, combining online and face-to-face methods, emerge as the most effective strategy.
The research underscores the potential of digital learning to increase accessibility and scalability
of health education, particularly in underserved areas or during pandemics. However, limitations
in virtual skill assessment highlight areas for further research and improvement in online health
education methodologies.
88 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Mass social media
Key action
Use various forms of communication to increase public awareness about first aid competencies
and normalize positive attitudes and beliefs around emergency response and to encourage
individuals to acquire or refresh their understanding of first aid principles and techniques.
Introduction
This section addresses both traditional media (such as television, radio, newspapers and
internet/websites) and new media (such as social media applications, platforms utilizing
short-form video content and text message-based engagement systems). Traditional and new
media can be a cost-effective way to spread first aid information widely and provide educational
information to diverse audiences, from individuals and local communities to global populations.
When selecting a media outlet for the distribution of first aid information, two critical factors are
considered:
1. Accessibility: The chosen media platform must be readily available to the target audience.
2. Frequency: It should be one that the intended recipients frequently use.
The information conveyed through these media channels should adhere to the following
standards:
1. Authenticity: The content must be factually accurate and reliable, ideally evidence-based.
Engage subject matter experts to provide targeted information through appropriate
media channels.
2. Relevance: The information should be relatable to the target audience's needs and
cultural context. Use real-life accounts from individuals who have been influenced by
first-aid media content to encourage behavioural change in others.
3. Engagement: The presentation of the content should be captivating and maintain the
audience's interest.
4. Educational value: The material should impart relevant knowledge and skills related to
first aid practices.
Guidelines
● Mass media should be considered for enhancing first aid awareness and the timely, rapid
distribution of clear first aid messaging, such as during or in advance of emergencies.*
● Mass media could be used to stimulate learning motivation by highlighting the necessity,
potential risks for the first aider and benefits of learning first aid.*
● First aid programme designers should carefully consider the appropriateness of media
types for the particular topics, lessons or messages they wish to convey.*
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 89

Good practice points
● Prioritize simplicity and clarity, incorporating textual and visual elements when feasible.
Content should be engaging and educational.
● Use mass media to offer “lower dose, higher frequency” learning opportunities to
enhance learner confidence and increase the likelihood of individuals helping. Develop
engaging media content using innovative approaches, such as incorporating popular
trends to increase visibility and shareability.
Chain of survival behaviours
Media platforms can play a crucial role in supporting the "prevent and prepare" domain.
Specifically, media can be an effective tool for:
1. Raising public awareness: Media channels can spread information about potential health
and safety risks, educating the general population about common emergencies and their
prevention.
2. Communicating appropriate responses: Through various media formats, organizations
can inform the public about the correct actions to take in different first aid scenarios,
thereby improving overall emergency preparedness.
Education considerations
Context considerations
Media selection and message tailoring
● Choose media outlets that align with your target audience's preferences and use.
● Adapt your message format to suit the selected platform. For instance, use storytelling for
radio broadcasts or create short, engaging video clips for social media platforms like
TikTok.
● Consider whether your message will be consumed proactively or reactively, as this will
influence the depth and detail of the content.
● Increase visibility by asking members of your organization to follow the conversations on
social platforms and be prepared to engage with users and respond to questions.
Cost-effectiveness and reach
● Evaluate the cost and reach of different media outlets to optimize your resource
allocation.
● Find a balance between the need for broad reach and targeted messaging for specific
audiences.
Cultural sensitivity
● Tailor your messaging approach to align with cultural preferences for information sharing.
● Be mindful of cultural nuances and adapt your content accordingly.
● Include the intended audience in the development of key messages and content to
ensure cultural appropriateness and resonance.
90 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Strategic timing and creativity
● In media-saturated environments, carefully consider the timing and delivery of your key
messages.
● Employ creative strategies to capture attention and stand out from competing
information.
Integrated approach
● Combine media messaging with other initiatives to influence policies and practices in
your region.
● Use a mix of traditional and modern communication channels, including text messaging,
news outlets, radio, social media and online learning platforms.
● Be aware that the effects of a media campaign may lessen once it is over (Eppler et al.,
1994), so consider ways to share messaging and frequently engage with your audience.
Collaboration with journalists
● Leverage daily news coverage of first aid emergencies by providing informative content to
journalists.
● Offer relevant preventive actions and first aid steps to enhance the educational value of
news stories.
Literacy and comprehension
● Consider the target population's general literacy level as well as their health literacy. These
factors should inform the complexity of the language used and the depth of explanations
provided.
Facilitation tips
● Always ensure the accuracy and credibility of the source of your media content before
sharing and provide proper attribution to the original creator.
Scientific foundation
Effectiveness of media campaigns
Several studies have assessed the impact of media campaigns on awareness and knowledge of
life-saving practices, such as CPR.
● Meischke et al. (1999) found that a 30-second public service broadcast significantly
improved CPR knowledge and awareness but did not influence participants’ intent to
perform CPR.
● Benoit et al. (2017) reported that a brief CPR training video displayed in a hospital waiting
room (passive exposure) improved CPR skills (e.g. compression rate and depth), but
lacked real-world behaviour testing.
● Becker et al. (1999) noted a significant increase in bystander CPR following public service
announcements on television; however, they did not assess CPR quality or long-term
effects.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 91

● Luepker et al. (2000) found that an 18-month multimedia campaign increased the use of
emergency medical services and public knowledge about heart attack symptoms, but
did not significantly change actual CPR behaviour.
Tailored messaging and demographics
Tailoring educational messages to specific audience demographics can enhance effectiveness.
● Alruwaili et al. (2024) found that burn care knowledge in Saudi Arabia varied by gender,
age and education. Tailoring messaging to these factors improves engagement and
learning.
● Farquharson et al. (2022) demonstrated that text messages sent after CPR training can
enhance intentions and self-rated competence in performing CPR, addressing barriers
such as low confidence and emotional responses.
Text and digital media communication
Text messaging and digital platforms are increasingly used to share public health information.
● Menn et al. (2021) found that text message emergency notifications, especially when
combined with other communication methods, are effective in rapidly distributing safety
messages and engaging diverse audiences.
● Bashekah et al. (2023) highlighted the growing role of social media in first aid education
but stressed the need for accurate information and clear, simple messaging, especially
when using visual formats.
Challenges and limitations of media campaigns
Media campaigns often face challenges in sustaining long-term behaviour change.
● Leavy et al. (2015, 2016) reviewed drowning prevention campaigns and found varied
outcomes, cautioning against relying on a single approach. They identified a gap in
research on multi-strategy water safety education.
● Eppler et al. (1994) noted that the effects of media campaigns diminish after they end,
highlighting the importance of continued engagement, such as by creating new content
on social media.
Misconceptions from media representations of first aid
Media depictions can contribute to misconceptions about first aid practices.
● Alismail et al. (2018) and Colwill et al. (2018) pointed out that television often portrays
unrealistic CPR outcomes, such as spontaneous breathing after a brief period of CPR,
which can mislead viewers about the duration and effectiveness of CPR.
Conclusion
Media campaigns can effectively raise awareness and knowledge of life-saving practices, such as
CPR, but their impact on actual behaviour is less consistent. Tailored, text-based messaging can
enhance long-term engagement, while challenges such as post-campaign engagement and
media inaccuracies remain. Multi-modal, sustained campaigns that provide clear, accurate
information are recommended for improving public health outcomes.
92 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Feedback devices for education
Key action
When implemented effectively, feedback devices enhance the experience of first aid education.
Introduction
High-quality instructors and educators provide feedback in most educational contexts.
Feedback devices and activities can supplement or replace instructors in some of those
contexts. Feedback devices are tools that provide auditory, visual or physical cues to learners,
helping them develop first-aid competencies. The most common use of feedback devices
occurs in resuscitation skills training. These devices have evolved significantly since the 1960s,
with the integration of augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) technologies, offering
more immersive and interactive learning experiences both in and outside the classroom.
Gamification can extend feedback to include behaviours and attitudes.
Gamification is the introduction of game elements (e.g. collecting achievements, earning points
or team play) to first aid education to increase learners' engagement and develop
competencies. An example of this is a mobile application that tests the ability to assess various
scenes for hazards. Gamification in both electronic and physical (non-electronic) forms can
captivate learners’ interest, provide feedback and offer opportunities to re-engage with the
content. Given the increased popularity of mobile technology, electronic gamification can
potentially reach a much broader audience with fun, engaging and effective first-aid education,
although traditional games that do not use technology can also be effective.
Guidelines
● Incorporate feedback devices as a standard component of cardiopulmonary resuscitation
(CPR) training programmes for healthcare professionals and lay rescuers.**
Good practice points
● Provide comprehensive instruction on the correct use of feedback devices to ensure
trainees can effectively interpret and respond to the feedback provided.
● Include training sessions both with and without feedback devices to ensure rescuers can
perform high-quality CPR independent of technological assistance.
● Regularly review CPR performance data collected from feedback devices to identify areas
for improvement and tailor training accordingly.
● Implement regular refresher training sessions using feedback devices to maintain and
improve CPR skills over time.
● Devices should be appropriate to how learners can or prefer to receive information.
● Devices may be used in remote or distance learning situations or for self-study.
● Gamification may be used to deliver first aid content either independently or as part of a
comprehensive educational strategy.
● Gamification should be purposeful and supported by an educational approach that
clearly identifies its benefits in relation to the intended learning outcomes.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 93

● Gamification tools should respect cultural norms and not trivialize life-threatening
scenarios.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevention and preparedness
While feedback devices are not typically used for teaching prevention strategies, training
programmes could incorporate simulations that use feedback devices to help learners identify
potential hazards and practise preventive measures related to scene safety to develop their
preparedness.
Early recognition
Feedback devices can be used to train individuals to recognize signs of life-threatening
conditions (e.g. abnormal breathing patterns or absence of breathing) while providing feedback
on the speed and accuracy of assessment.
First aid
This domain is where feedback devices can have the most significant impact:
● CPR training: Feedback devices are particularly useful for teaching high-quality CPR
(compression depth, rate, recoil and hand placement).
● AED training: Devices that simulate AED use and provide feedback on pad placement
and timing of shocks can enhance learners' confidence and competence.
● Choking management: Feedback devices could be developed to help trainees practise
the correct technique and force for back blows and abdominal thrusts.
Accessing help
While this domain primarily focuses on decision-making and communication skills, gamification
can be incorporated into scenario-based training to help learners perform concurrent actions
(e.g. calling emergency services while applying pressure to a wound) and provide feedback on
the clarity and completeness of the information provided to emergency dispatchers.
Self-recovery/early medical care
Feedback devices in this domain could focus on:
● Training for proper casualty positioning, using devices that provide feedback on the
correct angle for recovery positions.
● Simulations for ongoing assessment of a casualty's condition, using devices that offer
feedback on the frequency and accuracy of checks.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Feedback devices may add to training costs, making them less suitable for just-in-time
learning or in areas with limited resources. Free first aid apps may be available as an
alternative.
94 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Feedback devices may provide effective learning opportunities when a facilitator is
absent (such as during pandemic isolations). However, they may be less effective in
contexts where learners respect or expect facilitator-led programming.
● Virtual reality (VR) or augmented reality (AR) can be used to develop levels of stress
(beneficial stress) to support learning.
● Games can be used in learning across high- and low-resource settings. Consider how the
educational setting (face-to-face or online) may influence the types of games that can be
used. Also, consider how feedback can be effectively incorporated into these games, for
example, scoring points for putting first aid steps into the correct sequence.
Learner considerations
● Consider the learner audience and their learning context (e.g. devices that may work for
first aid providers may not meet the needs of professional responders).
● Feedback devices should be selected based on the outcomes that best serve the learners'
needs (e.g. selecting a device that provides visual feedback may be more suitable for
learners with hearing difficulties).
● While feedback devices offer numerous benefits, it is crucial to consider their impact on
cognitive load during the learning process. Some studies suggest that novice learners
may experience increased cognitive load when first introduced to these devices, which
could potentially impact their initial performance (Jeffers et al., 2022; Sweller et al., 2011).
Curriculum developers should be mindful of this and consider strategies to gradually
introduce feedback devices to minimize cognitive overload.
● Be aware that VR or AR could be a trigger for recalling previous trauma or phobias.
Facilitation tips
● Provide a brief overview of the topic and then introduce learners to the technology before
they begin using the feedback device.
● Train facilitators to set up the device or game, introduce it to learners, monitor and
support the feedback provided, and resolve any technical difficulties.
● Make use of games or feedback devices to motivate learners to perform an action or
behaviour again and again to improve performance through repeated use.
Facilitation tools
● Choose feedback devices that have demonstrated efficacy in improving CPR quality
through rigorous scientific evaluation.
● Select devices that are compatible with existing equipment and can be easily integrated
into current CPR protocols.
● Conduct thorough cost-benefit analyses when considering the implementation of
feedback devices, taking into account potential improvements in CPR quality and patient
outcomes.
Scientific foundation
The use of feedback devices in CPR training has been the subject of several studies, revealing
significant themes regarding their effectiveness and impact on skill acquisition and
performance. A prominent finding across multiple studies is the marked improvement in CPR
quality when feedback devices are employed during training and actual performance. The
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 95

International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (Lin et al., 2023) reaffirmed its treatment
recommendation based on a systematic review and meta-analysis for the use of feedback
during training.
Wutzler et al. (2018) demonstrated a substantial increase in the percentage of optimal
compressions from 14.6% without feedback to 58.9% with a feedback device. Similarly, Yeung et
al. (2009) and Zhou et al. (2020) reported enhancements in compression depth, rate and hand
positioning when feedback devices were utilized.
Effectiveness of real-time feedback
The role of feedback devices in skill acquisition and retention is another crucial theme emerging
from the research. Yeung et al. (2009) found that these devices facilitate better initial skill
acquisition compared to traditional training methods. While short-term skill retention appears
to improve with the use of feedback devices, Zhou et al. (2020) noted that the long-term
retention benefits are less definitive, suggesting an area for further investigation.
Real-time feedback devices have shown significant promise in improving the quality of CPR
performance. Studies indicate that these devices can enhance compression depth, rate and
overall CPR quality (Alcázar Artero et al., 2024; Cortegiani et al., 2017; Liyanage et al., 2019;
Suwanpairoj et al., 2020). The immediate nature of the feedback enables trainees to make
real-time adjustments, resulting in more effective skill acquisition and retention.
Virtual reality (VR) simulations for CPR training
Several studies have explored the use of VR technology to create immersive CPR training
experiences (Arthur et al., 2023; Buttussi et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2024; Hubail et al., 2022; Jeffers
et al., 2022; Leary et al., 2020; Liyanage et al., 2019). These VR simulations aim to provide realistic
practice environments. The VR systems can track user movements and provide feedback on
compression depth, rate and other CPR metrics.
Impact on skill retention
Research suggests that the use of feedback devices during training can lead to improved skill
retention over time. Learners who utilize these devices during practice sessions demonstrate
better performance in follow-up assessments compared to those trained without such devices
(Yeung et al., 2009). This finding has important implications for the design of first aid training
programmes, suggesting that incorporating feedback devices may lead to more lasting skill
acquisition.
Transferability of skills
An important consideration is the transferability of skills learned with feedback devices to
real-world scenarios where such devices may not be available. Research findings are mixed on
this point, with some studies suggesting good skill transfer (Yeung et al., 2009), while others
indicate potential over-reliance on the devices (Cortegiani et al., 2017). This highlights the need
for balanced training approaches that incorporate both device-assisted and traditional methods.
The effectiveness of feedback devices in training laypersons has been highlighted in several
studies (E. Aksoy, 2019; Ohle et al., 2021; Wutzler et al., 2017). There have been reported significant
96 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

improvements in CPR performance by lay rescuers when using feedback devices; however, the
type of device plays a role. This finding is particularly relevant as it suggests the potential for
Do-it-Yourself (DIY) devices to increase the accessibility of CPR training, especially in
resource-limited settings (Ohle et al., 2021; van Duijn et al., 2024).
Despite the evident benefits, some studies noted potential limitations of feedback devices.
Yeung et al. (2009) raised concerns about the potential over-reliance on devices during training
and mixed results for more complex skills, such as bag-valve-mask ventilation. They also
emphasized the importance of device calibration on various surfaces, such as floors versus
mattresses, to ensure accuracy.
Cost-effectiveness and accessibility
The implementation of feedback devices in training programmes raises questions of
cost-effectiveness and accessibility. While these devices can enhance training outcomes, their
cost may limit widespread adoption, particularly in resource-constrained settings (Yeung et al.,
2009). Educators and curriculum developers should consider the balance between the potential
benefits and the practical constraints of implementing these technologies.
Instructor role and guidance
The introduction of feedback devices does not diminish the importance of skilled instructors.
Research indicates that the most effective training outcomes are achieved when feedback
devices are used in conjunction with expert guidance (Cortegiani et al., 2017). Instructors play a
crucial role in interpreting the feedback, providing context, and guiding learners in applying the
information to improve their performance.
Romero et al. (2006) developed an adaptive web-based training simulator for cardiac life
support that personalized content and difficulty based on the learner's performance. The system
used artificial intelligence techniques to adapt the training experience to each user.
Cross-reality elements
There is limited evidence in favour of using cross-reality elements for learning. A longitudinal
prospective study was conducted in two high schools, comparing two mobile phone apps: a
cross-reality partner game and an information-based application. The partner game resulted in
a statistically significant increase in learners’ ability to predict their actions and in their
confidence to act, compared to the information-based application. The study also showed that
using animations as a feedback device can help learners achieve the intended learning
outcomes (Semeraro et al., 2017). Self-efficacy toward “taking responsibility to intervene” led to
bystander actions in a VR simulated emergency (Buckler et al., 2019).
Points system
There is some evidence in favour of using a points system where learners earn points by
completing activities on a mobile phone app. A randomized controlled trial showed that such a
method resulted in a statistically significant increase in knowledge scores, compared to a version
of the mobile app without the point system (Burgess et al., 2018). Mini games that provide
feedback messages and points were found beneficial for learning first aid for choking (Boada et
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 97

al., 2020). Scoring by device is more nuanced than by instructor observation, which, when
combined, may be more helpful to learners (M. E. Aksoy, 2020).
Face-to-face facilitation and a mobile app
There is limited evidence in favour of combining in-person facilitation with a mobile phone app.
A randomized controlled trial conducted in a school showed that when an app supported
in-person facilitation, there was a statistically significant increase in CPR assessment and
achievement scores compared to either method alone. However, the app method alone did not
result in a statistically significant difference in compression rate and showed a statistically
significant decrease in correct compression depth when compared to in-person facilitation
(Yeung et al., 2017).
Using games for knowledge development
Akaltan et al. (2023) identified increased knowledge, but no difference in skills between groups
using serious games and instructor-led Basic Life Support (BLS) courses. A grey literature report
on the development and trial of a water safety app for children (aged 13–14) demonstrated that
the app is an effective means for knowledge development when compared with facilitator-led
learning (Life Saving Victoria, 2016). The app guides students through mini-games that educate
them on how to keep themselves and others safe during an Emergency Action Plan. Similarly,
knowledge increased around microbes and infection prevention through the use of small
games, along with reporting of an enjoyable experience (Eley et al., 2019).
Computer game versus DVD-based learning
Finally, a military-based randomized controlled trial showed no significant difference between a
computer game (a 3D video, known as a “serious game”) and a DVD-based learning
intervention. However, the authors concluded the game could be more engaging (Planchon et
al., 2018).
98 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Peer learning
Key action
Use peer-to-peer learning activities to foster additional perspectives, promote cooperation and
encourage learners to engage more with the content to add value to the educational outcomes.
Introduction
Peer learning is a term that has many interpretations and includes a wide variety of learning
approaches. It has no universally agreed-upon definition, and there is no standard method for its
delivery. Related terms for this approach include co-learning, cooperative learning
(peer-to-peer), peer tutoring, peer-led instruction, peer evaluation, peer coaching and reciprocal
learning.
A “peer” is defined as someone from a “like-group” who shares some (or all) of the following
characteristics: gender, age, cultural background, religion or socioeconomic circumstances. Peer
learning is often used with young people or other specific audiences who may not recognize
themselves (or their values and experiences) in a traditional facilitator. The familiarity of a peer
can create confidence, as well as new opportunities to share knowledge or change behaviour
because the relationship is often based on trust and openness. It is especially beneficial to reach
audiences who may be underserved by traditional forms of education.
Good practice points
● Peer-led or cooperative learning strategies can be effective tools for teaching first aid
knowledge, skills and attitudes.
● The choice of strategy may depend on factors such as the novelty of the curriculum, the
level of relevant knowledge peers can offer, available time and financial resources.
● While peers can add significant value to the learning process, they should not replace
qualified professional facilitators.
● The peer-to-peer dynamic should enhance the learning experience, providing mutual
benefits for both learners and peer facilitators.
● To ensure effective peer leadership, it is important to establish standardized training and
evaluation methods.
Chain of survival behaviours
Peers can support the development of survival behaviours by sharing relevant context,
knowledge and skills, such as:
● Preparation and prevention: Strategies they have used to prepare for emergencies in
familiar settings like work, school or home.
● Early recognition: Signs or indicators that helped them recognize emergencies early in
those contexts.
● Providing first aid: Examples of improvised materials they have used to give care, and how
they coached peers during practice sessions.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 99

● Accessing help: Information about community or workplace resources that can assist
with emergencies.
● Self-recovery: Actions to care for peers or support others during or after an emergency
event.
Education considerations
Peer learning has the potential to shape learners’ attitudes and values, including their
willingness to act in emergency situations.
Context considerations
● Peer learning often requires minimal financial and human resources. However, training
peer facilitators to be effective can take time and effort.
● Peer learning approaches are beneficial in communities with limited access to the
internet or digital devices.
● Educators should consider cultural, gender and religious sensitivities when designing and
facilitating peer-based learning experiences.
Learner considerations
● Peer learning is mostly used with youth (aged 10–25), especially in schools where large
groups of learners share common experiences and peers can take on leadership roles.
● Peer learning can be effective for various audiences who benefit from shared experience
or connection, including:
○ young children (pre-school and elementary)
○ older adults
○ groups with shared life circumstances (e.g. new parents, caregivers, homeless
individuals, those with substance use challenges, etc.)
○ people living in similar environments (e.g. rural, remote or conflict-affected areas)
Programme designers should provide ongoing support to peer facilitators, not just during initial
orientation. This continued support is essential to keep them motivated and ensure consistent,
high-quality learning experiences.
Facilitation tips
● Involve peer facilitators early: Engage peer facilitators in programme planning to ensure
the approach and content resonate with the intended audience.
● Choose facilitators carefully: Ideal peer facilitators may be previous trainees or individuals
recommended by trusted community leaders.
● Encourage sharing: Encourage learners to share their newly gained knowledge, skills and
attitudes with others in their peer group (e.g. family, friends, co-workers).
● Prioritize hands-on learning: Include time for tactile, hands-on learning where learners
observe and support one another to improve their technique.
Facilitation tools
● Paired learners can follow instructions from a facilitator, then work together to apply what
they have learned.
100 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● After learning a skill (e.g. steps to perform first aid), peers can coach each other through
the process.
● Peers can brainstorm emergency preparedness strategies or develop a case study based
on shared experiences.
● Feedback from a peer tends to feel more personal and ongoing compared to automated
devices, which provide standardized responses.
● Some aspects of peer learning can also be delivered online, including through
educational games or interactive platforms.
Scientific foundation
This review examined how peer learning affects both learners and those receiving care,
compared to traditional education methods or no training at all. Rather than assessing whether
peer learning is better, the goal was to explore how it performs in comparison to other
strategies.
Peer-led learning
Four studies are included which consider peer-led learning in comparison to teacher or
instructor-led training.
Beck et al. (2015) studied peer-led Basic Life Support (BLS) training among middle school
students in Germany. The aim was to compare peer-led training with instruction by a
professional facilitator. Peer facilitators prepared in advance, and two independent evaluators
used a standardized checklist to assess performance. Results showed a 40.3% success rate in the
peer-led group and a 41% success rate in the professional-led group. However, concerns were
raised about the selection of peer facilitators who were often high-performing students. The
study did not explore how the relationship between peer educators and learners might have
influenced the results.
Wik et al. (1995) examined a “trickle-down” peer training model among Norwegian factory
workers, in which factory employees received cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) training (Tier
1), then trained co-workers (Tier 2), who in turn trained family members (Tier 3). In total, 1,303
people were trained: 41 in Tier 1, 311 in Tier 2, and 873 in Tier 3.
Three weeks after each tier group had received training, all tiers demonstrated their skills using
feedback mannequins. Results showed Tier 3 learners (taught by non-professionals) performed
comparably to a US control group trained directly by professionals. However, cross-country
differences between the intervention (Norway) and control (US) groups may have influenced the
outcomes.
Damvall et al. (2022) evaluated the effectiveness of student instructors teaching CPR to their
peers in a high school setting. Seventy-six student instructors (SIs) from three high schools
received a two-day training course from professional instructors, which included theoretical
learning via an e-learning programme. The student instructors then led 90-minute practical
CPR sessions for approximately 2,650 students during physical education classes.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 101

CPR performance was assessed using four-minute practical tests on Little Anne QCPR
mannequins. Data from 982 students were analysed. The compression rates were within
guideline recommendations for SIs (mean 110.6; SD 5.4) and students (mean 118.6; SD 8.6). The
corresponding numbers for mean compression depth were 7.2 cm (SD 0.7) and 7 cm (SD 1.0).
Students demonstrated greater variation in mouth-to-mouth (MTM) skills, with only 41%
performing at least 15 successful ventilations during the test. Except for the total number of
MTM ventilations (mean difference -5.6), CPR performance was deemed statistically equivalent
between professional instructors, SIs and students.
Allan et al. (2023) compared CPR metrics immediately post-training in children who were
trained by peers versus those who received traditional instructor-led training. In all five studies,
students were taught by peers, which included hands-on practice. No differences were observed
when comparing the proportion of children performing correct depth, rate and hand position in
compressions immediately post-training between those who received peer-training and those
who received traditional instructor-led training. No retention or self-confidence data were
available for comparison from the RCT.
Co-operative learning
Three studies examine peer-to-peer learning, where peers support one another following
instruction from experts.
Iserbyt et al. (2009) investigated reciprocal learning with peer evaluation. Students were divided
into pairs where one performed CPR tasks while the other was the helper, observing and
providing feedback. Roles switched every five minutes. The intervention group included peer
evaluation, while the control group did not. Both groups took a retention test two weeks later.
Results showed that more students in the peer-evaluation group completed CPR steps in the
correct order. No other significant differences were found. This suggests peer evaluation can
strengthen learning by encouraging both observation and feedback. Learners co-constructed
knowledge through discussion, analysis and active involvement.
Charlier et al. (2016) studied peer-assisted learning with master’s students in education.
Participants were first introduced to CPR by experts or instructional tools. They then paired up to
teach each other sections of the content. The control group spent the same time practising
bandaging. Outcomes were based on practical assessments and showed that peer-assisted
learning was as effective as traditional instructor-led approaches in meeting the European
Resuscitation Council’s 2010 CPR quality standards.
Knowlin et al. (2023) conducted a pilot study using virtual reality (VR) CPR training in high
schools with a near-peer mentoring approach, with students divided into pairs. In 1.5-hour
sessions, one student practised CPR on a mannequin guided by a VR avatar while the other took
notes and provided coaching. Roles were then reversed. Each student’s CPR skills were assessed
in VR using a cardiac arrest avatar superimposed on a real QCPR mannequin, with coaching
from their peers. Students then switched roles and debriefed on their experience. The trial
showed students performed well and valued the collaborative learning approach.
102 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Knowledge gaps and future research
Further research is needed to understand how peer learning can support first aid education
across diverse settings. Programme designers are encouraged to explore projects that could
strengthen the evidence base for future guideline updates.
Key questions for future study include:
● How does peer learning perform in training that leads to certification, compared to
non-certified sessions?
● What is the impact of peer learning on populations beyond youth, such as older adults,
families, people affected by opioid poisoning or other health risks?
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 103

Video facilitated learning
Key action
Consistently and effectively support both facilitator-led and self-paced learning by providing
learners with videos that demonstrate and apply key skills.
Introduction
Video-based learning involves individuals watching a video to build competencies on a topic.
This method provides an effective way to communicate a consistent message to audiences.
There are two main approaches to video learning:
1. Observation: Learners watch a video to gain knowledge.
2. Active participation: Learners practise alongside a video to develop their competencies.
Guidelines
● Videos can be a highly effective tool for learning and practising cardiopulmonary
resuscitation (CPR) if learners have access to a mannequin.*
● Videos can motivate learners to act in an emergency, such as starting CPR or providing
other first aid care.*
● While facilitator-led training is generally more effective, video learning can complement
and reinforce facilitator-led instruction.*
Good practice points
● Video as a supplement: Video-only learning is particularly useful when facilitator-led
training is not possible or when it serves as a supplement to facilitator-led training to
maintain consistency. Videos should provide clear and concise explanations of first aid
techniques. Subtitles and captions can be helpful to certain populations.
● Contextual learning: Videos can include realistic simulations of emergency situations to
help learners prepare for real-world scenarios. The videos can help learners visualize
emergency scenarios, providing context for the first aid skills being taught, and can be
adjusted to the learner's context. Virtual reality or 3D videos can provide a more
immersive and engaging learning experience.
● Focus on one skill: Videos that focus on teaching a single skill set (e.g. CPR, bandaging)
tend to be more effective.
● Self-paced training: Video-based training is a good option for non-medical staff to learn
and reinforce basic skills and knowledge at their own pace.
● Refresher training: Video learning is especially beneficial for refresher courses, particularly
for learners in remote areas or communities with limited access to in-person training.
Chain of survival behaviours
Video learning is most effective for teaching the first three steps of the CoSB
104 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

1. Prevention and preparation
2. Early recognition of an emergency
3. Immediate first aid
It is particularly helpful in reinforcing the recognition aspect, such as identifying when CPR is
needed.
Educational considerations
Context considerations
● Changing learning opportunities: As technology evolves, so do learning opportunities.
Video-based learning can be an effective alternative for individuals who are unable to
attend in-person training sessions.
● The role of social media and online learning: Educators are increasingly using social
media and online platforms for teaching. As a result, the role of video has expanded.
Consider which platforms your audience use most often to consume content, and tailor
your first aid training videos to meet their preferences.
Learner considerations
● The format, content and teaching methods (pedagogy) of the video must be appropriate
for the intended audience.
● For children with intellectual or developmental disabilities, video can be a powerful tool
for teaching single-step interventions.
● Images and animations help illustrate first aid concepts and techniques in a clear and
engaging way, helping learners to absorb information more quickly and effectively.
Additionally, emotionally charged content can stimulate imagination and creative
thinking, leading to a deeper understanding of the material.
Facilitation tips
● Complement, do not replace: Use video learning to support in-person facilitation, but not
as a replacement. The exception would be in cases of refresher training or when
face-to-face learning is not feasible.
