● Active practice: Ensure that learners have the opportunity to practise what they observe
by pausing the video. Hands-on application is key to reinforcing the skills demonstrated.
● Bridging self-led and facilitator-led learning: Prepare facilitators to connect self-paced
video learning with supervised training. The facilitator's role should be to build on the
skills learners have acquired independently, rather than merely repeating the content
shown in the video.
● Evaluate videos regularly to ensure they are effective and meet the needs of learners.
Facilitation tools
● Use short videos for impact: Even brief video clips (up to four minutes) can significantly
improve learners’ skills and increase their awareness.
● Incorporate testimonials: Testimonials from medical or first aid professionals, as well as
individuals with lived experiences, can be used to provide real-world tips and insights.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 105

Scientific foundation
Fourteen studies were included with findings across several relevant themes for this topic. Heard
et al. (2019) compared the effectiveness of video, short online tutorial and facilitator-led learning
in teaching CPR. The video group was the least effective, while the online group outperformed
the facilitator-led group on hand positioning but scored lower on compression depth. Godfred
et al. (2013) highlighted the impact of video length, showing that longer CPR videos resulted in
higher competence levels. In another study, Bylow et al. (2019) noted that while facilitator-led
training yielded greater immediate knowledge gains, long-term performance at six months was
similar across both self-led (video) and facilitator-led groups.
Further investigations underscore the nuanced role of video in learning contexts. Lawson et al.
(2012) found that video significantly improved knowledge retention in a children’s water safety
programme, while Sureshkumar and Zonneveld (2024) demonstrated the effectiveness of
telehealth for teaching first aid to children with intellectual disabilities. This was particularly
successful when teaching one skill at a time using limited steps. Beskind et al. (2016) compared
a short video learning with a 20-minute hands-on facilitator-led session. They found that the
quality of CPR and responsiveness of the high school students participating in the study
improved significantly with the video cohort, but that compression depth was only improved by
the hands-on cohort. De Vries et al. (2010) affirmed that while DVD training improved retention,
it could not match the effectiveness of traditional facilitator-led instruction. Chung et al. (2010)
found no significant differences between video-only and face-to-face facilitation, suggesting
that context-based variability exists. Additionally, Ali et al. (2019), Eisenberg et al. (1995), Capone et
al. (2000), Salehi Karizmeh et al. (2023), Nishiyama et al. (2009), and Blanchard et al. (2022)
contribute to a growing body of evidence highlighting the complexity of effective training
methodologies in CPR and first aid education.
Overall, while video can be a valuable educational tool, it is most effective when paired with
active facilitation, particularly for complex skills that require nuanced understanding and
practice. Furthermore, the studies emphasize the roles of instructional quality, video length, the
opportunity to practise and learner characteristics in achieving desired outcomes. Gaps exist
around video learning for first aid topics other than CPR.
106 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Refresh and retrain
Key action
Provide opportunities for learners to maintain their knowledge, skills, decision-making abilities
and intention to help after completing an initial first-aid education session.
Introduction
“Refresh” refers to strengthening or reminding learners of first aid competencies. “Retrain” refers
to individuals demonstrating first aid competencies following an initial educational experience,
typically for certification purposes. Currently, there is no agreed-upon definition of what refresh
and retrain efforts should consist of—they can include any type of learner engagement following
the initial training (e.g. self-directed learning, peer-led learning or instructor-led learning). They
can take many different forms, such as face-to-face, online, video learning, print (e.g. posters,
pamphlets, flashcards), or a combination of these. A refresh and retrain strategy supports
learners in maintaining their first aid competencies over time.
This topic requires further evidence on the best ways to refresh and retrain, as well as the
frequency of retraining different learner groups.
Guidelines
● Knowledge and skill abilities decline dramatically in the months following an initial
first-aid learning experience. Programme designers should use this evidence to
encourage individuals to regularly engage in first aid learning.**
● Refresh and retrain strategies should be considered to maintain first-aid learning
outcomes.**
● All methods reviewed in the scientific foundation section (video learning, feedback
devices, face-to-face learning, etc.) may be considered as appropriate refresh and retrain
methods.*
● Refresh and retrain sessions may be delivered between three to six months after the
initial educational experience. Waiting longer will lead to less effective retention of
learning.*
● While there is no recommended session length, refresh and retrain interventions of up to
45 minutes could be valuable.*
Good practice points
● Programme designers should work with different learner groups and policymakers to
develop the most suitable refresh and retrain strategy and delivery method.
● First aid volunteers should also take refresh and retrain sessions to ensure their
knowledge and skills are up to date.
● Repetition of learning can help to develop and retain knowledge. Although the optimum
frequency is unknown, annual refreshers over three years could be sufficient for
long-term skill retention for children.
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 107

Chain of survival behaviours
Refresh and retrain methods can be applied to all domains of the CoSB. For example, evidence
shows that technical aspects, such as correct compression depth and rate, can improve with
practice but will quickly worsen without it. Refresh and retrain methods also build confidence
and a willingness to act, helping learners achieve the desired outcomes within each domain
successfully.
Educational considerations
Context considerations
● Local requirements: Be aware of your organization's and country's rules for refresher and
retraining sessions. This includes understanding the qualifications needed and how
certifications are issued. For example, some places only require documentation that a
person has provided first aid and a formal retraining session is not required.
● Technology barriers: Using technology for refresher sessions can help reach a larger
audience, but remember that in some areas, not everyone has access to high-speed
internet or devices such as mobile phones or computers. However, offline technology can
still be useful for people in these areas to learn first aid.
● Travel distance: If offering face-to-face training, consider the distance learners may need
to travel and plan accordingly to make attendance easier for them.
● Focus on common injuries: Review local health data to understand which injuries occur
most frequently in the area. This can help you decide which first aid topics should be
prioritized in retraining sessions.
Learner considerations
● Different learning needs: Each group of learners may need different approaches for
refresher and retraining sessions. For example, you may need a different teaching style for
younger learners compared to adults, older adults or families. Consider each group’s
unique learning needs.
● Inclusive learning: Offering different ways to refresh and retrain first aid skills can make
the process more inclusive and accessible to a wider range of learners.
● Refresher sessions for trainers: Trainers also need to refresh their skills and ideas for
training. Meet regularly to foster a culture of sharing new ideas, collectively updating on
available resources and materials, addressing any concerns, and seeking support on
specific topics.
Facilitation tips
● Timing of refresher sessions: Plan refresher sessions about three to six months after the
initial training to help learners retain what they have learned. However, if this time frame
is not possible, research shows that even if more time has passed (like a year), knowledge
can still be refreshed.
● Easy access to information: Provide learners with simple, appealing ways to access first aid
information and remind them of the importance of keeping their skills up to date.
108 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Reminder methods: Ask learners how they prefer to be contacted so you can send
reminders to update their first aid knowledge. You could communicate through email,
text message, or social media.
● Peer support: Consider using peer-to-peer communication through social media to share
first aid updates and encourage others to refresh their skills.
● Different approaches for refreshers: People who need retraining may need to review basic
concepts they have forgotten, while those just refreshing their skills might benefit more
from advanced activities or real-life scenarios that build on their past experience.
● Motivate learners: Use motivational events, such as competitions, to encourage learners to
regularly maintain and refresh their skills.
Facilitation tools
● Variety of learning tools: There is no single "best" method for refresher and retraining
sessions, but tools like in-person courses, video tutorials and animations can help improve
knowledge and skill retention.
● Digital methods: Digital tools like text messages or emails with first aid tips can be a
cost-effective way to help learners refresh their skills regularly. Similarly, there are free first
aid apps that learners can use to refresh and update their knowledge.
● Face-to-face sessions: In-person learning can also be an effective way to help learners
retain their knowledge and skills.
● Games for learning: Simple games or card-based activities are a fun and engaging way to
reinforce first aid knowledge.
Benefits and limitations
● Costs: Developing refresher and retraining programmes can be expensive, depending on
the delivery method. However, shorter, more frequent sessions can be more affordable
and still help learners keep their skills over time.
● Learner motivation: Some learners may feel they do not need a refresher, especially if too
much time has passed since their original training. The longer the gap between training
and refresher sessions, the harder it may be to convince people to update their skills. On
the other hand, some learners may appreciate regular refreshers, as it means they will not
need to take longer courses as often.
Scientific foundation
We included studies focused on enhancing CPR education through simplified algorithms,
mobile technology and retraining methods. The studies we found did not cover a broader range
of first aid skills, nor did they consider the complexity of the different skills and knowledge points
needed for an intervention. These factors may influence how different engagement methods
are best suited to refresh or retrain efforts. Furthermore, the studies do not consider that there
could be a difference between optimal performance and other degrees of performance (or use
of practice) and there is a lack of consensus throughout the studies on what level of
performance and retention is acceptable. It is unclear what the target for success looks like.
Nguyen et al. (2021) found that simplifying CPR algorithms—specifically by removing
non-essential steps—improves adherence to guidelines and skill retention among lay persons.
Their comparison of the 2010 and 2015 European Resuscitation Council guidelines indicated that
FIRST AID EDUCATION | 109

the simplified 2015 version resulted in better performance in critical CPR skills three months
post-training. Farquharson et al. (2022) explored the use of text message reminders to combat
low confidence, showing that spaced, brief messages increased participants' intentions to
perform CPR in emergencies. Studies by Choa et al. (2009), Hsieh (2018), Nishiyama et al (2015)
and Ahn et al. (2011) demonstrated that videos (showing short lectures) sent to learners’ mobile
phones effectively refreshed knowledge and skills, while research on feedback mechanisms
(Nishiyama et al., 2017; Sato et al., 2019; Wik et al., 2002) highlighted the benefits of structured
refresher training for skill retention.
Avau et al. (2019) investigated face-to-face refresher courses and found significant
improvements in skill retention over time. However, the optimal frequency for refresher training
remains unclear. Hsieh et al. (2018) suggested that more frequent sessions (every three months)
lead to better retention, whereas Sato et al. (2019) found minimal long-term impact on CPR
skills, although compression depth could be improved with frequent self-led practice. These
studies emphasize the importance of simplified training protocols and innovative engagement
strategies in improving CPR education and retention among laypersons.
Bottiger et al. (2015, 2016) researched the “Kids Save Lives” (KSL) programme, founded by the
European Resuscitation Council (ERC) in 2015. The programme is a global initiative designed to
teach schoolchildren basic life-saving skills, particularly in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).
The KSL campaign recommended including CPR in the school curriculum worldwide,
encouraging two hours of annual CPR training for all schoolchildren aged 12 years and above.
Lukas et al. (2016) investigated whether pupils who were trained annually for three years
retained their resuscitation skills after six years, compared to those trained annually for six years.
They found that both groups were equivalent in terms of knowledge and skills after six years.
110 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

FIRST AID

The first aid topics included in the 2025 Guidelines reflect both the evolving science and the
shared humanitarian vision that unites first aid providers around the world. Selected through a
collaborative process that integrates the best available evidence, global and local expertise and
priorities identified by National Societies, these topics embody the principles of First Aid Vision
2030: universality, humanity and resilience.
The topics are organized by theme:
● General approach
● Unresponsiveness
● Breathing problems
● Trauma
● Medical conditions
● Environmental
● Mental distress
Each section translates research findings into clear, actionable guidance for those designing,
teaching and delivering first aid education. The recommendations are grounded in science yet
adaptable to diverse cultural and operational contexts—supporting the goal of ensuring that
everyone, everywhere can be a first aider in their home, workplace or community.
First aid is more than a set of skills—it is a global act of humanity rooted in impartiality, neutrality
and independence. It strengthens trust and resilience, connects communities and reflects the
universality of need that defines the Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. These Guidelines
aim to support that vision by equipping every first aider—with or without medical
background—with the knowledge, confidence and ethical grounding to act decisively and with
care.
First aid is driven by evidence, sustained by humanity and shared by all.
112 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

GENERAL APPROACH
FIRST AID | 113

General approach
Key action
Provide help while maintaining personal safety and the safety and security of the ill or injured
person and bystanders.
Introduction
The initial actions of individuals and groups can help or hurt an effective response. The
recognition of safety and health hazards to self and others is taught in the Early recognition
domain of the chain of survival behaviours. A systematic approach helps with remembering and
doing the initial behaviours in a general approach. Ongoing scene hazards (e.g. fire, violence or
hot environment) must be addressed by removing the hazard, removing people from the
hazard or calling for help.
Once the scene is safe enough to initiate care, a systematic approach to identifying
life-threatening injuries is important to prioritizing care. After life-threatening issues are treated,
first aid providers can identify all other first aid ailments to either care for or to inform advanced
care.
First aid scenarios require critical thinking, and a general approach helps individuals navigate
this process. Although we often teach first aid in distinct modules (e.g. breathing, bleeding, CPR,
poisoning), the hazards and their consequences complicate first aid actions. The general
approach allows for life-threatening illnesses or injuries to be identified and managed through a
primary survey of the person. The types of injuries or illnesses may overlap, and judgement may
be required on what to treat first and how to do it. For the purpose of these Guidelines,
life-threatening issues are:
● Unresponsiveness: When a person does not react to external stimuli (i.e. anything around
them).
● Breathing problems: Difficulty or inability to breathe normally.
● Trauma: Physical harm to the body that will not maintain life (e.g. arterial bleeding).
● Medical: An acute biological response to an illness or assault to the body (e.g. Poisoning or
sepsis).
● Environmental: Harm to the body from the weather conditions or surroundings.
● Mental distress: Psychological response that prevents a person from carrying out safe
behaviours.
First aid requires a responder to identify these life-threatening injuries or illnesses and to
prioritize care. Effective first aid education must go beyond individual skills and emphasize the
importance of situational awareness, personal safety and decision-making in dynamic and often
stressful conditions. In practice, many situations will be complex. In real life, they may be more
complex. Providers may need to care for multiple things at once, for example, applying pressure
to a serious cut to stop the bleeding while speaking with the injured person to help calm them
down during a panic attack. In most situations, providers will need to care for the physical illness
or injury while also applying psychological first aid skills.
Psychological first aid
Psychological first aid (PFA) is integrated with physical first aid competencies during
training. Acknowledging emotions and reactions to situations in the domain of Early
114 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

recognition and addressing them simultaneously reduces stress for the individual and the
first aid provider.
Research and guidance for psychological first aid is led within the Movement by the Red
Cross and Red Crescent Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Hub (MHPSS Hub). The
General approach section integrates considerations for psychological first aid into the
chain of survival behaviours to increase awareness of psychological first aid and support
first aid educators and providers in caring for the whole person.
See the psychological first aid section for background and specific skills for supporting
people in distress.
Good practice points
Assess the scene
● First aid providers should be taught about the ambiguity of emergencies and that
deciding to act is the most important first step.
● First aid providers should assess the scene for dangers to themselves and others before
providing direct help.
● In assessing a scene that contains areas of danger, first aid providers should also observe
which areas are safe (or have fewer dangers) to provide options for themselves and the ill
or injured person.
● If scene safety cannot be guaranteed, then the provider needs to adjust their approach
(e.g. calling for help or providing support from a distance), prioritizing their well-being
first.
● Where possible, the first aid provider should take steps to protect private information. This
can also help enhance the person’s feeling of personal safety.
Assess the person
● First aid providers should be taught a standard approach to assessment to help them
appropriately prioritize care for time-sensitive conditions and to feel more confident in
their competencies.
● If possible, first aid providers should approach the ill or injured person so the person can
see them. This will reduce the risk of scaring the injured or ill person or causing them to
move unnecessarily. It may also help keep them at ease.
● First aid providers should assess the ill or injured person by checking for a response,
normal breathing and normal blood circulation. These conditions should be treated as a
priority if abnormal.
● If the ill or injured person can talk or cry, it should be assumed their airway is open and
their breathing is adequate.
● An ill or injured person should be left in the position of most comfort (usually the position
in which they are found) unless there is a need to move them to a different location or
position for safety or well-being purposes.
● First aid providers may assess an ill or injured person further by asking them questions to
determine their mental status or medical history or more closely examining part of their
body (with consent). Providers should get as much information as possible about what
happened by using a calm, non-intrusive manner that respects the person’s needs.
● The first aid provider should communicate with the ill or injured person, explain what
they are doing to help, and act with respect and empathy.
FIRST AID | 115

Provide care or access help
● The first aid provider should access emergency medical services (EMS) as soon as they
think help is needed. If using a phone, care to the ill or injured person should be provided
simultaneously by activating the phone’s speaker function.
● A lack of first aid equipment should not be a barrier to providing care; first aid providers
should use whatever resources are available to them.
● Immediate basic and practical needs are also priorities. Check whether the person needs
water, shelter, clothing or a blanket, and try to provide them as quickly as possible. It is
difficult for someone to solve problems or reach out to others when feeling cold or thirsty.
● Providers should ask if the person needs social support such as restoring family links or
medical or legal assistance.
● In cases of children, connecting them with caregivers is a basic need that should be
prioritized.
● Filming an emergency incident is inappropriate, particularly if it blocks the path of
professional responders. First aid education should raise awareness of this fact.
Multiple casualties
● The first aid provider should protect themselves at all times from danger, aim to preserve
life and reduce injury and suffering.
● If there is more than one ill or injured person, it may be appropriate to call EMS first to
inform them of a potentially critical situation, and then call again once more detailed
information has been gathered.
● In a multiple-casualty incident, the first aid provider should assess the ill or injured, then
treat those with the most life-threatening conditions relating to breathing and circulation
first.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Know the common risks in your environment and the steps you can take to help
minimize the risk of an incident occurring or worsening.
● Ensure you have access to a first aid kit or the specific equipment (a disaster kit,
hi-visibility signs or clothing, etc.) according to your assessed needs and risks.
● Be aware that diffusion of responsibility (e.g. thinking someone else will help) and
ambiguity (e.g. wondering if the person really needs help) are common barriers to
overcome in helping an ill or injured person. Being prepared and empowered to assist
people not only provides care but also empowers individuals to act and can lessen
feelings of helplessness.
● Learn about the emergency services available in your context and how to access them.
This may involve knowing their phone number, location or the types of questions they
may ask.
● Understand the potential resistance an ill or injured person may have to certain
procedures, such as the associated costs of services. Advocate for them accessing care
when it is needed.
● Learn proactive means to de-escalate volatile situations.
Early recognition
Upon entering a first aid situation, be aware of the thoughts and feelings you may have that
may affect the help you provide. Be alert to the fact that people read situations differently, and
just because no one is responding does not mean that everything is fine (the bystander effect).
116 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Assess the scene
A scene assessment involves identifying any potential safety risks (to you, the ill or injured person
or bystanders), such as fire or exposed electrical wires. The location itself may be unsafe. If the
person is in or near a body of water or ice, in a conflict area or in a closed or confined space with
minimal oxygen or poisonous gas, extra care must be taken. Additionally, if there is an active
shooter, contamination by a chemical, radiological or biological agent, or any other risk you
cannot minimize, stay away and inform the relevant authorities (fire or police).
● If possible, seek the support of another person who can help you.
● Assess the scene for potential danger to yourself or the ill or injured person and try to
identify the potential cause of the illness or injury.
● If necessary, observe areas of safety (or ones with fewer dangers) to provide a safe place
for you and the ill or injured person.
● Take steps to make the scene safe. This could include accessing additional help or
equipment, or changing your approach based on the danger. Do not approach the scene
if it is not safe.
● Identify the number of ill or injured people.
● Remain aware of your surroundings and maintain your personal safety. This will be
particularly important in conflict, disaster or multiple casualty contexts.
Assess the person
Approach carefully, calmly, and in an appropriate manner. Both the behaviour and attitude will
influence how people in distress react to an offer of help. If they meet someone who is calm and
focused, this will help them to feel calm and safe. The first aid provider should introduce
themselves by name, and if relevant, by the name of the National Society or other organization
in a caring and non-threatening or interruptive manner. Tell them what you are doing before
you do it.
Identify any life-threatening emergencies requiring immediate action (e.g. the person is not
breathing) and what kind of first aid can support their condition.
● Is the person responsive?
● Is the person’s airway open and clear?
● Is the person breathing normally?
● Has the person got an injury that is bleeding severely?
This is often called the “ABC check” or “primary survey”. If there is any concern about
cross-infection, do this assessment by observing the person’s chest or abdomen for signs of
breathing.
Look directly at the person; do not use a phone while talking to someone; focus on what the
person says. Try to be at the same physical level as the other person. For example, if the person is
sitting on the ground, kneel to be at the same eye level. Practise active listening using both
verbal and non-verbal communication skills.
If the person does not have any life-threatening conditions, you may be able to identify and
provide support for any non-life-threatening conditions.
● Is the person showing signs of an altered mental status (confusion, aggression, etc.)?
● Does the person have any signs of injury? You may need to examine them more closely.
● Does the person have any history of an allergy or medical condition?
● How does the person say they feel?
● What other signs can you observe in them (temperature, movement, etc.)?
FIRST AID | 117

Immediate basic and practical needs are also priorities.
● Does the person need warmth, shelter or clothing?
● Does the person need social support (restoring family links, medical or legal assistance,
etc.)?
● If the person is a child, do they need to be connected with their caregiver?
Knowing how to help people access basic needs is an important PFA skill. While the first aid
provider does not need to have contact details for all the resources in the community, it is vital to
know how and where to get this information when needed.
A key part of first aid is recognizing emotional reactions, accepting these without judgment, and
responding in a caring, empathetic and understanding way. It is important to remain calm and
be mindful of your own verbal and non-verbal communication to support the person in distress.
If the person in distress has strong emotional reactions that endanger themselves or others or
begin to interfere with daily functioning over a longer period of time, they should be referred for
professional mental health support.
First aid steps
● Provide care for the conditions you find in the order of severity, focusing first on the
person’s breathing and circulation, which are critical to survival.
● Access help if necessary.
● Continue to assess and observe the person. Be alert to any changes in their condition.
Take note of any vital signs if necessary.
● Provide care until professional medical care can take over or the person and their support
network become self-sufficient. It may be helpful to share information about onward care
options if available.
The first aid provider will need to ask questions that can help the distressed person identify what
support they need. If someone does not want help, do not impose it. The questions should focus
on the help needed and the priorities, not on the details of what happened. Remember, it is
important to empower people to make their own decisions rather than making decisions for
them, particularly at a time when they may feel very disempowered or lacking control over a
difficult situation.
Handling sensitive disclosures
● Protect the person’s dignity and emotional safety by gently pausing the disclosure if it
becomes too personal.
● Offer to continue the conversation privately after the session.
● Set the expectation that a person does not have to share information in a group setting if
it may lead to regret or vulnerability.
Techniques to calm a person in distress
To help stabilize and comfort individuals experiencing acute distress, apply the following
techniques:
● Use a calm, soft tone of voice to convey safety and reassurance.
● Invite the person to share what is upsetting them and provide individual PFA if needed.
● If culturally appropriate, maintain eye contact (without staring) to show attentiveness and
empathy. Offer comfort through appropriate, culturally sensitive physical gestures (e.g. a
hand on the shoulder).
● Reassure the person of their safety and your intent to help, but only if the situation allows
and the statement is truthful.
118 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Engage in grounding or distracting activities to restore a sense of normalcy, such as:
○ taking a short walk
○ moving away from distressing sights
○ offering a drink or performing a simple task together.
● Allow space for grief and use the moment to introduce positive coping strategies through
group sharing.
Special situations
Managing agitated behaviours in emergencies
During emergencies, first aid providers may encounter individuals experiencing acute
psychological distress or trauma. This distress may stem from pre-existing conditions, be
triggered by the crisis itself, or emerge as a reaction to overwhelming circumstances. In some
cases, individuals may become emotionally agitated, or even pose a risk to themselves or others.
Understanding distress reactions
Distress reactions can appear in various ways, including:
● disorientation or confusion
● heightened anxiety or panic
● verbal aggression or shouting
● physical agitation or restlessness
● withdrawal or unresponsiveness
Principle of safety
In rare but serious cases, these reactions may escalate into violent or self-harming behaviour.
Such behaviours may be driven by fear, trauma or a perceived loss of control. The safety of the
first aid provider, the affected individual and bystanders is most important. Before engaging
with an agitated person:
● Assess the environment for safety risks.
● Do not intervene if the situation poses a threat to your own safety.
● Maintain a safe distance and avoid physical contact unless absolutely necessary.
● Seek assistance from security personnel, law enforcement or trained professionals if the
individual becomes violent or unmanageable.
If safe to do so, apply calming techniques such as:
● speaking slowly in a calm, non-threatening tone
● avoiding sudden movements or loud noises
● offering reassurance without confrontation
● redirecting attention to neutral or grounding activities.
Referral and follow-up
If the individual’s behaviour indicates a need for specialized care—such as persistent confusion,
inability to function, threats of harm, or substance misuse—refer them to appropriate mental
health and psychosocial services. Ensure follow-up where possible to confirm they have received
the necessary support.
Medication administration
Generally, a first aid provider is not authorized to prescribe or give medication. In some
countries, these legal restrictions are evolving. Depending on the scope of practice, the target
audience, medical oversight, as well as the extent and depth of the educational programme
offered, medication administration may be appropriate and used in particular first aid situations.
FIRST AID | 119

● In situations where a person has prescribed medication (such as an inhaler or
auto-injector), first aid providers may assist the person, if local regulations allow.
● First aid providers should learn about the common conditions they may encounter and
the types of medication that may be used by an ill person.
● First aid providers should become familiar with the various methods of administration
(e.g. how to use the auto-injector or inhaler).
● First aid providers should try to contact EMS before administering medication if possible.
● First aid providers should inform EMS of any medication the person has taken or been
administered, especially if the person is unable to communicate this information
themself.
● First aid providers should advise or assist the ill person to take their medication in
accordance with the prescribed dose and administration route.
First aid providers may typically administer or assist with medication for the following
conditions:
● heart attack (aspirin, nitroglycerin)
● asthma attack (inhaler)
● allergic reaction (antihistamine)
● anaphylactic reaction (epinephrine)
● decompression sickness and hypoxia (oxygen)
● opioid overdose (naloxone)
● fever (antipyretic)
● earache, headache, sore throat, period pain, Back pain (painkiller)
● diabetic emergency (insulin, glucose).
Multiple casualty incident
In a multi-casualty event, large numbers of people are affected simultaneously. The scene is
often chaotic, with survivors, families of victims and bystanders all experiencing varying levels of
psychological distress. When responding to a multiple casualty incident, call EMS first to inform
them about a potentially critical situation, and then call them again when you have detailed
information to give them.
Quickly assess all the ill or injured before providing care, and provide care first to those with the
most life-threatening conditions relating to breathing and circulation.
Who is affected and how they may react
1. Survivors
○ May be in shock, confusion, disorientation, or panic.
○ Some may be numb or silent, while others may cry, scream or express guilt for
surviving.
○ Physical injuries may compound emotional distress.
2. Family members
○ Often arrive at the scene or hospitals in a state of desperation and fear.
○ May express anger, helplessness or overwhelming grief, especially if loved ones are
missing or deceased.
○ Some may demand information or become agitated due to lack of updates.
3. Bystanders and witnesses
○ Even if not directly harmed, they may experience trauma due to the exposure.
○ Reactions include anxiety, nausea, trembling or feeling frozen.
○ Children and elderly bystanders may be especially vulnerable.
First aid providers are often the first point of human contact in these situations. While attending
120 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

to physical injuries, they can also provide basic emotional support using PFA principles.
Access help
● Identify what type of help is needed to address immediate needs and problems and
support the person’s ability to cope with the situation. It may be more than one type of
help. For example, the person may need rescue service, PFA and medical care.
● Access the next level of care as soon as the need is expected. This will get help to you
quicker, and if you are phoning, the call-taker may be able to guide and support your first
aid actions.
● Identify additional resources that could help you in accessing help or providing care, such
as bystanders or transport options.
● If life-saving first aid is being provided, access care in the most efficient way possible. This
may mean making a call using the speakerphone function or arranging transport that
allows care to be continued during the journey.
● Help the person prioritize their needs and find helpful solutions to the issues. The first aid
provider may have to accompany the person in taking the first steps and continue to
empower them to act. PFA aims to enable people to make their own informed decisions.
● In some cases, it might be better to access EMS quickly with incomplete information. For
example, accessing EMS early for a full bus crash, even without knowing the exact
number of injured, will start the process of getting help. In other contexts, more detailed
information may be needed. For example, a message sent from a remote setting should
have detailed information so EMS can send adequate resources to help.
● If calling EMS, remain calm and answer their questions as clearly and accurately as you
can. This will help them prioritize your call.
Recovery
In the case of minor illnesses or injuries, the role of the first aid provider is one of support and
empowerment to allow people to cope with the difficulties and be responsible for themselves.
Accurate information about the event, the rescue, as well as information about loved ones or
others who are impacted, their safety, rights and how to access the services and things needed
are essential for anyone in a crisis. Provide accurate and useful information relevant to the
individual. This could range from practical information on what happened, where to access
different resources, or what is possible and important to do at the moment, to psycho-education
that helps normalize the reaction of distress and prepare the person for possible reactions that
may follow in the coming days and weeks. Information helps people feel empowered to make
informed decisions about their situation.
It is a priority to connect a distressed person with their family or friends, either in person or by
phone, to update them on what is happening and check if the loved ones are fine. The first aid
provider should also know the services to help link people to their family in emergencies. Ask
about who should be informed and called upon in the social network.
Examples of helpful practical support are:
● contacting someone who can stay with the ill, injured, or distressed person
● arranging for the pick-up of children, or the provision of food, shelter, clothing and
reassurance to children
● helping the person with transport to a safe place
● helping the person to access a hospital or other support services.
Encouraging the use of helpful coping strategies and avoiding unhelpful ones is also essential.
FIRST AID | 121

Examples of unhelpful coping strategies include using alcohol or substances to try to forget the
problems, denial or isolating oneself. While these strategies may feel like they are helping the
person cope in the moment, if continued over long periods, they are likely to impact the person
negatively and cause more challenges. Having information on natural and normal reactions to a
crisis event can also help the person use healthy coping strategies.
Referrals
Referral means linking a person in distress with the appropriate care provided by other people,
agency or facility, either by contacting the other service directly or giving contact details to the
affected person. When a first aid provider assesses that the person needs help beyond that
which they can provide, they make a referral. It is good practice to follow up with the person
afterwards to ensure that they have received the support needed.
Refer specialized psychological help if someone:
● has not been able to sleep for the last week and appears confused and disoriented
● is so distressed that they are unable to function normally and care for themselves or their
children
● loses control over their behaviour and behaves unpredictably or destructively
● threatens to harm themselves or others
● uses substances or alcohol excessively.
Self-care for the first aid provider
In order to provide help responsibly, first aid providers must take care of their own health and
well-being. Providers can be affected by supporting others in crises or by their own distress.
Providing first aid can be difficult both physically and emotionally. It is not easy interacting with
people who are in distress and this could lead to feelings of guilt, sadness and frustration if first
aid providers feel they have not done enough. These may also result in cumulative stress and
burnout. It is also important to acknowledge that different situations may affect or resonate with
first aid providers who are unique individuals, shaped by their own experiences. There is no
shame in finding things difficult and it is essential to practise proper self-care in the first aid
providers’ own lives.
First aid providers can apply the PFA principles of Look, Listen and Link, and learn to recognize
their own risk factors to well-being, their own limitations and what kinds of situations may be
overwhelming. They can also discover what protective factors such as support systems and
healthy stress coping strategies they have in place.. Building awareness of their own strengths
and weaknesses as a first aid provider and knowing when to call for help from others can act as
preventive measures.
It is imperative that all first aid providers and volunteers are given supervision and continuous
support while providing care and assistance for others during times of crisis to ensure they are
not overwhelmed by the circumstances and can maintain their own psychological well-being. It
is recommended that all National Society staff and volunteers providing emotional support
within the principles of PFA have access to a support system where they can receive the
assistance they need. The support system team should take time to come together before,
during and after providing emotional support.
Education considerations
First aid education can increase the likelihood that a person will get the help they need in a first
aid emergency. Education can increase a learner’s intention to help by supporting them to
develop confidence and willingness to act. Weaving helping behaviour messages and activities
122 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

throughout the content of an educational intervention can increase a learner’s intention to help
by supporting them to develop confidence and willingness to act.
Context considerations
● Assessing the scene for danger will vary considerably across contexts. Discuss with
learners the types of incidents they are likely to encounter (road traffic collisions, accidents
at work or in the home, conflict, etc.).
● The role of the first aid provider will also be affected by who else is likely to be on the
scene at the time, and this might facilitate or inhibit intervention (Levine et al., 2020). For
example, their response might be different if they are alone, if they are with a colleague
who can help them or if they can enlist the help of bystanders.
● The role of the first aid provider might also be affected by the behaviour of other people,
and the relationship of the rescuer to those other people. In some contexts (usually
ambiguous, non-violent ones), the bystander effect can occur where no one responds to
an ill or injured person because no one else is doing anything to help (Van de Velde et al.,
2009; Levine & Manning, 2013). In other contexts (usually unambiguous, violent ones)
there may be a “reverse bystander effect” with bystanders coming together to respond
(Fischer et al., 2011, referenced by Levine et al., 2020).
● The type of help available will vary based on local factors. Programme designers should
ensure their programmes reflect local circumstances. Be aware that help might not be
available for all locations or contexts. Learners should be aware of what is available. For
example, an urban area may have easy access using an emergency phone number.
However, a remote area in the same country may not have access using the same phone
number. This may be even more complicated if people travel between the two contexts.
● Be aware of myths and realities that may exist in local areas that may influence efficient
access to care. For example, local taxis might provide faster transport than ambulances, or
this could be a perception which hinders efficient access to care (Jayaraman et al., 2009;
Mould-Millman et al., 2015).
● If there are any barriers to accessing care in a particular context, programme designers
should ensure that those barriers are addressed in learning activities (Watts et al., 2011).
For example, an emergency phone number may offer language translation or text
services, allowing those who do not communicate in the dominant language to still seek
help.
● Raise awareness that some first aid situations may be solved by helping the person
connect with their usual care providers.
● Prepare learners for the type of incidents they are most likely to encounter. For example,
in areas where there are frequent traffic collisions, learning should reference these and
identify the level and availability of emergency services and others who can help.
● In preparing people for events such as terror attacks, include individual safety measures
and local regulations in the first aid education.
Learner considerations
● Encourage learners with sensory or physical disabilities to consider how they can
complete the assessments of the scene and the person in a way that works with their
strengths. Also, work with them to devise strategies that they can use to safely move a
person without injuring themselves in the process.
● Work with learners to develop strategies to overcome fear when faced with an
emergency. Use educational approaches which encourage confidence and willingness to
act according to learner needs and preferences.
● Seek to understand learners’ intentions to act and their motivations or barriers to doing
so to make your educational approach effective (Miller & Pellegrino, 2017; Heard et al.,
2019).
FIRST AID | 123

Facilitation tips
Participants will naturally feel more comfortable identifying and caring for physical injuries as
they are perceived as more concrete. In each situation, reinforce that there is likely more to do
than simply addressing the physical concerns. First aid providers should also be prepared to
support the psychological needs of ill or injured persons.
Assess the scene
● First aid providers find it most difficult to decide to act. Learners should be taught about
ambiguity and how deciding to act is the most important first step (Vaillancourt et al.,
2008).
● Guide learners to reflect on their own experiences with helping in the past. The theme of
ambiguity and the bystander effect may come up in stories shared by learners (or the
facilitator). Bringing up these themes as lived experiences will help learners who have not
experienced these feelings to relate to these concepts.
● Since there are a large number of potential safety hazards, focus on the principle of scene
assessment: identify and manage dangers. Allow learners to identify potential dangers
within their contexts, as well as solutions that they would consider.
● Focus on how learners can eliminate, control or work around potential dangers. Avoid
focusing on what learners should not do when faced with danger as this may discourage
any action instead of encouraging safe action.
● Supplement discussion of potential dangers with specific instruction for any risks where
the probability of the risk is high or where there is misinformation present. Consider the
work and guidance of other programmes (both inside and outside the Red Cross Red
Crescent) to ensure synergy.
● Depending on the situation, the first aid provider may need to decide whether to move
the ill or injured person. The first aid provider should only move the person if they are in
danger, need to travel to medical care, or if the person is in a position that makes it
difficult to assess and provide care. First aid providers will need to balance the risk that
may be caused by moving the ill or injured person, versus the risk of not moving them.
Assess the person
● An organized framework to guide assessing the ill or injured person, (e.g. ABC) may help
learners to be thorough and prioritize the care that may be required.
● Emphasize that the environment may evolve, both quickly and slowly. For example,
tensions may suddenly escalate, or weather patterns may begin to shift over time. As well,
some characteristics of the ill or injured person may not be immediately known, especially
if they live with a disability. As a result, first aid providers should continue to remain
attentive to their environment and the ill or injured person, changing their approach with
the situation.
● Even though a first aid provider may not be able to access an ill or injured person in an
unsafe environment, first aid providers should consider creative solutions. For example,
the first aid provider may be able to verbally coach the ill or injured person in assessing
and caring for themselves from a distance.
Access help
● All first aid education should include identifying the types of help and resources they can
draw on and how to access them, as well as helping learners to determine whether to first
access help or provide care, depending on the situation.
● Emphasize to learners in contexts with an active EMS phone system in place that they
can access help as soon as they suspect they need it. This may help give them confidence
124 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

in their actions.
● Help learners understand the community resources they can draw on for help. This could
include neighbours or bystanders, or the environment (shelter, water, etc.). Bystanders, for
example, can be asked to control a crowd, to protect the dignity of the ill or injured by
forming a barrier, to create shade over or block cold wind or rain with a tarp (sheet), go to
the nearest phone to call for help or to get equipment such as a defibrillator.
Multiple casualty incident
● Simulations are an educational tool that may be used to develop critical thinking skills,
awareness and preparedness for a multiple casualty incident among first aid providers
and healthcare professionals.
● Highlight to learners that a multiple casualty incident is an event that results in multiple
injured people, outnumbering and overwhelming emergency medical services. This
situation includes road traffic collisions, terror attacks, multiple shootings and disasters.
● If possible, include professional responders, healthcare workers, Red Cross Red Crescent
volunteers, civil organizations and other appropriate groups in the exercise. Doing so will
help people to understand each other’s function and role before an actual event.
● Run simulations on-site or using virtual reality.
● Empower learners by removing any barriers to action and have them practise using the
equipment they might have close at hand (e.g. clothing to control bleeding).
● Explain the process of triage and prepare learners to manage onlookers.
● Help learners identify who may need immediate emotional support (e.g. those in visible
shock, crying uncontrollably or searching for loved ones).
● Simulate chaotic environments to practise offering calm, clear communication and basic
comfort measures.
● Emphasize the importance of working with other responders and knowing referral
pathways for mental health or protection services.
● Discuss how survivors, families and bystanders may all show distress differently and
require different types of support.
Facilitation tools
● Use role play to practise developing confidence in assessing the scene, moving people
and asking others to help. Role play can also be useful for practising the process of calling
for help, including the types of questions they may be asked.
● A memory tool may help learners remember the important things to do when assessing
a person. ABC is a common tool used.
● Encourage learners to programme emergency access phone numbers into their phones,
particularly if they do not have an easy-to-remember phone number, or if travelling to a
new area with different phone numbers.
● Create or find spaces which are awkward and ask learners to practise helping people in
different positions. For example, set up a scenario where a person has collapsed in a small
toilet cubicle or on the stairs.
Learning connections
● See the topics on disaster and conflict for situations where there could be many injured
people.
● The movement of someone with a suspected spinal injury should be minimized.
● If a person is unresponsive, open their airway and check for breathing.
● It is often helpful to highlight to learners that the mechanism (cause) of injury may be a
risk for their safety.
● Bleeding control is a key skill in a multiple casualty context.
FIRST AID | 125

Scientific foundation
Non-systematic review
Helping behaviour and the bystander effect
There is a large body of literature on helping behaviour and the bystander effect. We have
selectively drawn evidence from literature specific to first aid interventions.
Systematic reviews on this topic are provided by Vaillaincourt et al. (2008) and Van de Velde
(2009). Vaillaincourt examines bystander CPR rates and concludes that a lack of interest and
motivation to learn CPR skills contributes to the reluctance to intervene and provide CPR in real
situations. They also identify barriers, such as situational ambiguity, as important in influencing a
bystander's decision to intervene. Van de Velde concludes that first aid programmes that train
participants to overcome inhibitors of emergency helping behaviour could lead to better help
and higher helping rates.
Training in first aid is also linked to increased confidence to help in an emergency, as
documented by Heard et al. (2020) in a scoping review. The review considers public confidence
in first aid skills, willingness to help during an emergency and barriers to or enablers of learning
and delivering first aid in an emergency. The findings identify high levels of perceived
knowledge, confidence and willingness to help, supporting the idea that the public can play a
vital role during an emergency. However, the findings also point to low uptake levels and barriers
to learning first aid and helping, indicating that the first aid education landscape needs
improvement.
Another review of the literature is provided by Levine et al. (2020), which draws on prior literature
on the bystander effect and argues that more recent evidence shows that this phenomenon
does not apply to violent or dangerous emergencies. They cite meta-analyses and recent
research on closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage, which show that bystander intervention is,
in fact, the norm. They also draw on studies of social identity (such as Levine and Manning
(2013)), which support theories that individuals are most likely to help people they know (family
members, friends and colleagues). Nonetheless, in violent and dangerous situations, the
willingness of people to intervene could be due to the emergence of a “group membership” that
forms amongst strangers who are all present at the same event.
There is a lack of evidence to support any one particular approach to developing “helping
behaviour” through first aid education. Different situations, cultures and legislatures influence
behaviour and attitudes toward helping, and, where studies exist, they are incomparable in
terms of outcome measures and variables. We identify this gap while also acknowledging a
growing body of evidence on education that explores the development of learners' confidence
and willingness to act, such as Miller and Pellegrino (2017).
Role of first aid providers
As the first people on the scene, first aid providers can play a vital role in caring for the injured
before professional help arrives. Properly preparing providers is critical to ensuring their skills
and efficacy (van Romburgh & Mars, 2019). Awareness of this role is critical in all first aid
situations and supporting learners to act as well as to call for help is identified by Oliver et al.
(2017a, 2017b).
Confidence to act without proper equipment
Providers need to understand that a lack of equipment does not mean they are unable to help.
Providing CPR or controlling bleeding with clothing (or the person’s hand) are examples of
126 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

life-saving actions without any equipment (Jacobs et al., 2016; van Romburgh & Mars, 2019).
Bystander filming
Management of the accident scene has traditionally focused on preventing further danger.
However, cultural developments create new challenges for responders (Bazeli et al., 2017). One
such challenge is that some bystanders now film the incident, not only humiliating the injured
person but also actively blocking the path of professional responders. It is important to
emphasize the danger of this action to learners.
Implications of bystander first aid
Jacobs et al., (2016) noted the need to acknowledge bystanders as those able to respond
immediately to ill or injured people. Bystanders (first aid providers) should be included in a
reorganized response structure and be empowered through the recognition of their role in
responding to emergencies. Conversely, another paper warns of the potential damage
bystanders can do, such as pulling people out of cars at road traffic incidents (Bazeli et al., 2017).
Both studies identified that education is critical to increasing survival rates following multiple
casualty events.
Turner et al. (2016) identified that haemorrhage is the leading preventable cause of death in
trauma. They also stated that equipment to control bleeding, as well as public education on how
to do so, should be made widely available. In a separate opinion paper on improvised first aid
techniques for terror attacks, Loftus et al. (2018) suggested empowering providers to get creative
and use everyday items to make first aid equipment that can save lives.
Importance of agency coordination
Bazeli et al. (2017) conducted a qualitative study in Iran using semi-structured interviews to gain
insights from different participant groups on the management of multiple casualty traffic
incidents. Reports stated there was poor coordination between agencies, duplicated efforts by
different organizations and no centralised or integrated command system.
After a multiple casualty training and mock event trial in Sierra Leone, Leow et al. (2012)
concluded that when high-resource logistics are applied to environments with limited
resources, the result is insufficient systems for transport, tracking and adequate resourcing.
Participants identified inter-agency coordination as the most valuable lesson learned.
Turner et al. (2016) conducted a systematic review that identified a need for inter-agency
leadership and coordination to be developed in advance, as poor communication was a
consistent feature when responding to multiple casualty civilian shooting incidents.
Simulation training
Simulations are an opportunity to create a life-like environment where facilitators can teach
providing first aid in a multiple casualty incident.
● Wilkerson et al. (2008) showed that immersive training through a virtual reality simulation
is a powerful educational tool. The simulation helped first responders to identify how a
chaotic, stressful environment challenges the knowledge and skills learned in the
classroom.
● Cicero et al. (2018) had positive results when they tested a video game to explore whether
it would improve triage accuracy within the game.
● Yanagawa et al. (2017) explored a simulation used in a multiple casualty life support
course. The results showed that the intervention group (who completed the simulation)
performed first aid significantly better than the control group.
FIRST AID | 127

These three studies were conducted with healthcare professionals or trained rescuers and
should be adapted before implementation with first aid providers. However, they demonstrate
the value of simulations for multiple casualty education, and we encourage further exploration
of adapting this tool for general first aid providers.
Outside of healthcare and trained rescue professionals, a different paper concluded that regular
and specific preparedness exercises are essential. These activities should take place at schools
and other public areas and involve both the local public and private authorities (Turner et al.,
2016).
Triage by first aid providers
The study by Leow et al. (2012) in Sierra Leone included Red Cross personnel who correctly
triaged people in the simulation exercise.
Badiali et al. (2017) conducted a case-control study and assessed 400 basic life support
participants' use of a rapid assessment tool when responding to multiple casualty incidents. The
participants were non-medical ambulance crews, with 200 in each group (intervention and
control). The intervention group completed a brief START training with the tool 30 minutes
before the exercise, while the control group did not. The intervention group correctly triaged
94.2% of the cases; the control group did so in 59.8% of cases. However, this study was
theory-based rather than practical, meaning that triage may look very different in a real-life
situation.
128 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Psychological first aid
Psychological first aid (PFA) is integrated with physical first aid competencies during training.
Acknowledging the emotions and reactions to situations during the domain of early recognition
and addressing them simultaneously reduces the stress on the individual and first aid provider.
Several models of PFA have been developed, each with slightly different elements but all
following similar principles to ensure safety, promote calmness, connectedness, hope and a
sense of efficacy. By integrating various aspects of the different approaches, the World Health
Organization (WHO) developed the three action principles of Look, Listen and Link. It is an
approach particularly well-suited for the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent
Societies, as it is connected to the fundamental principle of humanity and the intention to help
prevent and alleviate human suffering.
PFA can be delivered by any individual who has received appropriate training, including
volunteers, first aid responders and members of the general public. PFA does not require
professional mental health qualifications. Training equips individuals with the skills to offer
supportive, non-intrusive assistance to people experiencing distress.
PFA is applicable in situations where individuals are experiencing acute psychological distress
due to crises or emergencies. While many people demonstrate resilience and adapt well, those
showing signs of distress may benefit from PFA. It is most effective:
● during or immediately after a stressful event
● in the days, weeks, or months following an incident
● even years later, when people recall the distressing events.
Stress reactions may be immediate or delayed, and prolonged crises can result in cumulative
emotional strain.
PFA can be administered in any safe and appropriate environment that ensures comfort and
privacy for both the provider and the affected individual. Suitable locations include:
● homes, schools, clinics, hospitals
● community centres, evacuation shelters
● public spaces such as airports, train stations or even outdoors
In cases involving sensitive experiences (e.g. sexual violence and abuses), privacy and
confidentiality are essential to uphold dignity and safety.
What do PFA providers do?
PFA is a non-intrusive, practical support approach. It is not counselling or psychotherapy. A
person does not have to be a mental health professional to provide PFA; it can be provided by
anyone trained to do so.
PFA providers will:
● Provide comfort and reassurance to individuals in distress, helping them feel safe, calm
and supported.
● Assess immediate needs and concerns to determine appropriate support and referrals.
● Protect individuals from further harm, including physical, emotional and psychological
risks.
● Offer emotional support through active listening, empathy and presence.
● Address urgent basic needs, such as access to food, water, shelter and warmth.
● Facilitate access to information, essential services and social support networks to promote
FIRST AID | 129

recovery and resilience.
● Respect individuals’ privacy, readiness, personal coping style and timing.
Providers should not ask individuals to recount traumatic events in detail, analyse or interpret
their experiences, or disclose personal details or feelings against their wishes. If they want to
share, however, we listen non-judgementally and let the person proceed at their own pace. The
focus should be on meeting present needs.
Look, Listen, Link
It is important to understand that, in reality, first aid providers may move through these actions
in different ways and sequences. PFA principles are considered a cycle which can be initiated at
any point and may involve repeating or cycling through “Look”, ”Listen”,”Link” multiple times
during the process.
Look Listen Link
● Information on what has ● Approach someone ● Access information
happened and is ● Introduce oneself ● Connect with loved
happening ● Pay attention and listen actively ones and social
● Who needs help? ● Accept others’ feelings support
● Safety and security risks ● Calm the person in distress ● Tackle practical
● Physical injuries ● Ask about needs and concerns problems
● Immediate basic and ● Help the person(s) in distress ● Access services and
practical needs find solutions to their immediate other help
● Emotional reactions needs and problems
The LOOK principle in PFA involves systematically observing the environment and individuals to
identify immediate needs, risks and safety concerns.
● Situational awareness
○ Gather factual information about what has occurred and the current context.
○ Use a calm, respectful and non-intrusive approach to understand the scope of the
crisis.
○ Assess whether additional support or emergency services are required.
● Identifying who needs help
○ Prioritize individuals based on visible distress, vulnerability and urgency of need.
○ Recognize stress reactions and be attentive to marginalized or at-risk groups.
○ Approach individuals safely and respectfully.
● Safety and security
○ Evaluate the environment for potential threats or hazards.
○ Ensure the safety of both the provider and affected individuals before proceeding.
○ Maintain confidentiality to support a sense of personal security.
○ If safety cannot be guaranteed, do not initiate PFA.
● Physical health status
○ Check for signs of injury or illness.
○ Administer basic first aid if necessary and refer to medical services as appropriate.
● Immediate basic needs
○ Identify and respond to urgent needs such as water, shelter, clothing or warmth.
○ Facilitate access to social support, family reunification, medical care or legal
assistance.
○ For children, ensure connection with caregivers is prioritized.
130 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Emotional reactions
○ Observe and acknowledge emotional responses without judgement.
○ Respond with empathy, calmness and supportive communication.
○ Monitor for severe or prolonged reactions that may require referral to mental
health professionals.
The LISTEN principle focuses on engaging with affected individuals through respectful
communication, empathetic presence and understanding their concerns and needs.
● Approach with care
○ Initiate contact calmly and respectfully.
○ Introduce yourself and your affiliation (if relevant) in a non-threatening manner.
○ Your demeanour influences how safe and supported the person feels.
● Practise active listening
○ Maintain eye contact (if culturally appropriate) and avoid distractions (e.g. mobile
phones).
○ Position yourself at the same physical level to foster connection.
○ Use verbal and non-verbal cues to show attentiveness and empathy.
● Validate emotional responses
○ Accept all emotional reactions without judgement.
○ Respond with compassion, even if behaviour appears challenging.
○ Recognize that distress may manifest in different ways and may change over time.
● Support emotional regulation
○ Be a steady, empathetic presence.
○ Allow individuals to express emotions freely when it is safe.
○ If needed, redirect focus to less distressing topics to help stabilize emotions.
● Explore needs and concerns
○ Ask open, respectful questions to identify immediate needs.
○ Do not force help or probe for details about the event.
○ Empower individuals to make their own decisions and regain a sense of control.
● Facilitate problem-solving
○ Help prioritize and address immediate needs.
○ Encourage self-efficacy and informed decision-making.
○ Support the use of healthy coping strategies and discourage harmful ones (e.g.
substance use, isolation).
○ Provide information on normal stress reactions to promote resilience.
The LINK principle focuses on helping individuals access relevant information, services and
social support systems to promote recovery and self-reliance.
● Provide accurate and relevant information
○ Share clear, factual updates about the event, rescue efforts and available services.
○ Offer psychoeducational information to normalize stress reactions and prepare
individuals for possible emotional responses in the coming days or weeks.
○ Empower individuals to make informed decisions by reducing uncertainty and
confusion.
● Facilitate connection to loved ones and social networks
○ Assist individuals in contacting family or friends to provide updates and emotional
support.
○ Identify key people in their social network who should be informed or involved.
○ In emergencies, link individuals to family tracing or reunification services.
● Address practical needs
○ Support with immediate logistical challenges, such as:
FIRST AID | 131

■ arranging companionship or supervision
■ coordinating child pick-up or care
■ providing access to food, shelter, clothing or transport
■ facilitating access to medical or legal services
● Enable access to services and support systems
○ Guide individuals to appropriate health, protection or community services.
○ Accompany or remain with the person until further assistance is available, if
needed.
○ Reinforce autonomy by encouraging individuals to take active steps toward
recovery.
Referrals
Referral in PFA involves linking individuals in distress to appropriate services, professionals or
facilities when their needs exceed the scope of PFA. This may include directly contacting the
service or providing the individual with accurate contact information. Follow-up is
recommended to ensure the person has accessed the necessary support. Refer individuals to
mental health professionals if they exhibit any of the following:
● Persistent sleep disturbance (e.g. unable to sleep for a week) accompanied by confusion
or disorientation.
● Severe distress that impairs daily functioning or the ability to care for themselves or
dependents.
● Loss of behavioural control, including unpredictable or destructive actions.
● Threats of self-harm or harm to others.
● Excessive use of alcohol or drugs as a coping mechanism.
● Children or elderly in vulnerable situations that need protection and social support.
Psychological first aid for children
Children may face significant challenges during crises, including illness, loss, separation,
exploitation or abuse. While the core principles of PFA for children mirror those for adults,
children require additional support given their developmental stage and dependence on
caregivers.
PFA for children is based on the same principles as for adults. However, children may require
more support than adults because they depend on others for protection and care. Children do
not have the same experience or physical and emotional maturity as an adult and therefore may
respond differently from an older person. Children’s reactions are influenced by how they
experience the distressing event first hand. They are also influenced by how their caregivers and
others around them react to the situation, and by changes in their daily life and interactions with
others. Children react according to what they understand about the crisis event, which in turn is
related to their stage of development, abilities and previous experiences.
Here are some key differences between helping adults and children, or additional actions
needed:
● Look
○ Determine whether the child is alone or accompanied by others.
○ Assess protection needs such as risks of exploitation or abuse.
● Listen
○ Use language appropriate for the child’s age and emotional and social
development (e.g. use of simpler words for younger children).
○ Ask about needs and concerns with age-appropriate questions.
● Link
132 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

○ Assess the child’s needs with the child and caregiver, whenever possible.
○ Help the child access protection and services for basic needs.
○ Give age-appropriate information.
○ Help parents and caregivers support their children.
○ Help children to distance themselves from the stressors or danger by providing
space for play or referring them to a child-friendly space together with their
caregivers.
Self-care for the first aid provider
First aid providers can be affected by supporting others in crises or by their own distress. To help
responsibly, providers must take care of their own health and well-being to avoid cumulative
stress and burnout. First aid providers can:
● acknowledge their unique situation and experience
● practise self-care for themselves
● apply the ‘Look’, ‘Listen’, and ‘Link’ principles to themselves to recognize their own risk
factors, limitations and the kinds of situations that may be overwhelming.
● identify and put in place the protective factors that will help them, such as support
systems and healthy coping strategies
● build awareness of their strengths and weaknesses so they know when to reach out for
help.
It is also important that support systems be put in place to supervise and provide continuous
support to all first aid providers and volunteers who provide care and assistance to others during
times of crisis.
Education considerations
When teaching PFA in the context of first aid, it is important to emphasize practical,
scenario-based learning that reflects the realities of high-stress, time-urgent or even
multi-casualty environments. Educators should ensure that learners understand how to balance
physical and emotional care, recognizing signs of distress in survivors, families and bystanders
while managing their own stress responses. Training should include role-playing exercises,
cultural sensitivity and communication skills that prioritize empathy, active listening and
non-judgemental support. Additionally, learners should be equipped with knowledge of referral
pathways and how to provide comfort without overstepping professional boundaries.
Integrating PFA into first aid education helps responders offer holistic care that addresses both
visible injuries and invisible emotional wounds.
Context considerations
● When teaching or applying PFA skills, it is important to consider the context of the crisis,
cultural norms and available support systems.
● In some countries, there may be confusion about the terminology used to refer to the
principles and helping skills in PFA, such as Mental Health First Aid (MHFA), psychosocial
first aid and others. While both PFA and MHFA aim to support people in distress, they
differ in context and focus. PFA is used in acute crises to reduce immediate distress and
promote stabilization, while MHFA is used in non-emergency settings to recognize and
respond to signs of mental health conditions. However, both share core principles such as
empathy, active listening, non-judgement and linking to support. It is important to note
that definitions and practices may vary across countries, depending on national
guidelines and training models.
● First aid responders may also experience post-traumatic stress injury (PTSI). It is a
non-clinical term that encompasses a range of mental health injuries, including some
FIRST AID | 133

operational stress injuries (OSI), anxiety and depression. It characterizes symptoms as
injuries caused to first aid providers as a direct result of their work or exposure to
traumatic situations.
● PFA in first aid is often applied in high-pressure, unpredictable environments such as
disasters, accidents or violent incidents. Training should reflect these realities through
scenario-based learning, helping learners practise staying calm, prioritizing safety and
offering support amidst chaos.
● First aid providers are not mental health professionals. Training should emphasize that
PFA is about basic emotional support, not diagnosing or treating mental health
conditions. Learners should understand when to provide comfort and when to refer
someone for specialized care.
● In low-resource or emergency settings, access to mental health services may be limited.
PFA training should include adaptable strategies for offering support when referral
pathways are unclear or unavailable, and for making the most of community-based
resources.
● Materials and instruction should be adapted to the learners' language, literacy level and
communication styles. Avoid clinical or technical terms and use simple, clear language
that resonates with local experiences.
Learner considerations
● Learners should be encouraged to reflect on how cultural beliefs and norms influence
emotional expression, help-seeking behaviour and perceptions of distress. Training
should help them adapt PFA approaches to be respectful and appropriate in diverse
cultural settings.
