● If mannequins are unavailable, old car tyres can be used to practise CPR compressions.
Dig the tyre about two-thirds of the way into the ground to simulate a chest, which will
recoil when pushed on.
● For learners who train regularly and are knowledgeable in CPR, extend skills training to
include performing CPR in different settings and situations (e.g. in noisy or distracting
environments, with anxious relatives present, in crowds or small spaces with restricted
access). Such training exercises encourage team-based approaches and lateral thinking.
● Use film clips or demonstrations to improve learners’ recognition of abnormal breathing
including agonal breathing and someone who is not breathing.
Learning connections
● In contexts where a defibrillator is likely to be available, pair this topic with unresponsive
and abnormal breathing when a defibrillator is available.
● Ensure learners also understand how to maintain an open airway if someone is
unresponsive and breathing normally.
● The most common condition to result in a person becoming unresponsive with abnormal
breathing is a heart attack.
● Conditions that may influence whether rescue breaths could be beneficial include opioid
overdose, drowning, choking or remote situations.
FIRST AID | 165

● Consider different learning methods, such as the use of feedback devices for education,
peer learning, or video learning with a mannequin.
● Consider other topics such as acute grief and traumatic event if appropriate to the
learners.
Scientific foundation
Prompt recognition of cardiac arrest
In 2010, the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) conducted an evidence
summary on the recognition of cardiac arrest (Berg et al, 2010; Koster et al., 2010). In this context,
recognition of cardiac arrest includes checking the pulse and recognizing agonal breathing.
To date, there are no studies that assess the accuracy of checking the pulse to detect cardiac
arrest. Additionally, first aid providers often struggle to master the pulse check and recall the
proper technique for performing it.
There is often a high frequency of agonal gasps after cardiac arrest, but several studies showed
that first aid providers and EMS dispatchers often do not recognize them. There are many terms
used to describe abnormal breathing, which can be confusing for first aid providers and
dispatchers alike. Sometimes these terms are limited due to cultural influences and translation
limitations, even in the same country. Teaching people to identify agonal breathing using a
video clip improved the accuracy of recognizing cardiac arrest.
Evidence shows that with EMS dispatchers, failure to recognize cardiac arrest may be associated
with a failure to follow cardiac arrest protocols while on the call. In a seizure complaint question
sequence used by dispatchers, the detection of cardiac arrest cases improved after introducing
the question, “Is he breathing regularly?” Special courses aimed at teaching dispatchers to
identify agonal breathing also increased their ability to recognize cardiac arrest.
Chest compression-only CPR versus standard CPR
Numerous studies have been conducted to compare the effectiveness of chest
compression-only CPR and standard CPR performed by lay rescuers, both with and without
dispatcher instruction and performed by emergency medical technicians (EMT).
Compression-only CPR versus standard CPR
For this topic, we utilized two systematic reviews: one conducted by ILCOR (Olaveengen et al.,
2017a; Olasveengen et al., 2017b) and another by Cochrane (Zhan et al., 2017), along with
the Adult Basic Life Support: 2020 International Consensus on CPR from ILCOR (Olasveengen et
al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b).
For the critical outcome of survival with favourable neurological function, a meta-analysis of two
cohort studies found no significant difference between individuals receiving compression-only
CPR and those receiving CPR with a compression-to-rescue-breath ratio of 15:2. Similarly, a
separate meta-analysis of three studies evaluating CPR with a compression-to-rescue-breath
rate of 30:2 also demonstrated no significant difference in this outcome.
For the critical outcome of survival only, a meta-analysis of six studies showed no significant
differences in survival rates between compression-only CPR and standard CPR with a 15:2 ratio.
However, one study reported poorer survival rates for compression-only CPR compared to
166 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

standard CPR using a 30:2 ratio. In contrast, a meta-analysis of three observational studies
revealed no significant difference between compression-only CPR and standard CPR with a 30:2
ratio.
For the important outcome of return of spontaneous circulation, a meta-analysis of three cohort
studies indicated no benefit in using a 15:2 compression-to-rescue-breath ratio over other ratios.
The Cochrane systematic review specifically compared chest-compression-only-CPR versus
standard CPR on non-asphyxial out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. The meta-analysis provided
high-quality evidence that continuous chest compression CPR without rescue breathing
improved survival to hospital discharge compared to interrupted chest compressions with
pauses for rescue breathing (ratio 15:2).
Since 2020, evidence updates conducted by the ILCOR Basic Life Support (BLS) group have
identified only mannequin and training studies, which have not altered the conclusions of earlier
reviews.
Compression-only CPR versus standard CPR (adolescent and adults): Dispatcher-assisted
For this topic, we referenced the 2017 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary
Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment Recommendations
Summary (Olaveengen et al., 2017a; Olasveengen et al., 2017b).
Low-quality evidence from a randomized controlled trial indicated no significant improvement
in favourable neurological outcomes when dispatchers provided instructions for continuous
chest compressions compared to instructions for compressions combined with rescue breaths
at a ratio of 15:2. Conversely, three randomized controlled trials comparing these two types of
dispatcher-assisted CPR found that compression-only CPR offered a modest benefit in
improving survival to hospital discharge.
In Japan, the nationwide recommendation of compression-only CPR for first aid providers was
associated with higher rates of bystander CPR when dispatcher assistance was provided.
However, outcomes for individuals receiving CPR were more favourable when bystanders
performed standard CPR, including rescue breaths, compared to compression-only CPR.
Compression-only CPR versus standard CPR (adolescent and adults): Emergency medical
services
This discussion references the 2017 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation
and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment Recommendations Summary
(Olaveengen et al., 2017a; Olasveengen et al., 2017b), three evidence updates from the ILCOR BLS
Task Force in 2021, 2022 and 2023 (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c; Berg et al., 2023a;
Berg et al., 2023b) and a systematic review conducted by the ILCOR BLS Task Force for the 2025
ILCOR guidelines (Nehme et al., 2024).
High-quality CPR prioritizes minimal interruptions to chest compressions. EMS utilize three
primary techniques to deliver continuous chest compression CPR during out-of-hospital cardiac
arrest (OHCA):
FIRST AID | 167

1. Continuous chest compressions with positive-pressure ventilation (PPV) using a
bag-mask device, typically administered at a rate of 10 breaths per minute.
2. Continuous chest compressions with PPV via a tracheal tube or supraglottic airway.
3. Continuous chest compressions with passive oxygenation, generally with an
oropharyngeal airway and simple oxygen mask (commonly referred to as minimally
interrupted cardiac resuscitation).
These approaches often delay advanced airway placement until after the return of spontaneous
circulation (ROSC) or completion of three CPR cycles.
Favourable neurological outcomes:
● A randomized controlled trial (RCT) found no significant improvement in neurological
outcomes when PPV via bag-mask device was delivered without pausing compressions,
compared to conventional CPR with a 30:2 compression-to-ventilation (CV) ratio.
● Observational data showed that continuous chest compressions with passive ventilation
for three cycles improved neurological outcomes compared to compressions with
ventilation during the transition from a 15:2 to 30:2 CV ratio.
● A cohort study reported no advantage in neurological outcomes for minimally
interrupted cardiac resuscitation (three cycles of 200 uninterrupted chest compressions
with passive ventilation and rhythm analysis/shock) compared to conventional CPR using
a mix of 15:2 and 30:2 CV ratios in patients with witnessed shockable cardiac arrest.
Survival outcomes:
● A high-quality RCT found no significant difference in survival to hospital discharge
between patients receiving continuous chest compressions and those receiving
conventional CPR with a 30:2 CV ratio.
● A very-low-quality cohort study indicated improved survival in 18 patients with witnessed
shockable cardiac arrest who received minimally interrupted cardiac resuscitation
compared to conventional CPR with mixed CV ratios (30:2 and 15:2).
● A large cohort study involving 26,810 OHCA patients showed an association between
continuous chest compressions (CCC) and improved survival to discharge compared to
standard CPR. However, adherence to the randomized strategies was inconsistent.
Further analysis revealed that patients who adhered to the intended CCC strategy had
significantly lower survival rates, whereas those following the 30:2 strategy showed higher
survival.
Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC):
● A moderate-certainty RCT found no significant difference in ROSC rates between patients
randomized to CCC and those receiving conventional CPR with a 30:2 CV ratio.
● A cohort study demonstrated that minimally interrupted cardiac resuscitation was not
associated with significant changes in ROSC compared to conventional CPR.
First aid provider fatigue in chest-compression-only CPR
A comparison of first aid provider fatigue in chest compression between first aid providers who
perform compression-only CPR versus standard CPR has been the subject of a scoping review
for the 2020 Adult Basic Life Support 2020 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary
168 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science With Treatment Recommendations
(Olaveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b)
Fifteen mannequin studies assessed rescuer fatigue and its impact on CPR quality across
different compression-to-ventilation ratios. The findings suggest that continuous compressions
maintain effective depth and frequency for the first two minutes but that short rest periods
(pauses in compressions) reduce fatigue and improve CPR quality.
The 2022 and 2023 evidence updates did not identify any new clinical or simulation studies.
Chest compression quality
Hand position during compression
The recommendations for hand placement during chest compressions, initially based on low- or
very low-certainty evidence in 2015, have undergone annual reviews since 2020. To date, no
studies have reported on critical outcomes such as favourable neurological recovery, survival or
return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC).
For the important outcome of physiological endpoints, three studies of very low certainty were
identified. One crossover study involving 17 adults undergoing prolonged resuscitation for
non-traumatic cardiac arrest reported improved peak arterial pressure during compression
systole and end-tidal carbon dioxide (ETCO2) levels when compressions were performed on the
middle of the sternum compared to the centre of the chest. However, other physiological
endpoints, including arterial pressure during compression recoil, peak right atrial pressure and
coronary perfusion, showed no significant differences in this study.
A second crossover study involving 30 adults in cardiac arrest found no significant differences in
ETCO2 levels associated with variations in hand placement. A third crossover study, conducted
on 10 children, reported higher peak systolic pressure and mean arterial blood pressure when
compressions were performed on the lower third of the sternum compared to the middle of the
sternum.
Chest compression components (rate, depth, chest wall recoil)
The scientific basis for chest compression components—rate, depth and chest wall recoil—is
grounded in the 2020 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) from
ILCOR (Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b) and subsequent ILCOR evidence
updates in 2022 and 2023 (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c; Berg et al., 2023a; Berg et
al., 2023b). Since 2015, a total of 30 studies have been included in systematic reviews and
evidence updates. Seven observational studies examined both chest compression rate and
depth, but none assessed chest wall recoil. Research specifically addressing chest compression
rate included one randomized controlled trial (RCT), one crossover trial, and six observational
studies. Studies on chest compression depth comprised one RCT and twelve observational
studies, while six observational studies focused solely on chest wall recoil.
For chest compression rate, findings showed an inconsistent association with survival and
favourable neurological outcomes. No significant differences were observed between various
chest compression rates for one-month survival, one-day survival or hospital admission while
alive. Among studies evaluating the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC), one study
FIRST AID | 169

reported that a chest compression rate of 121–140/min, compared to the reference rate of
100–120/min, was associated with an increased likelihood of ROSC. However, no studies
demonstrated a significant relationship between chest compression rate and either systolic or
diastolic blood pressure. The recommendation is 100-120/min.
Studies on chest compression depth have yielded mixed findings regarding its impact on
clinical outcomes. Increased chest compression depth has been associated with higher odds of
hospital admission, while other studies found no significant relationship between varying mean
compression depths and survival to hospital admission.
Favourable neurological outcomes appear largely unaffected by the depth of chest
compression. However, one study suggested that a compression depth of greater than five cm
in adults improved survival and neurological outcomes compared to other depths during
standard CPR. Regarding survival, several studies reported significant associations between
chest compression depth and one-day survival in adults. Specifically, one study indicated that
mean compression depths of five to six cm resulted in the highest rates of survival to emergency
department admission. Another study found that survival to hospital discharge decreased when
chest compression depth was less than 38 mm compared to depths exceeding 51 mm. However,
deeper chest compressions were associated with an increased risk of injuries. The mean
compression depth among individuals who sustained injuries was 56 mm, compared to 52 mm
in those without injuries.
Regarding chest wall recoil, no significant association has been observed between variations in
chest compression release speed and survival to hospital discharge. One study examining the
return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) found no statistically significant improvement with a
10 mm/s increase in release speed. Evidence from animal studies, however, suggests that
incomplete chest recoil may lead to reduced coronary perfusion pressure.
This scientific review underscores substantial gaps in the research evidence concerning chest
compression components. Most studies focused on individual components, while several
highlighted potential confounding interactions, emphasizing the need for caution when
assessing any single component in isolation. Importantly, no studies provided sufficient new
evidence to warrant a revision of the 2015 or 2020 resuscitation guidelines.
Check for circulation during basic life support
There is no new evidence to justify changing the 2015 and 2020 treatment recommendation
(Travers et al., 2015; Perkins et al., 2015; Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b)
outside of the ALS environment where invasive monitoring is available. There are insufficient
data on the value of a pulse check during CPR. Some relevant articles showing the effectiveness
of ultrasound in checking for circulation were identified. We therefore do not make a guideline
on the value of a pulse check.
Firm surface for CPR
This discussion references the Adult Basic Life Support (ALS) guidelines from the 2020
International Consensus on CPR (Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b) and a
2024 systematic review from ILCOR (Dewan et al., 2024). Since 2020, one small observational
study and six additional randomized controlled trials (RCTs) conducted on mannequins have
been identified.
170 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

It is important to note that no clinical studies have reported on the critical outcomes of survival
or favourable neurological outcomes, nor on the important outcome of chest compression
quality for this topic. Meta-analysis results from the mannequin simulation studies evaluated
CPR metrics such as compression rate and depth when comparing various surfaces: backboard
versus hospital mattress, floor versus hospital mattress, or floor versus firm home mattress and
other surfaces. These analyses revealed no significant effects or results.
While current guidance suggests performing chest compressions on a firm surface when
practical and without significant delays to their initiation, it is not recommended to move a
patient from a firm mattress to the floor solely to improve compression depth. Research has also
shown that effective compression depths can be achieved on softer surfaces if the CPR provider
compensates by increasing the overall compression depth to account for mattress compression.
Harm caused by CPR to a person who is not in cardiac arrest
Harm caused by CPR to a person who is not in cardiac arrest
Many first aid providers are concerned that they will harm a person who is not in cardiac arrest
or cause severe complications by giving chest compressions. This belief makes them reluctant
to start CPR. The intention of this recommendation is to strongly encourage and support lay
rescuers who are willing to initiate CPR in any setting when they believe someone is in cardiac
arrest.
For this topic, we use the Adult Basic Life Support, 2020 International Consensus on CPR from
ILCOR (Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b), and the systematic review that
supports the new treatment recommendation published in 2025 from the ILCOR First Aid Task
Force (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
The systematic review conducted in 2019 and published on the ILCOR website (Svavarsdottir et
al., 2019) included four observational studies that looked at 762 people who were not in cardiac
arrest but still received CPR by first aid providers outside the hospital. Pooled data from three of
these studies found an incidence of muscle damage of 0.3%, bone fractures (rib and clavicle) of
1.7%, pain in the area of chest compression of 8.7% and no visceral injuries. The fourth study
reported no injury. In the new systematic review (Williamson et al., 2024), pooled data from the
five included studies on 1,031 patients showed a frequency of less than 1% of harm/complication
or risk of complications.
This recommendation placed a much higher value on the potential survival benefits of CPR
initiated by a first aid provider for patients in cardiac arrest, and a lower value on the low risk of
injury in patients not in cardiac arrest.
Harm to the rescuer from CPR
This topic was initially examined in a 2009 scoping review by Semeraro et al. as part of the 2010
ILCOR International Consensus on CPR and Treatment Recommendations (Berg et al., 2010;
Koster et al., 2010). The review focused on injuries caused by CPR in individuals not experiencing
cardiac arrest and evaluated potential risks to first aid providers, including harm from chest
compressions, rescue breaths and defibrillator use. Subsequent ILCOR updates in 2021, 2022 and
2023 identified three new studies (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c; Berg et al., 2023a;
Berg et al., 2023b):
FIRST AID | 171

● One study reported a low risk of physical injury among volunteer citizen responders
dispatched to out-of-hospital cardiac arrests (OHCA).
● Another study noted slightly increased pain with two-handed CPR compared to
one-handed CPR in paediatric cases.
● A third study found a low risk of harm to rescuers wearing polyethylene gloves during
defibrillation.
In summary, the limited reports of adverse effects, despite the widespread and frequent use of
CPR and defibrillation in basic life support, suggest that these interventions are relatively safe.
The incidence and severity of defibrillator-related injuries to first aid providers remain low.
CPR before calling for help
For this topic, we used the Adult Basic Life Support, 2020 International Consensus on CPR from
ILCOR (Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b). Since its publication, three
evidence updates from ILCOR in 2021, 2022 and 2023 have not identified new evidence
necessitating changes to these guidelines (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c; Berg et al.,
2023a; Berg et al., 2023b). The optimal sequence for calling for help and starting CPR frequently
arises during CPR education and was the subject of a new question and recommendation in
2020. Increased availability of phones and hands-free options for lone first aid providers was
considered important.
For the critical outcome of survival with a favourable neurological outcome, only one
observational study is identified, and no meta-analysis is available. This cohort study, conducted
in Japan, found no overall benefit of a “CPR-first” strategy compared to a “call-first” strategy.
However, adjusted subgroup analyses suggested significant improvements in survival with
favourable neurological outcomes when a "CPR-first" strategy was used in specific contexts,
including: non-cardiac etiology out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, individuals under 65 years of age,
individuals under 20 years of age and cases combining non-cardiac etiology and age under 65.
The certainty of the evidence was rated as very low, and the findings are not generalizable to all
OHCAs. The study specifically addressed cases where bystanders witnessed the event and
initiated CPR spontaneously after a brief delay.
Starting CPR: Rescue breaths or compression
The scientific foundation for this topic is based on a systematic review conducted in 2019
(Considine, et al. 2019) as part of the ILCOR 2020 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary
Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment Recommendations
(Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b). This foundation was further strengthened
by an updated systematic review completed in 2024 (Bray et al., 2025a; Bray et al., 2025b). While
no human studies were identified in either review, the 2024 update included one new paediatric
mannequin simulation study, supplementing the four mannequin simulation studies from
previous ILCOR reviews. These prior studies consisted of three randomized controlled trials (one
in adults, two in paediatrics) and two observational studies in adults. For the important
outcomes, we identified five simulation studies, but as the observational studies and
randomized controlled trials were all at high risk of bias due to lack of blinding and a high
degree of heterogeneity, no meta-analyses could be performed, and individual studies are
difficult to interpret.
172 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Summary of key outcomes:
Time to commencement of chest compressions: All identified mannequin simulation studies
found that the C-A-B (compression, airway, breathing) sequence significantly reduced the time
to starting chest compressions compared to the A-B-C sequence.
Time to commencement of rescue breaths: Two randomized mannequin studies were reviewed.
In cardiac arrest scenarios, the mean time to initiating ventilations was significantly longer with
the C-A-B sequence. In respiratory arrest scenarios, ventilations began significantly earlier with
the C-A-B sequence.
Time to completion of the first CPR cycle (30 chest compressions and two rescue breaths): One
randomized mannequin study showed that the mean time to complete the first resuscitation
cycle (30:2) was shorter with the C-A-B sequence. The clinical significance of this finding remains
unclear.
Ventilation rate: A cross-over paediatric randomized mannequin study demonstrated that the
median number of ventilations delivered within the first minute was significantly higher with
the A-B-C sequence.
Compression rate: One paediatric randomized mannequin study and one adult observational
study found no significant difference in compression rates between the C-A-B and A-B-C
sequences.
Chest compression fraction (CCF): Two studies (one paediatric randomized and one adult
observational) analysed CCF. The paediatric randomized study showed a significantly lower
median CCF with the A-B-C sequence (due to delivering five rescue breaths before starting
chest compressions) whereas the adult observational study, however, reported no difference in
CCF between the two sequences.
Minute alveolar ventilation in the first minute of resuscitation: A paediatric randomized
mannequin study found that the A-B-C sequence resulted in higher alveolar ventilation during
the first minute of resuscitation, attributed to the delivery of five rescue breaths before
commencing chest compressions.
Compression-to-rescue-breath ratio
In 2017, ILCOR completed an evidence summary on the optimal compression-to-rescue-breath
ratio during CPR, providing a critical component of the scientific foundation for resuscitation
guidelines (Olaveengen et al., 2017a; Olasveengen et al., 2017b). Additionally, a new systematic
review conducted by the ILCOR BLS Task Force in 2024 will contribute to the 2025 ILCOR
treatment recommendations, further strengthening this scientific foundation (Nehme, 2024,
ILCOR website).
Favourable neurological survival at discharge or 30 days
Two very low-certainty cohort studies were identified:
● In a cohort study involving 3,960 out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA) cases with initially
non-shockable rhythms, the implementation of the 2005 resuscitation guidelines, which
included a compression-to-ventilation (CV) ratio of 30:2, was associated with improved
FIRST AID | 173

neurologically favourable survival at hospital discharge compared to an earlier period
using a CV ratio of 15:2.
● In another cohort study of 522 OHCA cases with initially shockable rhythms, treatment
under the 2005 guidelines with a CV ratio of 30:2 showed no significant change in
neurologically favourable survival at 30 days compared to treatment under the 2000
guidelines with a CV ratio of 15:2.
Survival to hospital discharge or 30-day survival
Six very low-certainty evidence sources were identified, summarized as follows:
CV ratio 30:2 vs. 15:2
Five retrospective before-and-after studies examined the implementation of the 2005
resuscitation guidelines, which included a CV ratio of 30:2.
● Among OHCA patients of all rhythms, two cohort studies reported improved risk-adjusted
odds of survival with a CV ratio of 30:2 compared to 15:2, while one study found no
significant difference.
● For OHCA patients with initially shockable rhythms, one study observed improved survival
to hospital discharge with a CV ratio of 30:2 compared to 15:2, but the effect was
completely attenuated after adjusting for temporal trends.
● For OHCA patients with initially non-shockable rhythms, one study reported increased
survival with a CV ratio of 30:2, while another study found no difference.
CV ratio 50:2 vs. 5:1
A before-and-after study of 200 bystander-witnessed OHCA cases with initially shockable
rhythms reported improved survival to hospital discharge after implementing a bundled
resuscitation protocol that included a CV ratio of 50:2 compared to 5:1.
Survival to 12 months
One very low-certainty cohort study involving 3,960 OHCA patients with initially non-shockable
rhythms reported improved 12-month survival with a CV ratio of 30:2 compared to 15:2.
Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
A very low-certainty cohort study of 1,243 OHCA patients reported no significant change in the
risk-adjusted odds of ROSC with a CV ratio of 30:2 compared to 15:2.
Reducing pauses between chest compressions
For this topic, we utilized an evidence summary from ILCOR published in 2015 (Perkins et al.,
2015) and a systematic review conducted for the 2022 ILCOR Consensus on Science with
Treatment Recommendations (CoSTR) (Olaveengen et al., 2022a; Olasveengen et al., 2022b). The
identified evidence was categorized into five key themes:
Interventions affecting CPR quality
● New AED strategies (e.g. different levels of energy or different waveforms): The
implementation of advanced AED strategies increased CPR fractions and reduced both
pre-shock and post-shock pauses. However, these improvements did not translate into
changes in overall survival rates.
174 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Chest compression strategy: This approach resulted in higher CPR fractions but was
associated with lower survival to hospital admission, with no notable difference in survival
to discharge.
CPR fraction over time and across systems
● From 2006 to 2016, incremental enhancements in CPR quality metrics contributed to
increased CPR fractions and a higher proportion of survivors with favourable outcomes.
Chest compression pauses and outcomes
● CPR fractions and survival: Higher CPR fractions were associated with improved survival
and return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC).
● Duration of CPR: For resuscitation lasting over 20 minutes, CPR fractions exceeding 80%
were linked to better neurological outcomes and higher survival to discharge compared
to fractions below 80%. However, for shorter CPR durations of five or ten minutes, no
significant differences in survival were observed.
● Variable survival trends: Lower CPR fractions (<40%) were sometimes linked to higher
survival to discharge compared to higher fractions (>80%). Conversely, higher survival
rates were observed with CPR fractions between 60–79% compared to fractions below
60%.
Preshock and perishock pauses
Shorter pre-shock pauses (<10 seconds) and peri-shock pauses (<20 seconds) were associated
with higher survival rates compared to longer pauses. However, no additional survival advantage
was observed when peri-shock pauses shorter than 10 seconds were compared to those longer
than 10 seconds.
CPR pauses in survivors vs. non-survivors
● Early CPR fractions: Non-survivors showed higher CPR fractions during the initial five
minutes of resuscitation.
● Patient outcomes and ROSC: Patients with downtimes exceeding 15 minutes without
ROSC exhibited higher CPR fractions, while those who achieved ROSC also demonstrated
elevated CPR fractions.
This evidence highlights the intricate relationship between CPR quality metrics and patient
outcomes, underscoring the importance of tailoring resuscitation strategies to optimize results.
Notably, high-quality CPR remains critical to improving patient outcomes. Key components of
high-quality CPR include achieving 100-120 chest compression rate and minimizing peri-shock
pauses.
Rhythm-check timing
This topic is grounded in scientific evidence, including the ILCOR treatment recommendation
by Ristagno et al. (2019) and the 2020 ILCOR International Consensus on CPR for Adult Basic Life
Support (Olasveegen et al., 2017). Evidence updates conducted in 2021, 2022 and 2023 did not
identify any new studies that would alter these conclusions (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al.,
2022c; Berg et al., 2023a; Berg et al., 2023b).
FIRST AID | 175

A correlation exists between interruption in chest compressions and adverse outcomes. One of
the most frequent disruptions occurs during rhythm checks following defibrillation. However,
any unnecessary pauses in chest compressions may negatively affect cardiac arrest outcomes.
Survival with favourable neurological outcome at discharge
For this outcome, one randomized controlled trial (RCT) and three observational studies were
reviewed. Both the RCT, which included 415 out-of-hospital cardiac arrests (OHCAs), and the
studies, which involved 763 OHCAs, assessed the impact of interrupting chest compressions to
check the rhythm immediately after shock delivery. The RCT demonstrated no benefit to this
interruption, whereas the observational studies indicated harmful effects.
Survival to hospital discharge
Evidence regarding survival to hospital discharge included two RCTs with 1,260 OHCAs and three
observational studies with 3,094 OHCAs. The RCTs showed no benefit to interrupting chest
compressions for rhythm checks after defibrillation. In contrast, the observational studies
revealed harmful effects associated with such interruptions.
Survival to hospital admission
For survival to hospital admission, two RCTs involving 1,260 OHCAs found no benefit to rhythm
checks immediately after defibrillation.
Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
Regarding ROSC, two observational studies encompassing 2,969 OHCAs reported harmful
effects when chest compressions were interrupted following shock delivery. Additionally, three
RCTs investigating the impact of chest compression fraction, involving 1,412 OHCAs, also
demonstrated harmful effects associated with interruptions for rhythm checks.
In summary, the evidence consistently highlights the detrimental impact of interrupting chest
compressions to perform rhythm checks immediately after defibrillation. These findings
underscore the importance of minimizing pauses in chest compressions to optimize outcomes
in cardiac arrest management.
Dispatch diagnosis of cardiac arrest
This topic draws on the 2020 ILCOR International Consensus on CPR for Adult Basic Life Support
(Olaveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b). and the 2019 ILCOR systematic review of
dispatch diagnosis of cardiac arrest (Drennan et al., 2019). These sources evaluate the
effectiveness of dispatch algorithms and criteria in identifying out-of-hospital cardiac arrests
(OHCAs) to ensure accurate emergency responder triage. Key findings include:
● Sensitivity: Dispatchers correctly identified cardiac arrest in 79% of cases (range: 69%–83%)
across 46 studies involving 84,534 OHCAs.
● False negatives: Dispatchers missed cardiac arrest diagnoses in 21% of cases (range:
17%–32%).
● Specificity: Dispatchers correctly identified the absence of cardiac arrest in 99% of cases
(range: 93%–100%) based on 12 studies with 789,004 OHCAs.
● False positives: Dispatchers misidentified cardiac arrest in 1% of cases (range: 1%–7%) when
it was not present.
176 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

These findings underscore the strengths and challenges of dispatch centres in identifying
life-threatening cardiac events.
Following evidence updates in 2021 and 2022, which identified one new randomized controlled
trial (RCT) and six observational studies, the ILCOR Basic Life Support Task Force conducted a
scoping review in 2023 (Malta Hansen 2023, ILCOR website). This review explored strategies to
optimize dispatcher-assisted (DA) recognition of OHCA and included 60 studies categorized into
four major areas:
1. Communication: Interaction between caller and dispatcher.
2. Technology: New tools to enhance OHCA recognition.
3. Patient characteristics: Symptoms and traits associated with OHCA.
4. Quality improvement: Implementation of protocols in emergency dispatch centres to
enhance recognition.
Most studies were retrospective and observational, focusing on the proportion of OHCAs
recognized by dispatchers and factors influencing recognition.
Key challenges and strategies
The primary challenge in dispatcher-assisted OHCA recognition is determining whether the
patient is breathing normally. Various strategies were explored:
● Bypassing the breathing assessment in the initial evaluation.
● Asking callers to place their hand on the patient’s stomach.
However, none outperformed the widely used "two-questions" strategy. No RCTs compared
different strategies directly.
Role of artificial intelligence (AI)
The only RCT examined the integration of an AI model to improve OHCA recognition. While the
AI model performed well, it did not improve telecommunicator recognition due to high
false-positive rates.
In conclusion, the scoping review found no new evidence to warrant changes in current
treatment recommendations.
Dispatcher-assisted CPR
This topic is informed by a systematic review published in 2019 (Nikolaou et al., 2019) for ILCOR
Adult Basic Life Support, the 2020 International Consensus on CPR (Olasveengen et al., 2020a;
Olasveengen et al., 2020b), and an ongoing systematic review by the ILCOR BLS Task Force for
the 2025 ILCOR treatment recommendations (Cash 2024 ILCOR website).
Four randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and six observational studies compared
dispatcher-assisted compression-only CPR (CCO-CPR) with conventional CPR (C-CPR)
performed at ratios of 15:2 and/or 30:2. The comparisons included adult populations and all age
groups, with or without bystander CPR ongoing at the time of the emergency call. The overall
quality of evidence for all outcomes was rated as low to very low.
Favourable neurological outcome
FIRST AID | 177

One RCT and three observational studies were identified.
● Two cohort studies assessing combined bystander and dispatcher-assisted CPR
suggested improved neurological outcomes with CCO-CPR compared to 15:2 CPR and
combined 15:2/30:2 CPR.
● The remaining three studies, including the RCT, found no significant difference between
CCO-CPR and either 15:2 or 30:2 CPR for survival with favourable neurological outcomes.
Survival to hospital discharge or 30-day survival
Two RCTs and five cohort studies were included.
● One large observational study of 143,500 presumed medical-origin OHCA cases across all
age groups reported significantly higher odds of 30-day survival with CCO-CPR compared
to C-CPR at ratios of 15:2 or 30:2.
● Two additional observational studies (one involving 5,406 all-age OHCAs and another
involving 24,947 adult bystander-witnessed OHCAs) found significantly lower odds of
survival to hospital discharge for CCO-CPR compared to C-CPR at 15:2 and 30:2, and lower
odds of 30-day survival for CCO-CPR compared to 30:2 CPR.
● The remaining two RCTs and two observational studies found no significant differences in
survival to hospital discharge or 30-day survival between CCO-CPR and C-CPR.
Survival to hospital admission
Four RCTs were identified.
● Three studies focused on adult patients, with two comparing CCO-CPR to 15:2 CPR and
one comparing CCO-CPR to 30:2 CPR.
● The fourth RCT included all age groups and compared CCO-CPR to 15:2 CPR.
None of the studies reported significant differences in survival to hospital admission between
the CPR strategies.
Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
One cohort study was identified. This study of 143,500 presumed medical-origin OHCA cases
across all age groups reported no significant difference in ROSC between CCO-CPR and C-CPR
at ratios of 15:2 or 30:2.
Feedback for CPR quality
This topic is informed by the ILCOR 2020 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary
Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science With Treatment
Recommendations (Olaveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et al., 2020b) and one evidence
update in 2021 (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c).
Three distinct types of real-time CPR guidance devices were identified:
1. Digital audiovisual feedback: Devices providing corrective audio prompts.
2. Analogue audio and tactile feedback: Includes a “clicker” mechanism that provides tactile
and auditory feedback for chest compression depth and release.
3. Metronome guidance: Facilitates maintaining an appropriate chest compression rate.
178 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

The analogue "clicker" device is specifically designed to be placed on the patient’s chest beneath
the hands of the CPR provider. It emits a "click" sound and sensation when adequate pressure is
applied during compressions. Due to substantial clinical heterogeneity among
studies—including variations in device type, mechanisms of CPR quality measurement,
feedback modalities, patient populations, settings (e.g. in-hospital versus out-of-hospital) and
baseline CPR quality—conducting a meta-analysis was not feasible. The majority of
higher-certainty data from key studies did not show a clinically or statistically significant
association between the use of real-time feedback devices and improved patient outcomes.
Furthermore, implementing these devices requires additional resources for procurement and
training.
However, some studies did report clinically meaningful improvements associated with feedback
device usage. Notably, a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 900 patients with in-hospital
cardiac arrest (IHCA) in Iran was conducted. This study demonstrated a significant increase in
survival to hospital discharge with the use of an analogue "clicker" device, which provides
real-time feedback on compression depth and recoil. Nevertheless, this study was an outlier, and
its findings should be interpreted with caution. Further research is needed to provide specific
recommendations regarding the type of real-time feedback devices that could enhance CPR
quality and patient outcomes.
Alternative compression techniques (cough CPR, precordial thump, fist pacing)
Cough CPR
The evidence regarding the effectiveness of cough CPR is limited to observational studies with
very low certainty. While cough CPR has shown potential in specific cases to maintain
consciousness and facilitate survival during arrhythmic events, the evidence is sparse,
heterogeneous, and of very low certainty. Further rigorous research is needed to establish its
efficacy and broader applicability.
Precordial thump evidence
The evidence on the effectiveness of the precordial thump in resuscitation is derived solely from
observational studies with very low certainty of evidence. This limits the interpretability of
findings, and no meta-analysis was feasible. In summary, the precordial thump may be effective
in specific cases, particularly for ventricular tachycardia (VT) termination or achieving ROSC.
However, its impact on survival to hospital discharge is unclear, with inconsistent results and
limited applicability to ventricular fibrillation (VF) cases. Further high-quality research to
establish its efficacy and appropriate use is necessary.
Fist pacing evidence
The evidence on fist pacing is based on observational studies with very low certainty of evidence.
The limited data make it challenging to draw definitive conclusions, and no meta-analysis was
performed. Fist pacing shows potential for temporary stabilization in cases of asystole or severe
bradycardia, with initial responses observed in most patients. However, survival rates and
long-term outcomes remain uncertain due to methodological limitations, small sample sizes,
and inconsistent reporting. Further high-quality studies are necessary to determine its clinical
effectiveness.
FIRST AID | 179

Video-based dispatch system
This topic is informed by a systematic review conducted by the ILCOR BLS Task Force for the
2021 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular
Care Science with Treatment Recommendations (Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2022a) and
two evidence updates in 2022 and 2023 (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c; Berg et al.,
2023a; Berg et al., 2023b).
Observational studies of adult out-of-hospital cardiac arrests assessed the impact of video-based
dispatch compared to audio-based dispatch. The studies provided very-low-certainty evidence
suggesting potential benefits of video-based dispatch on neurological outcomes, survival at
discharge and prehospital return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC). However, these benefits
were not sustained after multivariable adjustment or propensity score matching. Furthermore,
mannequin simulation studies have demonstrated improved CPR quality, including
compression rate and time to compression, with video-based dispatch in untrained participants;
however, these studies have also shown longer times (e.g. to recognition, first compression).
However, no significant differences were observed in other CPR quality measures.
The ILCOR guidelines recommend evaluating the effectiveness of video-based dispatch systems
through clinical trials or research initiatives.
Head-up CPR
Head-up CPR has been proposed as an alternative cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR)
technique with the potential to enhance cerebral perfusion by facilitating improved venous
return from the brain. This analysis draws upon a systematic review published on the ILCOR
website for the 2021 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency
Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment Recommendations (Wyckoff et al. 2021; Wyckoff et
al., 2022a), supplemented by an updated review incorporating new evidence currently under
preparation (Norii et al., 2024). The systematic review identified three observational studies from
a single research initiative, with no randomized controlled trials (RCTs) included.
For the critical outcomes of survival to hospital discharge and survival with favourable
neurological outcomes, the evidence suggests a potential survival advantage with head-up CPR
for patients with nonshockable rhythms. However, results regarding neurological outcomes and
broader populations remain inconsistent due to very low-certainty evidence and the inherent
risk of bias.
In terms of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC), findings varied among the three studies.
The study by Pepe reported a significant increase in ROSC rates with head-up CPR. In contrast,
the studies by Moore and Bachista did not find statistically significant differences in ROSC rates
between head-up CPR and conventional CPR.
These findings underscore the need for further high-quality research, particularly RCTs, to
establish the effectiveness and broader applicability of head-up CPR and while waiting for new
scientific evidence, head-up CPR during CPR should not be routinely used.
Cardiopulmonary resuscitation in obese patients
Given the increasing global prevalence of obesity, the ILCOR conducted a scoping review to
evaluate the current evidence on the treatment and outcomes of obese patients (adults and
180 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

children) experiencing cardiac arrest (Considine, 2024, 188). In adults, the findings regarding
neurological outcomes, survival to hospital discharge, long-term survival (months to years) and
return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) were inconsistent. Among children, two studies
indicated that obesity was associated with worse neurological outcomes, lower survival rates and
reduced ROSC compared to their normal-weight counterparts. The review also revealed a lack of
studies addressing resuscitation quality indicators or adaptations to CPR techniques for obese
patients. No studies specifically examined modifications to CPR protocols based on patient
weight.
This scoping review offers a comprehensive summary of the existing literature on CPR in obese
populations and underscores significant gaps in knowledge. While the variability in outcomes
does not currently warrant deviations from standard CPR protocols, there is some evidence
suggesting that CPR duration may be longer in obese adults, potentially affecting staffing and
resource allocation.
CPR during transport
This discussion draws on the ILCOR scoping review (Smyth et al., 2020) conducted for the 2020
Consensus on Science with Treatment Recommendations (CoSTR) (Olaveengen et al., 2020a;
Olasveengen et al., 2020b) and the ILCOR evidence update published in 2022 (Wyckoff et al.,
2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c). The reviewed studies were categorized into two groups: those
assessing the effect of transport with ongoing CPR on CPR quality and those examining its
impact on patient outcomes, including survival. Studies focused on CPR quality during transport
evaluated various metrics, including:
● Correct hand positioning, pauses, complete release and ventilation rates: Transport
appears to have minimal impact on these parameters.
● Chest compression rate and depth: While appropriate rates and depths can be achieved
during transport, there is greater variability compared to CPR performed at the scene.
● Chest compression fraction and overall CPR quality: Transport negatively affects both
chest compression fraction and overall CPR performance, showing significant variation in
these measures.
These findings highlight the challenges and variability in maintaining high-quality CPR during
patient transport.
Duration of CPR cycles
Note: CPR cycle = interval between pausing chest compressions to assess the cardiac rhythm
(expressed in time or number of compressions).
This topic is informed by two studies. The first study was included in the 2015 ILCOR review
(Nordseth et al., 2014). The second study is a systematic review published by ILCOR
in Resuscitation (J. C. Reynolds et al., 2015). Notably, the literature search did not identify any new
data. Additionally, many of the studies reviewed were conducted prior to the adoption of the
current 30:2 compression-to-ventilation ratio. For comparison of CPR Intervals:
FIRST AID | 181

Three minutes vs. one minute
The first study involved 200 adults experiencing out-of-hospital cardiac arrests. For critical
outcomes, such as survival to hospital discharge with favourable neurological outcomes, survival
to hospital discharge and return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC), no significant benefit was
observed when comparing the following groups:
● Intervention group: Immediate defibrillation (up to three stacked shocks) for VF/VT,
followed by three minutes of CPR regardless of the post-shock rhythm.
● Control group: Immediate defibrillation (up to three stacked shocks) for VF or pulseless VT,
followed by one minute of CPR for refractory VF/VT, and three minutes of CPR for patients
with non-shockable rhythms following initial shocks.
One minute vs. two minutes
The second study included 202 adults experiencing out-of-hospital cardiac arrests. For critical
outcomes, such as survival to hospital discharge and ROSC, no significant benefit was found
when comparing the following treatment protocols:
● Control group: Stacked shocks (up to three for refractory VF/VT), CPR with a 15:2
compression-to-ventilation ratio, and one-minute CPR cycles between defibrillation
attempts.
● Intervention group: Single shocks, CPR with a 30:2 compression-to-ventilation ratio, and
two-minute CPR cycles between defibrillation attempts.
A 2014 study (Nordseth et al., 2014) aimed to determine the optimal timing for the first rhythm
check in patients presenting with pulseless electrical activity (PEA) or asystole. The study
assessed the time to conversion to a shockable rhythm or ROSC. The authors suggested that the
first rhythm check could be delayed until four minutes for patients presenting with PEA and six
to eight minutes for those presenting with asystole. However, they did not provide an effect
estimate to definitively support this recommendation. Their conclusions were based on
observations that conversion from PEA/asystole to a shockable rhythm or ROSC typically
required more than two minutes and that the likelihood of conversion diminished with each
subsequent two-minute cycle.
In recommending chest compressions be paused every two minutes for rhythm assessment, we
prioritized consistency with existing guidelines and recognized the lack of compelling evidence
to justify altering CPR cycle duration. Additionally, we acknowledge the significant risks and
costs associated with guideline changes. Adapting CPR protocols involves retraining educators
and providers, who must implement new strategies in the context of complex and high-stress
medical emergencies.
Tidal volumes and ventilation rates
This topic is informed by the Adult Basic Life Support 2010 International Consensus on
Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment
Recommendations (Berg et al., 2010; Koster et al., 2010). No new studies have been identified on
this subject since 2010.
182 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Three human studies demonstrated that tidal volumes of 600 mL using room air were sufficient
to maintain oxygenation and normocarbia in apnoeic patients. In contrast, tidal volumes below
500 mL required supplemental oxygen to achieve adequate oxygenation.
Additionally, three studies using mechanical models reported no clinically significant differences
in tidal volumes when comparing inspiratory times of one second and two seconds. However, a
single human study involving eight participants found that expired air resuscitation with tidal
volumes of 500–600 mL resulted in hypoxia and hypercarbia.
These findings highlight the importance of delivering adequate tidal volumes during
resuscitation to ensure effective oxygenation and ventilation.
Barrier devices
Adverse events resulting from the administration of CPR are rare, despite its widespread
application globally. Only isolated cases have been reported, such as the transmission of
tuberculosis and severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (SARS) to individuals providing CPR.
Notably, there have been no documented cases of HIV transmission during CPR. To mitigate the
risk of infection, responders exposed to infectious agents while performing CPR are advised to
take appropriate prophylactic measures. Psychological distress has also been reported among
responders in some cases.
No human studies have specifically evaluated the safety, efficacy or feasibility of using barrier
devices during CPR. However, laboratory studies have demonstrated that nonwoven fibre filters
and barrier devices equipped with one-way valves effectively prevent the transmission of oral
bacterial flora from the victim to the rescuer during mouth-to-mouth ventilation.
Adverse effects for responders have been observed when performing mouth-to-mouth
ventilation on victims of organophosphate or cyanide intoxication. Additionally, one study found
that a high volume of air containing a highly virulent agent, such as the SARS coronavirus, can
surpass the protective capabilities of gowns, double gloves, goggles, full-face shields and
non-fit-tested N95 disposable respirators.
These findings emphasize the importance of proper precautions to minimize risks to responders
during CPR, particularly in cases involving infectious agents or toxic exposures.
Mechanical CPR devices
This topic is based on a recent ILCOR systematic review (Pocock et al., 2024) that assessed the
efficacy and safety of mechanical CPR devices compared to manual CPR for out-of-hospital
cardiac arrest (OHCA). The included studies evaluated critical outcomes, including ROSC,
survival, neurologic outcomes and adverse effects. The findings are presented below,
categorized by the type of mechanical CPR devices:
Load-distributing band devices
For neurological outcomes and hospital discharge, studies demonstrated no consistent benefit
from using these devices for CPR and, in some cases, slightly worse outcomes. Regarding ROSC,
the results were inconsistent across studies. Additionally, no significant differences were found in
resuscitation-related injuries between manual and mechanical CPR.
FIRST AID | 183

Piston-based devices
Regarding neurological outcomes, survival, ROSC and injury rates, the evidence did not show
clear or consistent benefits of piston-based devices over time. Overall, the evidence does not
support the routine use of mechanical CPR devices over manual CPR due to inconsistent
benefits and low certainty. However, these devices may be appropriate in specific scenarios
depending on the context and operational considerations.
Education review
We identified 59 papers through our search strategy that offer insights to support adults in
learning CPR. Many of the supported insights covered elsewhere in these Guidelines concern
educational approaches and learning methodologies; however, the evidence set out below
highlights issues most specific to CPR.
Adaptation and prioritization
● Papalexopoulou et al. (2014) and Sopka et al. (2013) identify the need for adapting
educational tools (e.g. mannequins and defibrillators), locations (e.g. waterfront scenarios
for lifeguards) and methods to make them accessible and appropriate for learners’ needs
and abilities.
● Tweed and Wilson (1977) advocate that first aid education providers prioritize training
community members most likely to encounter cardiac arrest emergencies in CPR, such
as medics, police officers, firefighters and lifeguards. They suggest that these groups,
given their status and role in the community, might make effective educators for the
general public.
● Huang et al. (2016) identify people with family members who are at high risk of cardiac
arrest due to illness as being more motivated to learn first aid.
Barriers and challenges to learning
● Some bystanders have concerns about disease exposure and transmission through
standard CPR, which has caused a significant decrease in their willingness to provide it to
both strangers and family members. Compression-only CPR is the preferred method
(Chien et al., 2019; Jelinek et al., 2001; Lam et al., 2007; Pei-Chuan Huang et al., 2019).
● For practical CPR courses, there is limited evidence to demonstrate that female-only
learner groups are beneficial to female learners; however, there is evidence to support the
notion that males are more likely to learn in mixed groups (Sopka et al., 2013).
Enhanced scenario-based learning
● Massive multiplayer virtual worlds allow learners to play a role and experience “real-life”
scenarios and environments in which to practise their CPR skills (Creutzfeldt et al., 2013).
Dispatcher-assisted CPR
● There is limited evidence in favour of the efficacy of dispatchers providing instructions to lay
responders on how to perform CPR. Providing CPR instructions using video-assisted
technology does not significantly improve the overall quality of bystander CPR compressions
or rescue breaths (Bolle et al., 2008). However, in a study by Bolle et al. (2011), participants
noted that video-equipped mobile phones should be utilized instead of audio-only phones
184 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

during a medical emergency. Given the fast-emerging technology associated with
smartphones, we identified this as a gap in evidence that requires further exploration.
Dispatchers who communicate using video-assisted emergency calls may need more training
for this tool to be effective and widespread within CPR education (Bolle et al., 2008). Bang et al.
(2000) suggest that dispatchers with further training (e.g. technical and emotional) are more
effective.
FIRST AID | 185

Unresponsive and abnormal breathing (baby and child)
Unresponsive and abnormal breathing (adolescent and adult)
Key action
Immediately start rescue breaths and chest compressions and access emergency medical
services (EMS).
Introduction
If a baby or child is unresponsive with abnormal breathing (e.g. taking irregular or noisy breaths,
or has stopped breathing altogether) and has no signs of life, it indicates they are in cardiac
arrest. When a baby or child is in cardiac arrest, their heart stops beating and cannot pump
blood around their body. This means that vital organs, such as the brain, do not get enough
oxygen and can start to deteriorate within minutes. While cardiac arrest occurs less frequently in
babies and children compared to adults, it is still life-threatening. Typically, cardiac arrest in
babies and children is the result of a breathing problem, whereas in adults, it usually has to do
with their heart itself. The causes vary by age, setting and any underlying health problems of the
baby or child; however, some common ones include choking, drowning and disease. Since the
cause of cardiac arrest is likely to relate to their breathing, rescue breaths play a critical role in
CPR for babies or children. Survival to hospital discharge is primarily linked to the early initiation
of CPR.
● The techniques of CPR need to be adapted depending on the size of the unresponsive
person and the size of the first aid provider. If the person looks like a baby (less than
about one year old), treat them as a baby. If the person looks like they are a child, treat
them as a child.
● If the person looks like an adolescent (they may have been through puberty) or an adult,
use the guidelines provided in unresponsive and abnormal breathing (adolescent and
adult).
● It is most important to initiate the chain of survival behaviours.
Guidelines
● First aid providers should use a response check and breathing check to ascertain whether a
baby or child is unresponsive and breathing abnormally. Checking for a pulse is not needed.*
● CPR should be performed on a baby or child who is unresponsive with abnormal breathing
(e.g. taking irregular or noisy breaths or has stopped breathing altogether).**
● Lay providers should perform both rescue breaths and chest compressions for a baby or a
child in cardiac arrest. If they are unable to provide rescue breaths, they should at least
perform chest compressions.**
● For a baby, chest compressions can be performed with the two-thumb-encircling hand
method or with the two-finger technique. In newborns, the two-thumb-encircling hand
method is preferred.*
186 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● For a child, chest compressions may be performed with one or two hands. For example, if the
first aid provider is small or the child is large, the first aid provider may use two hands.*
● A compression-to-rescue-breath ratio of 30:2 (30 compressions and two rescue breaths) may
be used on a baby or child who is unresponsive with abnormal breathing.*
● For a baby, chest compression depth should be at least one-third of the chest’s depth or
approximately four cm (one and a half inches).*
● For a child, the chest compression depth should be one-third of the chest depth, or
approximately five cm (two inches).*
● The rate of chest compressions should be 100–120 per minute for babies and children (this is
the same as for an adolescent or adult).**
● Chest compression should be performed on a firm surface when possible.*
● All emergency medical dispatchers should provide CPR instructions (referred to as
dispatcher-assisted CPR) to first aid providers who call regarding an unresponsive baby or
child with abnormal breathing.**
Good practice points
● While it is preferred to start with rescue breaths and then compressions, it is also acceptable
to start with compressions and then give rescue breaths. Both components are important
when providing CPR to children.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Refer to your local or national health authority to identify the main causes of baby and
child cardiac arrest in your region. Preventable and preparable causes include choking,
drowning or disease.
● Follow recommended baby and child safety practices, including supervision near water
and safe sleep habits for infants.
Early recognition
In the first few minutes after cardiac arrest, a baby or child may have agonal breathing (meaning
they are barely breathing or gasping noisily and irregularly). This type of breathing is abnormal. If
there is any doubt about whether breathing is normal, assume it is not.
Check for a response.
● Baby: Pick the baby up or tap on the sole of their foot. An unresponsive baby will be limp
and of an abnormal colour.
● Child: Gently tap the child’s shoulders. Speak loudly and clearly, asking a question such as,
“Are you alright?”
If the baby or child does not respond or react:
● Open their airway: For a baby, tilt their head slightly to a neutral position and lift their chin.
For a child, tilt their head back and lift their chin.
FIRST AID | 187

● Check for breathing: Look, listen and feel for normal breathing for up to 10 seconds. Look
for chest or abdominal movement, listen for breathing sounds, and feel for breath on your
cheek. If you have any doubt whether breathing is normal, take action as though it is not.
First aid steps
If the baby or child’s breathing is abnormal (or they are not breathing):
1. Immediately shout for help and ask bystanders to access EMS. If you are alone, access
EMS yourself, either using a telephone with the speaker function or–if there is no phone
available nearby–leave the person to access EMS. Do this as quickly as possible.
2. Give 30 chest compressions; push down on the centre of their chest at a fast and regular
rate (100–120 compressions per minute).
○ If you are a trained responder, you may use a ratio of 15 compressions to two rescue
breaths.
3. Give two initial rescue breaths using a mouth-to-mouth-and-nose technique for a baby or
a mouth-to-mouth technique for a child. Blow steadily into the mouth or
mouth-and-nose for one second until you see the chest or abdomen rise.
4. Continue with cycles of 30 chest compressions and two rescue breaths until emergency
help arrives or the baby or child shows signs of life (such as coughing, opening their eyes,
speaking or moving purposefully) and starts to breathe normally.
● If an automated external defibrillator is available, ask a bystander to bring it as quickly as
possible. Follow the voice prompts, interrupting CPR cycles as little as possible.
● If you are unwilling or unable to give rescue breaths, give chest-compression-only CPR at
a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute.
● If more than one first aid provider is present, change providers every one to two minutes
to prevent fatigue. Ensure there is minimal interruption to CPR as the next person takes
over.
● If you are alone and do not have a way to call EMS while performing CPR (e.g. no
speakerphone), perform CPR for one minute before pausing to call for help.
● While performing rescue breaths, be alert to any signs of life such as movement or
coughing.
● If the baby or child is unresponsive and breathing normally, maintain an open airway.
Local adaptation
● In contexts that do not have an EMS or other higher level of care, if CPR has not been
effective within the first 20 minutes, then (except in cases of drowning and hypothermia)
the first aid provider could stop CPR as it is unlikely to be successful.
● Other guidance allows greater flexibility, accepting either a compression-first or
ventilation-first approach, and permits pulse checks by trained providers. Overall, some
guidance prioritizes early ventilation, while other approaches allow more latitude for
trained responders and teams.
● Recommendations for compression–ventilation ratios vary by training and context. In
some guidance, a ratio of 15 compressions to two ventilations (15:2) is recommended only
for first aid providers who have received specific paediatric basic life support training,
while others are advised to use 30:2.
188 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● 15:2 is appropriate for two rescuers and 30:2 for a single rescuer is recommended,
although some recommendations require specific training to apply the 15:2 ratio.
● It is reasonable to perform compression-only CPR if the first aid provider is unable or
unwilling to give ventilations.
● Some recommendations prioritize the two-thumb encircling technique for all
compressions in all situations, using a one-hand (heel-of-hand) technique only when the
two-thumb technique is not possible, including during dispatcher-assisted CPR. Either
technique can be used, with the one-hand technique when chest encirclement is not
possible. Overall, some approaches consistently favour the two-thumb technique, while
others allow greater flexibility based on the situation and the first aid provider’s ability.
● Guidance may include different levels of detail on anatomical positioning, but both
anterolateral (front and side of chest) and anteroposterior (front and back of torso)
placements are accepted. Electrode pads must not overlap and a small gap (about one to
two cm) between pads is recommended.
● If transporting the baby or child from a remote area to medical care, continuous CPR on a
firm surface must be provided during transit.
Access help
● When accessing EMS, very clearly explain that the baby or child is unresponsive and
breathing abnormally; this will prompt EMS to prioritize the case appropriately.
● Ask bystanders for help accessing EMS and providing CPR, as well as bringing and using
an automated external defibrillator.
● The survival of the baby or child depends upon immediate and effective CPR; when
accessing help, keep any interruptions to chest compressions minimal.
Recovery
Even if the first aid responder has performed CPR and defibrillation and the baby or child is now
responsive and breathing normally, you must continue close monitoring until EMS attends to
them, as they may stop breathing again.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Refer to and follow the guidance of regional resuscitation councils or other national
protocols and tailor education accordingly.
● Consult local regulators to consider differences in regulation and liability protection for
first aid providers.
● In some countries, specifically those with a high rate of tuberculosis, rescue breaths may
be discouraged. Teach first aid providers chest compression-only CPR (or bag-valve-mask
resuscitation if they are professional responders).
● In contexts where there is no EMS available or higher onward care, resuscitation for
babies and children can still be attempted.
FIRST AID | 189

● Be familiar with local culture, resources and local expectations of healthcare ahead of
developing an education programme and contextualize it accordingly (Anderson & Taira,
2018).
● Programme designers could define within programmes two or five initial rescue breaths
to be given, depending on what is most commonly advocated in the context.
Learner considerations
● CPR training is an important intervention that can promote a sense of control and reduce
the feelings of anxiety and burden experienced by parents with a baby at risk of cardiac
arrest (Moser et al., 1999).
● Be aware and respect that some learners may not be comfortable practising with realistic
mannequins for psychological or emotional reasons. Arrange alternative practice options.
These considerations are specific to emergency medical dispatchers.
● Emergency medical dispatchers can play a critical role through the prompt recognition of
cardiac arrest, provision of CPR instructions by phone, and dispatch of the EMS with a
defibrillator. Consider as part of the education for this role:
○ The use of scripted protocols is a helpful way to confirm when a baby or child is in
cardiac arrest.
○ Additional training around the recognition of agonal breathing (e.g. playing sounds
on a phone or showing a video).
○ How to provide CPR instructions for a baby or child (including rescue breaths and
compressions).
Facilitation tips
● Focus on prevention is critical for the survival of babies and children.
● Emphasize the importance of the first aid provider, other bystanders and EMS working
together to provide care quickly and effectively.
● Explore the mechanics of the tongue and airway for the breathing check. Allow learners to
understand that when the muscles relax, the tongue can block the airway, (so the baby or
child cannot breathe). Tilting the head back for a child or into a neutral position for a baby
pulls the tongue forward, opening their airway. Sometimes, simply opening the airway will
enable the baby or child to breathe.
● If using mannequins, ensure they are realistic in terms of size, weight and features so learners
can get a sense of how to perform the skill correctly (Gesicki & Longmore, 2019).
● Help learners understand the desired outcomes of CPR: to get oxygen into the lungs (rescue
breaths), to pump blood around the body (chest compressions), and to keep the brain and
vital organs functioning until defibrillation can take place.
● Emphasize that learners should avoid excessively providing rescue breaths as the air will
enter the baby or child’s stomach.
● Emphasize that the goal of resuscitation remains the same for all ages, only the approach is
modified. However, first aid providers who know how to perform adult CPR but do not know
baby or child CPR may use the same sequence as adults.
190 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Highlight that the decision to start CPR should be immediate and without delay. Time is
critical to a positive outcome. Emphasize that rapid and continuous compressions are also
critical for a positive outcome.
● Learners can gain knowledge and some basic skills through self-directed learning, which
might save time for practice in face-to-face sessions (Weiner et al., 2011).
Facilitation tools
● Refer to the general approach for tools which can help learners to sequence and prioritize
their actions. Mnemonics such as DR ABCD (Danger, Responsiveness, Airway, Breathing,
Circulation, Defibrillation) can be of help to some learners to establish a clear course of
action.
● Sequence the learning to include discussions about possible scenarios, role play,
demonstration, practice with a peer and feedback. Involve learners in deciding on the
scene and encourage discussions about how they might access help.
● Discuss with learners the emotions and motivations that they might feel when helping a
baby or child who is unresponsive and breathing abnormally. Encourage learners to
express their fears and doubts and to share experiences to build confidence to help.
● Use alternatives to resuscitation mannequins when they are not available, such as teddy
bears or cushions.
Learning connections
● In contexts where a defibrillator is likely to be available, pair this topic with Unresponsive
and breathing abnormally when a defibrillator is available.
● Pair this topic with choking or drowning.
● Some learners learning this topic may benefit from learning about how to provide first aid
for mental distress.
Scientific foundation
Pulse check
This discussion draws on three key resources: the 2010 International Liaison Committee on
Resuscitation (ILCOR) evidence summary (De Caen et al., 2010; Kleinman et al., 2010), the 2020
ILCOR Paediatric Life Support Evidence Update (Maconochie et al., 2020a; Maconochie et al.,
2020b; Maconochie et al., 2020c; Maconochie et al., 2021), and a new systematic review
conducted by the ILCOR Pediatric Life Support Task Force for the 2025 Consensus on
Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment
Recommendations (Katzenschlager et al., 2024).
The 2010 ILCOR evidence summary on pulse checks versus no pulse checks found significant
limitations in the accuracy of pulse assessments performed by both first aid providers and
healthcare professionals. Studies demonstrated that neither group consistently identified a
pulse within ten seconds in healthy infants and children. Two blinded studies further revealed
that healthcare providers frequently misjudged pulse status in children without pulsatile
circulation and required a prolonged time to confirm the absence of a pulse. The average time
to determine pulse absence was 30 seconds (de Caen et al., 2010; Kleinman et al., 2010).
FIRST AID | 191

The 2020 ILCOR update did not identify new studies regarding the accuracy of pulse checks
compared to assessments of signs of life. Consequently, the 2010 treatment recommendation
was upheld, which states that palpation of a pulse (or its absence) is not reliable as the sole
determinant of cardiac arrest or the need for chest compressions.
The 2024 systematic review assessed the accuracy of pulse checks based on sensitivity and
specificity, using data from three studies with very low certainty evidence. Sensitivity ranged
from 76% to 100%, while specificity was lower (64% to 79%). Regarding the duration of pauses in
chest compressions, no studies specifically examined infants and children, but one study found
that only 39% of participants detected a pulse within 10 seconds, with a median decision time of
18 seconds and 85% accuracy. Inexperienced providers took longer, raising concerns about
prolonged interruptions in chest compressions. The findings were based on a less critical setting
involving perfused children with normal skin temperature and brisk capillary refill times.
The sequence of chest compressions and rescue breaths
We used the Paediatric Life Support 2020 International Consensus on Cardiopulmonary
Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care Science with Treatment Recommendations
and an ILCOR evidence summary from 2017 for this topic (Maconochie et al., 2017; Maconochie
et al., 2020a; Maconochie et al., 2020b; Maconochie et al., 2020c; Maconochie et al., 2021). In 2015,
the consensus on science indicated that in adults and children, if we compare CPR beginning
with compressions to CPR beginning with rescue breaths, the confidence in effect estimates
were so low that it was too speculative to make a recommendation. In 2020, for the same
comparison, no new human paediatric evidence was identified. So, the recommendations for
the sequencing of CPR steps for babies and children in cardiac arrest remain unchanged from
those published in 2015. In 2024, the ILCOR BLS Task Force released a new systematic review for
the 2025 Consensus on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care
Science with Treatment Recommendations about Commencing CPR in adults and children
with compressions first (30:2) versus commencing CPR with ventilations first (2:30) . The ILCOR
BLS Task Force considers that both an A-B-C (ventilation followed by compression) and a C-A-B
(compression followed by ventilation) approach are acceptable and that both ventilation and
chest compressions are important components of CPR in children (Bray et al., n.d.)
Chest compression-only CPR versus standard CPR
We used two evidence summaries from ILCOR from 2015 and 2017 (Maconochie et al., 2015;
Olasveengen et al., 2017a; Olasveengen et al., 2017b). One systematic review of two extensive
observational cohort studies compared chest compression-only CPR to standard CPR with a
ratio of 30:2 or 30:2 and 15:2. The review found that significantly fewer people (aged one to 17)
experienced favourable neurological outcomes and survived to the one-month mark after
receiving compression-only CPR. The result was also significant with fewer people achieving the
favourable outcome of the return of spontaneous circulation in the same age group. In babies,
