● Emphasize to learners that the motivation to go to a person in distress, especially of
relatives and friends, exposes the first aid provider to a significant danger of drowning
FIRST AID | 221

that can lead to death. The rescue should be provided at the lowest risk to the first aid
provider. Flotation is essential to any water rescue.
● Practise safely throwing floatation devices (such as life jackets) or using locally available
objects such as long tree branches to pull a person to shore. Education could include the
use of improvised and purpose-made rescue equipment and should be repeated
regularly.
● Where you are using a rescue device, discuss the dangers of the device such as the
rescuer being pulled into the water, and how to avoid this happening.
● Emphasize that rescue breaths are the most important initial treatment to restore oxygen
in the body and stop the drowning injury. Rescue breathing should begin as soon as
possible (i.e. when the first aid provider reaches a safe place with a stable surface).
Concerns of water in the airways should not delay rescue breaths. The rescue breaths will
push any water in the airways down into the lungs.
Facilitation tools
● Consider using media learning as part of a multi-approach to inform people of the risks,
safe behaviours and how to access help.
● Use the Water Competencies checklist found in the Education review section of this topic
to ensure you include physical, cognitive and attitudinal elements to provide a holistic
approach to water safety education.
Learning connections
● Highlight and practise the connections to the Unresponsive abnormal breathing topics
for baby and child or adolescent and adult, raising awareness of the importance of rescue
breaths combined with chest compressions for people who are not breathing as a result
of drowning (as opposed to chest-compression-only CPR).
● A person who has been in the water may be at risk of Hypothermia.
● If relevant, pair this topic with other aquatic topics such as Decompression illness or
Aquatic animal injuries.
● Consider any relevant education practice in the topics such, as mass social media for
education and online and blended learning.
● Consider pairing water competency education with environmental or WASH (water,
sanitation and hygiene) issues.
● Care for the person’s mental distress, see traumatic event.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Recognition of drowning by a lay person
A systematic review on the recognition of drowning, including the visual cues or signs a lay
person can use to identify a drowning person, was conducted and 23 studies were identified
(Pascual-Gomez & Petrass, 2020). The review highlights that there is very limited empirical data
describing how lay people recognize a person in the early stages of the drowning process. The
review therefore also draws on limited data describing how lifeguards recognize drowning.
222 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

One study found that lifeguards are commonly taught to look for a specific set of behaviours
that are considered to show drowning or distress, including splashing, frequent submersion,
changes in body position, impairment of swimming effectiveness and a lack of progress
through the water.
Another study found that given the drowning person’s rapid progression from distress to
submersion, recognition of even earlier signs of distress is critical, for example, a swimmer
moving slowly due to weakness, physical condition or fatigue or moving into water beyond their
skill level.
A separate study found that lay people were especially good at identifying even earlier events
that can lead to drowning, for example, children performing dangerous activities, such as
repeated submerging, horseplay or going too far from shore. Lay people need to be educated on
behaviours that characterize distress in the water or drowning. Results indicate that a person
shows some or all of the above behaviours in almost all instances of drowning. However, as these
behaviours are common in water settings, it may be challenging for bystanders to recognize
them. No studies reviewed addressed the fact that some drowning people display no signs. The
review made recommendations based on the very-limited and limited evidence available as well
as based on best practice and consensus expert opinion.
No new systematic reviews or relevant articles on the recognition of drowning were identified
from 2021 onward.
Water rescue equipment
A scientific review addressing the most effective types of aquatic rescue equipment for a lay
person or bystander to use to rescue a drowning person was updated in 2019 (Beale-Tawfeeq,
2019). It reported a lack of evidence identifying which items can be recommended based on
accuracy, buoyancy, the distance that they can be thrown and ease with which they can be
caught by the drowning person. Despite the limited data, the review puts forward the current
recommendation of rescue equipment including throw ropes and lines and ring buoys for
effective use by bystanders as this seems to contribute to positive outcomes.
No new systematic reviews on water rescue equipment for a lay person were identified from
2021 onward.
Interventions for resuscitation following drowning
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) Basic Life Support Task Force, in
collaboration with drowning researchers from around the world, carried out a scoping review on
resuscitation and emergency care in drowning (Bierens et al., 2021). This was followed by a
systematic review in 2023 (Bierens et al., 2023). Both reviews looked for evidence relating to
adults and children who had sustained a cardiac arrest following drowning. The following
interventions performed by lay people were examined in these two reviews: (1) bystander CPR;
(2) in-water resuscitation versus waiting until arriving on land; (3) resuscitation on a boat versus
waiting until arriving on land; (4) compression first (CAB) versus ventilation first (ABC) strategy;
(5) compression-only CPR versus standard CPR (compressions and ventilations); (6) use of an
automated external defibrillator first versus cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) first.
FIRST AID | 223

Furthermore, evidence regarding oxygen administration before arrival at the hospital (7) was
analysed. This intervention is intended for trained responders.
Bystander CPR
The scoping review by Bierens et al. (2021) included 65 studies, and identified 19 observational
studies that discussed bystander CPR as an intervention following drowning. Statistically
significant associations were found with improved neurological outcomes (two studies) and
survival (four studies). However, other studies found either a positive trend towards survival or no
association. Three studies compared compression-only CPR with conventional CPR and all
favoured conventional CPR with bystander ventilation in terms of neurological outcomes and
survival.
Based on this evidence, the recommendation is to start conventional CPR which includes rescue
breaths.
In-water resuscitation
Immediate in-water resuscitation is defined as delivering ventilations only to a non-breathing
person who is still in the water.
Five studies were identified by the ILCOR scoping review on the topic of in-water resuscitation
(Bierens et al., 2021). The evidence indicates that in-water resuscitation (composed of rescue
breaths only) is very challenging and should only be attempted by highly-trained rescuers if it is
safe to do so. Therefore, the emphasis for first aid providers should be on non-contact rescue
rather than in-water resuscitation.
One clinical study used a protocol of giving rescue breaths for up to one minute before moving
the unresponsive person to shore; in deep water, either the availability of a flotation aid or at
least two rescuers were required. Initial survival, survival to hospital discharge and favourable
neurological outcomes were all rated higher for in-water resuscitation. The other four studies
evaluated the capacity of lifeguards and lay people to perform in-water resuscitation and rescue
of a mannequin. In-water resuscitation was technically difficult and physically demanding,
particularly in open water. It increased the rescue time, number of submersions and breathing
in of water by the mannequin. Some trained lifeguards and lay people were unable to complete
the rescue. ILCOR found that the use of ventilation adjuncts by well-trained lifeguards might
facilitate in-water resuscitation.
Neither a systematic review from 2023 (Bierens et al.), nor an ILCOR evidence update conducted
in 2024 identified any further relevant and sufficiently reliable studies on this topic.
Resuscitation on a boat
The ILCOR scoping review (Bierens et al., 2021). found seven studies that evaluated resuscitation
on a boat. One case series looked at the outcome of survival from resuscitation on a boat (24
cases) and found no survivors. However, the CPR quality was reported to be sub-optimal. In the
other case series, six resuscitations were attempted on a boat or lifeboat and there was only one
survivor after one month. Four studies evaluated the capacity of lifeguards and fishermen to
perform CPR in inflatable rescue boats or fishing boats. The studies showed that CPR was
physically demanding but that resuscitation on a boat was feasible. The quality of the
224 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

resuscitation was affected by the boat speed and the sea conditions with ventilations more
affected than chest compressions. One simulation study showed that defibrillator use on rigid
inflatable rescue boats on calm water was feasible.
In the ILCOR systematic review in 2023 (Bierens et al., 2023) no studies were identified which
directly compared starting resuscitation on a boat with delaying resuscitation until arrival on
land. On-boat CPR may be delivered if rescuers trained in this technique determine that it is
feasible and safe to attempt resuscitation. If the rescuers feel that the application of immediate
CPR is or becomes too difficult or unsafe, then the rescuers may delay resuscitation until on
land.
Ventilation- or compression-first strategy
Cardiac arrest following drowning is usually the result of severe hypoxaemia. It is therefore
important to discuss whether resuscitation in this context should be started as usual with
compressions first (CAB) or with ventilation first (ABC).
In the 2023 ILCOR systematic review (Bierens et al. 2023), no studies were identified which
directly compared a compression first strategy with a ventilation first strategy during the
resuscitation of patients with cardiac arrest following drowning. Nevertheless, ILCOR (2025)
recommends that, as a good practice point, healthcare professionals and those with a duty to
respond to drowning (e.g. lifeguards) should consider providing rescue breaths/ventilation first
(A-B-C) before chest compressions if they have been trained to do so. No direct or indirect
evidence is available to support any certain number of initial ventilations if lifeguards or
healthcare professionals adopt a ventilation-first strategy. Most importantly, resuscitation should
not be delayed, regardless of the strategy (Bray et al., n.d.)
Compression-only CPR versus standard CPR
In the 2023 ILCOR systematic review (Bierens et al., 2023), two observational studies were
identified that compared compression-only CPR with standard CPR (ventilation and
compression) during the resuscitation of patients with cardiac arrest following drowning. Both
studies compared the impact of both strategies on the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
and a favorable neurological outcome. The results indicate that standard CPR leads to better
outcomes than compression-only CPR. The evidence is of very low certainty of evidence due to
wide confidence intervals and serious risks of bias.
Automated external defibrillator use in drowning
The ILCOR scoping review (Bierens et al., 2021) searched for studies reporting the impact on
outcomes after the use of an on-site automated external defibrillator (defibrillator) in cardiac
arrest due to drowning prior to the arrival of EMS but no studies were identified.
Indirect evidence of defibrillator use was found from 15 observational studies. In four studies
there was a range of defibrillator use in cases of suspected drowning prior to the arrival of EMS.
In 12 studies it was uncommon to detect a shockable rhythm. Seven observational studies found
that a shockable rhythm was not associated with the outcome of better survival. One study
found there was an association between shockable rhythm and increased 30-day survival. One
study found the use of defibrillators on boats feasible during moderate sea conditions. One
FIRST AID | 225

study with lifeguards reported the mean time from arrival to defibrillation was 62 seconds. There
was one case of inappropriate shock delivered to a person in an asystole in one study, with no
obvious consequences. There were no adverse events identified in any of the studies listed.
A systematic review (Bierens et al., 2023) and an evidence update (Bray et al., n.d.) did not identify
any relevant new studies on this topic.
Oxygen use
The ILCOR scoping review also searched for studies about the pre-hospital use of oxygen
following a submersion incident, but no studies were identified (Bierens et al., 2021). Indirect
evidence from observational studies found associations between hypoxia, oxygen administration
and worse outcomes. In the absence of specific research about the use of oxygen in drowning,
ILCOR concluded that the existing treatment recommendation for oxygenation after the return
of spontaneous breathing applies. This means, when using 100% inspired oxygen, waiting until
arterial oxygen saturation or the partial pressure of arterial oxygen can be measured to avoid
hypoxaemia and hyperoxia. If the person who has drowned is breathing normally, supplemental
oxygen with a target saturation of 94–98% may be used.
In a systematic review (Bierens et al., 2023) and an evidence update (Bray et al., n.d.), no new
studies were identified that directly compared the oxygen prior to hospital arrival with not using
oxygen.
Non-systematic reviews
Water rescue equipment
The scientific review outlined above by Beale-Tawfeeq (2019) addressing the most effective types
of aquatic rescue equipment for a lay person or bystander to use to rescue a drowning person
also recorded some expert opinion. Experts recommended that teaching rescue skills should
become a part of water safety classes and guidelines to reduce drowning rates. They also
recommended that targeted interventions are needed to address rescue skills in multiple
aquatic environments, particularly those that are high risk. Additionally, experts recommended
that the development of public-access safety programmes may help improve drowning
outcomes.
Finally, the review also reflected on the sentiment of “rescuer altruism” in a lay person or
bystander when a person needs rescue from the water; they will initiate rescue despite the
danger to themselves.
Lay rescuer techniques in drowning incidents
A scoping review (Barcala-Furelos et al., 2021) to identify the safest techniques and equipment
for an untrained bystander to use when attempting a water rescue included 22 studies. The
review identified three types of techniques used by laypersons:
● non-contact techniques for rescue out the water: throw and reach
● non-contact techniques for in-water rescue: flotation aids
● contact techniques for rescue in the water: swim and tow with or without flippers.
226 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

The review stated that the safest technique for a lay rescuer is the first option: to attempt a
non-contact rescue using a pole, rope or flotation equipment without entering the water.
Despite the recommendation that an untrained person should not enter the water to rescue a
drowning person, the literature shows a human tendency towards “altruism” in helping a
drowning person. In most incidents, a rescuer entered the water and made contact with the
drowning person, putting themselves at risk. This behaviour was especially manifested when the
rescuer had a close bond with the drowning person. However, in many cases, witnesses took
risks to help someone they did not know, based on the principles of the Good Samaritan and the
desire to “do the right thing”.
The review suggests it may be difficult to prevent the impulse of lay rescuers to enter the water
but it is possible to encourage potential rescuers to carry some sort of flotation aid if they
attempt a rescue. Studies in the review identified that the teaching of rescue techniques in
school should be ongoing if the skills are to be retained in adult life.
Removal of fluid
There is no indication that water blocks the airway as a foreign body. Manoeuvres such as
abdominal thrusts to relieve foreign body airway obstruction are therefore not recommended
for people who have drowned (International Life Saving Federation, 2016). Such manoeuvres are
unnecessary and may cause injury or vomiting and delay CPR. Resuscitation with rescue breaths
should begin immediately rather than attempting to remove fluids from the airways.
Little fluid–if any–can be removed from the airways by drainage techniques (suctioning,
abdominal thrusts or postural drainage). This is because the estimated amount of water
breathed in is small and, after just a few minutes of submersion, it is absorbed into the
circulation (Golden et al., 1997). However, autopsy studies show large amounts of swallowed
water in the stomach. Therefore procedures for foreign body airway obstruction should be used
only if the airway is completely obstructed by a solid object.
Positioning of an unresponsive person who is breathing
Szpilman (2012) recommends placing the breathing person on their side in the recovery
position, with their head positioned to allow free drainage of fluids. The position should be
stable, without pressure on the chest.
Spinal injury in instances of drowning
A person who is drowning is at risk for a spinal injury only if they have also sustained a traumatic
injury or been involved in a high impact activity (such as diving, water skiing, surfing or being
caught in moderate to severe surf breaks). Routine spinal immobilization does not appear to be
warranted solely on the basis of a history of submersion (R. S. Watson et al., 2001).
The International Life Saving Federation (2016) recommends that in the case of drowning as a
result of a traumatic injury, if the person is unresponsive, management of their airway takes
priority over any suspected spinal injury. The person can be handled to minimize spinal
movement if possible. If the person is responsive, the principles of minimizing movement apply
and if removal from the water is necessary, then it should be done while maintaining alignment
of the spine.
FIRST AID | 227

Education review
Prevention of drowning
A paper by Denny et al. (2019) summarized factors that contribute to the incidence of drowning
in the US and then made recommendations to prevent drowning. These strategies to prevent
drowning are particularly relevant to education and advocacy.
Factors that contribute to drowning in the US included the populations of young children ages
zero to four due to unsupervised access to bodies of water and adolescents who may
overestimate their water competencies, undertake risky behaviour or may be affected by
alcohol. Underlying health conditions such as epilepsy, cardiac arrhythmia and autism also
increase the risk of drowning. Socio-demographics are also a contributing factor.
The paper outlined that learning to swim is just one part of water competency. To prevent
drowning, Denny et al. recommended that people need broader water competencies including
knowledge of local hazards, understanding of one’s limitations, ability to put on a life jacket and
all the first aid related to drowning (recognition, rescue, etc.). Developing these skills takes time
and developmental maturity. The paper highlighted some existing drowning prevention
strategies such as the Haddon Matrix and ranks five major interventions as evidence-based:
four-sided pool fencing, life jackets, swim lessons, supervision and lifeguards.
Denny et al. went on to identify the role of parents, medical providers, pool operators and
policymakers in being informed of and promoting drowning prevention recommendations and
legislation. The paper identified the most evidence-based interventions as being four-sided pool
fencing, life jacket use, swim lessons, supervision and lifeguards and bystander CPR.
The World Health Organization’s (WHO) “Drowning Prevention: an implementation guide” (2017)
also formed much of the foundation for the prevention content. The paper provides practical
steps to preventing drowning. Later, in 2022, the organization also published its “Practical
guidance for the provision of day-care, basic swimming and water safety skills, and safe rescue
and resuscitation training”. Two systematic reviews formed the basis for this guideline, including
one on the impact of day care programmes for children under six years to prevent drowning in
low- and middle-income countries (De Buck et al., 2021), and one on basic swimming and water
safety skills training for drowning prevention in children, a review that was recently updated
(Criel et al., 2025).
Lifeline rescues
Pearn and Franklin (2009) carried out a study with 25 volunteers which tested their ability to
throw a lifeline (rope) to a person simulating drowning 10 metres from the shore. The time and
accuracy of achieving a successful lifeline throw were recorded for each of multiple attempts.
The results indicated that more than half of fit adults cannot throw a lifeline accurately to a
distance of 10 metres even with multiple attempts and that, in the heat of the moment, one in
five “rescuers” do not anchor the end of the rope, which is then lost when thrown. These results
were compared to competitive or trained rescuers who can deploy a lifeline with extreme
accuracy and great speed.
228 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

The study highlighted the importance of the safety of the rescuer and the use of non-contact
rescue to maintain this. However, it emphasized that to be successful in non-contact rescues,
people need training and practice in throwing a lifeline or buoy. Pearn and Franklin implied that
a person’s first time throwing a lifeline should not be in the midst of a life-threatening event.
Water competencies
Stallman et al. (2017) built on previous work to use evidence to select essential water
competencies. They argued swimming skills alone are not enough to address drowning
prevention; rather a broad range of competencies are required including:
1. safe water entry competence 11. knowledge of local hazards
2. breath control competence competence
3. stationary surface competence 12. coping with risk competence
(buoyancy or treading water) (awareness, assessment, avoidance)
4. water orientation competence 13. assess personal competence
5. propulsion competence (swimming) 14. rescue competence (recognize and
6. underwater competence assist safely)
7. safe exit competence 15. water safety competence (attitudes
8. personal flotation device and values)
9. clothed water competence
10. open water competence
In the “aquatic literacy” model, Mekkaoui et al. (2025) combine the concepts of water
competencies and physical literacy. The authors define aquatic literacy as follows: "A dynamic
state of individual aquatic education that enables lifelong voluntary and safe engagement in a
wide range of aquatic physical activity opportunities, supported by the development of
preservation of essential motor, psycho-social and cognitive skills, as well as the overlap between
them, related to movement experiences in, on and around water.” This work adds a solid
theoretical foundation to the prevention content presented in these guidelines.
FIRST AID | 229

230 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

BREATHING PROBLEMS
FIRST AID | 231

Breathing difficulties
Key action
Help the person get into a comfortable position (usually seated).
Introduction
A person with breathing difficulties may complain of tightness in the chest or a feeling of
suffocation; they may have very rapid or noisy breathing, or struggle to inhale. Life-threatening
causes requiring first aid are Choking, heart failure or an acute episode of a known condition
(such as Asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). Breathing difficulties can also lead
to unresponsiveness. Hyperventilation is also a relatively frequent breathing disorder that is
usually a consequence of anxiety or being upset and usually passes quickly. In babies and young
children, breathing difficulties could also be caused by croup or bronchiolitis.
Guidelines
● People with breathing difficulties may experience relief in a comfortable position, such as
a seated position or an arm-bracing position (leaning forward with arms braced and
leaning on a support).*
● A person who is hyperventilating may be reassured.*
Good practice points
● First aid providers may assist the person to take their medication if they have any.
● If the person is experiencing severe breathing difficulties, acute chest pain or a change in
mental status (such as confusion or drowsiness), the first aid provider should access
emergency medical services (EMS), monitor for unresponsiveness and continue to
observe and assist the person until help arrives.
● In certain cases, a specially trained first aid provider can give supplementary oxygen.
● If the person’s breathing does not improve after 10–15 minutes, medical care should be
considered.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Be aware of any conditions, such as asthma or lung disease, that family, friends and
coworkers may have, and learn how to help them in the event of an emergency.
● Avoid entering places with poisonous gases or smoke without appropriate breathing
equipment.
● Limit strenuous physical activity, particularly for people at risk during episodes of heavy
pollution, heatwaves or when pollen is in the air.
232 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Early recognition
Breathing difficulties can be subjective. What may seem severe to one person may not be severe
to another. Listen to the person and look for clues in their behaviour and the surrounding
environment.
The person may tell you or indicate that they are having difficulty breathing, or that they have a
pre-existing medical condition such as asthma or lung disease.
The signs and symptoms a first aid provider may look for to indicate breathing difficulties
include:
● shortness of breath
● laboured breathing
● noisy breathing
● coughing
● rapid breathing rate (more than 40 breaths per minute in babies; 30 in children; 20 in
adults)
● slow breathing rate (less than 25 breaths per minute in babies; 15 in children; 10 in adults)
● not being able to count to 10 after one deep breath in
● use of accessory muscles for breathing (neck, shoulders)
● movement of the nostrils
● bluish colour to the skin of the lips, ears, fingers and toes.
Hyperventilation is also characterized by other typical signs and symptoms, such as:
● dizziness
● headache
● sweating
● tingling sensation in hands, feet, fingers or mouth.
Be alert as dangerous gases and other substances can cause breathing difficulties for first aid
providers and bystanders. Providers should assess the scene. For example, the person may
have breathing difficulties due to carbon monoxide poisoning, which occurs during
incomplete combustion in an oxygen-poor environment.
Slowed breathing and an altered level of consciousness may be a sign of opioid toxicity.
First aid steps
Breathing difficulties
1. Help the person into a comfortable (usually seated) position and reassure them. They may
experience relief by sitting with their arms braced and leaning forward on a support.
2. Assist them in taking their medication if they have any. Loosen any tight clothing.
3. Access EMS immediately if:
a. their medication is ineffective after a couple of minutes
b. the person is experiencing severe breathing difficulties
c. the person has a change in mental status, such as confusion, drowsiness or
unresponsiveness
FIRST AID | 233

d. their breathing becomes slow
e. their lips, fingers or ears turn a bluish colour.
4. If properly trained and access to supplementary oxygen is available, give it to the person
as needed.
5. Continue to observe the person, keeping them calm and comfortable. Stay with the
person until their breathing returns to normal or EMS arrives.
Hyperventilating
If the person is hyperventilating:
1. Move the person to a quiet place and ask bystanders to provide a calm environment. If
possible, remove the cause of the panic attack.
2. Comfort the person and act calmly and predictably. Ask them to breathe in and out
slowly and steadily.
3. Loosen any tight clothing.
4. Stay with the person until their breathing returns to normal.
If the person is unresponsive, open their airway and check for breathing. If you suspect
opioid toxicity, consider using naloxone.
Access help
● In the case of hyperventilation, access EMS if:
○ the panic attack continues
○ you suspect that it may not be caused by a panic attack.
Recovery
● If this was the person’s first episode of breathing difficulties, teach them the signs and
symptoms so they can recognize any future episodes sooner. Early recognition helps the
person to access any medication earlier, get into a comfortable position sooner, and, if
necessary, seek EMS sooner.
● Encourage the person to seek specialized medical care for recurring breathing difficulties.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Research has identified that both within and between communities, people may treat
breathing difficulties differently. Some communities and individuals wait to seek
assistance when in a much more advanced phase of illness (Cartledge et al., 2017).
● Tailor education to learners needs according to the environment they are in (e.g. areas of
high air pollution), or if there is a particular situation where breathing difficulties might
become apparent (such as during a pandemic).
● Non-emergency medical services may be able to treat breathing difficulties that occur
slowly and over time. First aid education should include advice about the available
options in local health services.
234 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Highlight relevant context considerations, such as wearing a mask during smog season,
the potential aggravation of breathing difficulties by industrial zones, and the additional
breathing issues caused by wildfires or pollution.
Learner considerations
● Educators should be mindful of the cultural, gender and age-based factors that may
influence learners’ understanding of breathing difficulties and severity. Ensure first aid
programme content is inclusive.
● Learning about how to recognize and respond to breathing difficulties could benefit
carers of older adults who have chronic lung or heart conditions, parents with babies and
caregivers of children with asthma.
● Learners who spend time in smoky or dusty environments (such as firefighters or
construction workers), or in places where there might be chemicals (such as laboratories,
factories or farms), could benefit from learning about breathing difficulties.
Facilitation tips
● Emphasize the importance of listening to the person’s breathing to help identify any
problems that may worsen and result in a medical emergency. Highlight that breathing
can deteriorate over time.
● Revisit what “normal” breathing looks like by counting breathing rates and regularity.
● Ask learners to show how they think someone may look or act when experiencing
breathing difficulties.
● Explore learners’ understanding of the term “breathing difficulty”, as well as the causes
and their experience with it. They might have different interpretations that could affect
their response and willingness to act. For example, people with a specific type of
breathing difficulty called orthopnoea have trouble breathing when they lie down,
typically at night, and may not seek assistance if they feel they can resolve the symptom
by sitting upright.
● Run scenarios to rehearse recognizing and responding to the typical presentation of
various types of breathing difficulties. Assessing the scene for hazards should be a part of
the exercise.
● Explain to learners that individuals who are hyperventilating may benefit from adopting a
controlled breathing rhythm. Learners should practise setting an example and
encouraging someone to follow their breathing rhythm. This may involve counting one to
breathe in through the nose and three to breathe out through the mouth.
Facilitator tools
● Use videos to show normal breathing and changes in breathing (e.g. asthma attack), as
well as brief clips to demonstrate life-threatening breathing problems.
● Use scenario-based learning to help individuals integrate assessment skills.
● A method of assessing breathing difficulty is to ask the person to take a deep breath and
then ask them to count from 1 to 30, clearly but quickly (Roth Score). If the person cannot
count to 10, at most, they have breathing difficulties. Give learners time to practise this
method.
FIRST AID | 235

Learning connections
● Common causes of breathing difficulties include asthma, chronic obstructive lung
disease, bronchitis, croup (babies and children) and bronchiolitis (babies and young
children).
● Other life-threatening conditions which result in breathing difficulty include choking,
heart failure and asthma attack.
● Consider whether it is appropriate for learners to learn about oxygen administration.
● Any stressful situation, including ones where first aid for injuries and illness might be
required, could cause some people to panic. Learning how to calm a person who is
hyperventilating could be an important part of scene management.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
The Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) updated the evidence reviews on shortness of
breath and posture, as well as hyperventilation and breathing in a paper bag, but no new
studies could be identified.
Shortness of breath and posture
There is limited evidence from three experimental studies in favour of arm bracing and sitting.
A randomized controlled trial showed that arm bracing resulted in a statistically significant
decrease in the Borg dyspnoea scale. It was compared to leaning forward while standing or
erect standing in people with stable chronic obstructive pulmonary disease experiencing
shortness of breath (dyspnoea). A statistically significant increase or decrease in the Borg
dyspnoea score or standing erect compared to leaning forward while standing could not be
demonstrated. In a second randomized controlled trial, it was shown that a sitting position
resulted in a statistically significant decrease in Borg dyspnoea score, compared to lying on the
back (supine position). In a third trial, a statistically significant change in Borg dyspnoea score,
sitting compared to lying on the back (supine), could not be demonstrated. The evidence is of
low certainty.
Hyperventilation and breathing in a paper bag
There is limited evidence from one experimental study in favour of breathing in a paper bag (van
den Hout et al., 1988). This study, in which 12 healthy volunteers were instructed to hyperventilate
deliberately, showed that breathing in a paper bag resulted in a statistically significant decrease
in time to reach baseline CO2 levels and time to symptom disappearance, compared to not
breathing in a paper bag. A statistically significant decrease in time to symptom disappearance
when breathing in a closed-circuit tube system, compared to an open-circuit tube system, could
not be demonstrated.
Cold or humidified air
CEBaP updated the evidence summary on inhaling cold or humidified air in case of shortness of
breath in 2024, and identified one randomized and one quasi-randomized controlled trial. In one
study, it was shown that steam inhalation, in combination with standard care, resulted in a
236 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

statistically significant reduction in respiratory distress after 18 and 24 hours in infants with acute
bronchiolitis compared to standard care only in children with bronchiolitis. This was not the case
in children with pneumonia. A second study showed that steam inhalation resulted in
significantly higher respiratory distress than using nebulized salbutamol in infants with
bronchiolitis. No difference was found compared to nebulized saline (placebo). Therefore, steam
inhalation seems to have a positive effect on respiratory problems, but only when used with
other treatment modalities.
FIRST AID | 237

Choking
Key action
Dislodge the obstruction in the person’s throat so they can breathe.
Introduction
Choking occurs when the airway is partially or completely blocked by a foreign body, such as a
piece of food or an object (a magnet or small toy). Choking is also referred to as a foreign body
airway obstruction and is a common cause of breathing emergencies, especially in young
children. Deaths related to choking underline the importance of preventive and first aid
measures. It is one of the most common life-threatening emergencies seen by first aid
providers. The technique used to help someone who is choking depends on the person’s age,
size and level of responsiveness.
In this topic, a baby refers to someone who is less than one year old. A child refers to
someone who is one year or older.
Guidelines
● Immediately after recognition, bystanders can carry out interventions to support the
removal of a foreign body airway obstruction.*
● Back blows may be used initially on adults and children with a foreign body airway
obstruction and an ineffective cough.*
● Abdominal thrusts may be used on adults and children with a foreign body airway
obstruction and an ineffective cough where back blows are ineffective.*
● First aid providers may consider the manual extraction of visible items in the mouth.*
● First aid providers should use standard CPR, consisting of both chest compressions and
rescue breaths if possible, on an unresponsive person with a foreign body airway
obstruction.*
Good practice points
● When helping a responsive person, first aid providers must be able to recognize the signs
of partial airway obstruction (e.g. the person can speak, cough and breathe) and
complete airway obstruction (the person is unable to speak, has a weakened cough and
has difficulty breathing).
● A person with partial airway obstruction should be monitored until they improve, as it
could develop into a complete airway obstruction.
● Back blows and abdominal thrusts or chest thrusts should be applied in quick succession
until the obstruction has been cleared from the airway.
● Responsive babies may receive a combination of back blows followed by chest thrusts to
clear the obstruction from the airway.
● In responsive adults and children who are choking, chest thrusts, back blows or
abdominal thrusts are equally effective.
238 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● In infants who are choking, chest compressions with thumbs/hands in a circle, two fingers
or the palm of one hand are equally effective.
● An unresponsive baby should receive a combination of rescue breaths and chest
compressions to clear the obstruction from the airway.
● Chest thrusts may be used in unconscious adults and children with a foreign-body airway
obstruction.
● First aid providers should not use a blind finger sweep in people with a foreign-body
airway obstruction as this may push the object further down the person’s airway.
● First aid providers should not routinely use a suction-based airway clearance device to
remove a foreign-body airway obstruction.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Understand the difference between partial or complete choking and Asthma attack,
Allergic reaction and anaphylaxis and other conditions that may cause sudden breathing
difficulties.
● Be aware when people are eating as this is usually when choking occurs.
● Monitor a baby who is eating or might put something in their mouth.
● Be aware that people who have reduced responsiveness, a neurological impairment that
affects their swallowing and coughing reflexes (e.g. stroke, Parkinson’s disease, cerebral
palsy or dementia) or respiratory disease are at an increased risk of choking. People are
also at a higher risk if they are intoxicated (due to substances or alcohol), if their teeth are
in poor condition or if they are an older adult.
Early recognition
The person may have had something in their mouth, such as food. Ask the person: “Are you
choking?”
Partial airway obstruction:
● weak or forceful coughing
● can speak or cry out
● makes high-pitched noises while inhaling, but is still able to breathe
Complete airway obstruction:
● unable to cough, speak, cry or breathe
● clutches the throat with one or both hands
● panic
● bluish colour to the skin of the lips, ears, fingers and toes
● becomes unresponsive if the blockage is not removed
FIRST AID | 239

First aid steps
Adults and children
Partial airway obstruction
1. Encourage the person to cough to clear the blockage.
2. Monitor the person until they improve, as it could develop into a complete airway
obstruction.
Complete airway obstruction
1. If the person cannot cough, speak or breathe, give up to five firm blows between their
shoulder blades.
2. If the back blows are unsuccessful, give up to five abdominal thrusts by putting your fist
between their ribs and their belly button and pulling it sharply inward and upward using
the other hand.
3. If the abdominal thrusts are unsuccessful, immediately access emergency medical
services (EMS).
4. Continue alternating between five back blows and five abdominal thrusts until the
blockage clears or the person becomes unresponsive.
If the person has partial airway obstruction, avoid taking action as they may be able to clear
the airway more effectively on their own. Consider calling EMS.
If the person becomes unresponsive, give standard CPR.
Only remove solid material in the airway with your fingers if you can see it. Do not blindly
sweep the mouth with your fingers.
Babies
Partial airway obstruction
1. If the baby is coughing or crying, wait to see if they can cough to clear their own airway.
2. Monitor the baby until they improve, as it could develop into a complete airway
obstruction.
Complete airway obstruction
1. If the baby cannot cough, cry or breathe, give up to five firm blows between their
shoulder blades.
2. If the back blows are unsuccessful, turn the baby over and lay them on your knees. Use
the two-thumbs encircling technique to perform chest thrusts as advised for chest
compressions but compressing the sternum more sharply. Repeat up to five times or
until the obstruction is cleared.
3. If the chest thrusts are unsuccessful, access EMS immediately.
4. Continue alternating between five back blows and five chest thrusts until the blockage
clears or the baby becomes unresponsive.
240 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

If the baby becomes unresponsive, start standard CPR.
Only remove solid material in the airway with your fingers if you can see it. Do not blindly
sweep the mouth with your fingers.
Access help
● In the case of complete airway obstruction, always access EMS. The person may have
internal injuries from the abdominal thrusts, or the object may have caused some
damage to their airway. Even if they are no longer choking, they may have a piece of the
object in their lungs that can later cause complications.
● In the case of partial airway obstruction, the person should seek medical care if they
continuously cough, experience difficulty swallowing or feel as though the object is still
stuck in their throat.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Consider which contexts learners are most likely to encounter choking and if there are
specific signs to which they should be more alert. For example, those working with older
adults at an assisted living residence should recognize what it looks like to choke.
Conversely, the signs of choking present differently in babies so learners caring for this
age group will need to be aware of those signs.
● Because choking often happens while eating, a choking emergency could happen in a
public place. Programme designers may consider framing this as a “helping behaviour”
topic as it is one where the bystander effect could result in people feeling uncomfortable
to step forward and help. Emphasize the first step as asking the person if they are
choking.
● In babies and children, reported cases of choking occur while eating or playing with
non-food items such as coins or small toys. Learners should explore ways to reduce the
risk of babies and children putting objects in their mouths; for example, place small items
out of reach.
● Recommendations for treatment can vary across regions. Some recommendations use
chest thrusts applied with two thumbs on the breastbone, while others prefer chest
thrusts using the heel of one hand. If the infant becomes unconscious, some guidance
recommends starting with five ventilations, while other guidance recommends
beginning CPR with chest compressions. Across all guidance, there is agreement that
evidence is insufficient to support the use of suction devices.
Learner considerations
● Some babies experience partial airway obstruction frequently when they are learning to
eat solids. While usually not serious, the experience can be very distressing for caregivers.
Include an opportunity for caregivers to share their fears and their experiences as a way of
building confidence to help babies effectively.
FIRST AID | 241

● Learning to give back blows can be challenging as it is not possible to practise this on
another person for fear of hurting them. Stress that a gentle slap will not expel the object
stuck in someone’s throat; force is needed to dislodge the object, even if this might hurt
the person.
● If the learners are likely to need to help a pregnant or obese person with choking, see the
Facilitating choking skills resource for an adaptation for abdominal thrusts.
Facilitation tips
● Focus choking education on the prevention and first aid domains within the chain of
survival behaviours.
● Explain why it is important to do the different actions on someone who is choking. Back
blows create a strong vibration and pressure in the airway while abdominal thrusts and
chest thrusts squeeze the air out of the lungs, creating additional pressure in the airway.
These techniques may dislodge the object and help the person to breathe again.
● Explore how using gravity (by bending over or holding the baby’s head lower than the
body) can help dislodge the foreign object or liquids drain out.
● Learners practising back blows and chest thrusts for a choking baby should sit or kneel.
Although it can be easy to hold a mannequin baby in one arm (straddle arm technique), it
can be more challenging with a real baby, especially for younger learners. Supporting the
baby on their lap can be more effective and safer (Gesicki & Longmore, 2019).
● Explain that learners should not use abdominal thrusts for babies as their organs are still
developing and may get damaged.
● While facilitators may include participants in demonstrating how to position oneself to
give back blows and abdominal or chest thrusts, the real actions should be demonstrated
on a mannequin.
● If teaching children or demonstrating for a child, the facilitator should go on their knees
at the child’s height to demonstrate back blows and abdominal thrusts.
● Explore and address any misconceptions about choking, such as giving the person a
drink to clear the blockage or hanging a baby upside down.
Facilitation tools
● Use videos or demonstrations to help learners understand how to do back blows, chest
thrusts or abdominal thrusts. Emphasize that while these actions may seem aggressive,
they could save the person’s life. Explain that learners should adapt the force of their
actions depending on the size of the person, but that those actions still need to be
forceful enough to clear the airway.
● In addition to mannequins, use objects such as punching bags or stuffed sacks to practise
back blows and abdominal or chest thrusts.
● Develop scenarios to practise recognizing and helping someone who is choking. These
scenarios may be used to assess learners’ knowledge and skills on this topic.
Learning connections
● If someone who is choking becomes unresponsive, it is necessary to start CPR as the
chest compressions or rescue breaths may dislodge the object from their airway and help
242 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

them to breathe again. See Unresponsive and abnormal breathing adolescent and adult
or baby and child.
Scientific foundation
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) conducted a systematic review
on foreign body airway obstruction (FBAO) in 2020 (Olasveengen et al., 2020a; Olasveengen et
al., 2020b), which primarily included case series and observational studies and found very
low–certainty evidence for all commonly used interventions. Since then, this topic has been
transferred from the Basic Life Support (BLS) Task Force to the First Aid (FA) Task Force (2023),
reflecting its relevance to lay first aid providers. An Evidence Update published in 2025 identified
17 new publications (August 2019–September 2024) but did not fundamentally change the
certainty of evidence, which remains very low (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
Back blows
Earlier evidence (2020) reported survival and relief of obstruction in all treated individuals across
several case series, with a small number of reported injuries or complications. The 2025 Evidence
Update confirms that back blows remain effective and commonly successful, and one newer
study suggests they may be more effective than chest or abdominal thrusts. Overall certainty
remains very low because of serious risk of bias and indirectness, but back blows are still
considered an appropriate initial intervention.
In a cohort study of bystanders caring for FBAO (Bieliński et al.,2024; Dunne et al., 2024), relief of
FBAO occurred in 492 of 643 (90%) patients with BLS interventions. Back blows demonstrated
better outcomes than other interventions. For the important outcome relief of obstruction,
abdominal thrusts as a first intervention had an OR of 0.57 (0.39 -0.62) compared to back blows.
For the critical outcome of survival to discharge, abdominal thrusts as a first intervention had an
OR of 0.20 (0.07 – 0.59) compared to back blows. This is the largest cohort study to date of
bystander interventions and supports beginning with back blows.
Abdominal thrusts
In 2020, multiple case series reported successful relief of FBAO, but a substantial number of
reports described associated injuries or complications. The 2025 Evidence Update did not
materially change this balance: abdominal thrusts remain effective, particularly when back
blows fail, but carry a recognized risk of harm. Evidence certainty remains very low.
Chest thrusts / compressions
Previously, limited observational data suggested possible benefits, including in unconscious
patients, with a small number of reported complications. The updated evidence continues to
support their use primarily in unconscious patients with FBAO. As with other interventions,
certainty remains very low because of imprecision and risk of bias.
Finger sweep
The 2020 review found no clear benefit of blind finger sweeps and documented multiple injuries
and complications. The 2025 Evidence Update reinforces this conclusion: blind finger sweeps are
FIRST AID | 243

associated with harm and no demonstrated benefit. However, manual extraction of visible
foreign bodies in the mouth may be considered.
Foreign body airway obstruction removal by bystanders
Both the 2020 review and the 2025 Evidence Update suggest that early bystander intervention
is associated with improved outcomes compared with no action, despite very low–certainty
evidence. The updated evidence emphasizes that multiple interventions are often required
sequentially to achieve relief.
Other
In 2020, evidence on Magill forceps and suction-based airway clearance devices was limited or
not summarized. The 2025 Evidence Update notes an increasing prevalence of airway clearance
devices but concludes that evidence remains insufficient to make specific treatment
recommendations. Use of Magill forceps may be considered by appropriately trained individuals,
particularly in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest contexts.
Overall update and implications
The 2025 Evidence Update confirms that, despite additional publications, the certainty of
evidence for all FBAO removal techniques remains very low. No single technique has
demonstrated clear superiority across all contexts, and it is common for more than one
intervention to be required. Current treatment recommendations from 2020 are therefore
maintained, with clarification of age ranges and reinforcement of a stepwise approach: early
bystander action, initial use of back blows in patients with ineffective cough, escalation to
abdominal thrusts in adults and children over one year if needed, avoidance of blind finger
sweeps and consideration of chest thrusts in unconscious patients.
Education review
The papers we reviewed raised important considerations for learners, particularly concerning a
choking baby. Gesicki and Longmore (2019) compared the length of a standard baby
mannequin with the length of adult forearms. Based on data, they found that the traditional
straddle-arm technique was not physically possible for many first aid providers and
recommended a more supported version with a seated or kneeling first aid provider.
244 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Asthma attack
Key action
Help the person to sit in a comfortable position and ask them to use their inhaler.
Introduction
Asthma is a common chronic illness of the airways in the lungs. It affects both children and
adults worldwide. When an attack occurs, the airways in the lungs swell, narrow and produce
extra mucus, making it difficult to breathe. The person may also wheeze and cough. A severe
asthma attack can be life-threatening. Many people with asthma have been prescribed and can
self-administer medication through an inhaler. Immediate first aid can sometimes prevent the
person from needing to go to the hospital.
Guidelines
● People with breathing difficulties may experience relief from different body positions,
such as a seated position or an arm-bracing tripod position (leaning forward with arms
braced and leaning on a support).*
● A first aid provider familiar with the commonly used bronchodilator inhaler devices
(inhaler) may assist a person in using that person’s inhaler, if local regulations allow.*
● A first aid provider who is specifically trained may administer a bronchodilator at their
discretion, if local regulations allow.*
● Fitting a spacer device to an inhaler for medication administration may help improve the
person’s breathing by maximizing medication administration, especially in children.*
Good practice points
● A person experiencing an asthma attack should be reassured and helped into a
comfortable position.
● Loosening any restrictive clothing may help the person breathe more comfortably.
● If the person has no inhaler, the inhaler is ineffective or the person is experiencing severe
breathing difficulties (such as a change in mental status or slow and less noisy breathing),
the first aid provider should access emergency medical services (EMS). Continue to
observe and assist the person until help arrives.
● The first aid provider should move the person away from things that may be triggering
the attack, such as smoke or dust.
● Specifically trained first aid providers can provide supplementary oxygen to a person
having an asthma attack if local regulations allow this.
FIRST AID | 245

Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● People with asthma should regularly take preventive medicine as prescribed by their
doctor and learn about how to manage their condition.
● Educating school staff about asthma can be important to ensure children get the right
care when they have an asthma attack at school. This should include learning how to
lessen the likelihood of frequent and severe asthma attacks in children.
Early recognition
Usually, a person with asthma knows about their condition and may have their inhaler with
them. The person may be able to indicate they are having an asthma attack. They are usually
found in an upright position, sometimes sitting leaning forward.
The person may experience:
● difficult or laboured breathing accompanied by wheezing and coughing
● shortness of breath, or a feeling of suffocation or tightness in the chest
● rapid breathing and elevated heart rate
● altered mental status, including becoming anxious, confused or unresponsive.
First aid steps
1. Help the person into a comfortable position and reassure them.
2. Help the person to use their inhaler. Loosen any tight clothing.
3. Access EMS immediately if:
a. the person has no inhaler and the attack lasts for several minutes
b. the inhaler is ineffective within a few minutes
c. the person is experiencing severe breathing difficulties
d. the person’s lips, ears, fingers or toes turn a bluish colour
e. the person has a change in mental status, such as becoming confused or
unresponsive
f. their breathing becomes slow, less noisy, or if the person is getting tired.
4. If properly trained and access to supplementary oxygen is available, give it to the person
as needed.
5. Stay with the person and continue to observe them, keeping them calm and comfortable
until the attack is over. Support the person in following the directions for additional doses
of their prescription while waiting for medical help to arrive.
Local adaptation
● In contexts where inhalers or supplemental oxygen are not readily available, help the
person remain calm, for example, by sitting near an open window with access to fresh,
clean air.
246 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Access help
Access help if the person’s mental state is affected as they may become unresponsive. In a
critical case, cardiac arrest may occur, and the person may stop breathing.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Administration of an inhaler or other medication should be guided by local laws and
regulations. First aid educators may need to tailor their education to the specific context.
● In both high- and low-resource settings, a person with asthma may not have an inhaler
with them. Allow learners to practise what to do when there is no inhaler available. This
may include actions to calm the person or help them breathe more easily, such as
loosening clothing and sitting up. You may also move the person away from the trigger
causing the asthma attack (such as smoke).
● In areas where EMS services are extremely limited or non-existent, individuals should
learn strategies that can help to ease breathing until the attack passes.
● Check legal restrictions that govern the help first aid providers can give and educate
within these. If necessary, include these laws in the educational content.
Learner considerations
● Consider whether learners need to understand:
○ how to recognize someone is having an asthma attack
○ how to help someone use an inhaler
○ when and if to repeat the dose of inhaler
○ when and how to use a spacer device
○ how to administer supplementary oxygen.
● There is limited evidence that schoolteachers are under-prepared for asthma attacks and
could therefore be identified as an important audience for this topic (Neuharth-Pritchett
& Getch, 2001).
● Asthma attacks can be fatal, so working with health promotion teams and local clinics
can help assess community members for potential asthma and needed prescriptions.
Facilitation tips
● Spend time exploring how a first aid provider can help calm someone having an asthma
attack, as this may help improve their breathing.
● Administration of inhalers by first aid providers requires education in recognition and
medication use, depending on the method being used and the availability of equipment.
● Discuss different types of inhalers and other equipment, such as spacers, and how to
either administer the inhaler or help a person use it.
● Learners should have the opportunity to practise the steps they would use in helping
someone having an attack (Espinoza-Palma et al., 2009).
FIRST AID | 247

Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Evidence summaries from the Centre for Evidence-based Practice (CEBaP) were drawn upon to
develop these guidelines, as well as evidence review work by the International Liaison
Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR).
Posture
An evidence summary from 2019 identified three experimental studies, involving people with
chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, obese people with obstructive sleep apnoea or obesity
hypoventilation syndrome, and people with chronic heart failure, comparing different positions
to relieve dyspnoea. There is limited evidence in favour of arm bracing and sitting. It was shown
that arm bracing resulted in a statistically significant decrease in Borg dyspnoea score,
compared to leaning forward while standing or erect standing. A statistically significant change
in Borg dyspnoea score or standing erect compared to leaning forward while standing could not
be demonstrated. It was shown that a sitting position resulted in a statistically significant
decrease in the Borg dyspnoea score, compared to lying on the back. A statistically significant
increase or decrease in the visual analogue scale dyspnoea score or sitting compared to lying on
the back was not demonstrated. The evidence is of low certainty. The summary was updated in
2024 and no new studies were identified.
Cold or humidified air
CEBaP updated the evidence summary on inhaling cold or humidified air in case of shortness of
breath in 2024, and identified one randomized and one quasi-randomized controlled trial. In one
study, it was shown that steam inhalation, in combination with standard care, resulted in a
statistically significant reduction in respiratory distress after 18 and 24 hours in infants with acute
bronchiolitis compared to standard care only in children with bronchiolitis. This was not the case
in children with pneumonia. A second study showed that steam inhalation resulted in
significantly higher respiratory distress than using nebulized salbutamol in infants with
bronchiolitis. No difference was found compared to nebulized saline (placebo). Therefore, steam
inhalation seems to have a positive effect on respiratory problems, but only when used with
other treatment modalities.
Calmly breathing or breathing exercises
The evidence summary on breathing exercises for individuals with asthma or COPD was
updated in 2024; however, no new studies were identified.
Bronchodilator inhalers
ILCOR conducted a systematic review on bronchodilator use in 2015, which identified eight
randomized controlled trials, two observational studies and one meta-analysis. Two randomized
controlled trials showed an improved time to resolution of symptoms (e.g. wheezing, dyspnoea)
and six randomized controlled trials and two observational studies showed improved
therapeutic endpoints (e.g. oxygenation and ventilation). No studies were identified on the effect
on time to resumption of usual activity or on harm to the person. In three randomized controlled
248 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

trials and an observational study, a difference in complications could not be shown when using
inhalers compared to a placebo. The evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be
considered precise.
In 2022, ILCOR published an evidence update of this topic and identified a systematic review
concluding that among children with asthma exacerbations treated in the emergency
department, short-acting b-agonists delivered by metered-dose inhaler decrease hospital
admissions in younger children and decrease emergency department length of stay in older
children (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c). Based on the evidence update, it was
decided not to conduct a full systematic review update.
In 2023, ILCOR published a scoping review about the potential harms of bronchodilator
administration, including 13 studies that reported adverse events such as tachycardia,
arrhythmias, tremor, dizziness and a decrease in serum potassium concentrations. The
treatment recommendation that trained first aid providers assist with the administration of a
bronchodilator to individuals with asthma experiencing difficulty breathing remained unaltered.
Inhalers with spacers
A CEBaP evidence summary identified two Cochrane systematic reviews on the use of inhalers
with spacers. No new systematic reviews were identified during the 2024 update of the
summary.
The first Cochrane systematic review revealed that there is limited evidence from eight
randomized controlled trials in favour of using inhalers with spacers for medication
administration in children. It was shown that the use of inhalers with spacers resulted in a
statistically significant increase in final peak expiratory flow, a 15-minute rise in expiratory flow, a
15-minute rise in forced expiratory volume and a statistically significant decrease in pulse rate
and improvement of blood gases, compared to nebulizers. However, no statistically significant
decreases in hospital admission, development of tremor, rise in respiratory rate or deterioration
of blood gases were demonstrated. Likewise, no statistically significant increases in 30-minute
rise in forced expiratory volume and 30-minute rise in peak expiratory flow were demonstrated.
For adults, a difference in outcomes using inhalers with spacers compared to nebulizers could
not be demonstrated. Evidence is of low certainty, and results cannot be considered precise due
to limited sample sizes, low numbers of events and wide confidence intervals.
The second Cochrane systematic review indicated that there is limited evidence from six
randomized controlled trials, neither in favour of using home-made spacers nor commercially
available spacers for medication administration in children. A statistically significant decrease in
hospital admission, clinical score, heart rate or need for additional treatment when using
home-made spacers compared to commercially available spacers could not be demonstrated. A
statistically significant increase in peak expiratory flow rate or oxygen saturation when using
home-made versus commercial spacers could also not be demonstrated. Evidence is of low
certainty, and results cannot be considered precise due to limited sample sizes, low numbers of
events and wide confidence intervals.
FIRST AID | 249

Croup
Key action
Help the child to rest in a comfortable position that allows them to breathe easily.
Introduction
Croup is a breathing difficulty, usually seen in children six years or younger and triggered by a
viral infection in the upper airways (larynx). The infection causes the throat and upper airways to
swell, which produces a barking cough and makes the child’s breathing sound “squeaky” and
hoarse. Symptoms often worsen at night or if the child becomes distressed.
Good practice points
● Help the child into a comfortable position that allows for easy breathing (usually sitting).
● Encourage the child to breathe in warm, humidified air, which may help distract them
and calm them down.
● If there is significant shortness of breath or the first aid provider is in any doubt, the
provider should access emergency medical care.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Caregivers and parents of young children should be aware of croup, including how to
recognize it and what to do.
Early recognition
The child may have:
● shortness of breath
● a hoarse cough
● noisy breathing that sounds raspy or squeaky (also called stridor)
● a croaky voice
First aid steps
1. Calmly reassure the child and help them into a comfortable position (usually sitting).
2. Take the child’s temperature. If they are running a fever, treat it.
3. If possible, have the child breathe in warm, humidified air (e.g. sit them near a running
shower, or lean them over a bowl of hot water). Make sure the water is not too hot to avoid
burns.
4. Monitor their breathing and level of response closely. If the episode of croup is severe or
persists, access emergency medical care.
250 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

A child with croup may find their condition alarming. Remaining calm may help them to be
calm and ease their symptoms.
Access help
● If the child is having severe difficulty breathing (they are sitting up, bent forward, mouth
open, using other body parts to help them breathe — such as their neck and shoulders —
moving their nostrils or a hollow forms at the base of their neck), access emergency
medical care immediately. Be aware that symptoms can worsen from mild to severe
within a few hours.
● Epiglottitis is a croup-like condition which can cause a small flap in the throat to swell and
block the airway. A child with epiglottitis needs urgent medical care. If in any doubt,
access medical care.
Recovery
After the first episode of croup, carers of children may recognize the signs of a recurrence more
quickly and act accordingly.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● In some contexts, treatment has included putting the child in a steamy bath or
encouraging them to inhale the steam. In doing this, there is a risk of burning the child if
hot water is used, so this treatment should only be recommended in contexts where it
can be done safely.
● Words or expressions to describe the hoarse cough that accompanies croup vary in
different countries and regions. Programme designers should base the learning design
on the common words used by local learners.
Learner considerations
● Parents and caregivers of young children will benefit from learning about this topic.
Facilitation tips
● Talk about the different words that may be used to describe the sounds of coughing and
breathing during an episode of croup (e.g. barking cough, hoarse, squeaky, stridor). Create
a glossary to make sure learners understand what these words mean.
● Use audio clips to help learners identify the specific kind of cough that accompanies
croup.
● Many parents have experienced croup, so using storytelling, scenario-based learning and
sharing experiences can be reassuring and useful to learn about this topic (Hartling et al.,
2010; Luckie et al., 2018).
● Emphasize the importance of seeking medical advice if the condition does not subside.
● Learners may like to see a simple diagram of the upper and lower airways to understand
the mechanics of what is happening in the body during an episode of croup.
FIRST AID | 251

● Talk about how learners can create a safe, warm, humidified environment and the
dangers of using water that is too hot.
Facilitation tools
● Some facilitators use the acronym CRY to support education on croup (or learners could
make up their own that they find easy to remember):
○ C: cough and cry in a hoarse voice
○ R: respiratory distress (shortness of breath)
○ Y: young child
Learning connections
● Recognize differences between other conditions like stridor, Choking, sore throat,
Breathing difficulties and Asthma attacks. Use a diagram to show the relationship
between the upper airways, mouth and food pipe.
● Link to the risk of Burns if the method to create humidity uses water that is too hot.
Scientific foundation
Humidified air
While humidified air or steam is a well-known treatment for children with croup, there is not
much evidence to support it. An evidence review from the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice
(CEBaP) contains a systematic review and a randomized controlled trial, and no additional
studies were identified in the 2024 update. The review found that using humidified air did not
clearly improve outcomes for children with croup—such as heart rate, breathing rate, croup
score, hospital admissions or oxygen levels—when compared to no treatment or a placebo.
However, when humidified air was delivered in an optimal way, it did lead to a statistically
significant reduction in heart rate and breathing rate compared to traditional placebo delivery.
The evidence is of low certainty due to the risk of bias and imprecision.
Position
CEBaP identified very low-certainty evidence from one systematic review stating that a
front-lying (prone) position resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the number of
episodes with oxygen saturation levels lower than 80%, oxygenation index (from the 6th to the
12th-hour measurement) and tidal volume, compared to lying on the back (supine). These
results were all obtained in crossover trials.
A statistically significant difference in arterial oxygen saturation, partial pressure of carbon
dioxide, arterial partial pressure of oxygen, respiratory compliance, positive end-expiratory
pressure, respiratory resistance or adverse events between lying on the front (prone position)
and lying on the back could not be demonstrated. Additionally, the evidence is indirect, as most
of the studies were conducted in hospital settings, involved premature infants and included
participants who were intubated during the study.
252 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Hot drinks
CEBaP did not identify any studies on the effect of hot drinks on croup.
FIRST AID | 253

254 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

TRAUMA
FIRST AID | 255

Severe bleeding
Key action
Apply direct pressure to the area to control the bleeding as quickly as possible.
Introduction
Severe external bleeding is a life-threatening condition requiring urgent first aid. The human
body relies on blood circulating around the body to deliver oxygen and glucose to organs and
tissues such as the heart, brain and skin. If a person loses a lot of blood, their circulatory system
may fail and be unable to deliver enough oxygen to their body. This can lead to shock and,
ultimately, death if not addressed. Common causes of life-threatening bleeding include road
traffic collisions, machinery accidents, knife wounds and gunshot injuries. External bleeding
forms the basis of this topic. See also Chest and abdomen injuries and Amputation for
information on treating those types of injuries.
Internal bleeding, such as unseen bleeding into the chest or abdominal cavity, is also
life-threatening. Management of internal bleeding is outlined in Shock, with emphasis on
recognition and positioning.
Guidelines
● First aid providers should use direct manual compression for life-threatening external
bleeding.**
● First aid providers should not use pressure points (indirect manual pressure) or ice to stop
bleeding.**
● If direct manual compression is ineffective or unable to be performed, first aid providers
may use a tourniquet for severe, life-threatening external extremity bleeding.*
● If a tourniquet is used, a manufactured tourniquet is preferred. An improvised tourniquet
may be less effective than a manufactured tourniquet, but may be applied if that is all
that is available for severe, life-threatening external extremity bleeding.*
● First aid providers may use a manufactured windlass tourniquet for the management of
life-threatening extremity bleeding in children.*
● If direct manual compression is ineffective, and a tourniquet is not practical, available or
appropriate, a haemostatic dressing may be used for severe, life-threatening external
bleeding. The haemostatic dressing should be applied directly to the source of bleeding
with direct pressure.*
Good practice points
● Emergency medical services (EMS) should be accessed for all severe bleeding.
● First aid providers should protect themselves from the person’s blood by putting on
gloves or covering their hands (e.g. with plastic bags, bandages or clothing).
● First aid providers (or the injured person, if able) should apply direct manual compression
rather than applying a bandage that wraps around the body part to apply even pressure
256 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

(also called a circumferential pressure dressing) to a severe bleed. If bandages are
available, they can be used to apply pressure. Once severe bleeding has been controlled, a
bandage may be applied to the wound. However, if a bandage is unavailable, clean
materials such as clothes or towels may be used as improvised bandages.
● If the bleeding cannot be stopped, first aid providers should consider:
○ applying consistent, direct pressure with more force
○ applying a tourniquet to an extremity wound and ensuring it is sufficiently
tightened
○ applying a haemostatic dressing on the wound while continuing to apply direct
pressure.
● First aid providers should apply pressure around an embedded object (e.g. a knife) to
stabilize the object. Avoid removing the object.
● Tourniquets should only be used for life-threatening extremity (i.e. limb) bleeding. They
may help save a life, but may have severe consequences (e.g. amputation of the limb),
especially if applied for too long. Once a tourniquet has been applied, keep it in place until
EMS arrive.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Learn how to control bleeding using the resources likely to be available, such as
bandages, clothing or manufactured tourniquets.
Early recognition
● Blood is flowing or spurting from a wound.
● Action to stem the flow of blood should be taken as soon as possible. Even a cupful of
blood, although not immediately life-threatening, can lead to fast deterioration if not
stopped early on.
First aid steps
1. Ask the person to apply direct pressure to their injury with their hands.
2. Help the person to lie down.
3. Access EMS.
4. Apply direct pressure to the bleed. If blood soaks through the dressing, apply a second
dressing over the first one and apply greater pressure.
5. If direct pressure is ineffective and the person is bleeding from an arm or leg, consider
applying a tourniquet or haemostatic dressing, if available. Continue to put direct
pressure on the bleed. In the case that a tourniquet is ineffective, you can add a second
tourniquet above the first.
6. Shock is likely to develop from significant bleeding. Help the person into a comfortable
position and keep them warm by wrapping them in clothing or blankets if necessary.
If the injured person can apply pressure to their own wound, this can reduce the risk of
cross-infection and keep both the first aid provider and the injured person safe.
FIRST AID | 257

If the injured person becomes unresponsive, open their airway and check for breathing after
stopping the bleeding.
SPECIAL CASES
● In situations of disaster or conflict, including war, terror attack or violent attack (such as
shooting or stabbing), the safety and security of the first aid provider and the injured person
are paramount and take precedence over providing immediate care.
