● Paracetamol (also known as acetaminophen) may be effective at relieving some subsets
of acute lower back pain.*
● Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs, such as ibuprofen) may be effective at
relieving acute lower back pain but may cause side effects, including gastric irritation,
potential kidney interactions and high blood pressure.*
● Heat or cold application may provide short-term pain relief and reduce disability in those
with a combination of acute and subacute (pain that continues between four to 12 weeks)
low back pain.*
Good practice points
● The person should sit or lie down in a position most comfortable to them.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Exercise regularly.
● If working at a desk, use an ergonomic chair.
● Learn how to reduce the risks of lower back pain.
● Lift items by bending the knees and keeping the back straight.
● Wear a combination of accessories to protect the back, such as back belts or shoe insoles.
● Change positions regularly and avoid holding a certain position or movement for too
long.
Early recognition
The person was involved in a high-speed, direct impact incident, or may have been using their
back for physical activity such as lifting or stretching. They may experience:
318 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● sudden back pain
● back pain combined with neck pain or a tingling feeling running down the back of one
leg
● sudden inability to walk due to lower back pain.
First aid steps
1. Comfort and reassure the person.
2. Determine the cause of the back pain. If it is the result of a fall or other traumatic incident,
follow the first aid steps for Spinal injury.
3. Help the person move into a comfortable position and take the recommended dose of
painkiller (such as paracetamol or ibuprofen).
4. Apply a heat source (e.g. heating pad) or cold source to the lower back.
Access help
● Access emergency medical care if the acute lower back pain is severe or the result of a
traumatic incident (e.g. a fall or car accident).
● The person should seek medical advice if the back pain:
○ is related to an incident that occurred some time ago (e.g. car accident), or they
have been experiencing it for four weeks or more
○ spreads to one leg, especially if the leg feels weak
○ is combined with weak legs, sensory disturbances, or the person experiences
bladder or bowel problems, or issues with sexual functionality
○ is in combination with a fever.
The person should seek medical advice if they have a history of cancer, a weakened immune
system, osteoporosis or have used steroids (e.g. prednisolone) for a long time.
Recovery
● If the back pain does not improve within four weeks, seek medical advice.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Learners may have different beliefs about what lower back pain is and what causes it.
Facilitate a discussion to better understand these beliefs.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Emphasize that lower back pain has many different causes (some are common and mild,
while others can be serious). Helping the person get medical advice is an important first
aid action so they can learn how to manage their pain.
● Use scenario-based learning (e.g. a road traffic collision) to practise recognizing and
managing a spinal injury versus back pain.
● Facilitators can also make connections to lifting techniques used to reduce the risk of
back pain:
a. Hold the load firmly.
b. Keep the load close to you.
FIRST AID | 319

c. Lift the load by pushing up with your legs.
d. Ensure your legs are stable before moving.
Learning connections
Connect this topic to head injury and spinal injury.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Two evidence summaries from the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP), which were
updated in 2024, and three published systematic reviews were used for this topic.
Prevention
The CEBaP evidence summary looked at the prevention of lower back pain, comparing the
results of using specific lifting techniques to lift a heavy weight, other lifting strategies or no
technique at all. The evidence summary included three randomized controlled trials and three
observational studies. It was shown that there is a statistically significant association between an
increased risk of developing persistent low back pain or symptoms of a herniated lumbar disk
and frequent lifting of any load mass with knees straight and the back bent forward. There were
no new studies identified in the 2024 update.
There is also limited evidence supporting the benefits of lifting with knees bent and back
straight. It was shown there is a statistically significant association between a reduced risk of
developing symptoms of a herniated lumbar disk and lifting with knees bent and back straight.
A statistically significant association between a decreased risk of developing chronic low back
pain and occasionally and frequently lifting any load mass with an upright back could not be
demonstrated. Statistically significant associations between back pain and lifting posture could
not be demonstrated for non-chronic low back pain.
In addition, there is limited evidence showing no correlation between arm extension or twisting
while lifting and the symptoms of a herniated lumbar disk. A statistically significant association
between a decreased risk of developing symptoms of a herniated lumbar disk and arm
extension or twisting while lifting could not be demonstrated.
Finally, the evidence is limited to support either the effectiveness of a single training session
programme for low back health or not following the programme. A statistically significant
decrease in back pain, using single training sessions that include lifting advice and advice for the
prevention of back pain, compared to no training session, could not be demonstrated.
Evidence is of low to very low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to the low
number of events, limited sample size, lack of data and/or large variability of results.
One systematic review indicated that exercise, together with education, is likely to reduce the
risk of lower back pain. Physical activity alone might reduce the risk of a lower back pain episode
and subsequent sick leave, at least in the short term. The available evidence suggested that
back belts, shoe insoles, ergonomic equipment or education alone do not prevent lower back
pain. Additionally, due to the very low certainty of the evidence, it is unclear whether education,
320 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

training or ergonomic adjustments prevent sick leave caused by lower back pain (Steffens et al.,
2016).
First aid
Two systematic reviews examined evidence on over-the-counter medications like paracetamol
and ibuprofen.
One review looked at the evidence from 65 trials and suggested that NSAIDs effectively provide
short-term relief in people with acute and chronic lower back pain without sciatica. However, the
effect sizes are small. The evidence did not recommend a specific type of NSAID as being more
effective than the others. In the randomized controlled trials, the selective COX-2 inhibitors
showed fewer side effects compared to traditional NSAIDs. Still, recent studies have shown that
COX-2 inhibitors are associated with increased cardiovascular risks in specific populations
(Roelofs et al., 2008).
The second review shows that there is high-certainty evidence that paracetamol (4g per day) is
no better at improving acute lower back pain in the short or long term, compared to a placebo.
Additionally, paracetamol was no better than a placebo on other aspects, such as quality of life
and sleep quality. On average, one in five people recounted side effects with no difference
between the intervention and control groups. As most of the participants studied were
middle-aged, it is unclear if the findings are the same for other age groups (Saragiotto et al.,
2016).
An evidence summary from CEBaP searched for studies on heat or cold application in the case
of back pain, and identified three new studies in 2024, resulting in 10 randomized controlled
trials. Heat treatments included hot water bottles, bags with hot sand or hot salt, heat wraps or a
heating blanket. Cold treatments included ice wraps. The studies demonstrated that heat
application, whether in addition to education, exercises, analgesics or the use of naproxen cream
or not, resulted in a statistically significant increase in pain relief and function. In addition, it was
shown that cold combined with naproxen cream resulted in a statistically significant decrease in
pain eight and 15 days after intervention compared to naproxen cream only.
Evidence is of low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to limited sample size
and/or large variability of results.
Non-systematic reviews
A review of clinical practice guidelines identified several effective, conservative (non-invasive)
actions to manage acute and chronic lower back pain. Most high-quality guidelines recommend
education, exercise, manual therapy and paracetamol, or NSAIDs as first-line treatments for
lower back pain. However, the use of paracetamol has been challenged by recent evidence and
this recommendation needs to be updated (Wong et al., 2016).
Another review identified clinical factors to recognize the conditions of acute lower back pain
and sciatica. People with acute back pain experience improvements in the pain, varying levels of
disability and usually return to work within one month. Further (but smaller) developments
occur up to three months, after which the pain and disability levels remain almost constant. Low
FIRST AID | 321

levels of pain and disability last from three to at least 12 months. Most people will have at least
one recurrence within those 12 months (Pengel et al., 2003).
322 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Mammal bites
Key action
Clean the bite wound by rinsing it with clean water as quickly as possible to minimize the risk of
infection.
Introduction
Bites and scratches from mammals, such as dogs, cats, monkeys, camels, bats and humans, are
common and can cause infections. Most animal bites in adults and children are from dogs, and
young children are most likely to be bitten by an animal they know. Adults are more likely to be
bitten on the hand or wrist, while children more often receive bites on the face or neck (Elcock et
al., 2022; Ortiz & Lezcano, 2023). There are 4.5 million people bitten by dogs every year, with
885,000 seeking medical care, and cat bites make up to 50%of animal-bite injuries across the
world (World Health Organization, 2018). Bacteria can cause skin infections or complications to
the whole body, such as sepsis or tetanus. Depending on the local context, first aid providers
may also need to consider the risk of rabies.
Good practice points
● People should avoid approaching stray or wild animals and take extra care to keep from
getting bitten.
● Bleeding from an animal bite should be treated as per the guidelines for severe bleeding.
● Depending on availability, the first aid provider should clean the wound with either
potable (clean) water, saline or soap and water. Ideally, human and animal bite wounds
should be cleaned with potable water and soap to minimize the risk of bacterial and
rabies infections.
● The person should seek further medical care as soon as possible if vaccination is required
or if the wound is open or deep.
● If available, a water source should be used to clean the bite area (e.g. from a tap, squirting
device or water bottle).
● If warmth or pain develops around the bite area, this is an indication of infection and the
person should seek medical care immediately. The bite area should be monitored as
infection can happen in the hours or days after the bite occurs.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Avoid approaching stray or wild animals.
● If there is a prevalence of rabies in the area, people should follow the advice of local health
authorities.
● Be aware of specific diseases transmitted by animals in your area (rabies, anthrax,
hantavirus, bovine encephalopathy, etc.).
● Always supervise babies and children around animals.
FIRST AID | 323

● Teach children how to behave safely around animals. If in a new environment, ask local
people for advice on how to behave around the animals located there. Animals often bite
as a reaction to human behaviour they perceive as threatening.
Early recognition
● The person tells you they were bitten by an animal or human.
● The person has visible teeth marks on their skin.
● Crush injuries or skin, muscle or soft tissue that has been ripped open may indicate dog
bites, while deeper puncture wounds usually indicate cat bites.
First aid steps
1. If the wound is bleeding, apply pressure to the wound to stop the bleeding. Follow the
steps for severe bleeding. Once the bleeding has stopped, follow the first aid steps for
cuts and grazes.
Animal bites can transmit a number of diseases, such as Bartonellosis (transmitted by cats) or
Herpes B (transmitted by monkeys).
Access help
● Seek further medical care as soon as possible if skin closure is required.
● If the injured person has not been vaccinated against tetanus, seek further medical care.
● If the bite came from a wild animal, rabies is common in the area and the injured person
has not been vaccinated against it, seek further medical care.
● Monitor for signs of infection. If warmth or pain develops around the bite area, seek
medical advice.
Recovery
● Change the dressing on the bite area as necessary.
● Seek psychological support if necessary.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● In areas where animal bites are common, include this topic to raise awareness. Learners
should know the preventive actions, potential effects of and treatment for animal bites.
Emphasize caution when in areas with stray or wild animals.
● When relevant, show the signs of a rabid dog and how to behave in the presence of one.
● Depending on the availability of resources, the first aid provider should clean the wound
with either potable water, saline or soap and water.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Practise how to clean the wound as this may increase learners’ confidence to do it
themselves.
● Emphasize the importance of long-term wound care to prevent infection.
● Online or printed visuals of bite wounds, including ones that are and are not infected,
may help learners to identify this type of wound in the future.
324 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Suggest local psychological support resources to help people affected by bite incidents
involving aggressive animals or humans.
● Educational materials should reflect discussions with local public health authorities
regarding the need for vaccinations.
Scientific foundation
Systematic review
The Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) performed four evidence summaries in 2019.
One summary on dog bites was declared stable and the other three were updated in 2024, but
no new studies were identified. No relevant studies on the use of wound irrigation for human or
cat bites could be identified. For the use of wound irrigation for dog bites, one retrospective
cohort study showed limited evidence in favour of wound irrigation. It demonstrated that
irrigation of a dog bite with saline resulted in a significant decrease in wound infection,
compared to no irrigation. Evidence is of very low certainty (downgraded for risk of bias and
indirectness), and results cannot be considered precise due to the low number of events.
Non-systematic review
Potable (clean) water, saline and soap and water solutions are irrigants that benefit an animal
bite. There are no direct comparisons between these different interventions. The literature
reviewed in the section on cuts and grazes also supports irrigation as a method to prevent
infection.
Despite multiple recommendations in literature reviews and common clinical practices, there is
no evidence for the use of povidone-iodine to treat an animal bite.
FIRST AID | 325

Insect bites or stings
Key action
Remove the stinger or insect from the person to prevent the further spread of venom or disease.
Introduction
Insect bites and stings can transmit diseases (such as malaria, encephalitis or Lyme disease) and
can cause wounds that can become infected. Certain insect bites and stings can also cause an
allergic reaction in individuals who have been bitten or stung before.
Guidelines
● A bee’s stinger should be removed as soon as possible.*
● A tick may be removed with tweezers or by using a commercial tick removal device,
according to the manufacturer’s instructions.*
● The following tick-removal methods must be avoided: using gasoline, petroleum or other
solvents to suffocate the tick, using ice and burning the tick with a match.*
Good practice points
● If no commercial tick removal device is available, the first aid provider may grab a tick as
close to the skin as possible with very fine forceps or tweezers and pull it gradually and
firmly out of the skin.
● To remove a stinger from a person, the first aid provider may gently scrape the sting area
with a flat object, such as a bank card or butter knife. Using tweezers or any other object
that can pierce or press down on the venom bag should be avoided, as this may
aggravate the symptoms.
● The bitten or stung area should be thoroughly cleaned with potable (clean) water and
then an antiseptic solution. If no clean water is available, use an antiseptic solution.
● In the case of a bite or sting to the throat or mouth that starts to swell, the first aid
provider should immediately access emergency medical services (EMS) and apply ice to
the affected area to reduce the swelling.
● First aid providers should monitor the person for signs of an allergic reaction or
anaphylaxis and provide the appropriate care.
● The person should avoid scratching the bite as this can cause an infection, especially if the
fingernails are dirty.
● The skin may become redder, purple or darker, as well as warm and painful at the site of
an insect bite or sting. This should improve over a day or two. However, if it gets worse, the
person should seek medical advice, as this could be a sign of infection. Antibiotics or
vaccinations may be needed.
● Travellers should check to see what insect-related risks are prevalent in the area to which
they are travelling and seek medical advice. They may need vaccinations or medication
before leaving home.
326 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● To prevent diseases caused by insects, such as malaria and Lyme disease, use insect
repellent and mosquito nets, and promptly remove any ticks.
● Avoid tick-infested areas and wear long clothing in a "downward cascade" configuration.
● Consult local authorities for information on landscaping and pest management to
support any locally recommended behaviour or places to avoid to reduce risk.
● In some countries, per local regulations, report any tick bites to the authorities. Some
authorities request that you send the biting tick or ticks for identification and testing.
● Consider using anti-malarial medication when travelling. Consult a medical professional
for the recommended medication.
In first aid education, explore the following information with learners on avoiding bites and the
behaviours to adopt during an insect attack:
● Keep away from flowering plants, ripe fruit bushes and trees, rotten fruit, compost and
food waste. If this is not possible, wear long trousers and long-sleeved clothes and cover
your hands and face.
● Wear shoes when walking outside, particularly on grass or flowering ground.
● When outside, cover your drinks and food and check them before drinking or eating.
● Wipe off any food from the clothes, hands and faces of children, as this may attract bees
or wasps.
● Before moving objects with your hands (such as containers that have been left outside),
check underneath and inside them for insects that may bite or sting.
● Shake out shoes, socks and clothing before putting them on to remove any insects.
● Do not touch or disturb bee or wasp hives. If you want to harvest honey, protect yourself
by wearing long trousers and long-sleeved clothes and cover your hands and face as
much as possible.
● Remain calm if attacked by a bee or wasp, as they react to movement. Do not wave your
hands in an attempt to brush them away.
● Run and find shelter if attacked by a swarm.
Early recognition
A tick bite is not painful and is often not detected, or only discovered by accident. Initially, many
ticks are only a pinhead in size, making it difficult to spot. An engorged tick is about the size of a
small pea. After a few hours, the bite may start to itch.
A person who is bitten or stung by an insect may experience:
● initial sharp pain
● swelling, itching or a rash at the site of the bite or sting.
The person may develop a mild or severe allergic reaction following an insect bite or sting.
FIRST AID | 327

Observe the person closely for any development of an allergic reaction. A rash can be more
difficult to see on black or brown skin (Nolen, 2020). Checking lighter areas of skin, such as the
palms of hands or soles of feet, may help you to see it.
First aid steps
Tick bite
1. Reassure the person and remove the tick as quickly as you can. Use a manufactured tick
removal device to remove the tick according to the manufacturer’s instructions. If a
manufactured device is not available, use tweezers to grab the tick as close to the skin as
possible and pull it gradually and firmly out of the skin.
2. Wash the site of the bite with soap and water. See cuts and grazes.
3. Write down the date and the area where the person was bitten in order to monitor the
area around the bite.
4. Seek medical advice if there is concern about an infection (fever, inexplicable fatigue or
joint pain) or if a rash appears. The Lyme disease rash is a circular rash that appears as a
patch surrounded by a ring. However, this "classic bull's-eye" rash does not always appear.
CAUTION
● Do not squeeze the body of the insect, as this may trigger the release of the
disease-causing organisms onto the skin.
● Do not use chemicals or heat to numb or kill the tick.
● Note that insect bites are treated differently from snakebites.
Other insect bite or sting
1. Reassure the person and advise them not to scratch, as this may aggravate the injury.
2. If the stinger is still in the person, remove it as quickly as possible. Gently scrape the area
with a flat object, such as a bank card or the blunt side of a knife.
3. Thoroughly clean the site of the bite or sting with water. If no clean water is available, use
an antiseptic solution.
4. Cool the stung area with ice cubes in a bag of water or a cooling bag to limit swelling,
itching and pain. If the sting is on the hand, remove the rings.
CAUTION
When removing the stinger, avoid using tweezers, your fingers or any other object that can
pierce or press down on the venom bag, as this will make symptoms worse.
Access help
● Access EMS immediately if:
○ The person is bitten or stung in the mouth or on the throat. It can be dangerous as
any swelling could block their airway. While waiting for medical care, try to cool the
328 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

injured area with ice (e.g. by letting the person suck on an ice cube) to reduce
swelling.
○ The person shows any signs of a severe allergic reaction.
● Access medical care if:
○ The stinger will not come out.
○ The person develops a painful, itchy swelling or a rash.
○ The person feels progressively worse after being bitten or stung (this can occur up
to 24 hours after the bite or sting).
○ The person was bitten or stung many times.
○ The person suspects he is not sufficiently protected from tetanus.
Recovery
● Monitor for signs of infection. The skin may become red, purple or darker, as well as warm
and painful at the site of an insect bite or sting. This should improve over the course of a
day or two. However, if it gets progressively worse, the person should seek medical advice,
as this could be a sign of infection.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Programme designers should consult local medical centres to find out which
insect-related diseases are common in the area and what preventive measures to take.
These measures may include:
○ using repellent or a bed net
○ taking medication for malaria prevention
○ getting vaccinated for tick-borne encephalitis
○ wearing long sleeves and trousers (especially at dawn when insects are active).
● In Europe and North America, several species of Ixodes ticks are carriers of tick-borne
infections. One major infection is Lyme disease. Typical symptoms include fever,
headache, fatigue and a specific skin rash that is circular and appears as a ringed patch
on different parts of the body. However, the rash does not always appear. If left untreated,
the infection can spread to the joints, heart and nervous system. Most cases of Lyme
disease can be treated successfully with antibiotics. Encourage learners to familiarize
themselves with their local environment and to consult a health professional if they need
prophylactic antibiotics.
● In sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, malaria is transmitted by the bite of a mosquito. A
person with malaria is usually very sick with symptoms such as high temperature,
shivering and shaking, and flu-like illness. Many effective medicines are available to
prevent and treat malaria. Health professionals can advise you on how to take the
medicine that is best for you.
Learner considerations
● In regions where Lyme disease is prevalent, all people are at equal risk of contracting it,
though the visibility of skin rashes will vary according to skin colour (Fix et al., 2000). First
FIRST AID | 329

aid education should highlight where to find information on local tick-borne illnesses.
Education should also discuss rash recognition across all skin colours and describe other
symptoms that may be present, such as a yellowish, purple skin rash or a blush spot in
persons with dark skin. Programme designers should actively look for opportunities to
increase awareness of this topic in groups that are more likely to include learners who are
dark skinned.
● However, not all rashes are a sign of Lyme disease. The redness on the skin could be
caused by irritation from the tick bite, not a tick-borne infection.
● Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBE virus) can be transmitted days to weeks after the bite.
Symptoms typically appear shortly after a tick bite and include flu-like symptoms, such as
fever, headache and gastrointestinal issues.
● While nearly half the world faces the risk of malaria, children under five years old are the
most vulnerable group affected by it. Pregnant women, those with HIV or AIDS and
mobile populations (travellers) are also particularly at risk (World Health Organization,
2020a).
● Learners who are aware of family members, friends or colleagues who are allergic to
insect bites and stings should familiarize themselves with what support that person
might need.
Facilitation tips
● Ask learners to share their experiences of being bitten or stung, including how it feels and
how they responded, to help develop the group’s confidence in handling this type of
injury.
● Demonstrate how to use a manufactured tick removal device or tweezers to effectively
remove a tick and a bank card or the blunt side of a knife to remove a sting.
Facilitation tools
● Share photos of local insects and their associated injuries or infections to help learners
identify them.
● Bring a variety of tick removal tools to show learners.
Learning connections
● Ensure learners are comfortable monitoring a person for signs of an Allergic reaction and
anaphylaxis.
● Link this topic to the removal of stingers of marine and freshwater animals.
● Give time to practise giving emotional support and comfort to the injured person as a
bite or sting can be very distressing.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
The Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) conducted several evidence summaries on this
topic, and the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) updated a CEBaP
330 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

systematic review (Huygelen et al., 2017) in 2021 (Charlton et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff
et al., 2022a).
Ticks
Forceps
There is limited evidence from an observational study in favour of pulling with tweezers
compared to removal by hand. This study found a statistically significant decrease in ticks with
damaged mouthparts or mouthparts that broke off when pulling with tweezers compared to
manual removal. In a second study, a statistically significant decrease in ticks with damaged
mouthparts or mouthparts that broke off when pulling with forceps compared to twisting with
forceps could not be demonstrated. The evidence is of very low certainty and the results of these
studies are imprecise due to limited sample size, the low number of events and large variability
of results.
A randomized study with very low certainty evidence was identified that compared three
different types of forceps (angled forceps, jewellers’ forceps, economy forceps), and there was no
difference in the number of ticks with damaged mouth parts upon removal by angled or
jewellers’ forceps compared to economy forceps. ILCOR suggests pulling with tweezers as a
method to remove ticks (Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2022a).
Commercial tick removal devices
Six studies, including three randomized controlled trials and three non-randomized controlled
trials, compared commercial devices to pulling straight with tweezers, but results were mixed
and were either in favour of tweezers, in favour of the commercial devices or showed no
difference. Two randomized controlled trials compared different commercial devices to remove
ticks and showed that a slit and rotation device and pen-tweezers (opposing jaws and rotation)
resulted in less damage to the mouth parts than other devices (e.g. pulling with a card, twisting
with a lasso, or a slit and traction device). The evidence is of low to very low certainty.
ILCOR suggests using commercial devices according to the manufacturer’s instructions to
remove ticks (Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al, 2022a).
Chemical treatments or heat, followed by mechanical removal
When comparing different methods for removing ticks, two observational animal studies
compared chemical or heat treatments before mechanically removing ticks versus mechanical
removal alone. The results showed that applying substances like gasoline, nail polish or alcohol
did not make ticks detach from the animals.
ILCOR recommends against the use of chemicals or heat to remove ticks (Wyckoff et al., 2021;
Wyckoff et al., 2022a).
Freezing
One observational study with low certainty evidence compared the freezing of a tick with a
commercial freezing device against pulling straight with tweezers or pulling with commercial
FIRST AID | 331

devices. Removing the ticks was statistically significantly better with tweezers and the
commercial devices than with the freezing device, which could not remove a single tick.
ILCOR recommends against the use of ice to remove ticks (Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al.,
2022a).
Bee stings
With regards to bee stings, most people who get stung develop a raised, inflamed mark known
as a weal. The CEBaP evidence summary revealed limited evidence from one randomized
controlled trial in favour of quick removal of a bee sting. The trial showed a statistically significant
decrease in the weal area after 10 minutes with a quick time of removal, compared to no quick
removal. A second summary, including the same study, compared scraping with pinching to
remove bee stings, but a statistically significant decrease in weal area using either method could
not be demonstrated. The evidence is of low certainty and the results of this study are imprecise
due to limited sample size and lack of data. The 2024 update of the evidence summaries did not
identify any new studies.
Insect bites and stings, suction or household treatments
No studies were found about cooling with ice or using suction for an insect bite or sting.
Regarding household treatments (vinegar, baking soda, sugar, etc.), there is limited evidence
from one non-randomized controlled trial, neither in favour of sugar nor a placebo bandage. A
statistically significant increase or decrease of pain, swelling or itching using a sugar bandage
compared to a placebo bandage could not be demonstrated. The evidence is of low certainty
and results of this study are imprecise due to the limited sample size and lack of data.
Non-systematic reviews
Removing ticks as soon as possible reduces the infection risks of viruses they may carry, such as
the virus that causes Lyme disease. Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBE virus), however, is
transmitted shortly after the bite. So, quickly removing the tick will not always prevent infection.
TBE is an inflammation of the brain or meninges. Symptoms appear shortly after a tick bite with
individuals presenting flu-like symptoms of fever, headache and gastrointestinal complaints
(which are all uncommon in Lyme disease). Since its incidence in Europe is increasing due to the
broadening of endemic areas and longer tick activity season, the health burden of TBE is
increasing. There is no effective antiviral treatment for TBE, but the disease may be effectively
prevented by vaccination (Eikeland et al., 2024; Taba et al., 2017).
Preventing tick bites to avoid complications includes avoiding tick-infested areas, applying
diethyltoluamide (DEET) to the skin as directed on the product label, applying permethrin to
clothing and wearing clothing in a "downward cascade" configuration. For example, wearing
trousers that cover boot tops ensures that when ticks fall off, they do not lodge in boots (Benzoni
& Cooper, 2025).
332 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Education review
Commentary on medical education’s bias to use white skin as the standard for the recognition
of illness has identified a similar bias in first aid education, particularly regarding this topic.
Nolen (2020) referred to a paper on the recognition of Lyme disease by Fix et al. (2000) that has
informed our commentary for this section. The paper concluded that skin rashes caused by
Lyme disease are under-recognized in African American populations (and by extension, people
of colour). The article suggested a variety of reasons including:
● the rash is less visible
● less accessible care may make African Americans less likely to seek medical treatment
● healthcare professionals might be biased in their diagnosis, assuming African Americans
are less likely to be infected with Lyme disease.
FIRST AID | 333

Aquatic animal injuries
Key action
Rinse the area with seawater to reduce pain and remove any stinging cells from the skin.
Introduction
Aquatic animals include those found in both salt and freshwater. Most are harmless; however,
some jellyfish and venomous fish can produce stings that are painful and cause minor or severe
allergic reactions. Stinging injuries are a common occurrence on many beaches worldwide and
are problematic for people whose livelihoods depend on activities in which they can be stung.
For bite injuries from a non-venomous aquatic animal, see cuts and grazes or severe bleeding.
Guidelines
● Rinse the area that was stung with seawater.**
● For non-life-threatening jellyfish envenomation, either rinsing with, showering or placing
the area that was stung in heated water (40-45° C, 104-113° F) may relieve the pain.*
Good practice points
● First aid providers should protect themselves from being stung when removing any
tentacles or stinging cells from the skin.
● First aiders should remove all stinging cells from a jellyfish from the skin. They may cover
the stinging cells with sand or shaving foam and then scrape the cells off with cardboard
or a bank card.
● To remove visible jellyfish tentacles, use tweezers.
● The first aid provider may place the injury in heated water (up to a maximum of
45°C/113°F) for at least 20 to 30 minutes or apply a heat pack.
● If a heat pack or hot water are not available, it is reasonable to apply a cold pack to relieve
the pain.
● The choice of an agent applied to a jellyfish injury should be specific to the jellyfish.
○ Salt water may relieve pain from some jellyfish stings.
○ Vinegar may relieve pain for some jellyfish stings (e.g. box jellyfish).
● Avoid using aluminium sulphate, meat tenderizer or cold fresh water for pain relief.
● First aid providers should discourage the person from rubbing the site of the injury.
● In areas with deadly aquatic animals, when a person has been bitten or stung, medical
care should be accessed immediately. This is also the case if the person experiences any
signs of a severe allergic reaction. First aid providers should assess the person’s airway,
breathing and circulation while providing care for any other symptoms caused by the
injury.
● There is a risk of tetanus from an aquatic animal injury, so tetanus vaccinations might be
suitable as a preventive measure.
● The injury should be monitored as infection can happen in the hours or days after the bite
occurs.
334 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● If warmth or pain develop around the site of the injury after the initial first aid treatment,
this is an indication of infection and the person should seek medical advice immediately.
If injured by a sea urchin or sea star, the first aid provider should remove all foreign
material. Clean the injury with soap and fresh water. Seek medical attention if a spine has
punctured the person’s joint.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Injuries from venomous marine and freshwater animals can be related to livelihoods and
economic activity in addition to tourism and leisure, so target preventive measures and
education at local people at risk as well as visitors.
● For people at risk, protective clothing should be worn.
● Follow local guidelines about the risks of entering the water.
● Even if dead, a jellyfish can still sting due to the small capsules in the stinging cells of their
tentacles (nemotocysts).
● Be aware when near stingrays. A stingray injury is caused by the venomous tail spines and
depending on its size, usually stings people in the lower limb region.
Early recognition
The person has pain associated with puncture wounds from the stinging cells. You may be able
to see stinging cells attached to the skin.
For a stingray injury, symptoms may include extreme pain, muscle cramps and a laceration at
the site of the injury.
The person may develop a mild or severe allergic reaction following a sting. See Allergic reaction
and anaphylaxis. Observe the person closely for any development of an allergic reaction. A rash
can be more difficult to see on black or brown skin (Nolen, 2020). Checking lighter areas of skin,
such as the palms of hands or soles of feet, may help you to see it.
First aid steps
1. Remove all the stinging cells from the person’s skin. Protect yourself from being stung as
you do this.
2. Rinse the area of the sting with seawater.
3. If possible, apply heat such as warm water, a heat pack or ash (up to a maximum of
45°C/113°F) to the site of the injury for 20-30 minutes. You could also immerse the injury in
the water. The temperature of the water is hot enough to relieve pain, but should not
burn the skin or cause pain to other uninjured areas of the skin.
4. Advise the person to seek medical care if there is a risk of tetanus or persistent pain that is
not relieved by treatment.
FIRST AID | 335

Access help
● If the sting injury is from a deadly species of aquatic animal, or if there is any doubt,
access emergency medical services (EMS) immediately.
● If the person experiences a severe allergic reaction, access help immediately.
Recovery
Monitor for signs of infection. The skin may become red, purple or darker, as well as warm and
painful at the site of the sting. This should improve over the course of a day or two. However, if it
gets progressively worse, the person should seek medical advice as this could be a sign of
infection.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Consult local aquatic institutions and water rescue associations to identify the most
common and dangerous aquatic animals in the region. Include the first aid response for
these species only in your programmes.
● Emphasize using seawater to rinse the injury and remove any remaining tentacles and
stinging cells. Seawater should be available on site and requires no additional cost.
● If local authorities recommend applying medication to aquatic injuries (such as steroid
ointments or lidocaine gels), ensure that this action is in line with the local legislation
concerning first aid providers.
● In settings where groups at risk of aquatic animal injuries are defined by employment or
livelihoods, provide targeted education programmes on this topic using facilitators from
the affected population (de Aquino et al., 2016).
● Work with local aquatic institutions and authorities to ensure public notices are displayed
to alert people to the dangers of being stung by an aquatic animal and the appropriate
first aid.
● Aquatic animals tend to have localized names; be aware of what is in the area.
Learner considerations
● Adapt education to the literacy level of the learner group, being aware that illiteracy
might be a factor for some at-risk groups (de Aquino et al., 2016).
● Visitors to areas where there is a risk of being stung might not be informed about the
risks. Education in the form of public posters could be helpful. Where possible, these
should include clear illustrations and an emergency number to call for help, to avoid
language barriers.
Facilitation tips
● Emphasize preventive measures, such as identifying warning signs at the beach or
wearing protective clothing and workwear when doing aquatic activities.
● If possible, facilitators should be familiar with the aquatic environment about which they
are educating as this will allow them to add local knowledge about tides or dangerous
336 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

places to swim, as well as have local knowledge about how best to get help when
necessary (de Aquino et al., 2016).
● When educating people in locations where incidences of injury are high, identify and
share knowledge on any variations in times or locations where the risk from a particular
species is greater (e.g. if injuries occur more frequently at certain times of day or night, or
are more common in shallow or deep water).
Facilitation tools
● Share photos of venomous aquatic animals that are prevalent locally and provide basic
information such as where they can be found.
● Share photos of wounds with stinging cells or sting fragments to help learners identify
what these look like.
● Ensure learners know to use hot water at the optimum temperature range (up to a
maximum of 45°C/113°F) and what this feels like. Encourage learners to reflect on how
they would access hot water rapidly when in a typical aquatic environment, such as a
beach or lake.
● Demonstrate the use of a bank card or similar flat object for stinging cell removal.
Learning connections
● Reference could be made to similarities with insect sting removal.
● Highlight the risks of burning the skin if the water temperature is too high.
● Some people with a sting may experience an allergic reaction or anaphylaxis.
Scientific foundation
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) co-authored a Cochrane
systematic review on interventions for treating jellyfish stings, which only included randomized
controlled trials (McGee et al., 2023). Additionally, ILCOR conducted an additional systematic
review that included non-randomized research (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b). Finally,
the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) updated the evidence summary about the use
of pressure immobilization for jellyfish stings.
The education review identified evidence concerning freshwater animal injuries; however, a
scientific review of freshwater animal injuries has not been undertaken.
Systematic review
Jellyfish: Topical agents
The evidence from the Cochrane systematic review did not find a significant difference in
outcomes between different topical treatments (i.e. application of seawater, freshwater,
Sting-Aid®, Adolph's meat tenderizer, isopropyl alcohol, heated water, acetic acid, lidocaine or
sodium bicarbonate). In the additional systematic review, five non-randomized studies were
identified showing that topical lidocaine and seawater provided pain relief. However, using
acetic acid, ethanol or isopropanol resulted in increased pain compared to using seawater.
ILCOR has given a strong recommendation to rinse the area of a jellyfish sting with seawater
FIRST AID | 337

and a weak recommendation against the use of topical ammonia, isopropanol or ethanol (Djärv
et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
Jellyfish: Hot water
The Cochrane systematic review included four (quasi-) randomized controlled trials that
compared heat application (hot water, hot pack) to cold application for jellyfish stings. It was
shown that heat application may reduce pain compared to cold application; however, findings
depended on the jellyfish species. In the additional ILCOR systematic review, one additional
non-randomized study was identified, showing the benefit of heat therapy compared to ice pack
therapy. Based on the evidence, ILCOR suggests the use of heated water (40-45° C) (immersion,
irrigation or shower) or hot pack application compared with application of cold packs, topical
lidocaine, benzocaine, vinegar/5% acetic acid, papain, bromalin, aluminum sulfate or sodium
bicarbonate to relieve pain from a jellyfish sting in the case of non-life threatening jellyfish
envenomation (weak recommendation, very low certainty evidence) (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et
al., 2025b).
Jellyfish: Pressure immobilization
CEBaP could not identify any human studies about the use of pressure immobilization for
jellyfish stings.
Non-systematic reviews
Jellyfish: Providing comfort
Usually, jellyfish stings receive no treatment other than being washed with water and having the
stingers removed. This is because in most cases, itching caused by the sting does not last longer
than a couple of hours and the person does not need further assistance. In this situation, the
person affected does not need further aid. So, whatever first aiders use to treat the signs and
symptoms of jellyfish stings, they will be providing comfort (D’aes et al., 2024).
Jellyfish: Hot water, seawater
ILCOR remarks that, while hot water appears to demonstrate a benefit compared with other
treatments, access to hot water may not be feasible in many parts of the world. Hot water can
also cause skin burns if the temperature is too high. In some locations, solar-heated water bags
and instant hot packs are available at beach lifeguard stations for treating jellyfish stings.
Seawater is generally available at the site of envenomation and requires no additional cost.
While hot seawater is preferred for washing the area and removing any remaining tentacles or
stinging cells (nematocysts), it is recognized that only hot fresh water may be available. However,
fresh water may activate the stinging cells remaining on the skin; therefore, seawater should be
used before applying fresh water when possible (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
Jellyfish - Cold application
According to a narrative review by Niznik et al. (2024), the use of iced seawater or cold packs (as
opposed to fresh water) may exhibit a strong analgesic effect by reducing the spread of venom,
thereby functioning as a pain reliever (Niżnik et al., 2024).
338 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Jellyfish - Vinegar
Vinegar can prevent remaining nematocysts on the skin from discharging. The use of vinegar as
a method to prevent further nematocyst discharge has only been studied in in vitro studies or
animal studies. It was shown that rinsing the jellyfish sting with vinegar (5%) for 30-60 seconds is
effective in case of stings from the Australian box jellyfish (Prestwich & Jenner, 2007). However,
the effects of vinegar after stings from Physalia species (Portuguese Man O' War, bluebottle
jellyfish) are inconsistent and contradictory (Wilcox et al., 2017).
Other aquatic animals
Sea urchins and sea stars have sharp spines that easily penetrate the skin, wetsuits and shoes
and are brittle enough to quickly break off once embedded. Thoroughly wash the area with soap
and fresh water (tap water is acceptable). Remove any foreign material. Seek medical attention if
spines have entered joints. Monitor for signs of infection (Niżnik et al., 2024).
After a sting incident by the Lesser Weever (Echiichthys vipera) or the Greater Weever (Trachinus
draco), extreme pain occurs under the foot, lasting for 20-50 minutes. Hot-water immersion is
considered the most effective treatment (Niżnik et al., 2024). Without treatment, the pain can
persist for approximately 24 hours.
Education review
One study was identified that informs the education considerations for this topic. De Aquino et
al. (2016) identified a high proportion (at least 30%) of the target group that experienced injuries
from a specific type of venomous freshwater fish due to their livelihoods. Their injuries resulted in
a high frequency of trauma and venomous toxins, leading to systemic complications. The study
identified a high level of inappropriate treatment being applied. Combined, this had a direct
impact on the ability of the injured people to work and emphasized the importance of
education. The target group had a high degree of illiteracy and limited availability for extensive
classroom-based learning. The study also highlighted the importance of facilitators who
understand the local context (as opposed to facilitators from “outside”). The finding that
secondary infections and complications related to a lack of knowledge of effective treatment
demonstrate the importance of tailoring educational content to specific target groups. It also
places equal emphasis on prevention and treatment during first aid education, a principle that
can be applied more broadly to this topic.
FIRST AID | 339

Snakebites
Key action
Help the person keep as still as possible to slow the spread of the venom.
Introduction
Most snakes are harmless to humans, but some are venomous. A venomous snake injects
venom through its fangs, and the bite can be life-threatening. Identifying the type of snake will
help medical professionals determine whether the snake is venomous, the type of venom
produced and the best way to help the person who has been bitten.
Guidelines
● Limb injuries can be kept still as much as possible to slow the spread of venom. For a bite
to the arm, use a sling. For a bite to a lower limb, advise the person to limit any
movement, including walking.*
● If they are properly trained to do so, first aid providers may use the pressure
immobilization technique, by firmly applying a cotton or rubber pad under a non-elastic
bandage for special situations such as remote locations and wilderness environments.*
● A tourniquet should not be applied to snake envenomation because it may not be
effective and may result in an extended hospital stay.**
Good practice points
● The person should limit physical activity to prevent the spread of toxins. If the bite is on
the person’s leg, they should not walk on the immobilized leg unless no other option is
available.
● It is reasonable to keep the injured area at or lower than the level of the heart.
● It is reasonable to remove jewellery, watches or tight clothing to prevent blood flow from
being restricted if there is swelling.
● In areas with limited resources (e.g. a wilderness or remote environment with longer wait
times to emergency medical care), it is reasonable to wash the wound.
● Suction should not be applied to a snakebite because it does not allow complete removal
of the venom and it might aggravate the damage to the skin.
● A cold compress should not be applied to a snakebite because this may cause the blood
vessels to contract and make the venom spread more quickly through the body.
● The wound should not be rubbed as this may cause the venom to spread more quickly
through the body.
● Cutting the wound with a knife to increase bleeding is not recommended. It does not
reduce the effect of the venom and aggravates the injury.
340 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Wear appropriate footwear in areas where snakes are present.
● Learn to identify local snakes and determine if they are venomous.
● Keep away from snakes that you encounter, trying not to disturb them. Do not kill or
injure them.
Early recognition
A person with a snakebite may:
● be in pain
● have a visible bite mark
● experience swelling or discolouration of the skin around the area of the bite.
A venomous snake may also cause:
● thirst
● nausea and vomiting
● blurred or double vision
● sensory disturbances or paralysis
● sweating and excessive production of saliva
● a sudden drop in blood pressure, leading to Shock
● Seizures.
If possible and safe to do so, identify the type of snake that bit the person. Do not try to
catch or kill the snake. Consider taking a photo or making a note of its features for a
medical professional to identify. However, it is more important to provide care to the person
than to figure out what kind of snake caused the bite.
First aid steps
1. Ensure the area is safe or move to a safer location.
2. Help the person lie down in a comfortable position. Advise them to move as little as
possible.
3. Access emergency medical services (EMS).
4. Remove jewellery, watches or tight clothing to prevent blood flow being restricted if there
is swelling. Do this while moving the limb as little as possible.
5. Mark the site of the wound to track any changes and note the timing of the bite.
6. Monitor the person’s level of response and breathing. Encourage them to remain as still
as possible and provide emotional support as a snakebite can be a traumatic experience.
● There are many resources available to learn more about poisonous snakes, including
online databases and local resources (e.g. medical professionals, snake handlers,
exterminators or poison centres).
● Be aware that snakes are traded and move around the world. You may encounter a
snake that is not common to your area.
FIRST AID | 341

CAUTION
The following actions are not effective and may even cause further harm:
● applying a tourniquet
● sucking out the venom
● applying a cold compress
● rubbing the bite
● cutting the wound with a knife to increase bleeding.
Local adaptations
● If in an area with limited resources (e.g. a wilderness or remote environment with longer
wait times to EMS):
○ wash the wound with potable (clean) water
○ immobilize the limb with a non-elastic bandage to reduce the spread of the
venom and risk of infection. If this is unavailable, use clothing such as trousers or a
shirt.
● If necessary, carry the person, for example, by making an improvised stretcher.
● Dispel any non-evidence-based local ideas about snakebites, particularly on the use of
activated charcoal and black stone.
Access help
It is important to tell medical professionals about the type of snake that bit the person. This will
help them provide the best care as quickly as possible.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Contact the local biological centre to find out the types of snakes in the region, whether
they are venomous, where to get antivenom, and any recommended treatment specific
to the types of local snakes.
● Include education on first aid for snakebites in regions where venomous snakes are found
or for learners travelling to areas with exotic or venomous snakes.
● Specially trained first aid providers may use the pressure immobilization technique in
special situations such as remote locations and wilderness environments. Ensure
adequate guidance and practice of this technique as there is evidence it is often applied
with poor outcomes, even by those with specific training. Be clear that the technique
should only be used in special circumstances.
Learner considerations
● For learners who might be visiting an area where there are snakes, emphasize the
importance of researching which snakes are present, how to avoid them and what to do if
bitten.
342 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● For communities that live in places where there are snakes, prevention strategies tend to
be well known, but first aid treatment seems less well known. Public campaigns to
publicize snakebite first aid (immobilization of the bitten area and loosening restrictive
clothing) could be effective (Alcoba et al., 2020).
Facilitation tips and tools
● Introduce this topic with a focus on prevention.
● Emphasize the importance of getting treatment quickly—even if the snake is not
venomous, the bite can quickly become infected.
● Use pictures of the most common snakes in the region to increase learners’ ability to
identify snakes and raise awareness of the danger.
● Use pictures of the signs and symptoms to help learners understand the seriousness of
snakebites.
● Emphasize that people should not try to catch or kill a snake.
Scientific foundation
This topic references five evidence summaries from the Centre for Evidence-based Practice
(CEBaP) on the following topics: pressure immobilization, tourniquets, irrigation with water,
cryotherapy and suction of a snakebite. CEBaP found no relevant studies on irrigating or
washing a snakebite, nor on applying ice to a snakebite.
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) completed an evidence update
on pressure immobilization in 2021.
Systematic reviews
Pressure immobilization
Pressure immobilization is a technique that can be achieved in two ways: first, by using an
elastic bandage to apply uniform pressure around and along the limb and second, by firmly
applying a cotton or rubber pad under a non-elastic bandage, which sufficiently applies firm
pressure specifically to the injured site. However, the CEBaP evidence summary indicated that
the “feasibility of applying this technique” is challenging for first aiders. ILCOR performed an
evidence update concerning pressure immobilization and decided that there was no need for a
formal systematic review on this topic (Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2022a). CEBaP also did
not update its summary.
Elastic bandage and splint
There is limited evidence from two experimental studies, neither in favour of using an elastic
bandage and splint nor of using the control (no first aid treatment). Neither study demonstrated
a statistically significant decrease in the speed of venom spread, using an elastic bandage and
splint compared to no first aid treatment. Evidence is of very low certainty, and the results of
these studies are imprecise due to limited sample size and/or large variability of the results
(CEBaP, 2019).
FIRST AID | 343

Elastic bandage and splint, combined with rest
There is limited evidence from one experimental study in favour of rest with an elastic bandage
and splint. It was shown that using an elastic bandage and splint, in combination with rest,
resulted in a statistically significant decrease in transit of mock venom, compared to the same
treatment while walking. Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered
precise due to limited sample size and/or large variability of results (CEBaP, 2019).
Firm cloth or rubber pad held in place by non-elastic bandage at a certain pressure
There is limited evidence from four experimental studies in favour of using a firm pad (e.g. a
cotton cloth or a rubber pad), held in place with a broad non-elastic bandage under a minimum
pressure of 60-70 mmHg. It was shown that using such a pad resulted in a statistically
significant decrease in the speed of venom spread or transit of mock venom, compared to no
treatment. Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to
limited sample size and/or large variability of results (CEBaP, 2019).
Feasibility of applying pressure immobilization by lay first aid providers
There is limited evidence from two experimental studies in favour of training first aid providers to
apply an elastic bandage. It was shown that training resulted in a statistically significant increase
in bandage application with optimal pressure range, compared to written instructions. However,
it was shown that first aid provider volunteers did not succeed in a statistically significant higher
correct bandage application or achievement of correct pressure, compared to medical
volunteers. Evidence is of low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to limited
sample size and large variability of results (CEBaP, 2019).
Tourniquet
There is limited evidence from 10 observational studies in favour of not using a tourniquet. It was
shown that using a tourniquet increased local swelling, duration of hospital stay and severity of
local envenomation, compared to not using a tourniquet. The studies could not demonstrate a
statistically significant increase in haemorrhagic syndrome, acute renal failure, acute respiratory
failure, death, death or disability, local edema, envenoming, duration of hospital stay, tissue
necrosis, serum venom level before antivenom treatment and incidence of multiple organ
dysfunction syndrome when using a tourniquet compared to not using a tourniquet.
There is conflicting evidence from three observational studies regarding the amount of
antivenom required. One study showed that using a tourniquet resulted in a statistically
significant decrease in the amount of antivenom required, compared to not using a tourniquet;
however, the second study showed an increase in the amount required. Finally, the third study
demonstrated no effect. Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered
precise due to limited sample sizes, a low number of events and large variability of results
(CEBaP, 2024).
Suction
There is limited evidence from one observational study, neither supporting nor opposing the use
of suction. A statistically significant decrease in deaths or disability, the amount of antivenom
required or duration of hospital stay, using suction compared to no first aid, could not be
344 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

demonstrated. Evidence is of very low certainty and the results of this study are imprecise due to
the limited sample size, the low number of events and the large variability of results or lack of
data (CEBaP, 2024).
Education review
We found one paper to provide educational insight on this topic. Alcoba et al. (2020) conducted
a cross-sectional multi-cluster household survey in the Akonolinga health district of Cameroon
and found an incidence of 665 snakebites per 100,000 inhabitants in one year. Despite good
community knowledge of preventive measures, the researchers found that resulting fatalities,
acute complications and chronic disability were associated with consulting traditional healers.
Only 3% of people bitten by a snake received antivenom treatment. The authors concluded that
there was an urgent need for first aid training for traditional healers and health professionals.
FIRST AID | 345

Poisoning
Key action
Quickly try to identify the poison, the amount and when (or how long) the person was exposed
to it and access advanced care, such as emergency medical services (EMS) or a poison centre.
Introduction
Poisoning is when a person is exposed to a substance that can quickly damage their health or
endanger their life. Most poisons are swallowed or inhaled; however, they can also be absorbed
through the skin. Many poisonous substances are found in homes and workplaces, including
some cleaning products, illicit substances, medications and even some plants.
Opioids act as poisons when taken in excessive amounts or by people for whom they are not
prescribed, as they depress the central nervous system and can rapidly lead to respiratory
failure and death. See unresponsive and abnormal breathing with suspected opioid overdose.
Guidelines
● For a person who has swallowed a poisonous substance, the first aid provider should
consider laying them on their left side.*
● If the person ingested a chemical substance (one that could burn from the inside), the
first aid provider can give them a dilutant if advised to do so by a healthcare provider,
such as a poison centre.*
Good practice points
● The first aid provider should stop or limit further effects of the poison by stopping
continued exposure. In the case of inhalation of toxic gas, the person should be removed
from the area, but only if it is safe for the first aid provider to do so.
● In rooms that are potentially filled with unknown gases or vapours, exposure to all sources
of ignition, such as naked flames, electrical equipment, oxidizing chemicals and the
smoking of tobacco products, should be prevented.
● The nature and time of exposure and the name of the product or toxic substance should
be described to the poison control centre, or local equivalent, or EMS. All bottles,
packages or containers with labels or any other information about the poison should be
given to EMS.
● If life-threatening conditions are present (e.g. unresponsiveness or breathing difficulties),
the first aid provider should access EMS. The first aid provider should start CPR or provide
other first aid as necessary, taking the necessary steps to avoid cross-contamination.
● If non-life-threatening conditions are present, the first aider should access and follow the
instructions of the poison control centre (or local equivalent) or EMS.
● Rescue breaths should be avoided if poisoning by toxins such as cyanide, hydrogen
sulphide, corrosives or organophosphates is suspected. The bag-valve-mask device may
be used by those who are trained to do so.
346 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● If the person is responsive, the first aid provider should remove any poisonous liquid
remaining in the person’s mouth by allowing the person to use water to rinse and spit out
any remaining toxin.
● First aid providers should not give any diluents such as milk, water or activated charcoal
to a person who has swallowed a poisonous substance unless they are instructed to do so
by the poison control centre or equivalent poison expert.
● The person should NOT be encouraged to vomit as this may damage their throat.
● Button batteries contain poisonous chemicals which may leak into the body. If they are
swallowed, this should be treated as poisoning.
● First aid providers with appropriate training may administer oxygen to people who have
carbon monoxide or carbon dioxide poisoning.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Ensure that all poisonous substances (such as cleaning products and medicines) are
locked away and inaccessible to children. This includes brightly coloured or scented
chemicals such as washing machine tablets, e-cigarette liquids or any drug-infused
“treats” like THC gummies, also known as weed gummies or marijuana gummies.
● Carefully read the information leaflet that comes with any new medication to avoid an
overdose.
● Regularly service cooking and heating appliances that use fossil fuels and install a carbon
monoxide detector.
● Store chemicals out of access to any person who may accidentally or purposefully swallow
or sniff them and ensure they have child-resistant lids.
● Hand sanitizers should be kept out of reach of children under the age of six at all times
and used only with adult supervision.
● Depending on the type of work or hobbies, learn how to prevent direct contact with
poisonous substances. This may include contacting the person who is responsible for
workplace safety to teach specific safety regulations.
Early recognition
● Upon entering an environment where there may be poisonous substances, assess the
area. There may be open packaging near the person (e.g. a packet of tablets) or spilled
chemicals. A confined space may smell of gas (note that carbon monoxide is odourless, as
well as colourless and tasteless). The person may also be able to tell you that they have
had contact with a poisonous substance.
Poisoning signs and symptoms can include:
● burns around the mouth and lips
● breath that smells like chemicals, such as gasoline or paint thinner
● vomiting
● drowsiness
● difficulty breathing
● confusion or other altered mental status.
FIRST AID | 347

Poisoning signs and symptoms can mimic other conditions, such as Seizure, Stroke or an
insulin reaction.
First aid steps
1. Help the person reduce or stop further exposure to the poison.
2. Try to identify the poisonous substance, the amount and when (or how long) the person
was exposed to it. Only touch the packaging if it is safe to do so. If not, wait for EMS.
3. Access the poison control centre (or local equivalent) or EMS and report all of the
information about the poison and situation. Follow their instructions.
4. Help the person into a comfortable position on their left side if possible, and monitor
them for any change in their breathing or level of response.
5. Gather any bottles, packages or containers with labels and any other information about
the poison to give to EMS.
Special cases
● If the person is unresponsive, open their airway and check for breathing. See
Unresponsiveness topics.
● If the person has a chemical substance in their eye or around their mouth, see burns.
● If the person is having an opioid overdose, see Unresponsive and abnormal breathing
with opioid overdose.
● If the person has been bitten by a snake or stung by a jellyfish, see snakebites or aquatic
animal injuries.
● If the person vomits while lying on the ground, turn them on their side to prevent
choking.
CAUTION
● If a toxic substance is suspected, the first aid provider may need to wear personal
protective equipment such as a mask or gloves. If a harmful substance is suspected in or
around the mouth, providers should use chest compression-only CPR to avoid contact.
● Do not make the person vomit as this may damage their throat.
Access help
● It is important to tell the poison control centre (or equivalent) or EMS as much as you can
about the type of poison the person may have been exposed to. This allows the
professionals to give the most appropriate help based on the person’s needs.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Establish with learners what substances and sources are most likely to lead to poisoning
in their context and discuss ways to reduce the likelihood of poisoning. For example,
discuss living or work conditions and how to store harmful substances.
348 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Give learners opportunities to assess danger through photos or scenarios, for example of
a chemical spill or a gas-filled room. Discuss what signs they should look for. Explore how
they can keep themselves safe. This may be particularly relevant to some workplaces.
● Inform learners of any local, regional or national carbon monoxide detector schemes such
as free detectors from a local rescue service, regulated charity or commercial providers.
These can be professionally fitted and tested.
Learner considerations
● Tailor education to specific learning needs. For example, child first aid courses aimed at
parents and care providers may require more time around prevention.
● Decide if first aid education should include whether it is preferable to lay a person who
has swallowed poison on their left side. If including this, reassure learners that if they
forget which side to lay the person on, first aid principles remain, and the key is to move
the person into a safe position that will maintain an open airway.
● Children younger than five years of age are the most likely to ingest a button battery.
Most ingested batteries are from hearing aids, watches, games, toys and calculators
(Ikenberry et al., 2011).
● In adults, the swallowing of chemical substances is frequently intentional, involves large
amounts, and is life-threatening.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Use visual resources, such as danger labels and signs, as well as photographs of the
impact of poisoning, to help learners identify what to look for.
● Help learners identify a poison control centre (or equivalent) and learn how to contact
them.
● Use audio-visual resources to support facilitation and connect key first aid principles.
● Use information from official organizations when educating specific audiences and tailor
the content accordingly. For example, the National Health Service in the United Kingdom
provides clear guidance on what to do if you think a child may have swallowed a button
battery.
● Suggest reading leaflets distributed by authorities on the specific poisoning risks in the
region.
Learning connections
● Make connections to snakebites, aquatic animal injuries (e.g. jellyfish stings) and insect
bites and stings.
● Connect to learning about a person who is unresponsive with abnormal breathing with
suspected opioid overdose.
● The approaches of psychological first aid may be useful for this topic.
FIRST AID | 349

Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Body position
The Centre for Evidence-based Practice (CEBaP) systematic review (Borra et al., 2019) was
updated in 2024, but no additional studies were identified. The review included nine
randomized crossover studies in adult volunteers and showed that lying down on the left side
resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the uptake of several drugs (acetaminophen,
nifedipine, nitropyridine), compared to other body positions.
Dilution with milk or water
In 2022, the International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) conducted an update of
an evidence review completed in 2010 regarding the use of oral dilution if a caustic substance is
ingested. The updated study identified one animal study that compared the use of kefir and
distilled water with distilled water alone, examining the impact of an alkali injury to the
oesophagus. No difference was found in histopathological outcomes at seven days following the
injury. The ILCOR recommendation remains the same: a first aid provider may administer a
dilutant if a caustic substance has been ingested, provided they are advised to do so by a health
care provider. It is a weak recommendation as the evidence is very low-certainty (Wyckoff et al.,
2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c).
Induced vomiting – Ipecac
A CEBaP Cochrane systematic review identified five studies investigating the effectiveness of
ipecac syrup as a first aid intervention (Avau et al., 2018). None of the evidence on the use of the
syrup as a first aid intervention showed any benefit, and it may even cause harm.
One study provided evidence of low certainty on the use of ipecac versus no intervention in
asymptomatic participants with toxic berry ingestion. This study took place in a prehospital
setting and reported no clinical outcomes. While there may be little or no difference in
emergency department referral, there may be an increase in adverse events.
Four studies examined adding ipecac syrup to single-dose activated charcoal with a cathartic (a
medication that speeds stool passage). These studies either did not specify the type of overdose
or included multiple types. Low-certainty evidence indicates that ipecac may make little or no
difference in clinical improvement. Its effects on mortality, adverse events, clinical deterioration,
hospitalization or intensive care unit admission remain unclear. This review has not been
updated, and no recent systematic reviews are available on this topic.
Activated charcoal
A CEBaP Cochrane systematic review showed there is limited evidence for harm with
single-dose activated charcoal ingestion after poisoning (Avau et al., 2018).
It was shown that single-dose activated charcoal and a hospital intervention (mostly gastric
lavage) resulted in a statistically significant increase in several symptomatic features of
poisoning (hospitalization rate, intensive care unit admission rate, need for intubation rate),
350 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

compared to hospital intervention alone. However, a statistically significant increase in mortality,
convulsion rate, drug absorption (AUC, Cmax or Tmax), or the occurrence of adverse events
(vomiting, absence of bowel sounds), using single-dose activated charcoal and a hospital
intervention, compared to hospital treatment alone, could not be demonstrated. Furthermore, a
statistically significant increase in mortality or the occurrence of adverse events, using
single-dose activated charcoal compared to no intervention, could not be demonstrated.
Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to limited sample
size, the low number of events and/or large variability of results.
In addition, there is limited evidence either in favour of multiple doses of activated charcoal or
no intervention. A statistically significant decrease in mortality, symptomatic features of
poisoning (need for intubation, length of intubation, seizures, need for cardiac pacing or
antitoxin treatment), drug absorption (AUC, Cmax or Tmax), or the occurrence of adverse events
(absent bowel sounds), using multiple doses of activated charcoal and a hospital intervention
compared to hospital treatment alone, could not be demonstrated. Evidence is of very low
certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to limited sample size, the low number
of events and/or large variability of results.
This review was not further updated. A more recent systematic review concluded that the
majority of identified studies are case reports and that studies were unable to quantify the
precise potential benefit of activated charcoal (Hoegberg et al., 2021).
Safe storage of poisons for poisoning prevention
CEBaP conducted an evidence summary in 2020 concerning the safe storage of chemicals and
household poisons.
Evidence from six observational studies showed that chemicals and medicine, household
poisons, kerosene and petroleum, and a lack of child-resistant lids on bathroom bottles are risk
factors that resulted in a statistically increased risk of unintentional poisoning. However, the
following risk factors could not demonstrate a statistically significant increased risk of
unintentional poisoning: no child-resistant lids on household cleaning supplies, easy access to
bathroom beauty supplies and medications, easy access to cleaning supplies and the use of
unsafe product packaging. The evidence is of very low quality and results cannot be considered
precise due to a lack of data and large variability of results.
There is evidence from one systematic review and one experimental study in favour of using
child-resistant containers for paraffin and aspirin. It was shown that using child-resistant
containers resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the incidence of paraffin ingestion
