“emergency birth” takes place very quickly and not in the place where it was planned. Childbirth
occurs naturally, and the first aid provider’s role is to support the person through the process.
There are three phases, starting with contractions and the person’s water breaking, the second
phase during which the person pushes the baby out, and the third phase when the person
pushes the afterbirth out (including the placenta).
Guidelines
● Support the person to contact their chosen birthing partner(s), as their continuous
support during labour contributes to a positive childbirth experience.**
● Being in an upright position (sitting, standing or walking) may help to shorten the
duration of labour and the pushing phase. This may result in fewer assisted births, vaginal
tears and incision of the perineum.*
● During labour, massage of the lower back may reduce pain intensity.*
● During labour, breathing exercises, yoga or listening to music may reduce pain intensity
and improve the overall birthing experience.*
● During labour, heat application (e.g. warm compresses to the perineal area or lower back
and sacrum area, a hot bath, the use of a hot water bottle on the lower back and
abdomen, etc.) may reduce pain intensity. A warm perineal compress may also reduce
vaginal tears.*
● Delayed cord clamping (≥60 seconds after birth) for term and late preterm infants born at
or after 34 weeks’ gestation who are vigorous or deemed not to require immediate
resuscitation at birth may result in more favourable haemoglobin and haematocrit levels
of the baby.*
● Skin-to-skin contact between the mother and the baby may improve breastfeeding and
infant and maternal outcomes.*
● External uterine (self) massage in the immediate postpartum period may prevent
postpartum haemorrhage.*
Good practice points
● The first aid provider should manage the scene to protect the dignity and safety of the
person, as well as take care to comfort them and give emotional support.
● The person should be supported to move into the positions most comfortable, even if the
amniotic sac is broken (waters have broken).
● The person may drink or eat something during labour if they want to.
418 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Hygiene measures should be taken where possible, such as wearing gloves and using
clean cloths or towels, both under the mother and to wrap the newborn in.
● As soon as the baby is delivered, the first aid provider should check both the person and
the baby for their responsiveness, breathing and for any bleeding.
● If the baby is responsive (e.g. crying) and breathing normally, there is no immediate need
to cut the umbilical cord and the baby should be dried and kept warm to prevent
hypothermia. Place the baby on the mother’s chest and cover with a blanket.
● If the baby is unresponsive, the first aid provider should rub the baby dry and tap the soles
of its feet. If the baby remains unresponsive and does not start breathing, CPR should be
provided as soon as possible.
● If the presence of the umbilical cord makes this difficult, it should be cut. If possible, the
umbilical cord should be tied twice (a hand's width apart) with a ribbon and cut in
between the ribbons with the first ribbon about a hand-width from the belly of the baby.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Know how to contact a locally available midwife or other community healthcare provider
who can arrive quickly and provide support in an emergency.
● Encourage people expecting a baby to carry the contact phone numbers of their chosen
birthing partners, as well as their preferred midwife or birth attendant.
● Recommend pregnant people remain close to support as they near the end of their
pregnancy.
Early recognition
Talk to the person to try to determine whether they are starting labour (which may take several
hours) or whether they are having an emergency birth.
The following signs indicate that labour has started, and the baby has entered the birth canal:
● painful contractions occurring at increasingly short intervals
● abdominal discomfort
● localized back pains
● the person’s waters break
● sticky discharge.
In this instance, support the person in notifying their chosen birthing partner and preferred
birth attendants, or in arranging transport to their chosen birth facility.
Childbirth might be imminent when:
● the contractions quickly become more intense and painful
● the contractions come in short intervals (one contraction every five minutes)
● the person’s waters break.
If it becomes clear that the baby will arrive very soon or the person is in too much pain to travel,
prepare to support the person through the emergency birth.
FIRST AID | 419

First aid steps
Initially, the first aid provider’s key priority is the person in labour. Once the baby is born, the first
aid provider has two people to care for: the person and the baby.
1. Access help (such as medical facilities, emergency medical services or a midwife) in line
with the person’s wishes and follow their instructions. Support the person to contact their
chosen birthing partner.
2. In the first phase of childbirth, create a safe, comfortable, private and protected space for
the person. Help them move into a position of comfort. This may be sitting, standing or
moving around.
3. Offer to massage the lower back or offer relaxation methods such as yoga or listening to
music as this may reduce pain and anxiety and improve the overall birthing experience.
4. If possible and if the person wants it, apply heat to the perineal, lower back, sacrum or
abdomen. You can use a warm compress or hot water bottle, or offer a hot bath. The heat
may reduce the pain intensity.
5. In the second phase of childbirth, help the person find a comfortable position, preferably
an upright one. If lying on their back, put a small pillow under the right hip. In this way,
you prevent the baby from pressing on important blood vessels.
6. If possible, wash your hands well with soap and water and place a clean cloth under the
person where the baby will be born. When the person is ready to push, ensure they are in
a supported position.
7. Watch the baby’s head as it comes out and ensure it is supported. Newly born babies are
slippery, so be very careful. Wipe any fluids or mucus away from the baby’s mouth and
nose.
8. Use a clean cloth to dry off the baby. Wrap the baby in a clean cloth and cover the baby’s
head to keep it warm. Keep the mother warm as well and place the baby on the mother’s
chest or abdomen as soon as possible.
9. In the third phase of childbirth, support the person as they deliver the afterbirth. Keep the
afterbirth as a healthcare professional will need to check it is complete. Check to see if the
mother is bleeding. Mild bleeding from the birth canal is normal. If bleeding is severe,
help the person to lie down and keep them warm (see shock) until medical help arrives.
CAUTION
● Hygiene measures should be taken where possible, such as handwashing, wearing
gloves and using clean cloths or towels, both under the person and to wrap the newborn
baby in.
● Do not pull the baby’s head and shoulders during delivery.
● Do not push on the person’s stomach during labour or after delivery.
● If the umbilical cord is wrapped around the baby’s neck during delivery, check that it is
loose and carefully ease it over the baby’s head to prevent the baby from strangulation.
● Do not pull on the umbilical cord. The afterbirth usually comes out by itself within about
30 minutes of the delivery.
● If the baby is responsive and breathing normally, there is no immediate need to cut the
umbilical cord, which should be performed by a medical professional, if possible.
420 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

The person may drink or eat something during labour if they want to. This will help keep up
their strength.
Unresponsive and abnormal breathing baby
● If the baby is quiet, open their airway and check for breathing. If the baby is breathing
abnormally (gasping, taking irregular breaths or not breathing), place the baby on a firm
surface and begin CPR as described in unresponsive and abnormal breathing (baby and
child).
● If CPR is difficult because of the umbilical cord, cut the cord. To do this, if possible, tie a
ribbon or string around the cord in two places, a hand's width apart, so blood can no
longer flow through the cord. Then cut the cord in between the two ribbons.
Access help
● Emergency medical services (EMS) may be appropriate in many childbirth cases, or the
person may have a preferred midwife or birth attendant. Consider contacting another
local midwife or other community health worker if these are not available.
● Phone EMS, medical facilities or another healthcare provider, as they will be able to tell
you what to do and support you.
● Tell EMS (or chosen healthcare provider) about any changes in the person's or baby’s
condition.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Childbirth has very strong social and cultural references and expectations. Programme
designers should adapt the learning to address local cultures and contexts.
● Highlight the importance of knowing what to do in an emergency childbirth situation in
a remote environment.
● Programme developers should include content relevant to their region and country, and
provide education on the actions a first aid provider is permitted to do.
Learner considerations
● Childbirth can be unpredictable, so parents-to-be in any context should consider learning
this topic. Emphasis on the natural process of birth is important to reduce their anxiety.
● In remote areas where travel is difficult or there is no accessible maternity service nearby,
the partner and family members of a pregnant person may also consider learning about
this topic.
● In many countries, taxi drivers are a key learner audience for this topic.
● Learners who are more likely to do emergency childbirth in a vehicle could learn and
practise childbirth in the vehicle. The mother could lie on the seat, with one foot on the
floor and the other on the seat, with the knee and hip bent.
● Encourage community health workers and volunteers to understand this topic in the
context of first aid, and to develop connections with their local clinical team.
● People with varying gender identities can give birth (e.g. a man with a uterus). Be
sensitive to gender identities in discussing this topic according to the needs of the
audience and respecting all opinions without making value judgements.
FIRST AID | 421

Facilitation tips and tools
● Emphasize accessing medical help as soon as possible and supporting the person
through the childbirth process.
● Help learners understand the three phases of childbirth. Using pictures or diagrams can
be helpful.
a. Contractions and water breaking.
b. The birth itself.
c. The expulsion of the afterbirth (placenta and umbilical cord).
● A person who goes into childbirth unexpectedly might be very anxious. Allow learners to
practise reassuring the person, making them comfortable and helping them to breathe.
Reassure learners by emphasizing that during this time, if it is possible, they should be
following the instructions of EMS (or another healthcare provider).
● A unique aspect of this first aid topic is the changing focus for the first aid provider, who
initially needs to focus on the person and their well-being, but then needs to care for two
people once the baby is born. Priority must be given to the person until the baby is born.
● Use different learning materials and styles, including discussions and storytelling, to
develop confidence in learners. Encourage those who have experienced childbirth (even if
not as an emergency) to share their experiences with those who have not experienced it.
● If possible and appropriate, invite a midwife to the education session to talk to learners
and answer their questions.
Learning connections
● A newborn baby may be or become unresponsive with abnormal breathing.
● A person may experience severe bleeding during childbirth, and this may develop into
Shock.
● Emphasize hand hygiene.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
No scientific evidence is available which addresses specific issues of emergency birth, so the
Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) extrapolated from “normal” deliveries. The
International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) conducted a systematic review on the
timing of cutting the cord (Gomersall et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2021; Wyckoff et al., 2022a) and
on uterine massage in the immediate postpartum period (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
Birth companion
There is evidence from one Cochrane systematic review including 26 clinical trials in favour of
continuous support by a chosen birth companion. In making this evidence conclusion, a higher
value was placed on subjective rating or feelings about the birth experience over the other
outcomes.
It was demonstrated that continuous support from a chosen birth companion led to a
statistically significant decrease in the occurrence of negative ratings or feelings about the birth
experience, compared to standard care.
422 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

In contrast, it was shown that continuous support by a chosen birth companion did not result in
a statistically significant difference in the use of any analgesia or anaesthesia, synthetic oxytocin
and the occurrence of spontaneous vaginal birth, compared to standard care.
Furthermore, a statistically significant decrease in caesarean birth or admission to special care
nursery, using continuous support by a chosen birth companion compared to standard care,
could not be demonstrated. Evidence is of moderate certainty and results cannot be considered
precise due to the large variability of results and the low number of events.
This evidence summary was considered stable and was therefore not updated.
Massage during delivery
There is low-certainty evidence from two systematic reviews in favour of massage (of the lower
back).
● It was shown that massage resulted in a statistically significant reduction in pain intensity
in the first stage of labour, compared to usual care. However, a statistically significant
difference could not be demonstrated for pain intensity in the second and third stage of
labour, or the use of pain relief.
● It was shown that massage resulted in a statistically significant reduced anxiety in the first
phase of labour, compared to usual care. However, a statistically significant difference
could not be demonstrated for anxiety in the second and third stages of labour.
● It was shown that massage resulted in a statistically significant reduced risk of
resuscitation of the newborn, compared to usual care. However, a statistically significant
difference could not be demonstrated for admission to a neonatal intensive care unit.
● It was shown that massage resulted in a statistically significant reduced risk of perineal
trauma, compared to usual care. However, a statistically significant difference could not
be demonstrated for postpartum haemorrhage.
Relaxation (breathing exercises, yoga, music)
There is limited evidence from four systematic reviews in favour of breathing exercises, yoga or
music.
One systematic review provided moderate certainty evidence in favour of breathing exercises
during labour. It was shown that breathing exercises resulted in a statistically significant
decrease in the duration of the second stage of labour compared to usual or routine care. In
regards to using breathing exercises compared to usual or routine care, a statistically significant
increase in vaginal delivery rates or a decrease in the total duration of labour, the duration of
pain, caesarean delivery rates and episiotomy rates could not be demonstrated.
One systematic review provided moderate certainty evidence in favour of yoga during labour. It
was shown that yoga resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the use of pharmacological
pain relief and length of labour compared to usual care.
Three systematic reviews provided moderate-certainty evidence in favour of listening to music
during labour. It was shown that listening to music resulted in a statistically significant decrease
in labour pain, duration of the second stage of labour, heart rate and (systolic and diastolic)
blood pressure compared to usual care or placebo. It was also shown that listening to
FIRST AID | 423

self-selected music resulted in a statistically significant decrease in anxiety, but this could not be
demonstrated for music that was selected by someone else. A statistically significant decrease in
assisted vaginal birth rates, caesarean section rates, admission to a special care nursery and use
of pharmacological pain relief could not be demonstrated.
Restriction of oral fluid and food intake
Moderate-certainty evidence from one Cochrane systematic review could not demonstrate a
statistically significant improvement in health-related maternal or foetal outcomes, using any or
complete restriction of oral fluid and food compared to some fluid and food or freedom to eat
and drink. The evidence was declared stable and the evidence summary was not updated
further.
Heat or cold application
CEBaP identified low-certainty evidence (downgraded for risk of bias and imprecision due to the
low number of events and wide confidence intervals) from one Cochrane systematic review. The
review could not demonstrate a statistically significant difference for a first-degree tear or
episiotomy when using cold compresses compared to not using them. The current update of
the evidence summary identified two other systematic reviews and found evidence in favour of
heat therapy. It was shown that the application of heat (e.g. warm compresses to the perineal
area or to the lumbo-sacral area, a hot bath or the use of a hot water bottle on the lower back
and abdomen) resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the level of pain intensity during
the first stage of labour, compared to standard treatment. It was shown that warm perineal
compresses resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the risk of second-, third-, and
fourth-degree tears and episiotomy, and a statistically significant increase in the intact
perineum, compared to hands-off routine care or Ritgen's manoeuvre. However, a statistically
significant difference in first-degree tears was not demonstrated when using warm compresses
compared to hands-off, routine care, or Ritgen's manoeuvre.
Body position
First phase of labour
CEBaP identified moderate-certainty evidence (downgraded for risk of bias) from one Cochrane
systematic review, showing that an upright position (sitting, standing or walking) seems to have
better maternal outcomes than a lying position during the first phase of labour.
It was shown that upright and ambulant positions resulted in a statistically significant decreased
duration of first stage labour, a decreased risk of caesarean birth and a decreased risk of
maternal pain, compared to recumbent positions and bed care (Lawrence, 2013). However, a
statistically significant difference in spontaneous vaginal birth, operative vaginal birth, maternal
satisfaction, maternal comfort, maternal anxiety, duration of second stage of labour,
augmentation of labour using oxytocin, artificial rupture of membranes, estimated blood loss
>500 mL and perineal trauma, using upright and ambulant positions compared to recumbent
positions and bed care, could not be demonstrated.
It was shown that sitting resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the duration of
first-stage labour and a decreased risk of operative vaginal birth, compared to lying down, lying
424 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

on the back or lying on the side. However, a statistically significant difference in spontaneous
vaginal birth and caesarean birth in the sitting position compared to the lying down positions
could not be demonstrated. Furthermore, a statistically significant difference in the duration of
first labour and caesarean birth when sitting compared to bed care could not be demonstrated.
It was shown that walking resulted in a statistically significant increase in spontaneous vaginal
birth, a decreased duration of first stage labour and a decreased risk of operative vaginal and
caesarean birth, compared to recumbent/supine/lateral positions.
A statistically significant difference in the duration of first labour, spontaneous vaginal birth,
operative birth, and caesarean birth, when sitting, standing, squatting, kneeling or walking
compared to lying down on the back or side, could not be demonstrated.
There is low-certainty evidence from one Cochrane systematic review showing that a statistically
significant difference in foetal distress, use of neonatal mechanical ventilation, Apgar scores and
perinatal mortality, using upright and ambulant positions compared to recumbent positions
and bed care, could not be demonstrated.
Second phase of labour
Low certainty evidence from one systematic review showed that an upright position
(semi-sitting, kneeling, sitting on hands and knees) during the second phase of labour (pushing
phase) seems to ensure fewer assisted births, a shorter duration of the pushing phase, fewer
third- and fourth-degree perineal tears and fewer perineum incisions than a lying down
position.
Low-certainty evidence from another systematic review showed contradictory evidence about
squatting during the second phase of labour: on the one hand, there are fewer assisted births
and a lower risk of placenta retention, but on the other hand, it may lead to more caesarean
sections.
Early skin-to-skin contact
There is low-certainty evidence from one Cochrane systematic review in favour of skin-to-skin
contact between the mother and baby immediately after birth to improve breastfeeding and
infant and maternal outcomes. It was shown that skin-to-skin contact resulted in a statistically
significant increased number of mothers breastfeeding one to four months post-birth,
increased duration of breastfeeding, increased number of mothers exclusively breastfeeding at
hospital discharge to one-month post-birth, increased number of mothers exclusively
breastfeeding six weeks to six months post-birth, increased success of first breastfeeding,
increased successful first breastfeeding (IBFAT 10–12 or BAT 8–12), increased mean variation in
maternal breast temperature 30–120 minutes post-birth and reduced breast engorgement
(pain, tension, hardness) three days post-birth, compared to standard care.
However, a statistically significant difference in the number of mothers with a breastfeeding
status from day 28 to one month post-birth, number of mothers breastfeeding one year
post-birth, number of babies suckled during the first two hours post-birth, and using
skin-to-skin contact compared to standard care could not be demonstrated. It was shown that
skin-to-skin contact resulted in statistically significant increased SCRIP score (Stability of the
FIRST AID | 425

Cardio-Respiratory System in Preterm infants) first six hours post-birth, increased blood glucose
(mg/dL) at 75–180 minutes post-birth, increased infant axillary temperature 90–150 minutes
post-birth, increased number of infants who did not exceed parameters for stability, increased
number of infants who did not cry less than one minute during a 90 minute period and
decreased amount of crying in minutes during a 75 minute observation period, compared to
standard care.
However, a statistically significant difference could not be demonstrated between skin-to-skin
contact and standard care in terms of: respiratory rate (75–120 minutes post-birth), heart rate
(75–120 minutes post-birth), number of infants transferred to the neonatal intensive care unit,
infant body weight change (grams) day 14 post-birth and infant hospital length of stay in hours.
It was shown that skin-to-skin contact resulted in a statistically significant reduction in maternal
state anxiety eight hours to three days post-birth, increased PCERA (Parent-Child Early
Relational Assessment) dyadic mutuality and reciprocity 12 months post-birth, and increased the
mother’s most certain preference for similar post-delivery care in the future, compared to
standard care.
However, a statistically significant difference using skin-to-skin contact compared to standard
care could not be demonstrated for the following outcomes: PCERA maternal positive affective
involvement and responsiveness 12 months post-birth, maternal parenting confidence at one
month post-birth and maternal pain four hours post-caesarean birth.
The evidence was declared stable and the evidence summary was not updated further.
Cutting or clamping the umbilical cord
CEBaP could not identify any scientific studies on the umbilical cord cutting technique.
ILCOR conducted a systematic review, published in 2021, on the timing of umbilical cord
clamping. The review compared later/delayed cord clamping, defined as application of a clamp
to the umbilical cord ≥30 seconds after birth, to early cord clamping, defined as application of a
clamp to the umbilical cord <30 seconds after birth (Gomersall, 2021). The review includes 33
randomized controlled trials with 5,263 mothers and their infants comparing delayed and early
cord clamping. For the neonatal outcomes, delayed cord clamping showed more favourable
haemoglobin and haematocrit levels at birth; however, a difference in mortality, resuscitation,
intensive care admission, hyperbilirubinaemia, anaemia, ferritin concentrations and moderate to
severe neurodevelopmental impairment in early childhood, could not be demonstrated. In
addition, a difference in maternal outcomes (postpartum haemorrhaging, manual removal of
placenta) could not be demonstrated. Based on the evidence, ILCOR suggests later (delayed)
clamping of the cord at ≥60 seconds for term and late preterm infants born at ≥34 weeks’
gestation who are vigorous or deemed not to require immediate resuscitation at birth (Wyckoff
et al., 2021; Wyckoff, et al., 2022b; Wyckoff, et al., 2022c).
Manual uterine massage for postpartum haemorrhage
ILCOR conducted a systematic review, included in the CoSTR publication of 2025, about manual
uterine massage in the immediate postpartum period (Djärv et al., 2025a; Djärv et al., 2025b).
426 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

One study found that self-massage every 15 minutes for the first two hours after birth resulted in
a non-statistically significant decrease in blood loss and the need for blood transfusion. ILCOR
formulated a weak recommendation of external uterine (self-)massage in the immediate
postpartum period to prevent postpartum haemorrhage, which can lead to maternal death.
Non-systematic reviews
A few scientific studies are available that focus on the risk of umbilical cord prolapse after the
waters have broken (occurring in approximately 0.4% of births) (Dekker, 2018) The outcomes of
umbilical cord prolapse have improved within the past 10–20 years and more recent studies
(from 2002–2012) show that deaths of babies related to umbilical cord prolapse have become
extremely rare, mainly associated with prematurity.
Furthermore, there are no studies that test whether bed rest reduces the risk of cord prolapse in
women with term PROM (“premature” rupture of membranes before the start of labour past 37
weeks of gestation) (Dekker, 2018).
FIRST AID | 427

Sore throat
Key action
Advise the person to take the recommended dose of paracetamol (also known as
acetaminophen) to relieve their pain.
Introduction
A sore throat is characterized by a dry, scratchy pain in the throat, often worse when swallowing.
It can be caused by several conditions such as a viral or bacterial infection (also known as a cold)
and generally does not need medical treatment. Roughly four out of five people recover well
within a week (Gunnarsson et al., 2023). However, a bacterial infection can be serious. Individuals
between the ages of five and 24 are most likely to get a sore throat, especially during the early
spring or winter months (Rughani, 2019). Sometimes a sore throat can be severe enough to
cause the airway to swell. In these instances, the person should access medical care.
Guidelines
● Paracetamol can reduce the pain caused by a sore throat.**
● Drinking a hot drink may relieve the pain.*
● Medicated lozenges (containing benzocaine or hexylresorcinol) or mouth sprays
(containing chlorhexidine gluconate and benzydamine hydrochloride) may relieve the
pain.*
● Antibiotics should only be given if prescribed by a medical professional.**
Good practice points
● Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (such as ibuprofen) can be used as a second-line
treatment for sore throat, should paracetamol be ineffective.
● The first aid provider should encourage the person to drink water in small amounts.
● Harsh or high-pitched breathing sounds, the inability to swallow, severe pain or drooling
are signs and symptoms of potential airway swelling that should receive urgent medical
care.
● If a first aid provider suspects the person has a swollen airway, they should help the
person rest in a comfortable position and access medical care.
● Smoking, talking a lot, singing and shouting may make a sore throat last longer. The
person should speak as little as possible, speak at their normal volume and avoid
whispering.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Avoid people who are sick with an infectious illness such as influenza or strep throat
(streptococcal infection), or create a barrier (e.g. mask) between people.
428 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Wash your hands with soap and water or use hand sanitizer to prevent the spread of the
common cold (G. M. Allan & Arroll, 2014).
● Avoid smoking and smoky environments.
Early recognition
A person with a sore throat may:
● complain of a painful or dry, scratchy throat, especially when swallowing
● have swollen neck glands, redness at the back of the throat, swollen tonsils or whitish
spots in the throat
● experience difficulty eating and drinking.
The person may also have pain in their ear, see earache or fever. Children in particular may
experience vomiting or abdominal pain.
First aid steps
1. Advise the person to take the recommended dose of paracetamol to relieve their pain.
2. Encourage the person to drink lots of water in small amounts. A hot drink may relieve
their pain.
Access help
● The first aid provider should access medical care if someone with a sore throat:
○ has difficulty breathing
○ also experiences fever, dehydration, a painful neck or a rash
○ is not able to drink
○ has severe pain
○ is unable to manage any secretions (e.g. drooling)
○ develops an earache with pain radiating in one ear
○ has difficulty opening the mouth
○ has a high fever (39.5-41oC or 103.1-105.8oF) when taken with a thermometer that
measures heat from the tympanic membrane in the ear.
○ has severe throat pain and paracetamol has no effect
○ does not see any improvements after multiple days
○ experiences a sore throat frequently.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● It can be difficult for people to identify the cause of a sore throat (viral, bacterial or
environmental). Early actions, such as giving the ill person a drink, can help to eliminate
some causes and help the learner to identify if the condition is more serious.
● Encourage learners to look for other signs and symptoms and consider potential causes,
such as whether the person has been near someone with a cold or influenza, as this will
help inform treatment.
● In some contexts, local pharmacies can provide advice and treatment if the cause is viral
and help identify if the cause is more serious and requires medical attention.
FIRST AID | 429

● Follow your national regulations on who can administer non-prescribed painkillers as
their regulations overrule these guidelines.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Pictures that illustrate signs of more serious medical conditions can be helpful for
learners to understand if the person needs medical treatment.
● Emphasize that while most sore throats are usually not serious, they may be a symptom
of a more severe condition, and learners must recognize the situations in which to access
medical care.
● Draw connections to other topics within first aid when exploring sore throat, such as
recognizing signs of a serious illness, and assessment skills (e.g. asking about the history
of the person’s symptoms).
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
We identified four relevant evidence reviews on this topic, one by the British National Institute
for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), two systematic reviews and two evidence summaries
from the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP).
Medication options
For their guideline on acute sore throat, NICE conducted an evidence review that included
self-care interventions to relieve symptoms, such as paracetamol or ibuprofen, medicated
lozenges and mouth sprays (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, 2018)
No systematic reviews or randomized controlled trials were identified that compared
non-medicated lozenges, non-medicated mouthwashes or any other non-pharmacological
interventions with placebo or another intervention in people with acute sore throat.
Three randomized controlled trials found that aspirin, paracetamol and diclofenac potassium
were all more effective than placebo at improving pain and reducing fever in adults with an
acute sore throat, although it is not clear whether the improvements were clinically meaningful
(low- to moderate-certainty evidence).
Results from six randomized controlled trials found statistically significant improvements in pain
scores with medicated lozenges (containing benzocaine, hexylresorcinol or flurbiprofen)
compared with placebo, although the absolute improvements were small and may not be
clinically meaningful for some lozenges (low- to moderate-certainty evidence). In one
randomized controlled trial that provided evidence on the effectiveness of a chlorhexidine
gluconate and benzydamine hydrochloride mouth spray, the intensity of the clinical signs was
statistically significantly decreased, and the reported health state was statistically significantly
improved. No differences were observed in their quality of life (moderate- to high-certainty
evidence).
A CEBaP evidence summary from 2020 on the use of paracetamol in case of sore throat,
identified eight randomized controlled trials. The summary was updated in 2024 and did not
430 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

identify any new studies. It was demonstrated that paracetamol led to a statistically significant
decrease in pain intensity (upon swallowing) and a statistically significant increase in pain relief
and improvement in the swollen throat, compared to the placebo. A statistically significant
change in sore throat (ordinal outcome), sore throat or time point or spontaneous pain, using
paracetamol compared to placebo, could not be demonstrated in two studies. Evidence is of
moderate certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to limited sample size and/or
lack of data.
The Cochrane review (Spinks et al., 2013) determined that antibiotics can be used to treat a sore
throat. While most people usually recover within five to seven days, some develop complications.
A severe (but rare) complication is rheumatic fever, affecting the heart and joints. Streptococcal
infections rarely lead to airway swelling from abscess formation around the pharynx and tonsils.
Antibiotics reduce the duration of strep throat symptoms (streptococcal infection) by an average
of one to two days. However, these medications can also cause diarrhoea, a rash and other
unfavourable side effects. Additionally, when antibiotics are taken unnecessarily, they can
contribute to the development of bacterial antibiotic resistance. The review concluded that the
benefits do not outweigh the multiple and at times serious side effects. The NICE guideline also
states that antibiotics do not help alleviate throat pain caused by a virus (National Institute for
Health and Care Excellence, 2018)
Non-medication options
A CEBaP evidence summary found limited evidence in favour of consuming a hot drink (CEBaP,
2024). One uncontrolled before-and-after study showed that having a hot drink resulted in a
statistically significant decrease in the symptoms of sore throat. A statistically significant
decrease in symptoms of a sore throat when having a drink at room temperature could not be
demonstrated. Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to
limited sample size and lack of data. No new studies were identified in the most recent update
of the summary.
Non-systematic reviews
Limited evidence states that frequently gargling with warm water and salt may help to reduce
episodes of upper respiratory tract infection (G. M. Allan & Arroll, 2014).
In most patients, an acute sore throat is classified as uncomplicated; however, there are cases
where it is potentially complicated. It is rare for patients with a sore throat to develop a critical
illness and require immediate medical care (Gunnarsson et al., 2023).
We also reviewed two papers on the condition epiglottis, a type of airway swelling, that has
decreased over the last 20 years because of immunization; however, it is still prevalent in
unimmunized populations. Unimmunized people are at risk, including children, people in areas
with no immunizations, and people over 45 with outdated immunizations. Inflammation of the
epiglottis and surrounding tissues can lead to a narrowing of the airway and produce specific
stridor (high-pitched, abnormal breathing sounds). People with epiglottitis may initially
experience a sore throat, fever and breathing difficulty. They also characteristically do not have a
cough. First aid providers should help the person get into a position in which they are most
FIRST AID | 431

comfortable and help them access medical care immediately (Adil et al., 2015; Lindquist et al.,
2017).
432 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Earache
Key action
Advise the person to take the recommended dose of paracetamol (also known as
acetaminophen) and seek medical attention if the earache remains or gets worse over 48 hours.
Guidelines
● If trained and it is safe to do so, first aid providers may give the person paracetamol for
pain relief.*
Good practice points
● Advise the person not to pick their ear as this can cause damage to the ear.
● Medical advice should be sought when there is fever, fluid draining from the ear, vertigo
or loss of or decreased hearing associated with ear pain.
● The person should seek medical advice if the symptoms get worse or do not improve
within 48 hours.
● A source of heat can be held against the affected area and may reduce pain.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Teach children to avoid putting objects in their ears, such as cotton swabs or buds, or
pencils.
● People using noisy equipment should use ear defenders to reduce noise below 85
decibels.
● Be alert to earaches in people who use a hearing aid or who listen to music through
headphones.
● Be alert to earaches in people who spend a lot of time in the water, have an abscess or
infection in their gums or have had a cold or flu.
● Keep ears warm and dry where possible.
Early recognition
● The person may complain of pain, changes in hearing or a sensation of fullness in their
ear.
● The ear may be hot or sensitive to touch.
First aid steps
1. Help the person take the recommended dose of an over-the-counter painkiller (e.g.
paracetamol) to reduce pain.
2. Give them a warm compress, such as a hot water bottle wrapped in a towel, to hold
against their ear to reduce pain.
3. In the case that there is something in the ear canal (e.g. insects, beads), do not try to
remove it. Seek medical advice.
FIRST AID | 433

Access help
● Seek medical advice if there is discharge coming from the ear, hearing loss, vertigo or
fever. Seek medical advice if the earache does not improve within 48 hours.
Education considerations
Learner considerations
● Individuals who look after children or spend time in water contexts (e.g. public swimming
pool) could benefit from learning about earaches.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Three evidence summaries from the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice, all updated in 2024,
form the scientific foundation for this topic.
An evidence summary about using paracetamol in case of earache resulted in the identification
of a Cochrane systematic review, which included one randomized controlled trial. It was shown
that paracetamol resulted in a statistically significant decrease in pain in the ear compared to no
paracetamol. Evidence is of low certainty and results cannot be precise due to limited sample
size.
An evidence summary about heat or cold application in case of earache, as well as an evidence
summary about posture in case of earache, could not identify any studies.
Non-systematic reviews
Earache is an infection of the middle ear that causes inflammation and a build-up of fluid
behind the eardrum. An earache can develop from inflammation of the outer, middle or inner
ear and is often caused by an infection in the case of a cold or flu (Pukander, 1983). An ear can
become painful if an object is stuck in the ear canal. Pain from a tooth, such as an abscess, can
also cause an earache. An earache can cause temporary hearing loss and infection can cause
pus to gather in the middle of the ear, causing the eardrum to rupture.
Acute earache predominantly occurs in children. Canadian research shows that babies between
six and 15 months old are most commonly affected (Casselbrant & Mandel, 2003). It is estimated
that around one in every four children experiences at least one middle ear infection by the time
they are 10 years old. Medical care should be sought when there is fever, ear drainage, vertigo,
loss of hearing or decreased hearing associated with ear pain (Worrall, 2011).
An outer ear infection is one of the more common diseases and is also frequently encountered
in primary and paediatric care. More than 90% of cases are caused by bacteria (Wiegand et al.,
2019). An outer ear infection is thought to affect 10% of people at some stage and can present in
acute, chronic or necrotizing forms. The inflammation may be associated with eczema of the ear
canal and is more common in humid environments, in individuals with narrow ear canals, those
who swim regularly or use a hearing aid, and after mechanical trauma, such as from ear
cleaning or the use of earphones that go into the canal.
434 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Outer ear infection ranges in severity from a mild infection of the external auditory canal to a
life-threatening infection. Pain relief is an essential part of the treatment of outer ear infection
(Wiegand et al., 2019). A general practitioner should do the treatment. Individuals should not
clean their ears with cotton swabs, as this may damage them and encourage bacterial growth.
It has been estimated that in around 80% to 85% of children with an earache, the fever and pain
resolve within two to three days. After seven days, the absence of all symptoms and signs (except
for middle ear effusion) can be expected. The use of nasal drops or spray containing
xylometazoline has no effect on earache and is not recommended (Coleman & Moore, 2008).
FIRST AID | 435

Headache
Key action
Advise the person to take the recommended dose of paracetamol (also known as
acetaminophen).
Introduction
Headaches are a common condition. Typical symptoms include a mild to painful throbbing or
tight sensation in the head, pain behind the eyes (especially when looking at bright lights) or
dizziness. In more extreme cases, symptoms might include nausea or vomiting. A migraine is a
type of headache that is often reoccurring. Usually, a headache does not have a serious cause
and will pass; however, in some cases, it can indicate a more serious condition. First aid providers
should seek to understand the probable cause of the headache.
Guidelines
● If a person experiences a tension-type headache or an acute migraine headache, the first
aid provider should advise them to take 1,000 mg of paracetamol or nonsteroidal
anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen.**
Good practice points
● Paracetamol and other painkillers should only be used if a headache results from minor
causes such as tiredness or stress.
● Applying an ice pack or cold compress to the head or back of the neck may provide relief
from migraine symptoms.
● Bright lights (e.g. from an office or phone screen) may make a headache or migraine
worse. If the individual with either condition is sensitive to light, they should sit or lie in a
dark or dimly lit room to increase comfort and reduce the pain.
● Individuals with a headache should get fresh air, get enough sleep, drink water regularly
and take time to relax.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Understanding the causes of headaches can help prevent them from occurring.
Headaches can be caused by:
○ stress
○ poor sleep
○ flu or cold
○ meningitis
○ Hyperthermia
○ Head injury
○ Dehydration
436 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

○ alcohol consumption.
● The use of painkillers such as paracetamol is most effective when taken at the early stages
of the onset of pain. Taking a painkiller at the first sign of a headache may prevent a more
severe one from developing.
Early recognition
A person may have a headache if they:
● have pain behind their eyes
● feel a tightness around their head
● have painful throbbing in their head
● have an increased sensitivity to noise or light
● are nauseous.
The condition may indicate another more severe condition if the headache is accompanied by:
● fever
● vomiting (without diarrhoea or fever)
● skin rash
● memory loss
● neck pain or stiffness
● coordination problems
● problems with eyesight
● drowsiness or sleepiness
● trauma to the head.
The pain caused by a headache can range in frequency and severity.
First aid steps
1. Reduce anything that may worsen the headache (such as direct sunlight or noise). If safe
to do so, move the person to a quiet space.
2. Advise the person to take 1,000 mg of paracetamol.
3. Advise them to rest in a dark, quiet room. It may help to place an ice pack or cold
compress on their head or neck.
Access help
Headaches can sometimes indicate a more serious underlying disorder. Access emergency
medical care if the headache is severe and is accompanied by:
● vomiting (without diarrhoea or fever)
● a sore or stiff neck
● a rash, as this may indicate meningitis
● memory loss
● double or blurred vision
● fever
● signs of a Stroke.
FIRST AID | 437

Or if the severe pain:
● follows a head injury
● becomes worse very fast
● occurs during exertion (e.g. exercise) or a change in posture
● started suddenly within the last three months of pregnancy
● does not go away even after taking painkillers or reducing potential causes
● occurs regularly, without explanation, in children under the age of six.
Recovery
● The person may take painkillers for frequent tension headaches as required, following the
advice provided with the medication regarding frequency and over what time period.
However, if the headache persists or new patterns emerge, the person should seek a
medical assessment.
● Follow any recovery instructions from the healthcare professional.
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Areas with limited resources may not have painkillers readily available. Emphasize rest,
hydration, relaxation techniques and a cool cloth on the forehead or neck as effective first
aid support for mild headaches.
● Follow your local regulations on who can administer non-prescribed painkillers as the
regulations overrule these guidelines.
Facilitation tips
● Plan to facilitate the topic of headaches after other critical first aid skills so that learners
are better able to contextualize what a headache might indicate.
● Emphasize that while most headaches are usually not serious, they may be a symptom of
a more severe condition and learners must recognize the situations in which to access
medical care.
● Use scenarios as a way to connect headaches to other illnesses and injuries within first
aid. For example, run two to three scenarios where the person’s headache is caused by
something different. Learners must identify what the cause is and provide appropriate
care.
Learning connections
● Use this topic to practise assessment skills such as asking about the history of the
person’s symptoms.
● Draw connections to other topics within first aid when exploring headaches such as
recognizing signs of meningitis, Head injury and concussion and Stroke.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
Headache is the most prevalent neurological manifestation in adults and one of the leading
causes of disability worldwide. In children and adolescents, headaches have a substantial impact
438 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

on quality of life, limiting social and physical activities, and school attendance, which in turn
results in weaker learning outcomes, a higher risk of dropping out of school and a negative
effect on parents' careers. Migraine is also associated with the presence of two or more
conditions (comorbidities) such as allergies, sleep disorders, emotional and behavioural
problems, depression and anxiety, and academic performance (Onofri et al., 2023).
We used a 2020 evidence summary by the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP), as well
as an additional systematic review from 2010 on the topic of headaches. The CEBaP summary
was not updated, as the evidence was declared stable.
CEBaP identified a Cochrane systematic review, including 23 randomized controlled trials, on the
use of paracetamol for tension-type headaches, and selected five relevant randomized
controlled trials from a second Cochrane review about the use of paracetamol for acute
migraine headaches.
There is limited evidence in favour of using paracetamol for acute migraine headaches. It was
shown that 1,000 mg paracetamol resulted in a statistically significant decrease in acute
migraine headache, adverse events, functional disability and use of rescue medication
compared to placebo. Evidence is of moderate certainty and results cannot be considered
precise due to the low number of events and large variability of results.
In addition, there is high-certainty evidence in favour of using paracetamol for tension-type
headaches. It was shown that 1,000 mg paracetamol resulted in a statistically significant
decrease in tension-type headache and the use of rescue medication, compared to placebo. A
statistically significant decrease in tension-type headache and use of rescue medication, using
500–650 mg paracetamol compared to placebo, could not be demonstrated.
A systematic review from 2010 (Verhagen et al., 2010) evaluated the effectiveness of NSAIDs in
people with tension-type headaches. The evidence showed that NSAIDs are more effective than
a placebo medication for short-term pain relief. When comparing NSAIDs and paracetamol
there was a significant difference in favour of using NSAIDs. The review found no significant
differences between the different types of NSAIDs, but ibuprofen showed fewer short-term side
effects.
This review also concluded there is insufficient evidence for or against the effectiveness of the
preventive medication, physiotherapy, (spinal) manipulation or cognitive behavioural treatment
in people with tension-type headache.
Non-systematic reviews
One randomized controlled crossover clinical trial evaluated the benefits of cooling the blood
that passes through the carotid arteries versus no cooling treatment. An adjustable wrap
containing two ice packs was used as the cooling mechanism, targeting the carotid arteries at
the neck (where they are close to the skin’s surface). A total of 55 participants completed the
study successfully. They observed maximum pain reduction at 30 minutes, with a 31.8% ± 15.2%
decrease in pain in the treatment arm (where cooling was applied) compared to a 31.5% ± 20.0%
increase in pain at the same time interval in the control arm (no cooling). The study found that a
frozen neck wrap, applied at the onset of a migraine, targeting the carotid arteries in the neck,
FIRST AID | 439

significantly reduced the pain for participants with a migraine (p<.001) (Sprouse-Blum et al.,
2013).
440 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Hiccups
Key action
Comfort and reassure the person.
Introduction
A hiccup (or hiccough) is a sudden contraction of a muscle called the diaphragm that helps a
person to breathe in and that repeats several times per minute. During a hiccup, the muscle
contraction causes the person to take in a quick breath. This breath in is then interrupted by the
epiglottis closing over the windpipe, making a “hic” sound. Hiccups can occur “spontaneously”,
after eating or drinking, or in the context of a wide variety of illnesses or medical interventions.
Short hiccup spells are commonly experienced by healthy children and adults. Adult males are
more likely to experience episodes of hiccups (Lee et al., 2016).
Good practice points
● Home remedies such as holding one’s breath for a couple of seconds, doing the Valsalva
manoeuvre for a short time, sipping ice-cold water, swallowing some granulated sugar,
biting into a lemon or tasting vinegar may be effective.
● The use of gripe water is not recommended.
● A person with hiccups should seek medical advice if hiccups last longer than 48 hours or
if they have other symptoms in combination with hiccups, such as chest pain, weakness
in their limbs or face, headache or trouble keeping balance.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Giving babies a break from feeding and burping them may reduce the amount of air in
their stomachs and may help prevent hiccups.
● Eating slowly and avoiding alcohol may help reduce the likelihood of hiccups.
Early recognition
● A person with hiccups will take a short breath inward, followed by a “hic” sound. This can
occur between two and 60 times per minute.
First aid steps
1. Comfort the person and reassure them that hiccups usually pass after a few minutes.
2. If they can, encourage them to perform the Valsalva manoeuvre (take a deep breath in,
close the nose and mouth and gently blow out) for a short time.
There are other methods to stop hiccups that may be more suited to some contexts.
● Drink a glass of ice-cold water.
● Suck a sugar cube or an ice cube.
● Bite into a lemon.
FIRST AID | 441

● Hold your breath for a couple of seconds.
Access help
● Generally, hiccups pass within a few minutes. Seek medical advice if the hiccups last
longer than 48 hours or if the person has other symptoms in combination with hiccups,
such as chest pain, weakness in their limbs or face, headache or trouble keeping balance.
Education considerations
Learner considerations
● This topic may be useful for some audiences such as school nurses, new parents or other
people who care for children.
Facilitation tips
● Define what a hiccup is, how it happens and discuss different safe home remedies.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
The Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) developed an evidence summary concerning
techniques to stop hiccups, but no studies could be identified.
Non-systematic reviews
Hiccups are classified based on their duration. Short hiccup spells are commonly experienced by
healthy children and adults. Hiccups resolve spontaneously or with simple home remedies and
do not require medical attention. Short hiccup bouts are mostly associated with gastric
distention, such as drinking carbonated beverages, consuming spicy, very hot or very cold food
or alcohol intake. In the foetus, hiccups are normally present after the eighth week of gestation
during behavioural active phases and tend to persist after birth in babies (Launois et al., 1993).
Babies often get hiccups when feeding. Hiccups do not normally harm a baby and will go away
once the baby is one year old. Usually, a baby will have the hiccups for only a few minutes. Taking
a break from feeding to burp the baby may reduce the amount of air in their stomach and
prevent hiccups. No scientific evidence has been found for the use of gripe water (a mixture of
herbs and water traditionally used to treat colic and other stomach problems). It is not
recommended for babies (Adhisivam, 2012).
The purpose of some home remedies is the attempt to interrupt the reflex arc thought to
maintain repetitive diaphragmatic contractions. This is most often attempted by breath-holding,
the Valsalva manoeuvre (a particular way of breathing that increases pressure in the chest) or
breathing into a paper bag. Other home remedies to stop hiccups include sucking an ice cube,
sucking a sugar cube with vinegar, chewing a piece of lemon, holding your breath and scaring
someone with hiccups. These home remedies are, in general, not harmful and may be effective
only in shortening acute hiccups and not in the treatment of persistent or recurrent hiccups
(Goldstein, 1999; Quiroga et al., 2016).
Prolonged hiccups are rare in healthy people. However, hiccups lasting more than 48 hours are
not uncommon in people with advanced disease. They are disabling and may induce
442 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

depression, weight loss and sleep deprivation. A wide variety of pathological conditions can
cause chronic hiccups, including myocardial infarction, brain tumour, renal failure, prostate
cancer and abdominal surgery (Quiroga et al., 2016; Rey & Solari, 2010). Any hiccup episode
lasting longer than 48 hours is described as chronic and needs medical attention (Bacak &
Danner, 2023; Petroianu, 2005).
FIRST AID | 443

444 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

ENVIRONMENTAL
FIRST AID | 445

Hyperthermia
Key action
Rapidly cool the person to reduce their body temperature.
Introduction
Hyperthermia occurs when the body reaches abnormally high temperatures because it is
unable to regulate its internal temperature. It occurs when the body produces or absorbs too
much heat, or a combination of both. Hyperthermia can be mild (heat exhaustion) or severe
(heatstroke). There are two types of hyperthermia: exertional heat illness (EHI), when the body
produces too much heat, and non-exertional heat illness (or “classic” hyperthermia), when the
body absorbs too much heat. Children and the elderly are most at risk for a “classic”
hyperthermia.
Exertional heatstroke is a medical emergency characterized by life-threatening hyperthermia
and central nervous system dysfunction during or directly after exercise. Early recognition and
aggressive cooling reduce the morbidity and mortality of a person with heatstroke (Van den
Bersselaar, 2020).
Guidelines
● In the case of an adult experiencing severe hyperthermia (heatstroke) due to intense
physical activity, the first aid provider should consider immersing the person from the
neck down in cold water (1-26°C/33.8-78.8°F) until a core body temperature of less than
39°C (102.2°F) is achieved. If this is not possible, cool the person using any other active
cooling technique (e.g. a wet sheet, water or ice packs placed in the armpits, neck and
groin area).**
Good practice points
● The person should stop all physical activity and be removed from the hot environment to
a cool place.
● For people with hyperthermia due to intense physical activity, first aid providers can also
consider using other immediate active or passive cooling techniques available to them
(e.g. cold-water immersion of hands or feet, ice packs, cold showers, fanning, ice sheets
and towels).
● If the person has hyperthermia due to intense physical activity and is responsive enough,
they may have something cool to drink. This may include water, a water and sugar
solution or a water and salt solution (e.g. a homemade oral rehydration solution or a
sports drink). See Dehydration.
● The first aid provider should access emergency medical services (EMS) if the person:
○ shows unusual behaviour, confusion or becomes unresponsive
○ has a seizure
○ has a body temperature above 39°C (102.2°F)
446 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

○ stops sweating
○ cannot drink without vomiting.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● The risk of hyperthermia depends on a person’s ability to adapt to heat. This adaptability
depends on four factors:
○ activity level (e.g. limit physical activities during the hottest part of the day)
○ freedom of choice of clothing (e.g. wear appropriate clothing for the temperature
and activity)
○ adaptability to the environment (e.g. take frequent breaks to drink water)
○ personal factors (e.g. a person’s health and fitness level)
● In places where there is a high risk of hyperthermia, consider making cooling methods
available so there is no delay in first aid when hyperthermia occurs.
● Be aware that EHI can also occur in cool environments.
● Monitor weather conditions.
● Plan work and rest times if working in warm conditions.
● Develop an action plan in case conditions suddenly change and there is a higher risk of
hyperthermia (e.g. a sudden heatwave).
● Monitor urine colour for signs of dehydration.
Early recognition
The person’s condition will depend on how much the body has overheated.
Mild to moderate hyperthermia (heat exhaustion):
● normal or mildly higher temperature
● cool, pale, clammy skin
● excessive sweating
● thirsty
● headache
● muscle cramps
● rapid, weak pulse
● fainting or dizziness.
Severe hyperthermia (heatstroke or heat stress combined with an altered mental status):
● the person has stopped sweating (indicating they are severely dehydrated)
● high body temperature (above 40°C/104°F)
● dry, flushed, hot skin
● nausea
● muscle spasms
● feeling lightheaded or dizzy, disoriented
● pain throughout the body
● unusual behaviour (aggressive combativeness or drowsiness) or signs of confusion
● seizure or possible loss of responsiveness.
FIRST AID | 447

First aid steps
1. Advise the person to stop all physical activity immediately. Help them to rest in a cool
place and to remove any excess clothing.
Mild to moderate hyperthermia
1. Cool the person down using any technique available, such as immersing their hands and
feet in cold water, applying ice packs to their neck and groin areas, encouraging them to
have a cold shower or fanning them.
2. If the person is not vomiting, give them some water, sports drink or cold tea with sugar to
drink.
3. Monitor the person’s temperature and level of response. Try to reduce their temperature
to less than 39°C (102.2oF).
4. A person with mild to moderate hyperthermia will usually recover without medical care.
Monitor them closely in case their condition does not improve or worsens. And access
EMS if in any doubt.
Severe hyperthermia
1. Access EMS immediately. Heatstroke is life-threatening. Be alert to any unusual behaviour
(aggressive combativeness or drowsiness) or signs of confusion.
2. Help the person lie down in a cool place and remove any excess clothing.
3. Start cooling the person down immediately. Immerse the person in cold water (1-26°
C/33.8-78.8°F) for about 15 minutes, or until their temperature has dropped to less than
39°C (102.2°F). If this is not possible (e.g. cold water is unavailable or the person is confused
and refuses), cool the person using any other active cooling technique (e.g. place cool, wet
sheets or towels on the person or wet them with cold water and place ice packs on their
neck and groin areas). Fanning the person will also help cool them down.
● Severe hyperthermia (heatstroke) is a time critical illness and the priority is rapid and
effective cooling prior to transfer to a hospital.
● If medical personnel are available, the person’s core (rectal) temperature should be
recorded as soon as possible as an indicator of severity and to guide treatment (DeGroot
et al., 2022).
Recovery
● After cooling the person down, monitor them closely, as they may start to feel cold and
shiver, especially if they are still wearing wet clothes. If this happens, give them a blanket.
● Encourage the person to drink oral rehydration solutions, sports drinks or tea with sugar.
Start with a small amount and wait to ensure the person does not vomit the liquid back
up.
● Encourage the person to rest. In most cases, people with mild to moderate hyperthermia
will recover fully.
448 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Education considerations
Context considerations
● Heat illnesses have become more common in areas affected by climate change.
Education and prevention strategies should be developed in these areas, especially for
the most vulnerable populations.
● Programme designers should consider any cultural or legal implications involved with
removing the person’s clothing and emphasize the importance of dignity to learners.
● In areas where ice or cold water is not readily available, identify the cooling methods that
may be available, such as removing the person from the heat, wetting them, fanning
them, encouraging rest and removing any excess clothing.
● In areas with extreme heat, workplaces may have regulations in place, such as stopping
outdoor work during the hottest hours of the day throughout the hottest months of the
year.
Learner considerations
● Infants and older adults are susceptible to heat exposure as their body systems are less
efficient.
● Certain professions and athletes may have greater exposure to heat. Younger and faster
runners are at higher risk for exertional hyperthermia and would benefit from learning
about the risks and preventive strategies to avoid heat illness (Breslow et al., 2021).
Facilitation tips
● Focus on prevention and help learners recognize the warning signs of early hyperthermia
so they can prevent severe hyperthermia.
● The key action is to cool the person quickly, so the first aid provider needs to prioritize
readily available cooling methods.
● Emphasize that the body temperature should be reduced to 39°C (102.2°F) and not less to
prevent over-cooling.
Learning connections
● Combine this topic with other first aid emergencies (such as Breathing difficulties or
Seizures) so learners can recognize and differentiate the symptoms of hyperthermia.
● Dehydration is common in people with hyperthermia.
Scientific foundation
Systematic reviews
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) first aid task force completed a
systematic review of different techniques for cooling heatstroke and exertional hyperthermia
(Douma, 2020), and the Centre for Evidence-based Practice (CEBaP) developed evidence
summaries on reducing activity and drinking (2020). The CEBaP summaries were not updated
as the evidence was declared stable. The ILCOR review was updated in 2022 and two new
systematic reviews and two observational studies were identified. However, the topic did not
meet criteria for an updated systematic review and existing treatment recommendations
remained unchanged. For adults with exertional hyperthermia or exertional heatstroke,
immediate active cooling using whole-body (neck down) water immersion techniques or, if
FIRST AID | 449

unavailable, any other active cooling technique is recommended. For adults and children with
non-exertional heatstroke, no recommendations for or against any specific cooling techniques
were made (Wyckoff et al., 2022b; Wyckoff et al., 2022c).
Cold-water immersion (14°C–15°C/57.2°F–59°F)
The following studies all relate to adults with exertional hyperthermia. In seven non-randomized
controlled trials, a faster rate of core body temperature reduction was found with cold water
immersion of the torso compared with passive cooling (low certainty of evidence). In three
controlled trials, no significant mean difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction
was found between cold water immersion of the torso compared with temperate-water
immersion (20°C-26°C/68°F-78.8°F) (very low certainty evidence). One controlled trial showed no
significant mean difference in the rate of body temperature reduction between the use of cold
water immersion (14°C/57.2°F) of the torso and the use of colder water immersion (8°C/46.4°F)
(low certainty evidence). Two controlled trials showed no significant mean difference in the rate
of body temperature reduction when using cold water immersion (14°C/57.2°F) of the torso
compared with ice-water immersion (2°C-5°C/35.6°F-41°F) (very low certainty evidence).
Cold-water immersion (10°C–17°C/50.0°F–62.6°F) of hands and feet
In six controlled trials, a faster rate of core body temperature reduction was found with cold
water immersion of hands and/or feet (10°C-17°C/50.0-62.6°F) compared with passive cooling in
adults with exertional hyperthermia (moderate certainty evidence).
Colder-water immersion (9°C–12°C/48.2°F–52.6°F)
The following studies in this paragraph relate to adults with exertional hyperthermia. In three
non-randomized controlled trials, a faster rate of core body temperature reduction was found
with the use of colder-water immersion of the torso (9°C-12°C/48.2°F-52.6°F) compared with
passive (moderate certainty evidence). One controlled trial did not show a significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction with the use of colder-water
immersion (9°C/48.2°F) up to the waist compared with passive cooling (low certainty evidence).
One controlled trial showed a faster rate of core body temperature reduction with the use of
colder-water immersion of the torso (11.7°C/53.0°F) compared with temperate water
(23.5°C/74.3°F) (moderate certainty evidence). One controlled trial showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction with the use of colder-water
immersion of the hands or feet (10°C-12°C/50.0°F- 52.6°F) compared with the use of colder-water
immersion of the torso (low-certainty evidence).
Ice-water immersion (1°C–5°C/33.8°F–41.0°F)
In one small cohort study where ice-water immersion of the torso (5°C-10°C/33.8°F-41°F)
together with the administration of intravenous normal saline at room temperature 0.9% was
compared with applying ice packs to the armpits in adults with exertional heatstroke, there were
no deaths in either group (very low-certainty evidence). In four non-randomized controlled trials
in adults with exertional hyperthermia and one cohort study on people with exertional
heatstroke, a faster rate of core body temperature reduction was shown when comparing ice
water immersion of the torso (1°C-5°C/33.8°F-41°F) with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence).
The same was found in two non-randomized controlled trials comparing ice water torso
450 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

immersion (2°C/35.6°F) with temperate water immersion (20°C–26°C/68.0°F–78.8°F)
(moderate-certainty evidence). One small cohort study in adults with exertional heatstroke
showed a faster rate of core body temperature reduction in case of ice-water immersion
(5°C-10°C/33.8°F-41.0°F) combined with the administration of intravenous 0.9% normal saline,
compared with applying ice packs to the armpits (low certainty evidence).
Evaporative cooling (mist and fan)
Two controlled studies in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction between evaporative cooling and
passive cooling (low-certainty evidence). Two controlled trials showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction with evaporative cooling compared
with the use of ice packs applied to the neck, armpit and groin (one trial, low-certainty evidence),
or compared with the use of commercial ice packs applied to the neck, armpit and groin (one
trial, low-certainty evidence). One controlled trial showed no significant difference in the rate of
core body temperature reduction when using evaporative cooling and commercial ice packs on
the neck, armpit and groin compared with evaporative cooling alone (low-certainty evidence).
One controlled trial showed no significant difference in the rate of core body temperature
reduction when using a combination of evaporative cooling and commercial ice packs on the
neck, armpits and groin, compared with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence).
Commercial ice packs
Two controlled trials in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction when using commercial ice packs on
the neck, groin and armpits compared with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence). One
controlled trial in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean difference in
the rate of core body temperature reduction when using commercial ice packs on the whole
body compared with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence). One controlled trial in adults with
exertional hyperthermia showed a faster rate of core body temperature reduction when
applying commercial ice packs to the cheeks, palms and soles compared to passive cooling, as
well as when compared to applying commercial ice packs to the neck, groin and armpit
(moderate-certainty evidence).
Fan alone
Two controlled trials in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction with the use of fanning alone
compared with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence).
Cold shower (20.8°C/69.4°F)
In one non-randomized controlled trial in adults with exertional hyperthermia, a faster rate of
core body temperature reduction was found with cold showers compared with passive cooling
(moderate-certainty evidence).
FIRST AID | 451

Hand-cooling devices
Three controlled trials in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction when using hand-cooling devices
compared with passive cooling (low-certainty evidence).
Cooling vests and jackets
Two controlled trials in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction when using the Arctic Heat cooling
jacket compared to passive cooling (low-certainty evidence). Five controlled trials in adults with
exertional hyperthermia compared the use of various cooling vests with passive cooling. None of
the studies showed a significant mean difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction
when compared to passive cooling (very low-certainty evidence).
Reflective blankets
One controlled trial in adults with exertional hyperthermia showed no significant mean
difference in the rate of core body temperature reduction between the use of reflective blankets
and passive cooling (low-certainty evidence).
Physical activity
There is limited evidence from one observational case-control study in favour of reducing
physical activity. The study showed that decreasing activity resulted in a statistically significant
decrease in the risk of heatstroke during heatwaves. Evidence is of very low certainty and results
cannot be considered precise due to lack of data. (CEBaP, 2020.)
Drinking fluids
There is limited evidence from one observational case-control study in favour of taking in extra
liquids. The study found that consuming extra liquids led to a statistically significant decrease in
the risk of heatstroke during heatwaves. Evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be
considered precise due to a lack of data. (CEBaP, 2020.)
Non-systematic reviews
Early recognition and immediate cooling improve outcomes by reducing the area (in degree
minutes) under the body temperature versus time curve. Several high-profile cases have
demonstrated the catastrophic consequences associated with delayed diagnoses. Exertional
heat illness (EHI) can also occur in cool environments and should be considered a potential
cause of collapse in exercise settings, especially when accompanied by unexplained
neurological symptoms (DeGroot et al., 2022; van den Bersselaar et al., 2020).
EHI is one of the rare exceptions in which treatment (cooling) is prioritized over transport (Belval
et al., 2018). It is essential that organizers of mass-participation sporting events, athletic team
medical personnel and military leaders adequately prepare for the possibility of EHI casualties
when conditions warrant it (DeGroot et al., 2022).
According to Caldwell (2022), iced-sheet cooling (ISC) increases the cooling rate of those
recovering from exertional hyperthermia. Based on the observed cooling rate, we can
extrapolate that ISC would reduce core temperature by ∼approximately 2°C within 30 minutes in
452 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

a case of EHI. The conclusion is that ISC provides a safe and effective alternative in fields where
cold water immersion resources may not be readily available.
During cold-water immersion (CWI), several monitoring methods are available to determine the
appropriate cessation, including continuous temperature measurement, regular temperature
checks, the use of an equation to predict immersion time and symptom observation. There is no
consensus on the best system; however, a monitoring method should be used to prevent
overcooling. This case series highlights the importance of proper CWI execution to prevent harm
(Stone & Sanchez, 2023).
FIRST AID | 453

Dehydration
Key action
Give the person plenty of fluids to drink as tolerated.
Introduction
Dehydration is a shortage of water in the body and occurs when a person loses more fluid than
they take in. It can result from a range of conditions (vomiting, diarrhoea, heat stress, fever, etc.),
vigorous activity in hot and humid environments, or when wearing excess clothing. These
conditions result in a significant loss of water through sweat and other bodily fluids. They also
cause the person to lose electrolytes, which are essential for the body to function normally.
When the body is dehydrated, it can experience cramps, changes in mental capacity or even
shock (which can be life-threatening if left untreated), especially in small children or older adults.
Guidelines
● First aid providers should encourage people with mild dehydration to drink fluids (e.g.
water or diluted apple juice in children older than six months).**
● In more severe cases, first aid providers should rehydrate the person using either
commercially prepared oral rehydration salts or a pre-prepared salt package that
complies with the World Health Organization’s recommendations for oral rehydration
salts solutions.**
● First aid providers can use 4%–9% carbohydrate-electrolyte drinks to care for
exertion-related dehydration. If these are not available or not tolerated, alternative
beverages include water, 0%–3.9% carbohydrate-electrolyte solution, coconut water or 2%
or low-fat milk.*
● Breastfeeding for babies should be continued.**
Good practice points
● Breastfed babies require more frequent feedings as they generally receive less milk each
time they feed than bottle-fed babies. Breastfeeding can be supplemented with oral
rehydration solution (ORS).
● If using formula, babies should receive normal amounts of milk (powder dissolved in
