individuals in coping with a traumatic event.
● Be sure to correctly define sexual and gender-based violence.
● Show video(s) explaining the difference between empathy and sympathy. Learners can
discuss and attempt to use empathetic responses in different scenarios or conversations
when supporting individuals.
● Learners can share their own experiences of being listened to. Explore the good qualities
of an active listener and discuss some attitudes or behaviours to be avoided when
recalling personal experiences.
A common tool used in PFA is Look, Listen, Link. See Psychological First Aid published by the
Red Cross Red Crescent Movement MHPSS Hub for details of this.
It is incredibly important to create and maintain a safe learning environment. Learners
should feel safe to share and discuss their ideas and experiences without fear of judgment.
FIRST AID | 489

Scientific foundation
Systematic review
Critical incident stress debriefing (individual level)
A single-session debrief is when people are encouraged to share their emotions and thoughts
after experiencing a traumatic event. Several systematic reviews and meta-analyses found that a
single session debrief does not prevent the onset of post-traumatic stress, and may even
increase the risk (Ancarani et al., 2025; Rose et al., 2002; Stileman & Jones, 2023; van Emmerik et
al., 2002). As a result, the WHO guidelines and National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) guidelines do not recommend using single-session individual debriefing after a
traumatic event.
Psychosocial support group intervention
The Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) developed an evidence summary about the
effect of a brief psychosocial support group intervention shortly after a potential traumatic event
on the development of adverse mental health outcomes. The review identified five randomized
controlled trials, two non-randomized controlled trials, and eight cohort studies which studied
the effect of Critical Incident Stress Debriefing (CISD), stress management education and a
group intervention called “512 Psychological Intervention Model” based on CISD with additional
training on group cohesion. The largest experimental study and three smaller randomized
controlled trials found no evidence of an effect of CISD on post-traumatic stress disorder
symptoms. Limited evidence was found for an effect of CISD on aggression (but only in
participants with high exposure to potentially traumatic events, not in the total sample), alcohol
use, non-specific psychopathology and coping. On the other hand, an effect of CISD on
depression, anxiety and quality of life could not be demonstrated. Limited evidence was found
showing that the 512 Psychological Intervention Model resulted in a decrease of post-traumatic
stress, anxiety and depression, compared to CISD or no intervention, which might suggest that
stimulating group cohesion is an added value. In two studies the effect of psycho-education as a
group intervention on mental health outcomes could not be demonstrated.
Communication and social support
An evidence summary developed by CEBaP and updated in 2024 identified seven cohort
studies. The results are outlined below.
The following factors are thought to be associated with fewer symptoms of post-traumatic stress
disorder after a traumatic event:
● Higher engagement and concern (“expressed concern”) from the romantic partner, such
as asking how the partner is feeling, encouraging them to talk about feelings and
showing interest in what they are going through
● Positive disclosure, specifically sharing positive emotions linked to military missions with
individuals without combat experience
The following factors are associated with more symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder after
a traumatic event:
490 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● Negative response or social reaction (turning against the victim, such as blaming the
victim) to the disclosure of an attack of (sexual) violence
● Positive disclosure, specifically sharing positive emotions linked to military missions with
individuals with combat experience
An effect on symptoms of post-traumatic stress could not be demonstrated for the following
factors:
● Negative disclosure: sharing negative emotions linked to the event.
● Positive social reaction to disclosure of an attack of (sexual) violence.
● Negative social response in the form of unsupportive acknowledgement.
● Social support, including positive support (i.e. the availability of others, confiding in others,
emotional and practical support), satisfaction with perceived support, and the extent to
which a person experiences emotional support (i.e. the number of people a person can
talk to and share concerns with)
● Dysfunctional communication during conflict: (lack of) mutual constructive
communication, demanding/withdrawn communication, mutual avoidance and
withholding and ineffective discussion
The evidence is of very low certainty and results cannot be considered precise due to lack of data
and limited sample sizes.
Psychological first aid
Psychological first aid is a method of helping people in distress to feel calm and supported in
coping with their challenges. It addresses both the emotional and social needs of individuals,
with the intention of helping people use their resources, enhance their resilience and make
informed decisions. There are a limited number of studies that researched the effectiveness of
psychological first aid as a complete programme. Two outdated systematic reviews on
psychological first aid both concluded that there is a lack of controlled studies to support it
(Dieltjens et al., 2014; Fox et al., 2012). However, more recently, eight randomized controlled trials
became available, although they are at high risk of bias (Hermosilla et al., 2022; Wang et al.,
2024). In these studies no consistent improvement of PTSD symptoms or depression could be
demonstrated; however in four studies a significant reduction of anxiety was shown when
compared to the control being psychoeducation, a group discussion, social acknowledgement
or drawing (Wang et al., 2024).
Non-systematic review
Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid, provided by trained people, is widely supported by expert opinion and
rational conjecture as a tool to help people who have experienced a traumatic event(s) (Shultz &
Forbes, 2013).
Through observation and expert experience, there is a widespread consensus and support for
the five intervention principles that should guide and inform any psychosocial support
intervention and prevention efforts at the early to mid-term stages of an emergency. These
principles facilitate survivors’ short-term adjustment and long-term adaptation after a crisis
(Hobfoll et al., 2007). The five principles involve the promotion of:
FIRST AID | 491

● calm
● hope
● connectedness
● a sense of safety
● a sense of self-efficacy and community efficacy.
First aid for mental health problems
In 2019, the Belgian Red Cross-Flanders developed guidelines to provide first aid to people
experiencing mental health problems based on a systematic review of scientific evidence
(evidence summaries developed by CEBaP, as mentioned above), expert opinion and the
preferences of the target population. The guidelines, updated in 2024, include specific
recommendations to support people exposed to shocking events (Stroobants et al., 2023).
Sexual and gender-based violence (SGBV)
Within the scope of providing first aid, individuals may encounter situations where the injured
people have experienced some forms of sexual and gender-based violence, such as genital
injuries in both adults and children. Sexual and gender-based violence (SGBV) is a broad term
referring to any harmful act that leads to (or is likely to lead to) physical, sexual or psychological
harm or suffering to someone on the basis of their gender. Gender-based violence is a result of
gender inequality and abuse of power, including but not limited to the imposition of sexual
violence, domestic violence, trafficking, forced or early marriage, forced prostitution and sexual
exploitation and abuse (IFRC, 2015). It was estimated that about one-third of women experience
some type of SGBV in their lifetime (World Health Organization, 2024c). It is also crucial to
consider SGBV committed against men, boys and sexual minority groups despite the lack of
data on its occurrence globally. SGBV can take different forms and happen in diverse situations
and contexts across the world and is now a focus of humanitarian challenges (International
Committee of the Red Cross and the International Federation of Red Cross and Crescent
Societies, 2015; International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, 2015).
Reactions to trauma
The IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support (2018) found that during or immediately
after a severely distressing event, many people react with a feeling of shock (numbness, disbelief
or like time stands still). They may also have physical reactions such as an increased heart rate,
sweating, trembling or shortness of breath. Some people feel dizzy or nauseous and may find it
difficult to think clearly or grasp the situation at hand. These reactions can last for minutes or
hours during or after an event. In some cases, people may be severely impaired by their
reactions. In other instances, people may remain relatively calm and actively stick to their
routines, especially if they have already had the chance to develop practices for the emergency.
Particularly in the first hours and days after the event, reactions can vary significantly between
people, and may also change rapidly on an individual basis. A rapid change commonly appears
as “loud” reactions like shouting or crying and “silent” reactions like feeling numb or being
unable to recognize the full impact of the event.
Reactions after an initial state of shock vary depending on each person’s perceived severity of
the event. If the event was traumatic and frightening, the person might feel relieved that they
survived, but also guilty, sad or angry if others were hurt or killed. Sometimes there can be fear
492 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

that the frightening event will happen again, such as aftershocks after an earthquake or the
possibility of further violence in a situation of armed conflict. In this state, it can be challenging
to make decisions and to communicate clearly with others.
Many people experience physical reactions in the first few days after a distressing event, such as
physical pains, and may lose their appetite or ability to sleep. If a person’s life has changed
dramatically due to a distressing event, it may be difficult to carry out everyday activities and
focus on the next steps. Some people may feel enormously angry with other people and the
world. In contrast, others may experience a sense of deep sadness and grief or hopelessness
about the future and lose interest in interacting with others or doing things they used to do.
Withdrawal, disappointment, avoiding others and feeling misunderstood are also common
reactions to a crisis.
FIRST AID | 493

Suicidal ideation
Key action
Engage the person in conversation, assess the suicidal threat, ensure safety and link to
appropriate resources while providing empathetic support.
Introduction
Suicidal ideation refers to the situation in which a person thinks about ending their own life. The
presentation of suicidal ideation can vary from being fleeting and vague to very concrete.
Concrete ideation involves method selection, planning and/or intention to complete suicide. A
person could act upon ideation, undertaking an action in which they expect to end their own
life. Depending on the fatality of the outcome, the action is considered a suicidal attempt or
suicide. According to the World Health Organization, close to 800,000 people die due to suicide
every year (World Health Organization, 2025). It is a global phenomenon that spans multiple
countries. Yet, suicides are preventable with a comprehensive multi-sectoral prevention strategy.
Vulnerability for suicidality is determined by a complex interaction of biological, psychological
and social factors. Exposure to stressful life events can contribute to the development of suicidal
ideation. During or immediately after a severely distressing event, many people react by going
into what is commonly known as a state of shock, where it feels like time stands still, along with
feelings of numbness and unreality. This can be accompanied by physical and emotional
reactions. In some cases, people may think of attempting suicide or even perform suicidal
behaviour (Howarth et al., 2020).
Self-harm is when someone intentionally hurts themselves, for example, by cutting or burning
their skin, or poisoning themselves (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2018). It should be noted that not
all self-harm is suicidal behaviour, so it is important to explore what the self-harming actions
mean to the person affected. The most important distinction between self-harm and suicidal
behaviour is the intent to take one's life.
Suicide is when someone intentionally takes their own life (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2018).
Suicidal ideation refers to thoughts about or a preoccupation with killing oneself (American
Psychological Association, n.d.-b). Attempted suicide, also called a suicide attempt, is a
deliberate but incompleted attempt to take one’s own life (American Psychological Association,
n.d.-a). It is important to always take someone who threatens to harm or kill themselves seriously
and not leave the person alone till more help arrives (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2018).
Guidelines
● Having a confidant or someone to talk to may decrease the risk of suicidality.*
● Staying connected to and befriending the person at risk may decrease psychological
distress in people with suicidal ideation.*
494 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Good practice points
● Psychological first aid principles can be used when helping people with suicidal ideation.
● The following actions may help people with suicidal ideation:
○ Engaging the person in conversation.
○ Assessing the risk of suicide and harm.
■ Talk openly about suicidal thoughts.
■ Ensure personal safety in case of an immediate threat.
○ Listening non-judgmentally.
○ Encouraging the person to seek professional support and other social support.
○ Ensuring the safety and protection of the person.
In emergency and imminent risk situations:
● DO NOT leave the individual alone.
● Call emergency services.
● Remove any means of self-harm.
● Bring the individual to a safe, supportive environment, if possible.
● Include trusted supports of the individual (such as a friend or family member), if possible.
● Consult with and involve the supervisor or a Mental Health and Psychosocial Support
(MHPSS) specialist as soon as possible during the situation.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Obtain updated information about safe and available resources and referrals from
supervisors, MHPSS-trained professionals or staff.
● Before offering support to others, first aid providers should pay attention to their own
psychological well-being and readiness because they may also find themselves exposed
to trauma, loss and devastation, injury and even death in emergencies or crises. It is
important for the first aid responders to work closely and communicate their conditions
and needs in challenging situations with their team (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2018).
Early recognition
Individuals may present themselves in very different ways when they are in despair and at risk of
self-harm or suicide. One person may never mention their intent to harm themselves and may
appear calm, despite how they are feeling inside. Another person may be openly expressing
their desire to end their life. There are a variety of ways that individuals may present themselves,
including:
● visibly distressed
● apathetic
● calm
● depressed
● anxious
● affected by alcohol or drug use
● erratic.
FIRST AID | 495

Warning signs in adults
There are certain warning signs that may indicate a person is thinking about or planning
suicide. These include:
● Threats of suicide or self-injury.
● Planning for suicide (e.g. searching for methods, acquiring means). The risk that
somebody will take their own life increases if somebody has a clear plan (how, where,
when), has the means to execute the plan, and/or declares the intention to act.
● Talking, writing or online posting about death or suicide.
● Making arrangements, appearing to “say goodbye”, getting affairs in order.
● Affective changes include pronounced negative feelings (e.g. hopelessness, feeling stuck
with no way out, anger, vengefulness, seeing no reason to live, feeling like a burden to
others, anxiety or dramatic mood changes.
● Changes in behaviour such as increased risk behaviour (e.g. recklessness, increased
consumption of alcohol or other substances), sleep problems or social withdrawal.
Warning signs in youth
Certain warning signs may indicate a young person has a higher risk of performing suicide.
These include:
● changes in sleeping and eating habits
● loss of interest in activities they previously enjoyed, such as sports or art
● isolating themselves or withdrawing from family and friends
● headaches, stomach aches and other physical symptoms that cannot be explained by a
medical condition
● refusing to go to school or beginning to do poorly in school
● talking about death often or asking questions about death
● researching or looking up ways to die
● drawing pictures, writing stories, plays, or creating other artwork about death or suicide
● hopelessness
● giving away their favourite things
● saying things like "I wish I were dead" or "It would be better if I weren't here".
First aid steps
To address the immediate needs of people having suicidal ideation, the principles, skills and
techniques from psychological first aid (PFA) are useful. You may provide support with the
concepts of LOOK, LISTEN and LINK.
Communicate clear limits on confidentiality. The person who is at risk must understand that if
the first aid provider believes the person is in imminent risk of harming themselves, the first aid
provider will need to break confidentiality to help keep the person safe.
1. Never leave a person alone if they have expressed that they are at risk.
496 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

a. If you are on the phone or working remotely with the person, try to get emergency
backup numbers for people in their lives with whom they trust and feel safe, if at all
possible, as well as information on their current location. [LOOK, LISTEN]
b. If the person is at home, invite someone from the household to support the
person, if it is safe to do so. [LINK]
2. Have the individual remove anything that could be used to harm themselves [LOOK,
LISTEN]. Ask carers to help with this, if possible. [LINK]
3. If you are meeting someone in person and you think they may be at risk of harm, make
the space as safe and secure as possible.
a. This may mean finding a quiet, calm place to speak, removing anything
dangerous, or perhaps moving away from a room on an upper level of a building or
from one with the windows open. [LOOK]
4. Use stabilization, calming techniques and basic helping skills to help keep the person
calm and to reduce distress. [LISTEN]
5. Always contact your supervisor or refer to MHPSS-trained professionals or staff when
responding to someone at risk. [LINK]
6. When someone has already attempted suicide (e.g. intoxication or excessive bleeding),
the main course of action is to provide first aid and medical attention immediately. After
stabilization, refer to a mental health professional for follow-up. [LINK]
Access help
● Ensure the person is not alone before leaving to get help. [LINK]
● Ask if the person knows where to seek help and advice. Be aware of and inform the
person about the range of professional help available. [LINK]
● Encourage the person to see a health/mental health professional. [LINK]
Education considerations
This section provides only a brief introduction of how to support people having suicidal ideation.
It aims to provide tips for learners to develop more awareness and to streamline the first aid
procedures. It does not expect learners to deliver PFA without formal training.
Context considerations
● In some contexts, suicide is considered illegal, and there are corresponding laws and
regulations of which first aid providers should be aware.
● Talking about suicide does not increase the likelihood of someone attempting it.
Discussing suicide openly and sensitively can help reduce stigma, provide support and
encourage individuals to seek help.
● Consider whether there is any stigma surrounding mental health and suicide within the
context and adjust education accordingly, aiming to diminish stigma.
● Language used to refer to suicide and self-harm should be carefully considered. Phrases
like "commit suicide" should be avoided, as they suggest a criminal or immoral element
to the act, which may increase stigma and discourage people from seeking help.
Language such as "attempted suicide," "took their own life," and "died by suicide" is
generally more accepted among those affected by suicide.
FIRST AID | 497

● Programme designers should consider the local statistics of suicide (e.g. increased
numbers of children or older adults feeling suicidal and completing suicide) and develop
programmes specific to the groups with higher risk, being careful not to stereotype.
● Trainers should avoid causing psychological harm and should not ask trainees to share
personal experiences related to the topic (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2021).
Learner considerations
● Children and young people may focus on recognizing unusual behaviour in friends and
how to appropriately inform a trusted adult. Often, they will be concerned about
confidentiality and breaking “trust”. Emphasize the importance of getting help.
● Programme designers should ensure the depth and level of training and the support
available are appropriate to the first aid provider. This will vary among different countries
and first aid education programmes.
● Avoid stigmatizing language (e.g. in English, “commit suicide” emphasizes criminality).
● Consider the culture and faith of learners and adapt education appropriately.
● Complete standard PFA training.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Explain the concepts as follows:
○ Suicidal ideation is when someone is thinking about taking their own life.
○ Suicidal behaviours are actions that a person might take to attempt to take their
own life.
○ Suicide attempt is when someone actively tries to take their own life.
● Create a safe space for learners to report if they are affected by this topic and access any
necessary support. Establish ground rules with the group to ensure confidentiality,
respect and sensitivity.
● Have two facilitators present for sessions so that specific support can be offered to
individuals if necessary.
● Explore and address myths or misconceptions that the learners may have. These may
include concerns about “planting thoughts” or the types of at-risk people.
● Use case studies that explore the behaviours mentioned and risk factors (e.g. a person
who has had significant life events). Focus on recognizing signs of concern rather than on
“assessment”.
● Discuss how to have difficult conversations. What things can you say? What to avoid?
● Practise asking difficult questions to understand concerns and fears. Stress the
importance of doing so.
● Use a video of a person’s story, as people often find it difficult to empathize. Ensuring a
human approach is vital.
● Emphasize that it is the first aid provider’s role to get assistance if necessary and that they
should take any threat of suicidality seriously.
A common tool used in psychological first aid is Look, Listen, Link.
498 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Scientific foundation
Systematic review
An evidence summary developed by the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) identified
12 studies that looked at communication to people with suicidal ideation (Stroobants et al.,
2023). The evidence is of low quality, and results cannot be considered precise due to the low
number of events and large variability of results. The evidence has been declared stable, so no
further updates were conducted.
Appraisal of or having a confidant
There is limited evidence to support the benefit of appraisal of having a confidant (i.e. the
perceived availability of someone to talk to about one’s problems). It was shown that the
appraisal of or having a confidant resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the risk of
suicidality, compared to not having a confidant.
Evidence is of very low quality and results cannot be considered precise due to the limited
sample size, the low number of events and a large variability of results.
Staying connected to and befriending
There is limited evidence in favour of staying connected to the person at risk, such as through
means of sending postcards. In one study, after hospital discharge, postcards were sent every
few weeks or months. In these postcards, a doctor asked how the person was doing and
whether they wished to drop them a note. It was shown that sending postcards resulted in a
statistically significant decrease in suicidal ideation and suicide attempts, compared to not
sending any postcards. A statistically significant decrease in suicidal death when sending
postcards compared to not sending postcards could not be demonstrated.
There is limited evidence in favour of befriending. This involved using a trusting and
non-judgemental approach to providing care and opportunities for people to talk about their
life events in a calm, relaxing and safe environment. In the study, the people stayed in a respite
centre for a time-limited four-night period. It was shown that this method resulted in a
statistically significant decrease in psychological distress from pre to post and follow-up
measurements. Evidence is of very low quality and results cannot be considered precise due to
the limited sample size and lack of data.
Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid is a method of helping people in distress to feel calm and supported in
coping with their challenges. It addresses both the emotional and social needs of individuals,
aiming to help them utilize their resources effectively, enhance their resilience and make
informed decisions. There are a limited number of studies that have researched the
effectiveness of psychological first aid as a complete programme. Two existing systematic
reviews on psychological first aid both concluded that there is a lack of controlled studies to
support it (Dieltjens et al., 2014; Fox et al., 2012). However, more recently, eight randomized
controlled trials became available, although they are at high risk of bias (Hermosilla et al., 2022;
Wang et al., 2024). In these studies, no consistent improvement of PTSD symptoms or
depression could be demonstrated; however, in four studies, a significant reduction of anxiety
FIRST AID | 499

was shown, when compared to the control being psychoeducation, a group discussion, social
acknowledgement or drawing (Wang et al., 2024).
Non-systematic review
Guidelines for suicide developed via Delphi methodology
The Delphi method is a methodology of reaching expert consensus. Several guidelines were
developed using Delphi methods (Colucci et al., 2011; Kelly et al., 2008; Ross et al., 2014).
There are five basic actions when responding to suicide - “ALGEE” (Mental Health First Aid
Australia, 2014):
● Assess the risk of suicide and/or harm.
● Listen non-judgementally.
● Give reassurance.
● Encourage professional support.
● Encourage other supports.
Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid provided by trained persons is widely supported by expert opinion and
rational conjecture as the tool to be provided by trained persons for people who have
experienced a traumatic event (Shultz & Forbes, 2013).
Through observation and expert experience, there is widespread consensus and support
(Inter-Agency Standing Committee (IASC), 2007) for the five principles that should be used to
guide and inform any psychosocial support intervention and prevention efforts at the early to
mid-term stages of an emergency. These five principles facilitate survivors’ short-term
adjustment and long-term adaptation to the impact of disasters (Hobfoll et al., 2007) and involve
the promotion of:
● calm
● hope
● connectedness
● a sense of safety
● a sense of self and community efficacy.
First aid for mental health problems
In 2019, the Belgian Red Cross-Flanders developed guidelines to provide first aid to people
experiencing mental health problems based on a systematic review of scientific evidence
(evidence summaries developed by CEBaP, as mentioned above), expert opinion and the
preferences of the target population. The guidelines include specific recommendations to
support people with suicidal ideation) (Stroobants et al., 2023).
Signals of suicidal ideation
Many people experiencing suicidal ideation exhibit signals during the suicidal process which
could be observed by others.
500 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Warning signs of suicidal threat include (Belgian Red Cross-Flanders, 2025):
● Threats of suicide or self-injury.
● Planning for suicide (e.g. searching for methods, acquiring means). The risk that
somebody will take their own life increases if somebody has a clear plan (how, where,
when), has the means to execute the plan and/or declares the intention to act.
● Talking, writing or online posting about death or suicide.
Other possible signals of suicidal ideation include (Belgian Red Cross-Flanders, 2025):
● Making arrangements, appearing to “say goodbye”, getting affairs in order.
● Affective changes include pronounced negative feelings (e.g. hopelessness, feeling stuck
with no way out, anger, vengefulness, seeing no reason to live, feeling like a burden to
others, anxiety or dramatic mood changes.
● Changes in behaviour such as increased risk behaviour (e.g. recklessness, increased
consumption of alcohol or other substances), sleep problems or social withdrawal.
Risk and protective factors for suicide
Suicide risk is assessed based on the risk factors, protective factors and circumstances of the
suicide attempt if the person survives after such an attempt. Examples of risk factors for suicide
include the following (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2020):
● presence of depression
● presence of psychosis
● sex (the risk ratio of male: female is 2:1)
● age (the older the age, the higher the risk)
● single, separated, divorced or widowed
● presence of alcohol or substance misuse
● previous history of a suicide attempt
● presence of a suicide plan
● lack of social support
● presence of chronic illness (e.g. chronic pain).
Examples of protective factors for suicide involve (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2020):
● strong perceived social support
● close family relationship
● good coping and problem-solving skills
● having a sense of meaning and purpose in life
● ability and willingness to seek help
● access to resources.
Circumstances of non-fatal suicide attempt that indicate a higher risk:
● planning in advance
● precautions to avoid discovery
● no attempts to obtain help afterwards
FIRST AID | 501

● final acts (e.g. writing a suicide note or making a will, transferring savings to a close
relative’s account, asking someone to help take care of small children)
● dangerous method (e.g. a lethal dose of substances was used; the use of a violent
method). The person’s perception of the lethality of the method used should also be
considered.
Professional help in the case of suicidal ideation should always be encouraged. A trained
healthcare professional should conduct a thorough assessment of suicide risk and consider the
possibility of an underlying mental illness that can lead to the same.
Supporting a suicidal person can be stressful, so first aid providers should guard their personal
boundaries and take care of themselves. A first aid provider should do their best for the person
they are helping. However, despite best efforts, some people will still die by suicide.
502 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Acute grief
Key action
Support the person in experiencing their grief in a way that respects their individual needs, and
link them to their social supports.
Introduction
Grief is a normal response to the loss of a loved one or important things (IFRC Psychosocial
Centre, 2020a). It is often caused by bereavement, or a challenging, devastating, yet perhaps
common experience—especially if the loss was sudden and unanticipated. Typical acute grief
reactions are often characterized by emotional and physical distress and difficulty functioning,
but should not be diagnosed as a psychiatric disorder (C. F. Reynolds III et al., 2023). Although
the grief reactions usually become less intense over time, grieving processes do not involve a
fixed number of stages or a standardized linear recovery pattern. Rather, they reflect highly
unique experiences, symptoms, evolutions and durations. Grief fluctuates over time, with an
individual balance of alternating behaviours that move between loss and recovery.
Despite the often painful and disruptive experience, most people adapt quite well to managing
grief in daily life. However, bereavement increases the risk of lingering physical and mental
health problems. People can become stuck in their grieving process, experiencing persistent
intense loss reactions and disruptions to daily life. While all kinds of losses are unpleasant, some
losses can be devastating. The following section focuses largely on loss of life, which often has an
immense impact on survivors’ lives (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2017).
Witnessing or experiencing a loved one’s sudden decease, such as in an accident, severe disease
or suicide, can be one of the most distressing and traumatizing experiences in one’s life. People’s
response is often intensified when death comes unexpectedly. The common reactions are shock,
denial, anger, guilt, despair, hopelessness and depressive feelings (IFRC Psychosocial Centre,
2017). The sudden loss may lead people to be under the condition of acute grief. It is critical for
first aid providers to support the bereaved at this vulnerable stage.
Sometimes, people who lost their loved ones may not be able to be with them in their final
moments and express their love and respect. In these circumstances, some individuals may feel
intense sadness, pain, remorse and guilt, and some may struggle to go about their daily lives.
Despite not being able to be near the body of a loved one, there are still many things that
survivors, or those left behind, can try out to ease the agonizing grief and loss and honour the
memory of those who have died. In addition to the usual rituals, there are several actions that
may help during mourning (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2023).
This section has only provided a brief introduction on how to support people experiencing acute
grief. It aims to provide tips for learners to develop more awareness and to streamline first aid
procedures. It does not expect the learners to deliver psychological first aid (PFA) without formal
training.
FIRST AID | 503

Guidelines
● Family, parents or caregivers should be allowed time to hold or be with their deceased
family members after death to say goodbye. Letting loved ones know how and why they
died may be helpful to deal with their grief.*
● Provide family members the option to be present during adult resuscitation from cardiac
arrest.*
● Talking about grief, communicating with people grieving and providing emotional
support may be helpful for the grieving person to deal with their grief. Communication
avoidance may contribute to unresolved grief and anxiety.*
Good practice points
● During the process of first aid, it may be beneficial to keep relatives or friends updated
regularly during the process of resuscitation and allow time alone with a deceased
person.
● The following interventions are recommended to support those who are experiencing
acute grief:
○ Listen to the person’s concerns.
○ Respect each person’s unique grieving process. Let individuals share their
experiences on their own terms.
○ Offer empathetic support.
○ Encourage accessing other support.
● Linking the person to their social support is an essential part of the healing process.
Meanwhile, first aid providers can listen actively, provide general care and practical help,
support the person to arrange family or community memorial ceremonies for the
deceased and offer to accompany the bereaved to a support group (IFRC Psychosocial
Centre, 2017).
● First aid providers should refer to mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS)
trained staff or volunteers and religious personnel to facilitate cultural or religious rituals,
providing information and discussion of mourning or related issues, and look for
follow-up care in the healthcare setting in facilitating the grieving process.
● Allowing family or caregivers time to hold or be with their children after death to say
goodbye and letting loved ones know how and why children died, may be helpful to deal
with their grief.
● Institutions should develop clear policies and protocols to support healthcare
professionals in making decisions about family presence and equip them with the
necessary education to manage these stressful situations effectively.
● PFA principles and skills may be used in helping people facing death and dying,
especially in the acute stage.
● In addition to the usual rituals, there are several actions that may help during mourning,
especially for those who are not able to be with their loved ones in their final moments, to
say goodbye:
○ Feelings of gratitude: Thanking loved ones who have died and acknowledging how
they contributed to feelings of worthiness and love.
504 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

○ Reconciliation with difficult feelings experienced in the past: This may be a time to
honestly express difficult feelings and express forgiveness.
○ Expressing love and affection: Express your love, care, and prayers for the deceased.
○ Expressing your farewell: Say farewell words to loved ones, expressing the
importance of their role in life and memories built together
(IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2023)
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Be open-minded and aware that the process of grief is unique to each individual. Some
people may grieve intensely while others may be very controlled and reserved. Learn to
adapt and accommodate the different ways that people express their needs.
● Before offering support or comforting others in grief, pay attention to your own
psychological well-being and readiness because you may also be exposed to trauma, loss
and devastation, injury and even death in emergency or crisis situations. It is important to
work closely and communicate your conditions and needs in challenging situations with
your team (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2015).
Early recognition
The way a person looks, feels, or behaves when grieving is unique to that person. However, early
recognition can focus on observing and attentively listening to the following:
● Disruption of normal life:
○ The world feels fundamentally changed after a significant loss.
○ Usual routines, plans and activities lose meaning or importance.
○ The person may feel directionless or question the purpose of life.
● Shift in priorities and beliefs:
○ Things once considered important may now seem trivial.
○ The loss can challenge personal beliefs, including religious or spiritual values.
○ Questions about life’s meaning and existence often arise.
● Grief as a natural process:
○ Grief is a necessary emotional response to loss, not a sign of weakness.
○ It allows the person to process and release emotional ties to the deceased.
○ It can be seen as an extension or expression of love for the person who died.
● Cognitive and emotional signs:
○ Constant thoughts about the deceased person.
○ Vivid recollections of shared memories or key moments.
○ Overwhelming feelings of sadness, sorrow, anger or resentment.
● Behavioural signs:
○ Tendency to isolate from others and withdraw socially.
○ Avoidance of social interaction may initially help, but long-term isolation is harmful.
● Duration and complexity:
○ Grief is a long-term emotional process that varies in length and intensity.
○ Patience and understanding from others are crucial.
FIRST AID | 505

(IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2017)
First aid steps
To address the immediate needs of people experiencing acute grief, skills and techniques from
Psychological First Aid (PFA) are useful. You may provide support with the concepts of LOOK,
LISTEN and LINK.
1. Introduce yourself and say you are available to support if the person does not know you.
[LOOK, LISTEN]
2. Encourage the person to move to a safe space if there are potential hazards. Ensure the
person does not feel crowded; create space if possible. [LOOK]
3. Directly ask the person how you can assist them. Express concern and understanding.
[LISTEN]
4. Give the person your full attention. Listen carefully to the grieving person and accept their
feelings and reactions, whatever they may be. [LISTEN]
5. Encourage the person to be with loved ones within their household or keep in touch
remotely with other members of their family and with other support systems, rather than
self-isolating. [LINK]
6. Encourage taking breaks from painful emotions by suggesting activities that provide
time for a break and allow the person to focus on something else other than their
emotions all the time. [LISTEN]
7. Encourage the person to reach out to their support network when needed. [LINK]
(IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2020-b)
A first aid provider can provide support to someone witnessing resuscitation by:
● Including significant others on what is happening. Regular updates of the person’s
situation during resuscitation may reduce the feeling of helplessness.
● Asking the significant other if they are comfortable witnessing the resuscitation.
Witnessing the resuscitation process may help the bereaved to cope better with grief
over time. If the significant other is seeing the resuscitation, ensure that someone is
there to support them.
● Allowing relatives and friends time alone with the body; to see, touch, and talk to the
deceased as much as they are comfortable. They should be prepared for what the
deceased will look like before viewing the body, especially when the deceased has been
severely injured or disfigured. Spending time with the body can facilitate grieving and
reduce feelings of guilt, helplessness and isolation.
Access help
● Ensure the person feels okay before leaving them. [LOOK, LISTEN]
● Ask if the person knows where to seek help and advice if the impacts of grief continue. Be
aware of and inform the person about the range of professional help available. [LINK]
● Encourage the person to see a health/mental health professional if grieving causes
distress. [LINK]
506 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Education considerations
Context considerations
● Acknowledge that when the loss of life is sudden, for example in a car accident, a violent
assault or an earthquake, the response is often intensified. There is little or no opportunity
to prepare for the loss, to say good-bye and to prepare for bereavement. Shock, denial,
anger, guilt, despair, hopelessness and depressive feelings are common reactions when
death comes unexpectedly (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2017).
● If someone survives a disaster where others have died this can complicate the grieving
process. Especially in the case of sudden loss, survivors may find that memories of the
event dominate their minds. They may suffer from survivor guilt. Parents may accuse
themselves of not being able to protect and save their children. While people process
their reactions to loss in different ways, for many people it is important to talk about
feelings of guilt or shame, to prevent those feelings from growing and becoming
unbearable (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2017).
● First aid providers should be aware of the cultural factors that are related to grief. The way
people deal with death or loss is also strongly influenced by the culture. Different cultures
have different ways of expressing grief, of paying respects to the dead and of mourning
those who have been lost.
○ Burial and mourning rituals help people grieve. They mark the point where the
chaos caused by the death of loved ones moves onto a stage where acceptance
and grieving of the loss become possible.
○ Rituals give religious or spiritual meaning and provide comfort to the bereaved.
○ Rituals and traditional ceremonies provide a space for family, friends and other
members of the community to share a common loss. They provide crucial
moments in which the bereaved feel supported and others acknowledge their
pain.
○ The grieving process may be complicated in situations where traditional rituals
supporting grief cannot be carried out as usual (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2020b).
● Apart from being aware of the cultural factors, first aid providers can take the following
into consideration:
○ Reassure a grieving person that what they feel is normal.
○ Avoid telling the bereaved what they should be feeling or doing.
○ Emphasize there is no right way to grieve nor is there a timeline.
○ Do not judge or take grief reactions personally as grief may involve extreme
emotions and behaviours that can be difficult to understand.
○ Do not pressure anyone to move on or make them feel like they have been
grieving too long; this can actually slow their recovery.
○ Be aware that anniversaries and commemorations can trigger the pain related to
the loss, even years later (IFRC Psychosocial Centre, 2020b).
Learner considerations
● Each individual will come to the learning environment with their own set of experiences
and personal history. It is likely that they have experienced elements of trauma, distress
and loss. It is important for facilitators to acknowledge this at the beginning of the session
and plan for any disclosures or emotional moments.
FIRST AID | 507

● Complete standard PFA training.
Facilitation tips
● Work with a co-facilitator if possible to be able to privately support a learner who is openly
emotional, as well as to be able to check in with each other emotionally and professionally
(i.e. on the educational process). If you are unable to work with a co-facilitator, consider
finding a break early in the session and allowing participants to connect with you
privately about their past experiences. This may allow you to meet participants in a safer
space.
● At the beginning of a session, establish a communication plan between the facilitator and
learners. If a topic is triggering distress, learners need a way to communicate their need to
address it or take a break from the session to decompress.
● Emphasize the importance of self-care. Learners must understand that supporting others
in crisis or who are experiencing loss(es) and grief can be overwhelming. They need to
learn to recognize their triggers and have strategies in place to maintain their own mental
and physical health.
● Provide information on local support networks and how learners can contact them.
● Learners should practise active listening. This involves addressing the verbal and
nonverbal cues (such as facial expressions and body language) of a person.
Facilitation tools
● Learners can work together with MHPSS-trained staff or volunteers to develop case
studies that support individuals in coping with grief and loss. This also enables learners to
make the learning relevant to their environment.
● Develop interactive activities that enable learners to openly explore the concepts of grief
and loss with each other. Consider using “think, pair, share” activities where learners think
about a concept on their own, discuss it in pairs, then share their learning with the larger
group.
● Integrate the LOOK, LISTEN, LINK techniques of PFA.
Scientific foundation
Systematic review sources
We identified several systematic reviews on the different aspects of caring for acute or sudden
bereavement and an evidence summary from the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP)
about communication with bereaved or grieving people.
Spending time alone with the deceased
A systematic review of the sudden death of children found consistencies across many studies
showing that parents want time to hold and be with their deceased child to say goodbye. Some
qualitative studies described that when parents desired but were unable to have a private and
peaceful space to say goodbye to their child, it increased their regret and grief. However, a
minority of bereaved parents strongly felt that they did not want to see their deceased child
(Garstang et al., 2014).
508 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Family presence during resuscitation
The International Liaison Committee on Resuscitation (ILCOR) performed a systematic review on
family presence during adult cardiac arrest resuscitation (Berg et al., 2023a; Berg et al., 2023b).
The included studies were heterogeneous, and the evidence was of very low certainty. Overall, no
evidence of harm to patients or families was found across the studies, notwithstanding
variability in practices and outcomes of family presence during resuscitation.
Based on these findings, ILCOR suggests offering family members the option to be present
during both in-hospital and out-of-hospital adult resuscitation. In addition, two good practice
statements were issued: (1) Institutions should develop policies or protocols to guide healthcare
professionals in family presence decisions. (2) Professionals should receive education on
managing these high-stress situations, including family distress and their own emotional
responses. In summary, family presence is supported as an option that respects family choice
and may improve coping, provided it is implemented with structured guidance, staff training
and sensitivity to context.
Knowing the “how” and the “why”
Based on the same systematic review by Garstang et al. (2014), evidence found consistencies
across many studies of child deaths that parents want to know how and why their child died.
Studies revealed that the information about the child’s death helps parents in making sense of
the tragedy and facilitates the grieving process. Particularly when the death is unexpected,
finding out the cause of death is important to help reduce parents’ emotional stress.
Information can also reassure parents that the child did not suffer and that everything possible
was done to save their life. This knowledge can help to reduce any guilt parents might feel.
Some bereaved parents tend to suspect that a lack of information means authorities are
deliberately withholding knowledge from them.
Talking about grief
An evidence summary from CEBaP in 2019 found limited evidence demonstrating the benefits
of talking about grief (Stroobants et al., 2023). The evidence has been declared stable, so no
further updates were conducted. While we did not identify a causal relationship between the
results, we did conclude specific associations between grief and speaking about it.
In bereaved parents of deceased children, evidence showed that there is a statistically significant
association between a decrease in grief and positive communication about one’s grief as time
increased from the loss of the child. However, a statistically significant association between a
partner’s concern for their grieving partner, and actual grief of both partners could not be
demonstrated.
Studies also identified statistically significant associations between relationships outside that of
a parent and child.
● In bereaved siblings, there was a statistically significant association between talking about
loss and a decrease in grief and anxiety. There was also an association between
satisfaction and the amount of time spent talking about the loss.
FIRST AID | 509

● In the bereaved children of a deceased parent, studies showed a statistically significant
association between the remaining parent or caregiver’s positive, emotional words and a
decrease in the bereaved children’s anxiety and depression, especially as time increases
since the loss. Moreover, there is a statistically significant association between the
decrease in unhealthy grief and the increase in mother communality. However, a
statistically significant association between a decrease in depression and an increase in
mother communality could not be demonstrated.
● In bereaved partners, a statistically significant association between increased disclosures
of emotion at 4, 18 or 25 months post-bereavement and a decrease in distress could not
be demonstrated. However, at 11 months post-bereavement, there was a statistically
significant association between increased disclosure of emotions and an increase in
distress.
● In bereaved students, a statistically significant association between more open family
communication about grief and a decrease in feelings of grief could not be
demonstrated.
The same evidence summary demonstrated harm caused by avoiding communication. The
evidence is of very low quality and results are considered imprecise due to a low number of
events and lack of data. While we did not identify a causal relationship between the results, we
did identify several specific associations:
● There was a statistically significant association between not talking to one’s parents about
a deceased sibling and both unresolved grief and anxiety.
● The evidence showed that increased partner-oriented self-regulation (when partners
avoid talking about their loss and try to appear strong in each other’s presence) resulted
in a statistically significant increase in bereaved parents’ grief seven months after a loss.
There was also a statistically significant association between an increase in current
partner-oriented self-regulation and an increase in grief.
● A statistically significant association between bereaved students’ grief and
communication avoidance could not be demonstrated.
The evidence is of very low quality and results are considered imprecise due to a low number of
events and lack of data.
Communicating and receiving emotional support
The evidence summary also identified studies that looked at communicating with grieving or
bereaved people in general (not specifically grief-related communication). The evidence is of
very low quality and the results are considered imprecise due to limited sample size and large
variability. They could not infer any causal relationship from the results outlined below.
● In bereaved fathers of deceased children, results showed a statistically significant
association between decreased grief and talking with friends.
● In bereaved children of a deceased parent, results showed a statistically significant
association between both decreased anxiety and depression and an increase in
parent-child communication.
● In bereaved adults, a statistically significant association between grief and
communication within a family could not be demonstrated.
510 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

● In a healthcare setting, results showed that a phone call from the neonatologist led to a
statistically significant decrease in loneliness, depression and feelings of guilt, compared
to no phone call. However, a statistically significant decrease in anger and hostility with or
without a phone call could not be demonstrated.
Psychological first aid
Psychological first aid is a method of helping people in distress to feel calm and supported in
coping with their challenges. It addresses both the emotional and social needs of individuals,
with the intention of helping people use their resources, enhance their resilience and make
informed decisions. There are a limited number of studies that researched the effectiveness of
psychological first aid as a complete programme. Two existing systematic reviews on
psychological first aid both concluded that there is a lack of controlled studies to support it
(Dieltjens et al., 2014; Fox et al., 2012). However, more recently, eight randomized controlled trials
became available, although they are at high risk of bias (Hermosilla et al., 2022; Wang et al.,
2024). In these studies no consistent improvement of PTSD symptoms or depression could be
demonstrated; however, in four studies, a significant reduction of anxiety was shown, when
compared to the control being psychoeducation, a group discussion, social acknowledgement
or drawing (Wang et al., 2024).
Non-systematic review
In addition to the evidence from the systematic reviews, we also identified best practices as
described by Kent and McDowell (2004) and from bereavement care practice guidelines.
Good practice from bereavement care practice guidelines
There are some studies regarding the development or application of bereavement care practice
guidelines. A systematic review further identified a large variation in the quality of these
guidelines (Kent et al., 2020). Nonetheless, the guidelines from the review share the following
core values:
● Work with respect and integrity.
● Provide dignity to bereaved individuals and their deceased loved one.
● Provide high-quality, collaborative, accessible and adequately resourced care.
Updating friends and family every 10–15 minutes on any resuscitation progress
The bereaved must not feel excluded during the resuscitation process. Studies suggested that
frequent updates of the patient’s situation during resuscitation reduced the feeling of
helplessness. Meanwhile, the feeling of exclusion and helplessness and remaining uninformed
may lead to anger in the grieving process. Studies also showed that witnessing the resuscitation
may help the bereaved to cope better with grief over time (to which there is no evidence that
viewing would interfere with the resuscitation process). However, a nurse needed to accompany
the bereaved.
Spending time alone with the deceased
It is important to allow relatives and friends time alone with the body; to see, touch and talk to
the deceased as much as they are comfortable. They should be prepared for what the deceased
will look like before viewing the body, especially when the deceased has been severely injured or
FIRST AID | 511

disfigured. Spending time with the body can facilitate grieving and reduce feelings of guilt,
helplessness and isolation.
Accommodating cultural or religious rituals
Accommodation of cultural and religious rituals shows respect for the deceased and the
bereaved, which will facilitate the grieving process. In supporting a bereaved family, it is
important to pay attention to how the family addresses the dead body and to be aware of and
receptive to the cultural and religious differences of different families. Doing so allows the helper
to facilitate saying goodbye to the deceased in a humane and dignified way (World Health
Organization, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, & International
Committee of the Red Cross, 2016).
Providing information and discussing relevant issues
If appropriate, the bereaved may wish to discuss issues such as organ or tissue donation
post-mortem, funeral arrangements and attending support groups. Some studies revealed that
the donation of organs or tissues makes death meaningful and helps the bereaved family
members accept their loss.
Providing a hospital contact number and name of support nurse or doctor for follow-up care
Follow-up care from the hospital allows the bereaved family an opportunity to get more
information about the death and may also be a source of comfort.
Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid, provided by trained persons, is widely supported by expert opinion and
rational conjecture as a tool to help people who have experienced a traumatic event(s) (Shultz &
Forbes, 2013).
Through observation and expert experience, there is a widespread consensus and support for
the five intervention principles that should guide and inform any psychosocial support
intervention and prevention efforts at the early to mid-term stages of an emergency. These
principles facilitate survivors’ short-term adjustment and long-term adaptation after a crisis
(Hobfoll et al., 2007). The five principles involve the promotion of:
● calm
● hope
● connectedness
● a sense of safety
● a sense of self and community efficacy.
First aid for mental health problems
In 2019, the Belgian Red Cross-Flanders developed guidelines to provide first aid to people
experiencing mental health problems based on a systematic review of scientific evidence
(evidence summaries developed by CEBaP, as mentioned above), expert opinion and the
preferences of the target population. The guidelines include specific recommendations to
support people experiencing grief (Stroobants et al., 2023).
512 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Supporting families in facing death and dying or ambiguous loss
Witnessing loved ones’ sudden death or not knowing the whereabouts of loved ones can be one
of the most distressing and traumatizing experiences in one’s life. It is critical for helpers to
provide appropriate and adequate support for the families at this vulnerable stage. Unlike death,
when the loved ones go missing or disappear, there is uncertainty about whether they are dead
or alive and such uncertainty leads to an ambiguous loss for the family. The fact that the person
may still be alive somewhere, the fact that no remains have been recovered, means that the
family cannot hold a burial ceremony and move forward with the grieving process as if the
person is dead. This ambiguous loss sometimes remains unacknowledged, which causes further
distress to the families (International Committee of the Red Cross, 2017).
Families who experience loss can react in a multitude of different ways. Below are just some of
the ways people may respond to loss (Terlonge et al., 2012).
● Feelings: The person who experiences a sudden loss may have a range of feelings,
including shock, numbness, intense sorrow and pain, sadness, depression, dejection,
anxiety, tension, denial, anger, irritability, guilt, self-blame, self-criticism, insecurity,
helplessness, hopelessness, yearning and nostalgia. Families with ambiguous loss may
also feel emotionally isolated as they may believe that others do not understand their
suffering (International Committee of the Red Cross, 2017).
● Thoughts: Grief can cause worrying, feelings of disbelief, denial of what has happened,
confusion and problems with memory and concentration. It can also cause
preoccupation and rumination over the loss and the idea of the presence of the missing
or dead family member. Some may also experience feelings of alienation, unfamiliarity
with their surroundings, the experience of unreality, suicidal thoughts, depersonalization
and hallucinations.
● Physical sensations: Grief can cause physical sensations such as tightness and heaviness
in the chest or throat, choking, nausea or stomach-ache, dizziness, headaches, numbness,
muscle weakness, tension or fatigue. It may make the person vulnerable to illness.
● Behaviours: Grief can cause avoidance of memories of the deceased, weeping, sobbing,
crying, hypo-or hyperactivity, sleep problems (reduced or interrupted sleep or excessive
sleep), and increased use of medication, alcohol or other substances. Physical complaints
such as headache, stomach-ache or nausea may arise without an identifiable physical
cause. Sudden loss may trigger shock symptoms such as screaming, howling to shaking,
difficulty in drinking or eating, crying spells and becoming more aggressive or irritable.
Other extremes on the continuum of behaviour, such as staying numb, unresponsive to
the outside world, inability to talk or move and loss of interest in daily activities, may also
arise. Families with ambiguous loss may also present obsessive thoughts and speech, as
well as repetitive and rigid patterns of behaviour (International Committee of the Red
Cross, 2017).
● Social behaviours: Grief can cause social withdrawal or isolation. Families with ambiguous
loss may feel unable to resume their familial and marital roles, rules and rituals, as they do
not know whether the missing family member will ever return. In extreme cases, life
seems to come to a standstill. They may also isolate themselves and refuse to reach out
for help to avoid stigmatization. Stigmatization can be even more serious especially when
the family member went missing in a violent conflict between two or more groups and
FIRST AID | 513

others became sceptical that the family is connected to “rebel” groups (International
Committee of the Red Cross, 2017).
Children may have distinct reactions and changes too (International Committee of the Red
Cross, 2017; Terlonge et al., 2012), such as:
● restlessness and change in activity level
● fearfulness and/or anger, especially when being left alone, or asking questions like “Why
did it happen?” or “Will this happen to me or others?”
● regression to younger behaviours such as bedwetting despite having been toilet-trained,
thumb sucking and refusal to sleep alone
● clinging to parents or showing anxiety to separation or fear of strangers
● withdrawal and unwillingness to discuss the loss
● symptoms of illness such as nausea, loss of appetite and diffuse aches and pains
● feeling guilty and placing blame on themselves.
Families of missing persons often require intensive psychosocial support during the difficult
process of investigating disappearances. During the process of recovering and identifying
remains, painful memories and intense emotions may surface particularly when the families are
asked to provide ante-mortem data and blood or saliva samples for obtaining conclusive proof
of death of the missing person, are informed of their loved one’s death, are present when the
remains are recovered and/or are asked to identify or claim the remains and personal
belongings of their loved ones (International Committee of the Red Cross, 2017).
514 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Anxiety and panic
Key action
Help people feel calm.
Introduction
Anxiety is an emotion when people feel worried or tense and have physical changes like higher
blood pressure (American Psychological Association, n.d.-a). It is a natural human response
when people feel worried, afraid or tense about things that may happen in the future, or are
under threat (Mind, n.d.). Along with fearful emotion, physical signs like muscle tension, fast
breathing or rapid heart rate can be observed and may lead to a sudden onset of a panic attack
(American Psychological Association, n.d.-b; American Psychological Association, n.d.-c).
A panic attack is a distinct episode of high anxiety during which a person feels fear or
apprehension. The anxiety reaches its peak within 10 to 15 minutes. During a panic attack, the
person can have many different physical symptoms, such as a faster heartbeat, shortness of
breath, chest discomfort, profuse sweating, dizziness and light-headedness and nausea. Many
people also feel very afraid, for example, of dying, losing control or fainting (Chalmers et al., 2022;
IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
It is important to note that experiencing the emotion of anxiety is not equal to the mental health
condition of anxiety disorder, which can only be diagnosed by mental health professionals like
psychiatrists or psychologists.
This section only provides a brief introduction on how to support people experiencing anxiety or
panic attacks. It aims to provide tips for learners to develop more awareness and to streamline
the first aid procedures. It does not expect the learners to deliver psychological first aid (PFA)
without formal training.
Guidelines
● Integrating PFA principles when providing support (through listening, being empathetic,
maintaining contact and connecting to other resources) may help to reduce anxiety.*
● Using clear and direct communication may be helpful for a person experiencing anxiety.*
Good practice points
● First aid providers should help the person feel calm, provide a safe environment and listen
to the person. The provider may also try quiet and focused breathing exercises to help
someone feel calmer (IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
● First aid providers may try some of the following examples to help calm the person:
○ Keep a calm and soft tone of voice.
○ Remain calm as this will have a calming effect on the person in distress.
FIRST AID | 515

○ If culturally appropriate, try to maintain eye contact (without staring) while talking
to the person. Maybe gently put an arm around the person or gently stroke their
back (only if it is appropriate and they are comfortable with this action).
○ Remind the person of the intent to help and that they are safe (if it is true).
○ Encourage the person to breathe through the nose, feel the breath fill up their
belly slowly and then breathe out slowly through the mouth (IFRC Psychosocial
Centre, 2017).
● If the physical reactions continue, the first aid provider should call for medical help.
● Encourage professional support if someone is experiencing excessive anxiety that
interferes with daily life.
Chain of survival behaviours
Prevent and prepare
● Acknowledge that reactions of anxiety or panic attacks may occur in distressing events,
crises or stressful environments. These reactions may take place immediately or develop
gradually over time (IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
● Recognize that panic attack symptoms can resemble those of a heart attack or other
medical conditions.
Differences between a panic attack and a heart attack:
● Onset: Panic attacks often come on suddenly and peak within minutes, while heart
attack symptoms can start slowly and persist.
● Duration: Panic attacks typically last for a few minutes, whereas heart attack symptoms
can last longer and may come and go.
● Triggers: Panic attacks can occur without a clear trigger, while heart attacks are often
related to physical exertion or stress.
● Chest pain: Panic attack chest pain is usually sharp and localized, whereas heart attack
chest pain is more likely to feel like pressure or squeezing and may radiate to other areas.
Early recognition
● If you suspect a panic attack, ask the person if they know what is happening and if they
have experienced a panic attack before.
● If the person has not had a panic attack before and does not think they are having one,
see chest pain, and feeling faint.
● Inquire if the symptoms are related to a known medical issue.
First aid steps
To address the immediate needs of people experiencing anxiety or a panic attack, skills and
techniques from Psychological First Aid (PFA) are useful. You may provide support with the
concepts of LOOK, LISTEN and LINK.
Look
1. Introduce yourself if the person does not know you.
2. If the person is disoriented or confused, reassure them that they are safe and you are
available to support.
516 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

3. Encourage the person to move to a safe space if there are potential hazards or create a
safe space and ensure the person does not feel crowded if multiple people are present.
4. Remove any object or person causing obvious distress.
● While with the person, observe their body language to guide your support (e.g. is the
person sitting still or moving around?).
● If you need to leave to find additional help, try to find someone else to check on the
person.
Listen
1. Directly ask the person how you can assist them. If the person cannot communicate
verbally, ask yes/no questions and encourage non-verbal responses (e.g. thumbs up for
“yes”). Only ask questions that will help you assist the person.
2. Validate that their experience is uncomfortable, terrifying and stressful.
3. Reassure the person that there is nothing to be ashamed of.
4. If they feel like they are “going crazy”, reassure them they are not.
5. Help the person get their breathing under control by demonstrating slow, even breaths.
6. When speaking with the person:
a. Use positive, supportive language like "You will be okay, this will pass soon."
b. Speak clearly, slowly, and use short sentences.
c. Remain patient and calm.
Listen and Link
1. If coping strategies are working, encourage the person to continue using them.
2. After they use coping strategies, let them know they are doing a good job.
If the person refuses your help, ask if there is someone else they would like you to call.
Panic attack
The following interventions are recommended to support those who experience a panic attack:
1. Introduce yourself and explain that you are there to support.
2. Ensure safety. Look for a safe and private environment for the person to talk (IFRC
Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
3. Ask the person if they realize that their symptoms could be a result of a panic attack.
a. Explain that a panic attack is a reaction to a stressful situation and will likely lessen
gradually (IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
b. If in doubt whether it is a panic attack or a medical emergency, access emergency
services.
4. Listen to the person’s concerns.
5. Accept the person’s feelings and reactions (IFRC Reference Centre for Psychosocial
Support, 2018).
6. Offer empathetic support.
7. Speak clearly and softly, and be patient.
FIRST AID | 517

8. Remain calm and help the person slow their breathing.
a. Demonstrate breathing in slowly and calmly and ask the person to do it with you.
Inhale through the nose for one count and exhale through the mouth for three
counts.
b. Loosen any tight clothing.
c. Encourage breathing from the abdomen.
9. Stay with the person until they are no longer in distress.
10. Connect the person to additional support resources or networks.
Access help
● Ensure the person is not alone before leaving to get help. [LINK]
● Ask if the person knows where to seek help and advice. Be aware of and inform the
person about the range of professional help available. [LINK]
● If the person has recurring panic attacks or has changed their life to prevent them,
reassure them that effective professional help is available. Encourage them to see a
health/mental health professional if the panic attacks continue or cause distress. [LISTEN,
LINK]
● Understand that a person experiencing a panic attack may want to seek medical
attention due to fear of their symptoms. [LINK]
Recovery
● Provide psychoeducation about panic attacks (e.g. potential triggers) and guide them to
the proper breathing exercises when experiencing a panic attack. [LISTEN]
Education considerations
Context considerations
● Cultural norms influence how a first aid provider responds to a person experiencing
anxiety or a panic attack. It is necessary to develop cultural awareness. For example, is it
appropriate to touch a person when helping them remain calm? A general rule is not to
touch a person without permission or if it is unacceptable to them (IFRC Reference
Centre for Psychosocial Support, 2018).
Learner considerations
● Complete standard PFA training.
Facilitation tips and tools
● Use role play or scenarios and ask learners to practise staying calm and responding to
someone experiencing anxiety or a panic attack in a calm and relaxed manner.
● Invite learners to share how they help themselves calm down when feeling anxious, and
then practise some of the calming exercises (e.g. slow breathing) outlined in this topic.
● Emphasize that communication is key to providing support effectively. Brainstorm
appropriate communication strategies, such as:
○ Avoid talking too much, as this may overwhelm the person and increase their
panic.
○ Understand that unfriendly or rude behaviour is not personal.
○ Allow the person to continue what they are doing unless it is a safety risk.
518 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

○ Do not pressure the person to explain their panic.
○ Acknowledge and validate the person’s symptoms or experience.
○ Avoid using phrases like "Don't panic" or "Just calm down".
○ Acknowledge the person is having a panic attack and reassure them that they are
safe; do not criticize or express pity.
○ Ask permission before touching the person and do not grab, hold or restrain them.
Learning connections
● Integrate the LOOK, LISTEN, LINK techniques of PFA. (IFRC Reference Centre for
Psychosocial Support, 2018).
Scientific foundation
Systematic review
An evidence summary developed by the Centre for Evidence-Based Practice (CEBaP) identified
12 studies that looked at communication with people with anxiety (Stroobants et al., 2023). The
evidence is of very low quality and the results of these studies are imprecise due to a limited
sample size and a lack of data. No causal relationships can be inferred from these results. The
evidence has been declared as stable, so no further updates were conducted.
Talking with friends, clear and direct communication
There is limited evidence on the benefits of talking with friends/others and for clear and direct
communication.
It was shown that there is a statistically significant association between a decrease in general
anxiety and talking with friends and talking weekly or more to others, following a traumatic
event such as the loss of a sibling or the loss of a teacher.
It was shown that there is a statistically significant association between indirect and unclear
communication, family avoidance of communication, lower patient/family communication or
lower perceived open communication regarding illness and an increase in anxiety in cancer
patients or in people who lost a sibling. It was also shown that there is a statistically significant
association between a child's emotional expression and a decrease in anxiety in the case of the
loss of a parent. In addition, it was shown that there is a statistically significant association
between good parent-adolescent communication in adolescents with higher reactive
aggression and a decrease in anxiety; however, this could not be demonstrated in adolescents
with higher proactive aggression.
In the specific case of family members of patients dying in the intensive care unit (ICU), it was
shown that close communication with the ICU staff, where ICU staff listen, acknowledge family
members’ emotions and give room for questions, resulted in a statistically significant decrease
of anxiety in the family members, compared to usual practice.
For children who lost a parent, a statistically significant association between a decrease in the
child’s anxiety symptoms and the surviving caregivers’ positive emotion words or positive
parental reinforcement could not be demonstrated.
FIRST AID | 519

Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid is a method of helping people in distress to feel calm and supported in
coping with their challenges. It addresses both the emotional and social needs of individuals,
