with the intention of helping people use their resources, enhance their resilience and make
informed decisions. There are a limited number of studies that researched the effectiveness of
psychological first aid as a complete programme. Two existing systematic reviews on
psychological first aid both concluded that there is a lack of controlled studies to support it
(Dieltjens et al., 2014; Fox et al., 2012). However, more recently, eight randomized controlled trials
became available, although they are at high risk of bias (Hermosilla et al., 2022; Wang et al.,
2024). In these studies, no consistent improvement of PTSD symptoms or depression could be
demonstrated; however, four studies showed a significant reduction in anxiety when compared
to the control of psychoeducation, a group discussion, social acknowledgement or drawing
(Wang et al., 2024).
Non-systematic reviews
Guidelines for panic attacks developed via the Delphi methodology
The Delphi method is a methodology of reaching expert consensus. Several guidelines were
developed using the Delphi method for supporting someone with a panic attack (Chalmers et
al., 2022; Kelly et al., 2008). These guidelines recommend the following:
● When assisting someone with a panic attack, identify yourself, speak clearly and slowly,
and be patient.
● Acknowledge the person’s fear, reassure them it will pass soon, and ask if they need help
or have had a panic attack before.
● The first aider should encourage the person to try to slow their breathing down gradually.
Guidance on specific treatments for a panic attack (e.g. breathing into a paper bag, repeating
coping statements, grounding techniques) was not endorsed in these guidelines.
Psychological first aid intervention principles
Psychological first aid provided by trained persons is widely supported by expert opinion and
rational conjecture as the tool to be provided by trained persons for people who have
experienced a traumatic event (Shultz & Forbes, 2013).
Through observation and expert experience, there is widespread consensus and support
(Inter-Agency Standing Committee (IASC), 2007)for the five principles that should be used to
guide and inform any psychosocial support intervention and prevention efforts at the early to
mid-term stages of an emergency. These five principles facilitate survivors’ short-term
adjustment and long-term adaptation to the impact of disasters (Hobfoll et al., 2007) and involve
the promotion of:
● calm
● hope
● connectedness
● a sense of safety
● a sense of self and community efficacy.
520 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

First aid for mental health problems
In 2019, the Belgian Red Cross-Flanders developed guidelines to provide first aid to people
experiencing mental health problems based on a systematic review of scientific evidence
(evidence summaries developed by CEBaP, as mentioned above), expert opinion, and the
preferences of the target population. The guidelines include specific recommendations to
support people experiencing anxiety or panic attacks (Stroobants et al., 2023).
FIRST AID | 521

522 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

GLOSSARY AND REFERENCES

Glossary

Acetylsalicylic acid
used to treat pain, fever or inflammation.
The application of external heat sources to the skin (e.g. electric
Active warming
heating blanket).

Automated external
device which can analyse the rhythm of the heart and if necessary,
defibrillator (AED,
may deliver an electric shock to help the heart return to its normal
defibrillator)
rhythm.
The continued cooling of the body temperature after rewarming has
Afterdrop
started.
A medical term used to describe when a person is gasping or
Agonal breaths struggling to breathe. It tends to be associated with life-threatening
conditions such as a heart attack or stroke.
AMS Acute mountain sickness is a mild form of altitude sickness.
Analgesics Any type of medication that provides pain relief.
A diagnosed condition that can be treated with medication. The
Angina person’s arteries leading to the heart narrow, limit blood flow and
cause chest pain.
A substance capable of preventing a product from exerting its toxic
Antidote
effects.

Apnoea The breathing stops, usually for a short time during sleep.
To drain a blister by puncturing it, leaving the top layer of skin in
Aspiration
place.
Blood pressure is measured using two measurements. The systolic
pressure is the force at which the heart pumps blood through the
Blood pressure
arteries. The diastolic pressure is the pressure in the arteries between

Blunt force trauma is physical trauma to a body part, either by a
Blunt force trauma
violent or high-velocity impact.
Borg Dyspnoea Scale is used to measure the breathing difficulty
Borg Dyspnoea Scale
reported by a person to assess the severity of their disease.
Buccal glucose Glucose is put inside the mouth on the lips or cheeks, where it is
administration absorbed into the bloodstream.
524 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

The phenomenon that the more people who are present at an
Bystander effect emergency, the less likely anyone is to help, due to a diffusion of
responsibility.
Major blood vessels in the front of the neck that supply blood to the
Carotid arteries
brain, neck, and face.
Cervical spinal motion restriction is the reduction or limitation of
Cervical spinal motion cervical spinal movement using a cervical collar, or sandbags with
restriction tape, combined with a long backboard. The definition may differ
slightly according to the organization.
Community efficacy is the extended sense that one’s group can cope
Community efficacy
with the traumatic event and its associated tasks.
Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is a technique of using chest
compressions, usually combined with rescue breaths, to pump blood
CPR
around the body, with the aim of keeping a person’s vital organs alive
until defibrillation can take place.
Cross reality is a mixture of virtual and real-world elements. For
Cross-reality example, playing a character in a fictional story (virtual world) but
having to physically give CPR to someone in the story (real world).
An electrical shock to the heart to restore a normal rhythm using a
defibrillator. Using a defibrillator device increases survival of people
having sudden cardiac arrest due to fibrillation. Fibrillation is rapid,
Defibrillation
disordered and inefficient heart contractions. There are automated
external defibrillator devices which are safe for first aid providers to
use on a person who is unresponsive with abnormal breathing.
Deroofing Remove the top layer of skin of a blister.
A solution used to wash away chemicals spilled on the body and
Diphoterine
eyes.
A dose attenuator modifies electrical energy delivered by a
Dose attenuator
defibrillator based on the person’s size.
DR ABC is a commonly used tool in English to help learners
DR ABC remember how to assess a scene and person. The letters stand for
Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation.
Duty of care is a legal obligation imposed on an individual or
Duty of care company requiring that they adhere to a standard of reasonable care
while performing any acts that could foreseeably harm others.
Difficult or laboured breathing, a feeling of shortness of breath,
Dyspnoea
tightness in the chest and the feeling of suffocation.
For the Guidelines, the term “emergency medical services” (EMS)
Emergency medical
signifies that the first aid provider should quickly access the next
services (EMS)
available higher level of care. In some places, EMS may mean an
GLOSSARY | 525

ambulance service and hospital, while in others it may mean
contacting a local health worker or travelling to a field hospital.
Programme designers will need to adapt the language according to
the local context in which it is used.
Encephalitis An infection of the brain that is usually caused by a virus.
A condition or disease regularly found among a group of people or in
Endemic diseases
a certain area.
Epigastric pain Pain or discomfort right below the ribs in the upper abdomen.
The flap at the back of the throat that can close to stop food and
Epiglottis
drink from going down the windpipe.
Epithelium is a body tissue which covers the surfaces of internal
Epithelium
organs and body parts.
The process of a layer of tissue forming to cover exposed internal
Epithelialization
vessels or organs as part of the healing process.
Increased blood flow to an area of skin due to injury or infection. The
Erythema
skin may become redder, purple or darker.
ETCO2 indicates the amount of carbon dioxide in the air exhaled
ETCO2 when breathing. A high level of carbon dioxide in the exhaled breath
is an indicator of good breathing.
Extremity Extremities of the body including arms, legs, fingers and toes.
The proportion of resuscitation time without spontaneous circulation
Fraction
during which chest compressions were administered.
High altitude cerebral High altitude cerebral edema is altitude sickness affecting the brain,
edema (HACE) behaviour and alertness.
HAINES stands for “high arm in endangered spine” and the positions
HAINES positions are adapted recovery positions which raise the arm to support the

Hanks’ Balanced Salt Hanks’ Balanced Salt Solution is a solution composed of salts and
Solution (HBSS) supplemented with glucose.
The process of stopping bleeding. The blood thickens and clots to
Haemostasis
keep blood inside the damaged tissues.
High altitude High altitude pulmonary edema is altitude sickness affecting the
pulmonary edema lungs and breathing.
(HAPE)
Hydrocolloids are a type of dressings for wounds which maintain a
Hydrocolloids
moist and protected environment ideal for the healing process.
526 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Hydrogel dressings are used on wounds and provide a moist
Hydrogel
environment to promote healing.
Hyperlipidemia A high concentration of fats or lipids in the blood.
Hypernatremia High sodium levels in the body.
When the body or area of the body receives an excessive supply of
Hyperoxia
oxygen.
Hyperventilating Rapid or deep breathing, usually caused by anxiety or a panic attack.
Hyponatremia Low sodium levels in the body.
Hypopnea The breathing is very soft and shallow, usually during sleep.
Hypoxic means there are low levels of oxygen in the person’s blood
Hypoxic resulting in their organs and tissues not receiving enough oxygen to
work properly.
In vitro studies are studies done in a controlled environment such as
In vitro studies
a petri dish.
Ischaemia is a condition in which the blood flow (and therefore
Ischaemia
oxygen) is restricted or reduced in a part of the body.
Los Angeles Prehospital Stroke Screen (LAPSS) is a method of
LAPSS
identifying potential stroke with glucose measurement.
Often called the voice box, the larynx is in the throat and holds the
Larynx
vocal cords.
Melbourne Ambulance Stroke Screen is a scale including glucose
MASS
measurement.
Mbar Millibar, a metric unit of pressure.
An inflammation of the membranes surrounding the brain and
Meningitis
spinal cord.
Meta-analysis Examining many different research studies to find common results.
It is how many people have a disease in a particular society or
Morbidity of a disease
population.
The number of deaths within a particular society and within a
Mortality
particular period of time.
MRSA is a type of bacterial infection, which is resistant to some types
MRSA
of antibiotics.
Myocardial revascularization is a medical procedure to improve
Myocardial
blood flow to the heart (myocardium). Examples include angioplasty,
revascularization
stent or surgical coronary artery bypass grafting.
GLOSSARY | 527

Narrowcasting is the transmission of television programmes to a
Narrowcasting
localized or specialized audience.
Necrotizing fasciitis is a rare but serious infection that affects the
Necrotizing fasciitis
tissue beneath the skin and surrounding muscles and organs.
New-born A new-born is a baby from birth to about one month old.
The National Institutes of Health (NIH), is part of the US Department
National Institutes of
of Health and Human Services and is the nation’s medical research
Health (NIH)
agency.
No-flow time is the reported time from cardiac arrest to the start of
No-flow time
bystander CPR.
Non-asphyxial cardiac Cardiac arrest due to cardiac disease origin.
arrest
A dressing that does not seal the wound, allowing air and moisture
Non-occlusive dressing
to pass through it.
NSAIDs Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
An occlusive dressing seals the wound and does not allow air or
Occlusive dressing
moisture to pass through it.
Oral glucose Glucose is swallowed by mouth.
administration
This is all things related to the oesophagus which is the pipe that
Oesophageal
carries food from the mouth to the stomach.
P6 is a point on the forearm that can be stimulated using techniques
P6
taken from acupuncture.
A defibrillator that can analyse the heart rhythm of children. It also
Paediatric defibrillator
delivers the right level of energy in the electric shock for children.
Painkiller is an analgesic such as paracetamol (also known as
Painkiller
acetaminophen) or ibuprofen.
A painkiller which also reduces fever. Acetaminophen is the common
Paracetamol
name for the same product in some countries.
The abnormal state or function of part of the body due to disease or
Pathophysiology
injury.
Perceived A person’s subjective perception of the risks.
susceptibility
Perineal trauma or tearing is damage to the tissues and muscles in
Perineal trauma
the vagina and anal area during childbirth.
528 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

Peripheral pulse is a pulse at one of the peripheries of the body such
Peripheral pulse
as at the wrists or on the top of or just behind the feet.
A sticking plaster is a small adhesive dressing, widely available in
Plaster pharmacies. They are used on minor cuts and grazes that do not
require a bandage.
Pleuritic pain Pain when breathing in and out.
PPE Personal protective equipment such as face masks or gloves.
Pressure A technique for immobilizing and applying pressure to a leg or arm,
immobilization to slow down the movement of the venom to vital organs.
technique
Pruritus The sensation of itchiness on the skin.
Radial pulse A pulse that can be felt on your wrist.
Involves individuals taking turns to coach each other on different
Reciprocal learning
knowledge or skills that they have already mastered.
Rhythm check timing refers to the timing of the analysis of the heart
Rhythm check timing rhythm. This can be done by a first aid provider taking the pulse
immediately after the defibrillator has given a shock (or not).
Pain in the sciatic nerve (a nerve that starts in the lower back and
Sciatica
extends down the legs).
Sexual and gender-based violence (SGBV) is a broad term referring
Sexual and
to any harmful act that leads to (or is likely to lead to) physical, sexual,
gender-based violence
or psychological harm or suffering to someone on the basis of their
(SGBV)
gender.
Signs of life Signs of life include things such as moving, crying or coughing.
An injection using a short needle to inject medication just under the
Subcutaneous
skin.
Sublingual glucose Glucose is put under the tongue where it is absorbed into the
administration bloodstream.
Pain just below or behind the sternum or breastbone (the bone in
Substernal pain
the centre of the chest).
Sucking or blowing An open chest wound in which air enters and exits with each
chest wounds breathing movement.
Tension pneumothorax can be caused by an open chest wound. Air
enters the cavity (the pleural space) between the lung and the chest
Tension pneumothorax
wall. The air builds up, creating pressure on the lung and reducing its
ability to expand, making breathing difficult.
GLOSSARY | 529

Thermal burns are burns caused by heat such as fire, steam, or
Thermal burns
contact with something hot.
The use of medication to break down blood clots that have formed in
Thrombolysis
the blood vessels.
Thrombolytic therapy is a treatment to break down blood clots to
Thrombolytic therapy
improve blood flow.
Transcutaneous pulse Transcutaneous pulse oximetry is a non-invasive method of
oximetry measuring the oxygen level of the tissue below the skin.
Transthoracic Electrical activity in the chest that varies with changes in body size
impedance and composition.
Tympanic temperature The tympanic temperature is that taken from the ear.
Umbilical cord prolapse means that part of the umbilical cord passes
Umbilical cord out of the uterus before the baby. This can result in pressure being
prolapse applied to the cord as the baby is born, interfering with the supply of
oxygenated blood reaching the baby during birth.
Uvula The bit of flesh that hangs down at the back of the throat.
The Valsalva manoeuvre involves taking a deep breath in, closing the
nose and mouth and gently blowing out. This increases pressure in
Valsalva manœuvre
the chest resulting in reduced cardiac output and lower blood
pressure.
Ventricular fibrillation is an abnormal heart rhythm in which the
heart trembles and no longer pumps blood. This means blood
Ventricular fibrillation circulation in the body stops, so it is a life-threatening condition. This
type of heart rhythm (fibrillation) is most responsive to a shock from
a defibrillator.
Visceral pain is pain from inside the pelvis, abdomen or chest. The
Visceral pain nerve supply is less specific than skin nerves, and the pain in these
areas may be vaguer than skin pain.
Visual analogue scale A scale used to rate the severity of breathing difficulties during a test.
Vital signs that a first aid provider may be able to monitor include the
Vital signs
person’s breathing or pulse rate.
Warming the body using items that actively warm it such as an
Warmed actively
electric heating blanket.
Warming the body using items that will help the body retain heat
Warmed passively
such as a sleeping bag.
Washout periods Washout periods are the periods between administering treatments.
Slightly protruding solid red pimple with no liquid that fades when
Weal
the skin is pulled.

THE FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLES OF THE
INTERNATIONAL RED CROSS AND RED CRESCENT
MOVEMENT
Humanity
The International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, born of a desire to bring assistance
without discrimination to the wounded on the battlefield, endeavours, in its international and
national capacity, to prevent and alleviate human suffering wherever it may be found. Its
purpose is to protect life and health and to ensure respect for the human being. It promotes
mutual understanding, friendship, cooperation and lasting peace amongst all peoples.
Impartiality
It makes no discrimination as to nationality, race, religious beliefs, class or political opinions. It
endeavours to relieve the suffering of individuals, being guided solely by their needs, and to give
priority to the most urgent cases of distress.
Neutrality
In order to enjoy the confidence of all, the Movement may not take sides in hostilities or engage
at any time in controversies of a political, racial, religious or ideological nature.
Independence
The Movement is independent. The National Societies, while auxiliaries in the humanitarian
services of their governments and subject to the laws of their respective countries, must always
maintain their autonomy so that they may be able at all times to act in accordance with the
principles of the Movement.
Voluntary service
It is a voluntary relief movement not prompted in any manner by desire for gain.
Unity
There can be only one Red Cross or Red Crescent Society in any one country. It must be open to
all. It must carry on its humanitarian work throughout its territory.
Universality
The International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, in which all societies have equal
status and share equal responsibilities and duties in helping each other, is worldwide.
592 | International first aid, resuscitation and education guidelines 2025 ⚙

REFERENCES | 593

The International Federation of Red Cross and
RedCrescent Societies (IFRC) is the world’s largest
humanitarian network, with 191 National Red Cross and
Red Crescent Societies and around 17 million
volunteers. Our volunteers are present in communities
before, during and after a crisis or disaster. We work in
the most hard to reach and complex settings in the
world, saving lives and promoting human dignity. We
support communities to become stronger and more
resilient places where people can live safe and healthy
lives, and have opportunities to thrive.
first.aid@ifrc.org globalfirstaidcentre.org
